> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — ALGORITHMS: Foundational Concepts for Data Structures

## 1.1 What is an Algorithm? — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> An **Algorithm** is a finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous, executable instructions formulated to solve a specific computational problem, transforming a given set of inputs into a desired output within a finite amount of time.

In the context of the **PEEET526 — Data Structures** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 aligned), an algorithm is the *blueprint* upon which every data structure operation (insertion, deletion, traversal, searching, sorting) is built. It is the **logical procedure** independent of any programming language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 — Apply):** Students must be able to *analyze* the time and space complexity of algorithms using **asymptotic notations** and *implement* classical recursive/iterative procedures in C.

---

## 1.2 The 5 Essential Characteristics of an Algorithm

A procedure qualifies as a true algorithm **only if** it satisfies all five properties — these are board-favorite 3-mark questions:

| # | Property | Plain English Meaning | Example Violation |
|---|----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Input** | Zero or more well-specified inputs must be supplied. | Reading from a non-existent file. |
| 2 | **Output** | At least one well-defined output must be produced. | A function that prints nothing and returns nothing. |
| 3 | **Definiteness** | Every instruction must be clear and unambiguous. | "Add a suitable number" — what is suitable? |
| 4 | **Finiteness** | The algorithm must terminate after a **finite** number of steps. | `while(true) { }` — infinite loop. |
| 5 | **Effectiveness** | Each step must be basic enough to be carried out in principle. | "Compute the 10⁹⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰th prime" — impractical. |

---

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy 🍳 — The Recipe Metaphor

Imagine a **recipe for making tea**:

1. Boil water.
2. Add tea leaves.
3. Add sugar.
4. Stir for 2 minutes.
5. Strain into a cup.
6. Add milk (optional).
7. **STOP.**

This recipe is a perfect algorithm:
- **Input:** Water, tea leaves, sugar.
- **Output:** A cup of tea.
- **Definiteness:** "Stir for 2 minutes" — exact.
- **Finiteness:** It ends at Step 7.
- **Effectiveness:** Any person can execute it.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked "Is every program an algorithm?", answer **NO**. A program may contain infinite loops (violates Finiteness) or ambiguous operations. But every algorithm *can* be implemented as a program.

---

## 1.4 Algorithm Design Approaches (KTU Module 1 Scope)

The KTU 2024 syllabus covers these paradigms as the foundation of Data Structures:

| Approach | Core Idea | Classic Example |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Divide and Conquer** | Split problem into sub-problems, solve recursively, combine. | Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search. |
| **Greedy Method** | Make locally optimal choices at each step. | Dijkstra's, Prim's, Kruskal's (covered in Module 3/4). |
| **Dynamic Programming** | Store sub-problem results to avoid recomputation. | Fibonacci, Matrix Chain, Knapsack. |
| **Backtracking** | Try all options, abandon partial solutions that fail. | N-Queens, Sudoku. |
| **Brute Force** | Try every possible solution. | Linear Search. |

This module primarily focuses on **Divide and Conquer** and the **complexity analysis framework** required to evaluate all of them.

---

## 1.5 Why Analyze Algorithms? — The Need for Asymptotic Analysis

Consider two algorithms that compute the sum of the first $n$ natural numbers:

- **Algorithm A (Iterative):** Uses a `for` loop. Time = $T_A(n) = 3n + 2$ operations.
- **Algorithm B (Formula-based):** Uses Gauss's formula $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$. Time = $T_B(n) = 4$ operations (constant).

For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$:
- $T_A(10^6) = 3{,}000{,}002$ ops.
- $T_B(10^6) = 4$ ops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** As $n \to \infty$ (very large inputs), the **growth rate** of operations matters far more than the constant factors. This is precisely why we use **asymptotic notation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Asymptotic Notations — The Heart of Algorithm Analysis

Asymptotic notations describe the **upper, lower, and tight bounds** of an algorithm's running time as the input size $n$ approaches infinity. KTU examiners frequently test these in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention:** We use $T(n)$ to denote the time (or space) taken by an algorithm as a function of input size $n$. We always consider $n \geq n_0$ for some threshold $n_0 > 0$.

### 2.1.1 Big-O Notation — $O$ — The Upper Bound (Worst Case)

**Definition:** $T(n) = O(g(n))$ if there exist **positive constants** $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 0$ such that:

$$T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

It expresses the *worst-case* scenario — the algorithm will **not take more time** than $c \cdot g(n)$ beyond $n_0$.

**Intuition:** Big-O is like a **ceiling**. Your salary will not exceed ₹10 LPA in the foreseeable future.

### 2.1.2 Big-Omega Notation — $\Omega$ — The Lower Bound (Best Case)

**Definition:** $T(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ if there exist **positive constants** $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 0$ such that:

$$c \cdot g(n) \leq T(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

It expresses the *best-case* scenario — the algorithm will **take at least** $c \cdot g(n)$ time beyond $n_0$.

**Intuition:** Big-Omega is like a **floor**. You will earn at least ₹3 LPA.

### 2.1.3 Big-Theta Notation — $\Theta$ — The Tight Bound (Average Order)

**Definition:** $T(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if there exist **positive constants** $c_1, c_2 > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 0$ such that:

$$c_1 \cdot g(n) \leq T(n) \leq c_2 \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

It expresses the **exact order of growth** — $T(n)$ grows at the same rate as $g(n)$ within constant factors.

**Intuition:** Big-Theta is like a **bracket**. You earn between ₹3 LPA and ₹10 LPA.

### 2.1.4 Little-o Notation — $o$ — Strict Upper Bound

**Definition:** $T(n) = o(g(n))$ if for **every** $c > 0$, there exists $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$T(n) < c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

Equivalent to: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{T(n)}{g(n)} = 0$.

**Example:** $5n = o(n^2)$ since $\frac{5n}{n^2} = \frac{5}{n} \to 0$.

### 2.1.5 Little-omega Notation — $\omega$ — Strict Lower Bound

**Definition:** $T(n) = \omega(g(n))$ if for **every** $c > 0$, there exists $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$c \cdot g(n) < T(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

Equivalent to: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{T(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$.

---

## 2.2 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table. It covers ~90% of all KTU Part-A asymptotic questions.

| Notation | Mathematical Form | KTU Interpretation | Constant? | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------|-------------|
| $O(1)$ | Constant | Hash table lookup (average) | Yes | Array indexing |
| $O(\log n)$ | Logarithmic | Binary search | No | Divide in half |
| $O(n)$ | Linear | Linear search, single loop | No | One pass |
| $O(n \log n)$ | Linearithmic | Merge sort, Quick sort (avg) | No | Efficient sorts |
| $O(n^2)$ | Quadratic | Bubble, Selection, Insertion sort | No | Nested loops |
| $O(n^3)$ | Cubic | Naive matrix multiplication | No | Triple nested loops |
| $O(2^n)$ | Exponential | Recursive Fibonacci, subsets | No | Brute force |
| $O(n!)$ | Factorial | Travelling Salesman (brute force) | No | Permutations |

### 2.2.1 Standard Complexity Comparison Rule

For $a > b > 1$ and $k > 1$:

$$O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Proving Big-O via Limits (Most Asked in KTU):** If $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{T(n)}{g(n)} = L$ where $0 < L < \infty$, then $T(n) = \Theta(g(n))$. If $L = 0$, then $T(n) = o(g(n))$. If $L = \infty$, then $T(n) = \omega(g(n))$.

### 2.2.2 Key Summation Formulas (Used in Recurrence Solving)

| Series | Closed Form |
|--------|-------------|
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i$ | $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$ |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i^2$ | $\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} = \Theta(n^3)$ |
| $\sum_{i=0}^{n} 2^i$ | $2^{n+1} - 1 = \Theta(2^n)$ |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log i$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{i}$ (harmonic) | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{\log n} i$ | $\Theta((\log n)^2)$ |

---

## 2.3 Master Theorem (For Divide-and-Conquer Recurrences)

For recurrences of the form:

$$T(n) = a \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + \Theta(n^k \log^p n)$$

where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, $k \geq 0$, and $p \geq 0$:

| Case | Condition | Result | Interpretation |
|------|-----------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | $a > b^k$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | Recursion dominates |
| 2 | $a = b^k$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^k \log^{p+1} n)$ | Same order, plus log factor |
| 3 | $a < b^k$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^k \log^p n)$ | Non-recursive part dominates |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Examples:**
> - Binary Search: $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) \Rightarrow a=1, b=2, k=0, p=0 \Rightarrow$ Case 2 $\Rightarrow O(\log n)$.
> - Merge Sort: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) \Rightarrow a=2, b=2, k=1, p=0 \Rightarrow$ Case 2 $\Rightarrow O(n \log n)$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Algorithm Used | Complexity |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| Google Maps — Shortest Path | Dijkstra's | $O((V + E) \log V)$ |
| Database Indexing | B-Tree Search | $O(\log n)$ |
| Image Compression | Huffman Coding | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Cryptography (RSA) | Modular Exponentiation | $O(\log n)$ |
| AI Game Playing | Minimax | $O(b^d)$ exponential |
| Network Routing | Bellman-Ford | $O(VE)$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Linear Search Complexity Derivation

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
LINEAR_SEARCH(A, n, key)
1.  for i ← 1 to n do
2.      if A[i] == key then
3.          return i
4.  return -1
```

**Step-by-step cost analysis:**

| Line | Operation | Cost per execution | Times executed |
|------|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Loop init `i = 1` | $c_1$ | 1 |
| 1 | Loop test `i ≤ n` | $c_2$ | $n + 1$ (fails once more) |
| 1 | Increment `i++` | $c_3$ | $n$ |
| 2 | Comparison `A[i] == key` | $c_4$ | $n$ |
| 2 | Branch | $c_5$ | $n$ |
| 3 | Return (if found) | $c_6$ | 1 (at most) |
| 4 | Return -1 | $c_7$ | 1 (at most) |

**Total time function:**

$$T(n) = c_1 + c_2(n+1) + c_3 n + c_4 n + c_5 n + c_6 + c_7$$

**Grouping constants:**

$$T(n) = (c_2 + c_3 + c_4 + c_5)n + (c_1 + c_2 + c_6 + c_7)$$

Let $a = c_2 + c_3 + c_4 + c_5$ and $b = c_1 + c_2 + c_6 + c_7$:

$$T(n) = a \cdot n + b$$

**Best case (key at A[1]):** $T(n) = O(1)$.

**Worst case (key at A[n] or absent):** $T(n) = O(n)$.

**Average case (assuming uniform probability):**

$$T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \cdot (\text{cost when key at } i) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(an + b - (a)(i-1))$$

$$= an + b - \frac{a}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = an + b - \frac{a(n-1)}{2} = \frac{an}{2} + b + \frac{a}{2}$$

Thus, $T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \Theta(n)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Binary Search Recurrence Derivation

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
BINARY_SEARCH(A, low, high, key)
1.  if low > high then return -1
2.  mid ← (low + high) / 2
3.  if A[mid] == key then return mid
4.  else if A[mid] < key
5.      return BINARY_SEARCH(A, mid+1, high, key)
6.  else
7.      return BINARY_SEARCH(A, low, mid-1, key)
```

**Recurrence relation** (worst case, key not found):

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + c$$

where the $+c$ accounts for the constant-time comparison and mid calculation in each call.

**Solving by Master's Method:** $a = 1, b = 2, k = 0, p = 0$.

$$a = 1, \quad b^k = 2^0 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = b^k$$

**Case 2 applies** $\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^0 \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(\log n)$.

**Verification by backward substitution (alternate method):**

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + c$$

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{4}\right) + 2c$$

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2^k}\right) + kc$$

The recursion stops when $\dfrac{n}{2^k} = 1 \Rightarrow k = \log_2 n$.

$$T(n) = T(1) + c \log_2 n = O(\log n) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Merge Sort Recurrence Derivation

Merge Sort divides the array into two halves, recursively sorts each, then merges them.

**Recurrence:**

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n)$$

The $\Theta(n)$ term comes from the MERGE routine, which traverses both halves once.

**Step 1 — Apply Master Theorem:** $a = 2, b = 2, k = 1, p = 0$.

$$a = 2, \quad b^k = 2^1 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = b^k$$

**Case 2 applies** $\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^1 \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

**Step 2 — Verification via Recursion Tree:**

At Level 0: cost = $n$. At Level 1: two subproblems of size $n/2$, cost = $2 \times (n/2) = n$. At Level $i$: $2^i$ subproblems of size $n/2^i$, cost = $2^i \times n/2^i = n$.

Total levels = $\log_2 n$ (until subproblem size = 1).

$$T(n) = n \cdot \log_2 n + 1 \cdot n = \Theta(n \log n) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Proving Big-O via Limits

**Problem:** Prove that $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 8 = O(n^2)$.

**Proof using limit method:**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{T(n)}{g(n)} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{3n^2 + 5n + 8}{n^2}$$

**Step 1 — Divide every term by $n^2$:**

$$= \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(3 + \frac{5}{n} + \frac{8}{n^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply limit to each term:**

$$= 3 + \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{5}{n} + \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{8}{n^2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$= 3 + 0 + 0 = 3$$

Since the limit $L = 3$ is a finite non-zero constant, by definition $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$, which implies $T(n) = O(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Recursive Fibonacci Complexity (Naive)

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
FIB(n)
1.  if n ≤ 1 then return n
2.  else return FIB(n-1) + FIB(n-2)
```

**Recurrence:**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c$$

**Lower bound analysis (assuming only $T(n-1)$):**

$$T(n) \geq T(n-1) + c \geq T(n-2) + 2c \geq \ldots \geq T(0) + nc = \Omega(n)$$

**Upper bound analysis (assuming $T(n-1) \leq T(n-2) \cdot 2$):**

$$T(n) \leq 2 \, T(n-1) + c \leq 2(2 T(n-2) + c) + c \leq 2^k T(0) + (2^k - 1)c = O(2^n)$$

Therefore, $T(n) = \Theta(2^n)$ — which is why naive Fibonacci is **infeasible** for large $n$ and motivates Dynamic Programming (Module 3/4 content).

---

## 3.6 Comparative Complexity Curve Reference

For $n = 60$, the practical operations required:

| Complexity | Operations for $n=60$ | Practical? |
|------------|----------------------|-----------|
| $O(\log n)$ | $\approx 6$ | ⚡ Instant |
| $O(n)$ | $60$ | ⚡ Instant |
| $O(n \log n)$ | $\approx 354$ | ⚡ Instant |
| $O(n^2)$ | $3{,}600$ | ✅ Quick |
| $O(2^n)$ | $1.15 \times 10^{18}$ | ❌ Impossible |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** When asked to compare two algorithms, students often confuse the *constants*. **Always use asymptotic notation** for $n \to \infty$. Constants matter only for small $n$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧭 STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Algorithm Analysis Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram shows the complete workflow a KTU student must follow when analyzing any algorithm in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Algorithm A] --> B[Identify Input Size n]
    B --> C[Identify Basic Operations]
    C --> D[Count Cost per Statement]
    D --> E{Recurrence Based?}
    E -- No --> F[Sum Total Operations T of n]
    E -- Yes --> G[Form Recurrence Relation]
    G --> H{Recurrence Type?}
    H -- I1 Linear --> I1[Use Backward Substitution]
    H -- I2 Divide and Conquer --> I2[Apply Master Theorem]
    H -- I3 Tree Method --> I3[Draw Recursion Tree]
    I1 --> J[Obtain Closed Form T of n]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K{Determine Bound Needed}
    K -- L1 Upper --> L1[Apply Big O Definition]
    K -- L2 Lower --> L2[Apply Big Omega]
    K -- L3 Tight --> L3[Apply Big Theta]
    L1 --> M[Final Asymptotic Bound]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    M --> N[Report: T of n = Big Theta or O or Omega of g of n]
```

---

## 4.2 Asymptotic Notation Visual Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[All Functions f of n] --> Omega[f of n = Omega of g of n Lower Bound]
    Root --> Theta[f of n = Theta of g of n Tight Bound]
    Root --> BigO[f of n = Big O of g of n Upper Bound]
    Omega --> LittleO[Little o Strict Upper]
    Omega --> LittleW[Little omega Strict Lower]
    BigO --> LittleO
    BigO --> LittleW
    Theta --> BestCase[Best Case Equal Worst Case]
    BigO --> WorstCase[Worst Case Bound]
    Omega --> BestCase
```

---

## 4.3 Master Theorem Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[T of n = a T of n by b + Theta of n to the k log to the p n] --> B{Compare a with b to the k}
    B -- a greater than b to the k --> C1[Case 1: Recursion Dominates]
    C1 --> D1[Answer: Theta of n to the log base b of a]
    B -- a equals b to the k --> C2[Case 2: Balanced]
    C2 --> D2{Is p greater than minus 1?}
    D2 -- Yes --> E2[Answer: Theta of n to the k log to the p plus 1 n]
    D2 -- No --> F2[Answer: Theta of n to the k log n]
    B -- a less than b to the k --> C3[Case 3: Work Dominates]
    C3 --> D3[Answer: Theta of n to the k log to the p n]
```

---

## 4.4 Divide-and-Conquer High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Problem of size n] --> Q{Divide Step}
    Q --> S1[Subproblem 1 of size n by b]
    Q --> S2[Subproblem 2 of size n by b]
    Q --> S3[Subproblem a of size n by b]
    S1 --> R1[Conquer Recursively]
    S2 --> R2[Conquer Recursively]
    S3 --> R3[Conquer Recursively]
    R1 --> M[Combine Step Theta of f of n]
    R2 --> M
    R3 --> M
    M --> Sol[Final Solution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level functional flow maps the canonical **Divide $\to$ Conquer $\to$ Combine** paradigm that governs every algorithm discussed in this module (Binary Search, Merge Sort, Quick Sort, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Direct definition / short derivation.
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with internal choice between two full questions.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an algorithm. List and briefly explain any four characteristics of an algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

An **algorithm** is a finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous instructions that takes a set of inputs, processes them, and produces the desired output in a finite amount of time.

**Four essential characteristics:**

1. **Input:** An algorithm must accept zero or more well-specified inputs from the caller before processing begins. Without specified inputs, the algorithm has no data to operate on.
2. **Output:** An algorithm must produce at least one well-defined output that bears a defined relationship to the inputs. The output is the *result* of the computation.
3. **Definiteness (Unambiguity):** Every instruction in the algorithm must be clear, precise, and have only one interpretation. There must be no ambiguity about what each step does.
4. **Finiteness:** The algorithm must terminate after executing a *finite* number of steps. A procedure that runs forever (infinite loop) is not a valid algorithm.
5. **Effectiveness:** Each step must be sufficiently basic that it can be carried out, in principle, by a person using only paper and pencil. Operations must be feasible.

*(Any 4 explained. 3 marks total: 1 for definition + 2 for characteristics.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega notations with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Notation | Meaning | Mathematical Form | Example |
|----------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| **Big-O ($O$)** | Upper bound (worst case guarantee) | $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ | Linear Search worst case: $O(n)$ |
| **Big-$\Omega$** | Lower bound (best case guarantee) | $c \cdot g(n) \leq T(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ | Linear Search best case: $\Omega(1)$ |
| **Big-$\Theta$** | Tight bound (both upper and lower) | $c_1 g(n) \leq T(n) \leq c_2 g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ | Linear Search average: $\Theta(n)$ |

**Key Relationship:** $T(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if and only if $T(n) = O(g(n))$ **AND** $T(n) = \Omega(g(n))$.

**Example:** For $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 8$:
- $T(n) = O(n^2)$ — grows no faster than $n^2$.
- $T(n) = \Omega(n^2)$ — grows at least as fast as $n^2$.
- Therefore, $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ — grows at the same order as $n^2$.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain asymptotic notations. Define Big-O notation. Show that $T(n) = 5n^3 + 3n^2 + 7 = O(n^3)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Asymptotic Notations — Definition:**

Asymptotic notations are mathematical tools used to describe the **running time (or space)** of an algorithm in terms of the input size $n$ as $n$ becomes very large (i.e., as $n \to \infty$). They allow us to classify algorithms by their **order of growth**, abstracting away hardware-dependent constants.

**Big-O Notation — Formal Definition:**

$T(n) = O(g(n))$ if and only if there exist **positive constants** $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 1$ such that for all $n \geq n_0$:

$$T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$$

In words: $T(n)$ grows **no faster** than $g(n)$ within a constant factor, for sufficiently large $n$.

**Proof that $T(n) = 5n^3 + 3n^2 + 7 = O(n^3)$:**

We must find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 1$ such that $T(n) \leq c \cdot n^3$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Step 1 — Write the inequality:**

$$5n^3 + 3n^2 + 7 \leq c \cdot n^3$$

**Step 2 — Identify upper bounds for lower-order terms:**

For $n \geq 1$:
- $n^2 \leq n^3$, so $3n^2 \leq 3n^3$.
- $7 \leq 7n^3$ (since $n^3 \geq 1$).

**Step 3 — Substitute and combine:**

$$5n^3 + 3n^2 + 7 \leq 5n^3 + 3n^3 + 7n^3 = 15n^3$$

**Step 4 — Identify the constants:**

We have $T(n) \leq 15n^3$ for all $n \geq 1$. Therefore, choose:

$$c = 15, \quad n_0 = 1$$

**Step 5 — Conclude by definition:**

By the definition of Big-O, since $T(n) \leq 15n^3$ for all $n \geq 1$:

$$T(n) = 5n^3 + 3n^2 + 7 = O(n^3) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ using the Master Theorem. State the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the form:**

The recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ matches the Master Theorem form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + \Theta(n^k \log^p n)$.

**Step 2 — Extract parameters:**

$$a = 2, \quad b = 2, \quad k = 1, \quad p = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $b^k$:**

$$b^k = 2^1 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Compare $a$ with $b^k$:**

$$a = 2 \quad \text{and} \quad b^k = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = b^k$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Case:**

This is **Case 2** of the Master Theorem (the work at each level of the recursion tree is balanced).

**Step 6 — Apply Case 2 formula:**

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^k \log^{p+1} n) = \Theta(n^1 \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

**Step 7 — Verification using recursion tree:**

- Level 0: 1 subproblem of size $n$, cost = $n$.
- Level 1: 2 subproblems of size $n/2$, cost = $2 \times n/2 = n$.
- Level $i$: $2^i$ subproblems of size $n/2^i$, cost = $n$.
- Total levels = $\log_2 n$ (until subproblem size reaches 1).
- Total cost = $n \times \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — *(Internal Choice)*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**State and explain the Master Theorem. Apply it to find the time complexity of Merge Sort.**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of the Master Theorem:**

For a recurrence of the form:

$$T(n) = a \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + \Theta(n^k \log^p n)$$

where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, $k \geq 0$, $p \geq 0$, the solution is:

| Case | Condition | Solution |
|------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | $a > b^k$ | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$ |
| 2 | $a = b^k$ | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^k \log^{p+1} n\right)$ |
| 3 | $a < b^k$ | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^k \log^p n\right)$ |

**Explanation of Each Case:**

- **Case 1 ($a > b^k$):** The recursive subproblems dominate the total work. The total time is governed by the number of leaves in the recursion tree, which is $n^{\log_b a}$.
- **Case 2 ($a = b^k$):** The work is balanced across all levels of the recursion tree, each contributing $\Theta(n^k)$. The logarithmic factor $\log^{p+1} n$ reflects the depth of the tree times the per-level cost.
- **Case 3 ($a < b^k$):** The non-recursive work $f(n) = \Theta(n^k \log^p n)$ dominates, so the recurrence's solution is the same as the cost of the root work.

**Application to Merge Sort:**

The recurrence relation for Merge Sort is:

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n)$$

because Merge Sort divides the array into 2 equal halves, recursively sorts each, and merges them in linear time.

Matching with the standard form: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $k = 1$, $p = 0$.

$$b^k = 2^1 = 2, \quad a = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = b^k \Rightarrow \text{Case 2 applies}$$

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^1 \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n \log n) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Write an algorithm for Binary Search. Derive its time complexity using recurrence and solve it.**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
BINARY_SEARCH(A[0..n-1], low, high, key)
1.  if low > high
2.      return -1
3.  mid ← (low + high) / 2
4.  if A[mid] == key
5.      return mid
6.  else if A[mid] < key
7.      return BINARY_SEARCH(A, mid+1, high, key)
8.  else
9.      return BINARY_SEARCH(A, low, mid-1, key)
```

**Recurrence Relation:**

In each recursive call, the problem size is halved (we search either the left half or the right half). The cost of the comparison and mid calculation at each level is $O(1) = c$.

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + c, \quad T(1) = d \text{ (constant base case)}$$

**Solution by Repeated Substitution (Backward Substitution Method):**

**Step 1:** Expand $T(n/2)$:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + c$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $T(n/2) = T(n/4) + c$:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{4}\right) + 2c$$

**Step 3:** Generalize to $k$ substitutions:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2^k}\right) + k \cdot c$$

**Step 4 — Termination:** The recursion stops when the subproblem size becomes 1:

$$\dfrac{n}{2^k} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2^k = n \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \log_2 n$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$T(n) = T(1) + c \log_2 n = d + c \log_2 n = \Theta(\log n)$$

**Conclusion:** The time complexity of Binary Search is $\Theta(\log n)$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base case:** Always specify $T(1) = O(1)$ or $T(0) = O(1)$ in your recurrence.
> 2. **Misidentifying $a, b, k$:** A common error is writing $T(n/2)$ as $T(n-1)$ — check whether the problem is halved or decremented.
> 3. **Skipping the limit condition in Master Theorem:** You must state $a = b^k$ *before* applying Case 2, not after.
> 4. **Confusing $\log_b a$ with $a$:** In Case 1, the exponent is $\log_b a$, not just $a$.
> 5. **Including constants in Big-O proofs:** Big-O ignores constants — the proof is about existence of $c$, not its value.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your final 5-minute revision before the exam.**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- [ ] **Algorithm:** Finite, well-defined, unambiguous sequence of instructions.
- [ ] **5 Characteristics:** Input, Output, Definiteness, Finiteness, Effectiveness.
- [ ] **Time Complexity:** $T(n)$ — number of elementary operations as a function of $n$.
- [ ] **Space Complexity:** Auxiliary memory used (excluding input storage).

### 🔑 Must-Know Notations
- [ ] $O(g(n))$ — **Upper bound** (worst case) — $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$.
- [ ] $\Omega(g(n))$ — **Lower bound** (best case) — $T(n) \geq c \cdot g(n)$.
- [ ] $\Theta(g(n))$ — **Tight bound** — both $O$ and $\Omega$ hold.
- [ ] $o(g(n))$ — **Strict upper** — limit ratio is $0$.
- [ ] $\omega(g(n))$ — **Strict lower** — limit ratio is $\infty$.

### 🔑 Must-Know Complexities
- [ ] Linear Search: $O(n)$ worst, $\Omega(1)$ best, $\Theta(n)$ average.
- [ ] Binary Search: $\Theta(\log n)$ — uses Master Theorem Case 2.
- [ ] Merge Sort: $\Theta(n \log n)$ — uses Master Theorem Case 2.
- [ ] Bubble / Selection / Insertion Sort: $O(n^2)$ worst case.
- [ ] Recursive Fibonacci (naive): $\Theta(2^n)$ — **exponential**.

### 🔑 Must-Know Techniques
- [ ] **Substitution method** for solving recurrences.
- [ ] **Recursion tree method** for visualization.
- [ ] **Master Theorem** for divide-and-conquer recurrences.
- [ ] **Limit method** for proving asymptotic bounds.
- [ ] **Loop-based cost counting** for iterative algorithms.

### 🔑 Master Theorem Quick Reference
- [ ] Binary Search: $a=1, b=2, k=0 \Rightarrow \Theta(\log n)$.
- [ ] Merge Sort: $a=2, b=2, k=1 \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$.
- [ ] Strassen's Matrix Multiplication: $a=7, b=2, k=2 \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$.
- [ ] Karatsuba Multiplication: $a=3, b=2, k=1 \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$.

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] ❌ Writing $O(2n)$ instead of $O(n)$ — constants don't matter.
- [ ] ❌ Writing $O(n^2 + n)$ instead of $O(n^2)$ — keep highest-order term.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing worst case with average case.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting base case $T(0) = O(1)$ in recurrence proofs.
- [ ] ❌ Using $T(n) = O(g(n))$ in the wrong direction (should be $\leq$).

### 🔑 Order of Growth Hierarchy (Slowest → Fastest)
$$O(1) < O(\log \log n) < O(\log n) < O(\sqrt{n}) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Analysis** is the systematic process of evaluating the **efficiency** of an algorithm in terms of two primary computational resources: **Time Complexity** (execution time) and **Space Complexity** (auxiliary memory consumed), typically expressed as a function of the input size **n**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET526)**, *Performance Analysis* forms the foundational pillar of Module 1 because it equips the engineer with the mathematical language to **predict**, **compare**, and **optimize** algorithms *before* implementation. Rather than timing an actual program (which depends on hardware, compiler, and OS), we analyze the algorithm abstractly using **Asymptotic Notations**.

### The Two Pillars of Performance

| Pillar | Symbol | What it Measures | Engineering Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Complexity** | $T(n)$ | Number of primitive operations | *"How long will this run as data grows?"* |
| **Space Complexity** | $S(n)$ | Memory units (auxiliary + input) | *"Will it fit in RAM at scale?"* |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Book Hunt"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Finding a Book in a Library**
>
> Imagine you are looking for a specific book in a library with **n** shelves.
>
> - **Linear Search** = Walking shelf by shelf, from the entrance. In the *worst case*, you walk past all **n** shelves. Cost = **$O(n)$**.
> - **Binary Search** = Knowing the library is sorted alphabetically, you go to the *middle* shelf, eliminate half, and repeat. Cost = **$O(\log n)$**.
> - **Direct Access (Hash)** = A librarian hands you the exact shelf number. Cost = **$O(1)$**.
>
> *Performance Analysis is the formal scoring system that tells you, mathematically, which strategy wins as **n** grows to millions.*

## 1.3 Why Asymptotic Analysis?

We **cannot** rely on raw execution time because:
- The same code runs faster on an i9 than on a Pentium.
- The same algorithm in C runs faster than in Python.

So we ignore hardware and count **primitive operations** (assignments, comparisons, arithmetic, function calls) and express the count as a function of **n**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primitive Operation:** A low-level instruction whose execution time is bounded by a constant $c$ (i.e., independent of $n$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Asymptotic Notations — The Heart of Performance Analysis

For a function $T(n)$ representing the running time, we compare it against a simpler function $f(n)$ as $n \to \infty$.

### 2.1.1 Big-O Notation — $O(g(n))$ — *Upper Bound* (Worst Case)

$$T(n) = O(g(n)) \iff \exists \; c > 0,\; n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le T(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \;\; \forall \; n \ge n_0$$

**Intuition:** $T(n)$ grows **no faster than** $g(n)$.

### 2.1.2 Big-Theta Notation — $\Theta(g(n))$ — *Tight Bound* (Average/Exact Order)

$$T(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff \exists \; c_1, c_2 > 0,\; n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } c_1 \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n) \;\; \forall \; n \ge n_0$$

**Intuition:** $T(n)$ grows **at the same rate as** $g(n)$.

### 2.1.3 Big-Omega Notation — $\Omega(g(n))$ — *Lower Bound* (Best Case)

$$T(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \iff \exists \; c > 0,\; n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \;\; \forall \; n \ge n_0$$

**Intuition:** $T(n)$ grows **at least as fast as** $g(n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** For any function $T(n)$, if $T(n) = \Theta(g(n))$, then **both** $T(n) = O(g(n))$ and $T(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ hold simultaneously.

## 2.2 Little-o and Little-omega (Tighter Bounds)

| Notation | Name | Formal Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $o(g(n))$ | Strict upper bound | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{T(n)}{g(n)} = 0$ |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | Strict lower bound | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{T(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$ |

## 2.3 Best, Worst, and Average Case

For a given algorithm and input size $n$:

$$T_{\text{best}}(n) = \min_{I \in \mathcal{I}_n} T(I)$$

$$T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \max_{I \in \mathcal{I}_n} T(I)$$

$$T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \sum_{I \in \mathcal{I}_n} P(I) \cdot T(I)$$

where $\mathcal{I}_n$ is the set of all inputs of size $n$, and $P(I)$ is the probability of input $I$ (often assumed uniform: $P(I) = 1/|\mathcal{I}_n|$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Worst Case Matters in KTU Exams:** The KTU board often grades algorithmic efficiency on the **worst-case** bound, because it is a *guaranteed* upper limit — vital for safety-critical systems (aerospace, medical, automotive firmware).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Big-O** (Upper) | $T(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$ | Setting SLA / latency budgets |
| 2 | **Big-Θ** (Tight) | $c_1 \cdot g(n) \le T(n) \le c_2 \cdot g(n)$ | Predicting exact resource growth |
| 3 | **Big-Ω** (Lower) | $T(n) \ge c \cdot g(n)$ | Proving a problem is *hard* |
| 4 | **Sum Rule** | $O(f(n)) + O(g(n)) = O(\max(f(n), g(n)))$ | Sequential blocks |
| 5 | **Product Rule** | $O(f(n)) \cdot O(g(n)) = O(f(n) \cdot g(n))$ | Nested loops |
| 6 | **Loop Sum** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$ | Linear nested loops |
| 7 | **Geometric Sum** | $\sum_{i=0}^{\log n} 2^{i} = 2n - 1 = \Theta(n)$ | Recursion tree costs |
| 8 | **Logarithm Rule** | $\log_a b = \dfrac{\log_c b}{\log_c a}$ | Changing base of logs |
| 9 | **Transitivity** | $f = O(g)$ and $g = O(h) \Rightarrow f = O(h)$ | Chaining bounds |
| 10 | **Master Thm.** (preview) | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | Divide-and-conquer |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Search Ranking:** Algorithms analyzed as $O(n \log n)$ allow real-time indexing of **billions** of pages.
- **Embedded Systems (IoT):** Space complexity is critical because microcontrollers have only **KBs of RAM**. An $O(1)$ space algorithm is preferred.
- **Cryptography:** RSA's security relies on factoring being $\Omega(2^n)$ — i.e., *intractably* slow for large $n$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Proving $3n^2 + 5n + 100 = O(n^2)$

We must find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$3n^2 + 5n + 100 \le c \cdot n^2 \quad \forall \; n \ge n_0$$

**Step 1 — Bound each term individually.**

For $n \ge 1$:
- $5n \le 5n^2$ (since $n \le n^2$)
- $100 \le 100n^2$ (since $1 \le n^2$)

**Step 2 — Substitute the bounds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3n^2 + 5n + 100 &\le 3n^2 + 5n^2 + 100n^2 \\
&= (3 + 5 + 100)n^2 \\
&= 108 \cdot n^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the constants.**

Choose $c = 108$ and $n_0 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Verify the definition.**

For all $n \ge 1$: $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \le 108 n^2 = c \cdot n^2$.

$$\therefore \; 3n^2 + 5n + 100 = O(n^2) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule (drop lower-order terms and constants):** $a_k n^k + a_{k-1} n^{k-1} + \dots + a_0 = \Theta(n^k)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Loop Complexity — Single Loop

**Code Pattern:**
```
sum = 0;
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++)
    sum = sum + i;
```

**Step-by-step count of operations:**

| Line | Cost per execution | Times executed | Total cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| `sum = 0;` | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| `i = 1;` | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| `i <= n` (test) | 1 | $n+1$ | $n+1$ |
| `i++` | 1 | $n$ | $n$ |
| `sum = sum + i;` | 1 | $n$ | $n$ |

**Total:**

$$T(n) = 1 + 1 + (n+1) + n + n = 3n + 3 = \Theta(n)$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Nested Loop — Triangle Pattern

**Code Pattern:**
```
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++)
    for (j = 1; j <= i; j++)
        print("Hello");
```

**Step 1 — Outer loop** runs for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ → **n** iterations.

**Step 2 — Inner loop** runs $i$ times for each value of $i$.

**Step 3 — Sum the inner loop work across all outer iterations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} i \\
&= 1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + n \\
&= \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \quad \text{(arithmetic series formula)} \\
&= \dfrac{n^2 + n}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply asymptotic simplification:**

$$T(n) = \dfrac{n^2 + n}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Recursive Algorithm — Factorial $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$

**Step 1 — Write the recurrence.**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + c, \quad T(1) = d$$

**Step 2 — Expand repeatedly.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c \\
&= [T(n-2) + c] + c = T(n-2) + 2c \\
&= [T(n-3) + c] + 2c = T(n-3) + 3c \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(n-k) + kc
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply base case** $T(1) = d$ when $k = n - 1$:

$$T(n) = T(1) + (n-1)c = d + (n-1)c = (c)n + (d - c)$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic order:**

$$T(n) = \Theta(n)$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Binary Search — $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$

**Step 1 — Recurrence.** Each step halves the problem and does 1 comparison:

$$T(n) = T(n/2) + c, \quad T(1) = d$$

**Step 2 — Expand $k$ times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + c \\
&= T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{4}\right) + 2c \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2^k}\right) + kc
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Termination.** $T(1)$ is reached when $\dfrac{n}{2^k} = 1 \Rightarrow 2^k = n \Rightarrow k = \log_2 n$.

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$T(n) = T(1) + c \log_2 n = d + c \log_2 n = \Theta(\log n)$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Time Complexity of Common Patterns

| Code Pattern | Frequency Count $T(n)$ | Asymptotic $O$ |
|---|---|---|
| A few statements | $O(1)$ | Constant |
| Single loop | $n$ | $O(n)$ |
| Nested loop (independent) | $n \cdot n = n^2$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Nested loop (triangular) | $n(n+1)/2$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Divide-and-conquer (Binary Search) | $\log_2 n$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Three nested loops | $n^3$ | $O(n^3)$ |
| Recursive factorial | $n$ | $O(n)$ |
| Fibonacci (naive recursion) | $2^n$ | $O(2^n)$ |
| Logarithmic loop `i = i*2` | $\log_2 n$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Permutations / Subsets | $2^n$ | $O(2^n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Asymptotic Notation Hierarchy (Visual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Function T of n] --> B{Behavior as n grows to infinity}
    B -->|Upper Bound| C[Big O notation]
    B -->|Tight Bound| D[Big Theta notation]
    B -->|Lower Bound| E[Big Omega notation]
    C --> C1[O of 1 - Constant]
    C --> C2[O of log n - Logarithmic]
    C --> C3[O of n - Linear]
    C --> C4[O of n log n - Linearithmic]
    C --> C5[O of n squared - Quadratic]
    C --> C6[O of n cubed - Cubic]
    C --> C7[O of 2 to n - Exponential]
    C --> C8[O of n factorial - Factorial]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E
```

## 4.2 Best / Average / Worst Case Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Algorithm plus Input of size n] --> B{Input Distribution}
    B -->|Most favorable| C[Best Case T sub best n]
    B -->|Average over all inputs| D[Average Case T sub avg n]
    B -->|Most unfavorable| E[Worst Case T sub worst n]
    C --> F[Omega notation Lower Bound]
    D --> G[Theta notation Tight Bound]
    E --> H[Big O notation Upper Bound]
    F --> I[Performance Classification]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Performance Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INP[INPUT STAGE]
        A1[Algorithm Source Code] --> A2[Input Size n]
    end
    subgraph ANA[ANALYSIS STAGE]
        B1[Count Primitive Operations] --> B2[Construct Cost Function T of n]
        B2 --> B3[Identify Dominant Term]
    end
    subgraph ASN[ASYMPTOTIC STAGE]
        C1[Apply Big O Notation] --> C2[Compare with Standard Classes]
        C2 --> C3[Rank Algorithm Efficiency]
    end
    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
        D1[Time Complexity Label] --> D2[Space Complexity Label]
        D2 --> D3[Optimization Recommendation]
    end
    INP --> ANA
    ANA --> ASN
    ASN --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Rate of Growth Comparison Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RANK[SLOWEST TO FASTEST GROWTH]
        direction TB
        R1[O 1 - Constant] --> R2[O log n - Logarithmic]
        R2 --> R3[O n - Linear]
        R3 --> R4[O n log n - Linearithmic]
        R4 --> R5[O n squared - Quadratic]
        R5 --> R6[O n cubed - Cubic]
        R6 --> R7[O 2 to n - Exponential]
        R7 --> R8[O n factorial - Factorial]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked to *"order the complexities,"* always place $O(\log n)$ **before** $O(n)$, and $O(n \log n)$ **between** $O(n)$ and $O(n^2)$. The above ladder is the **canonical answer** expected in viva.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Big-O notation. Show that $5n^2 + 3n + 10 = O(n^2)$.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Big-O Definition:** $T(n) = O(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that $0 \le T(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

**Proof:**

We need to find $c, n_0$ such that $5n^2 + 3n + 10 \le c \cdot n^2$.

For $n \ge 1$: $3n \le 3n^2$ and $10 \le 10n^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
5n^2 + 3n + 10 &\le 5n^2 + 3n^2 + 10n^2 \\
&= 18n^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Choose $c = 18, n_0 = 1$. Then $5n^2 + 3n + 10 \le 18n^2$ for all $n \ge 1$. $\blacksquare$

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between worst-case and average-case time complexity with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Worst Case | Average Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Maximum operations over all inputs of size $n$ | Expected operations over all inputs of size $n$ |
| **Symbol** | $T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \max_{I} T(I)$ | $T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \sum P(I) T(I)$ |
| **Guarantee** | Hard upper bound on time | Probabilistic estimate |
| **Example: Linear Search** | Element at the *last* position: $O(n)$ | Element at *random* position: $O(n/2) = O(n)$ |
| **KTU Use** | Safety-critical systems | Expected performance planning |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q. (a) Explain asymptotic notations in detail. Derive the time complexity of the following code segment. (7 Marks)**

```
count = 0;
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++)
    for (j = i; j <= n; j++)
        for (k = 1; k <= j; k++)
            count = count + 1;
```

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Asymptotic Notations (3 Marks):**
As explained in Section 2.1: $O$, $\Theta$, $\Omega$ represent upper, tight, and lower bounds respectively.

**Part 2 — Time Complexity Derivation (4 Marks):**

The inner `k`-loop executes $j$ times. The middle `j`-loop runs from $i$ to $n$, and the outer `i`-loop from 1 to $n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=i}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{j} 1 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=i}^{n} j \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{n} j - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} j \right] \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} - \dfrac{(i-1)i}{2} \right] \\
&= \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot n - \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (i^2 - i) \\
&= \dfrac{n^2(n+1)}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} - \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \right] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

After algebraic simplification, the dominant term is $\dfrac{n^3}{3}$.

$$\therefore \; T(n) = O(n^3)$$

---

**Q. (b) Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ using the recursion tree method and state the asymptotic complexity. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Recursion Tree Construction:**

| Level | Number of Sub-problems | Size of Each | Work per Node | Total Work at Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (root) | 1 | $n$ | $n$ | $n$ |
| 1 | 2 | $n/2$ | $n/2$ each | $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$ |
| 2 | 4 | $n/4$ | $n/4$ each | $4 \cdot (n/4) = n$ |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| $i$ | $2^i$ | $n/2^i$ | $n/2^i$ each | $n$ |
| Last ($\log_2 n$) | $n$ | 1 | 1 | $n$ |

**Total Work:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n} n \\
&= n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) \\
&= n \log_2 n + n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic Complexity:**

$$T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is the classical cost of **Merge Sort** and **Quick Sort** (average case). It is the *optimal* complexity for comparison-based sorting.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**Q. (a) Define Space Complexity. Analyze the space complexity of recursive Fibonacci function. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Space Complexity Definition (2 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Space Complexity** $S(n)$ is the total memory an algorithm uses as a function of input size $n$, including the input storage, auxiliary (temporary) variables, and **call-stack space** for recursion.

$$S(n) = S_{\text{input}}(n) + S_{\text{auxiliary}}(n) + S_{\text{stack}}(n)$$

**Part 2 — Recursive Fibonacci Analysis (5 Marks):**

The naive recursive Fibonacci:
```
int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}
```

**Time:** Exponential $O(2^n)$ (each call spawns 2 more, forming a binary tree of depth $n$).

**Space (Call Stack):** The **maximum depth** of the recursion stack determines the space.

For $\text{fib}(n)$, the deepest chain of calls is $\text{fib}(n) \to \text{fib}(n-1) \to \text{fib}(n-2) \to \dots \to \text{fib}(1)$.

This chain length is **n**, and each stack frame stores:
- The return address
- The parameter $n$ (current value)
- Local variable overhead

**Total space used = depth × frame size:**

$$S_{\text{aux}}(n) = c \cdot n = O(n)$$

Hence, even though time is exponential, **space is only linear** because only one branch of the recursion tree exists in the call stack at any time.

---

**Q. (b) With a suitable example, explain the procedure to determine the best, average, and worst-case complexities of an algorithm. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Example: Linear Search** — searching for key $K$ in an unsorted array $A[0..n-1]$.

**Algorithm:**
```
for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
    if (A[i] == K) return i;
return -1;
```

**Best Case (1 Mark):**
$K$ is at the *first* position $A[0]$.
- Number of comparisons = **1**.
- $T_{\text{best}}(n) = \Omega(1)$.

**Worst Case (2 Marks):**
$K$ is at the *last* position or not present.
- Number of comparisons = **n**.
- $T_{\text{worst}}(n) = O(n)$.

**Average Case (3 Marks):**
Assume key $K$ is equally likely to be at any of the $n$ positions, or absent (with probability $1/(n+1)$ each).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{1}{n+1}\right) \cdot i \;+\; \left(\dfrac{1}{n+1}\right) \cdot n \\
&= \dfrac{1}{n+1} \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} + \dfrac{n}{n+1} \\
&= \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{n}{n+1} \\
&= \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{n}{n+1} \approx \dfrac{n}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\therefore \; T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \Theta(n)$$

**Conclusion Table:**

| Case | Comparisons | Asymptotic |
|---|---|---|
| Best | 1 | $\Omega(1)$ |
| Average | $(n+1)/2$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Worst | $n$ | $O(n)$ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constants $c$ and $n_0$** when proving Big-O. Merely stating "$3n^2 + 5n + 100 \le 108n^2$" is incomplete — you must **explicitly name** the constants.
> 2. **Confusing $O$ and $\Theta$**: $O$ is an *upper* bound; $\Theta$ is a *tight* bound. Writing $T(n) = O(n^2)$ is true for $T(n) = n$, but $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ would be **false**.
> 3. **Off-by-one errors in loop counting**: The test condition `i <= n` executes $n+1$ times, not $n$. Marks are deducted for ignoring this in rigorous derivations.
> 4. **Average case requires probability assumptions** — failing to state "uniformly distributed" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Recursion tree height** must equal $\log_b n$ (not $\log n$) in Master Theorem applications.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this before exams!):**
>
> - **Performance Analysis** evaluates algorithms using *time* and *space* complexity.
> - **Big-O** = Upper bound (worst case); **Big-Θ** = Tight bound (exact order); **Big-Ω** = Lower bound (best case).
> - **Constants and lower-order terms are dropped** in asymptotic analysis: $5n^3 + 100n^2 \to \Theta(n^3)$.
> - **Loop counts:**
>   - 1 loop → $O(n)$
>   - 2 independent nested loops → $O(n^2)$
>   - Loop with `i = i*2` → $O(\log n)$
>   - Loop with `i = i/2` → $O(\log n)$
> - **Recurrences:**
>   - $T(n) = T(n-1) + c \Rightarrow \Theta(n)$
>   - $T(n) = T(n/2) + c \Rightarrow \Theta(\log n)$
>   - $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$
>   - $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + c \Rightarrow \Theta(2^n)$
> - **Growth hierarchy (slowest → fastest):** $1 < \log n < n < n \log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!$
> - **Worst-case** is the gold standard for KTU and safety-critical engineering.
> - **Space complexity** includes call-stack depth for recursive functions.
> - Always **state assumptions** (e.g., uniform probability, $n \ge 1$) in derivations.
> - **Arithmetic series:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$.
> - **Geometric series:** $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^i = 2^k - 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Space Complexity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Space Complexity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Space Complexity** is the formal measure of the total amount of **memory (RAM/heap/stack space)** that an algorithm requires to run to completion, expressed as a function of the size of the input data ($n$). It quantifies *how much extra storage* the algorithm needs *in addition to the input itself*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based framework, Space Complexity $S(n)$ is expressed using asymptotic notation (Big-O) and is formally defined as:

$$S(n) = C + S_P(n)$$

Where:
- $C$ = Constant space (fixed memory for variables, program code, simple data)
- $S_P(n)$ = Variable space that depends on the input size $n$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "Space Complexity of an algorithm is the amount of memory required to run it to completion, including the memory for the input data, the auxiliary memory (temporary variables, function call stack, recursion stack), and the memory occupied by the program code."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Moving House" Intuition

Imagine you are **moving to a new house** (this is the algorithm execution). You have:

1. **The Truck (Input Data)**: This is the furniture you *must* carry. This space is *not counted* in auxiliary space because the problem *gives* it to you.
2. **The Boxes You Buy (Auxiliary Space)**: These are the temporary cardboard boxes, bubble wrap, and labels you purchase. This is the **real cost** you measure — the *extra* memory you manage.
3. **The Path on the Street (Program Code)**: The route you walk while instructing movers — this is a **constant** cost, same for every house size.

A good algorithm is like a smart mover: a great mover rearranges an entire 5-BHK house using **only 10 boxes** (low auxiliary space, $O(1)$ or $O(\log n)$), while a poor mover uses 200 boxes scattered everywhere ($O(n^2)$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Space Complexity Matters in KTU Exams:** With mobile devices and embedded systems (IoT, RTOS, microcontrollers) having **strict memory limits** (e.g., 256 KB RAM), an $O(n^2)$ space algorithm can crash a drone, whereas $O(1)$ runs smoothly. KTU examiners love this real-world connection in 14-mark answers.

## 1.3 The Two Sub-Components of Space

Space complexity is composed of **two clearly distinct parts** that students frequently confuse:

| Component | Symbol | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Part (Instruction Space)** | $C_I$ | Memory for compiled code, constants, simple variables | `int i; float pi = 3.14;` |
| **Variable Part (Data + Stack Space)** | $S_V(n)$ | Memory for input data, dynamically allocated arrays, recursion stack | `int arr[n]; recursive call frames` |

The relationship is:

$$S(n) = C_I + S_V(n)$$

Where $C_I$ is a **constant** (independent of $n$), so it is dropped in asymptotic analysis, leaving us with **just $S_V(n)$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Memory Used by an Algorithm

When the KTU examiner asks "Calculate the space complexity," you must account for these **three memory regions**:

### Pillar 1: Instruction Space
- Memory needed to store the **compiled program code**.
- Size = constant (does NOT grow with $n$).
- Example: A `for` loop's compiled bytecode occupies the same memory whether $n=10$ or $n=10^{10}$.

### Pillar 2: Data Space (Heap)
- Memory for:
  - **Input data** (constants, arrays passed to the function).
  - **Output data** (result variables).
  - **Local variables** (the famous `int i, sum, temp;`).
  - **Dynamically allocated memory** (`malloc`, `new`, vectors, hash tables).

### Pillar 3: Environment Stack Space (Call Stack)
- Memory used by the **system call stack** to store:
  - Return addresses of function calls.
  - Local variables of *active* functions.
  - **This is where recursion costs its memory.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Trick:** For **iterative algorithms**, ignore the call stack (it is bounded by $O(1)$ for `main`). For **recursive algorithms**, the call stack is *the dominant term* in space complexity.

## 2.2 Auxiliary Space vs. Total Space Complexity

This distinction is a **favourite KTU Part-A question** (3 marks):

- **Total Space Complexity, $S_{total}(n)$** = Memory for input data + Auxiliary space.
- **Auxiliary Space, $Aux(n)$** = Extra/temporary space *over and above* the input.

$$S_{total}(n) = \text{Input Size} + Aux(n)$$

In most KTU textbook questions, the input size is **not counted** (because it is "given to you"), and the answer is the **auxiliary space**.

## 2.3 Asymptotic Notation for Space

Space complexity uses the **same Big-O, Big-$\Theta$, Big-$\Omega$** families as time complexity. The standard rule is:

| Space Bound | Meaning | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| $O(1)$ | Constant — independent of $n$ | Swapping two variables |
| $O(\log n)$ | Logarithmic | Iterative binary search |
| $O(n)$ | Linear | Linear search, copy of array |
| $O(n^2)$ | Quadratic | Adjacency matrix of a graph |
| $O(2^n)$ | Exponential | Naive recursive Fibonacci |
| $O(n!)$ | Factorial | Brute-force TSP |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table. It covers 90% of KTU 2-mark and 14-mark derivation questions.**

| # | Algorithm | Input Size | Auxiliary Space | Total Space | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sum of array (iterative) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Linear Search | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Binary Search (iterative) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Binary Search (recursive) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Bubble / Selection Sort | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Merge Sort | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Quick Sort (in-place) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Factorial (recursive) | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Fibonacci (naive recursive) | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Tower of Hanoi | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | Matrix Multiplication | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n^2)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 12 | BFS / DFS on graph | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Space complexity directly impacts:
1. **Embedded Systems (Arduino, ESP32, STM32):** RAM is measured in KB. $O(n^2)$ is a deal-breaker.
2. **Smartphones (Android Battery + RAM):** Garbage-collected languages (Java, Kotlin) trigger GC pauses proportional to heap usage.
3. **Server-Side Scalability:** An $O(n)$ auxiliary space algorithm serving $10^6$ concurrent users = 8 GB of RAM just for state.
4. **Cryptography (RSA, AES):** Constant-space algorithms are preferred for hardware tokens.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains **exhaustive, step-by-step derivations** for the most frequently tested KTU algorithms. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sum of $n$ Array Elements (Iterative)

**Algorithm (C-style pseudo-code):**
```c
int sumArray(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, total = 0;          // Step 1
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {  // Step 2
        total = total + arr[i];
    }
    return total;              // Step 3
}
```

**Memory Accounting (Exhaustive):**

| Variable / Object | Memory Size | Lifetime | Counts? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `arr` (input pointer) | 4 / 8 bytes | Whole function | Yes — input data |
| `n` (input) | 4 bytes | Whole function | Yes — input data |
| `i` (loop counter) | 4 bytes | Whole function | Yes — auxiliary |
| `total` (accumulator) | 4 bytes | Whole function | Yes — auxiliary |
| Function call frame | ~32 bytes | Whole function | Yes — stack |
| Recursion depth | 0 (no recursion) | N/A | No |

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(n) &= \text{Input Data} + \text{Auxiliary Memory} + \text{Stack Memory} \\
&= \underbrace{(4 + 4 \cdot n)}_{\text{array + size}} + \underbrace{(4 + 4)}_{\text{i + total}} + \underbrace{32}_{\text{stack}} \\
&= 4n + 4n + 4 + 4 + 32 \\
&= 8n + 40 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying asymptotic dominance (drop constants and lower-order terms):

$$
S(n) = O(n)
$$

> **Conclusion:** Space complexity is **$O(n)$** because of the input array, and **auxiliary space is $O(1)$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Factorial Using Recursion (Classic KTU Favourite)

**Algorithm:**
```c
long fact(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;     // Base case
    return n * fact(n - 1);   // Recursive call
}
```

**Call Tree Visualization for `fact(4)`:**

```
fact(4)  ──► 4 * fact(3)
                fact(3)  ──► 3 * fact(2)
                                fact(2)  ──► 2 * fact(1)
                                                fact(1) ──► 1
```

**Memory Accounting (Exhaustive):**

Each recursive call creates a **new stack frame** containing:
- The argument `n` (4 bytes)
- Return address (4 bytes)
- Local temp for the multiplication (4 bytes)
- Saved base pointer (4 bytes)

Each frame ≈ **16 bytes**.

For input size $n$, the recursion depth is exactly $n$ (it goes $n, n-1, n-2, \ldots, 1$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of stack frames} &= n \\
\text{Memory per frame} &= c \text{ (a constant, say 16 bytes)} \\
S_P(n) &= c \cdot n = 16n \text{ bytes} \\
S(n) &= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** **Auxiliary space = $O(n)$** because the call stack grows linearly with input.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Merge Sort (The Ultimate Space-Time Trade-off)

**Algorithm (High-level):**
```c
void mergeSort(int arr[], int l, int r) {
    if (l < r) {
        int m = (l + r) / 2;
        mergeSort(arr, l, m);     // Left half
        mergeSort(arr, m+1, r);   // Right half
        merge(arr, l, m, r);      // The expensive step
    }
}
```

**Memory Accounting:**

1. **Input array `arr`:** $O(n)$ — does not grow.
2. **Auxiliary array (the temporary array in `merge`):** The merge routine allocates a helper array of size $n$ to do the two-finger merge. This array persists for the entire recursion.
3. **Recursion stack:** Depth = $\log_2 n$ (because we halve the array each time).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{aux}(n) &= \underbrace{n}_{\text{merge helper array}} + \underbrace{\log_2 n}_{\text{recursion stack depth}} \\
&= n + \log_2 n \\
&\approx n \text{ (asymptotic dominance)} \\
&= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** **Auxiliary space = $O(n)$** — this is the classic example of the **time-space trade-off**: Merge Sort runs in $O(n \log n)$ time, but unlike in-place sorts, it pays $O(n)$ memory.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Iterative Binary Search

**Algorithm:**
```c
int binarySearch(int arr[], int n, int key) {
    int low = 0, high = n - 1, mid;
    while (low <= high) {
        mid = (low + high) / 2;
        if (arr[mid] == key) return mid;
        else if (arr[mid] < key) low = mid + 1;
        else high = mid - 1;
    }
    return -1;
}
```

**Memory Accounting:**

| Item | Count | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `arr` (input) | 1 pointer | $O(1)$ reference to $O(n)$ data |
| `n` | 1 | Constant |
| `key` | 1 | Constant |
| `low`, `high`, `mid` | 3 integers | Constant |
| Recursion | 0 | Iterative version |
| Loop overhead | 0 | No new memory |

$$
S(n) = O(1) \text{ (auxiliary)} \quad ; \quad S_{total}(n) = O(n)
$$

> **Comparison Insight:** Compare with **recursive** binary search which uses $O(\log n)$ auxiliary space due to the call stack.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Time vs Space Trade-off

This table is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark question pattern**:

| Algorithm | Time | Space (Aux) | Trade-off Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Slowest, most memory-efficient |
| Binary Search (iterative) | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | **Best of both worlds** |
| Binary Search (recursive) | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Slightly worse space |
| Recursive Fibonacci (naive) | $O(2^n)$ | $O(n)$ | Disastrous time, decent space |
| Fibonacci (DP bottom-up) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Linear time, linear space |
| Fibonacci (DP space-optimized) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | **Optimal** |
| Merge Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Needs helper array |
| Quick Sort (worst) | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ | In-place, but risky pivot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** *Time and space are interchangeable resources*. An algorithm can usually be made faster by spending more memory (e.g., memoization, hashing) or made memory-efficient by recomputing (e.g., recursive DFS vs explicit stack).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of Space Complexity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Space Complexity S n"]:::root --> B["Fixed Part C I Constant"]
    A --> C["Variable Part S V n Grows with n"]:::branch

    B --> B1["Compiled code size"]
    B --> B2["Constants e.g. pi = 3.14"]
    B --> B3["Simple variables"]

    C --> D["Data Space Heap"]:::sub
    C --> E["Environment Stack Space"]:::sub
    C --> F["Input Data n"]:::sub

    D --> D1["Local variables"]
    D --> D2["Dynamic allocations malloc new"]
    D --> D3["Output variables"]

    E --> E1["Function call frames"]
    E --> E2["Return addresses"]
    E --> E3["Recursion stack"]

    F --> F1["Arrays passed as args"]
    F --> F2["Linked list nodes"]
    F --> F3["Graph edges and vertices"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef branch fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart — Iterative vs Recursive Memory Usage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Algorithm Execution Begins"]:::start --> B{"Is the algorithm recursive?"}:::decision

    B -- "NO Iterative" --> C["Call stack depth = 1"]:::iter
    C --> D["Auxiliary space O 1"]:::ok
    D --> E["Memory is allocated only for local variables"]:::note

    B -- "YES Recursive" --> F["Call stack grows with n"]:::rec
    F --> G["Recursion depth = n for factorial and Hanoi"]:::rec
    F --> H["Recursion depth = log n for binary search and merge sort"]:::rec
    F --> I["Auxiliary space = O n or O log n"]:::ok

    G --> J["Each frame = constant memory e.g. 16 bytes"]:::note
    H --> J

    classDef start fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef iter fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#000
    classDef rec fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000
    classDef ok fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

    end
