<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define space complexity. How is it different from auxiliary space complexity?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Space complexity** of an algorithm is the total amount of memory it requires to run to completion, including the memory for input data, output data, local variables, dynamically allocated structures, and the call stack.

**Auxiliary space** is a subset of total space complexity — it refers to the *extra* temporary space used by the algorithm **over and above** the input data size.

> The relationship is:
> $$S_{total}(n) = \text{Input size} + Aux(n)$$
> 
> For example, the sum-of-array iterative algorithm has $S_{total}(n) = O(n)$ (because of the input array), but $Aux(n) = O(1)$ (only `i` and `total`). **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is the space complexity of a recursive function to compute $n!$? Justify with a call stack diagram."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The recursive factorial function calls itself $n$ times (from $n$ down to $1$). Each invocation creates a new stack frame on the call stack, holding the parameter `n`, return address, and local multiplication variable.

> Since there are $n$ simultaneous stack frames at the deepest point of recursion:
> $$S_{aux}(n) = O(n)$$
> 
> **Justification (call stack):** `fact(n) → fact(n-1) → fact(n-2) → ... → fact(1)`, with $n$ frames alive concurrently. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** "Answer any **one full question** from each module. Each full question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**."

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

#### **Q-A(a)** [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**
**"Explain the concept of space complexity in detail. With the help of suitable examples, differentiate between total space complexity and auxiliary space complexity."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition [2 marks]:**
Space complexity $S(n)$ is the formal measure of the memory an algorithm needs to run to completion, expressed as a function of input size $n$.

It includes:
- Memory for compiled code (constant, $C_I$)
- Memory for input/output data
- Memory for local variables
- Memory for the call/recursion stack
- Memory for dynamically allocated structures

**(ii) Mathematical Formulation [2 marks]:**
$$S(n) = C + S_P(n)$$
where $C$ is constant and $S_P(n)$ varies with $n$.

**(iii) Distinction Table [2 marks]:**

| Aspect | Total Space Complexity | Auxiliary Space Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Includes input size? | YES | NO |
| Counts temp variables? | YES | YES |
| Counts recursion stack? | YES | YES |
| Notation | $S_{total}(n)$ | $Aux(n)$ |
| Relation | $S_{total}(n) = n + Aux(n)$ | $Aux(n) = S_{total}(n) - n$ |

**(iv) Examples [1 mark]:**
- **Iterative sum:** $S_{total}(n) = O(n)$, $Aux(n) = O(1)$.
- **Recursive factorial:** $S_{total}(n) = O(n)$, $Aux(n) = O(n)$.

#### **Q-A(b)** [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**
**"Write an algorithm to find the sum of first $n$ natural numbers using recursion. Determine its space complexity with justification."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Algorithm [3 marks]:**
```c
int sumNatural(int n) {
    if (n == 0)              // Base case
        return 0;
    return n + sumNatural(n-1);  // Recursive call
}
```

**(ii) Call Trace for $n = 4$ [1 mark]:**
```
sumNatural(4) → 4 + sumNatural(3)
sumNatural(3) → 3 + sumNatural(2)
sumNatural(2) → 2 + sumNatural(1)
sumNatural(1) → 1 + sumNatural(0)
sumNatural(0) → 0   [BASE CASE]
```

**(iii) Space Analysis [3 marks]:**
Each recursive call pushes a stack frame of constant size $c$ (parameter, return address, locals). For input $n$, the maximum recursion depth is exactly $n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack frames at peak} &= n \\
\text{Memory per frame} &= c \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Total auxiliary memory} &= c \cdot n \\
S_{aux}(n) &= O(n) \\
S_{total}(n) &= O(1) + O(n) = O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Space complexity = $O(n)$. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q-B(a)** [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Compare iterative and recursive approaches with respect to space complexity. Illustrate with the example of computing Fibonacci numbers."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Iterative Fibonacci [3 marks]:**
```c
int fibIter(int n) {
    int a = 0, b = 1, c, i;
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    for (i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        c = a + b;
        a = b;
        b = c;
    }
    return b;
}
```
- Variables: `a, b, c, i` (4 integers) — **constant**.
- No recursion → call stack depth = 1.
- $Aux(n) = O(1)$, $S_{total}(n) = O(1)$.

**(ii) Recursive Fibonacci [3 marks]:**
```c
int fibRec(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return fibRec(n-1) + fibRec(n-2);
}
```
- Branching factor = 2, but only the **longest path** determines peak stack height.
- For input $n$, recursion depth = $n$ (the path $n → n-1 → n-2 → ... → 1$).
- $Aux(n) = O(n)$.

**(iii) Comparison Table [1 mark]:**

| Property | Iterative | Recursive |
|---|---|---|
| Time | $O(n)$ | $O(2^n)$ |
| Space (Aux) | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Scalability | Good | Poor (exponential time) |

> **Verdict:** Iterative is vastly more efficient for Fibonacci. **(7 marks)**

#### **Q-B(b)** [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the time-space trade-off. Show how the recursive Fibonacci can be optimized using memoization (Dynamic Programming) and analyze its new space complexity."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Time-Space Trade-off Concept [2 marks]:**
A fundamental principle in algorithm design: you can usually **reduce running time by spending more memory**, or **reduce memory usage by accepting slower execution**. The two resources are interchangeable to a limited extent.

**(ii) Memoized Fibonacci Algorithm [2 marks]:**
```c
int memo[100];  // Global DP table
int fibMemo(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    if (memo[n] != -1) return memo[n];   // Already computed?
    return memo[n] = fibMemo(n-1) + fibMemo(n-2);
}
```

**(iii) New Complexity Analysis [3 marks]:**

- **Time:** Each unique $n$ is computed only once → $O(n)$.
- **Space:**
  - `memo[100]` table = $O(n)$ **data memory**.
  - Recursion stack depth = $O(n)$ **stack memory**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{aux}(n) &= O(n) + O(n) = O(n) \\
S_{total}(n) &= O(1) + O(n) = O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Memoization traded **exponential time for linear time**, but paid with $O(n)$ auxiliary space. We can go further: bottom-up DP uses **only $O(1)$ space** by keeping just the last two values, achieving the optimal solution. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing input size with auxiliary space:** A student writes "$S(n) = O(n)$ for sum-of-array" without clarifying that this is *total* space, while the *auxiliary* is $O(1)$. KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this imprecision. **Always state both!**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the call stack in recursive analyses:** Students compute the number of recursive calls but forget to count the stack frames. Each frame is constant memory, so $n$ frames = $O(n)$ space. This is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up time and space complexity:** Writing "$O(n^2)$ space for binary search" is a guaranteed zero. The **time** of binary search is $O(\log n)$, the **space** is $O(1)$ iterative or $O(\log n)$ recursive.
> 
> 4. **Not distinguishing merge sort from quick sort:** Merge sort needs $O(n)$ auxiliary (helper array), quick sort is $O(\log n)$ auxiliary (in-place partition). This distinction appears in KTU papers every year.
> 
> 5. **Asymptotic notation misuse:** Writing "$S(n) = n + 5$" instead of "$S(n) = O(n)$". Always convert to Big-O form at the end.
> 
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark answers:** KTU 2024 OBE pattern **expects a diagram** (call tree, stack frame visualization, or memory layout). Absence of one = **−1 to −2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Space complexity = total memory needed to run an algorithm to completion, as a function of input size $n$, expressed in Big-O.
- **Three components:** Instruction space (constant) + Data space (heap) + Environment stack space (call stack).
- **Auxiliary space** ≠ Total space. Auxiliary = extra temporary memory; Total = Input + Auxiliary.
- **Iterative algorithms** usually have **$O(1)$** auxiliary space (call stack depth is 1).
- **Recursive algorithms** have space complexity equal to **maximum recursion depth** multiplied by constant frame size.
- **Factorial recursive:** $O(n)$ space (recursion depth = $n$).
- **Fibonacci recursive (naive):** $O(n)$ space, but $O(2^n)$ time.
- **Fibonacci DP memoized:** $O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ space.
- **Fibonacci DP space-optimized:** $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space — **the gold standard**.
- **Merge Sort:** $O(n \log n)$ time, $O(n)$ space (helper array is the cost).
- **Quick Sort (in-place):** $O(n \log n)$ average time, $O(\log n)$ space (recursion stack).
- **Binary Search iterative:** $O(1)$ space. **Binary Search recursive:** $O(\log n)$ space.
- **Time-Space Trade-off:** You can trade memory for speed (e.g., memoization, hash tables) or speed for memory (e.g., in-place algorithms, recomputation).
- **Big-O rules:** Drop constants. Drop lower-order terms. Keep the dominant term.
- **Always state both** $S_{total}(n)$ and $Aux(n)$ in KTU 14-mark answers.
- **Draw a diagram** — call tree or memory layout — to earn full marks.
- **Common KTU traps:** Merge sort vs Quick sort space, recursive vs iterative Fibonacci, hash table $O(1)$ lookup vs $O(n)$ memory.

> **Final Tip:** Space complexity is the *silent killer* in mobile and embedded systems. KTU examiners love linking it to real applications (IoT, drones, smartphones, cloud servers). Always include one real-world sentence in your 14-mark answers — it boosts the impression of conceptual depth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Complexity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Complexity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity** is a computational complexity metric that quantifies the **amount of computational time** taken by an algorithm to run, expressed as a **function of the length of the input** (typically denoted as $n$). It provides a theoretical estimate of the number of elementary operations performed, enabling us to compare the efficiency of algorithms independently of hardware, compiler, or programming language.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)** curriculum for Data Structures, time complexity is studied under the framework of **Asymptotic Analysis**, where we are concerned with the *order of growth* of the running time $T(n)$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$, rather than the exact wall-clock time.

**Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
- **Input Size ($n$):** The number of data elements to be processed.
- **Running Time $T(n)$:** Number of primitive operations executed.
- **Asymptotic Upper Bound ($O$):** Worst-case growth rate.
- **Asymptotic Lower Bound ($\Omega$):** Best-case growth rate.
- **Asymptotic Tight Bound ($\Theta$):** Exact order of growth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Library Card Catalogue" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine you walk into a massive library with **10 million** books and need to find a specific title. You have two strategies:
>
> 1. **Sequential Search (Linear):** Walk to every shelf, check every book, one by one. If the book is at the end, you walk past *all 10 million* books. Time grows **directly proportional** to the number of books.
> 2. **Indexed Search (Binary-like):** Use the computer catalog to jump to the correct aisle, then the correct shelf, narrowing down by half each time. You find the book in roughly **logarithmic time** — about 23 jumps for 10 million books.
>
> Both strategies *solve the same problem*, but their *time grows at fundamentally different rates* as the library grows. **Time complexity is the mathematical language that captures this rate of growth.**

## 1.3 Why Time Complexity Matters in Engineering

In real-world engineering (compilers, databases, network routers, embedded systems), even a *linear* vs *quadratic* difference can mean the difference between **1 second** and **30 years** for large inputs. Therefore, choosing algorithms with optimal asymptotic complexity is a critical engineering decision.

## 1.4 Types of Complexity Analysis (KTU Board-Favorite)

| Analysis Type | Description | Notation Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Worst Case** | Maximum time over all inputs of size $n$ | Big-$O$ |
| **Average Case** | Expected time over all inputs of size $n$ | Big-$\Theta$ |
| **Best Case** | Minimum time over all inputs of size $n$ | Big-$\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Note:** When a question simply asks "find the time complexity", by KTU convention, it almost always refers to the **Worst-Case Time Complexity** using **Big-$O$ notation**, unless explicitly stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Asymptotic Notation

### (a) Big-$O$ Notation — Asymptotic Upper Bound (Worst Case)

$$O(g(n)) = \left\{ f(n) \;\middle|\; \exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le f(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \; \forall\, n \ge n_0 \right\}$$

**Plain English Intuition:** $f(n)$ grows **no faster than** $g(n)$, up to a constant factor.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Identify the dominant term in $f(n)$ that controls growth for large $n$.
2. Strip away constants and lower-order terms.
3. The remaining term is $g(n)$.

### (b) Big-$\Omega$ Notation — Asymptotic Lower Bound (Best Case)

$$\Omega(g(n)) = \left\{ f(n) \;\middle|\; \exists\, c > 0,\, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } 0 \le c \cdot g(n) \le f(n) \; \forall\, n \ge n_0 \right\}$$

**Plain English Intuition:** $f(n)$ grows **at least as fast as** $g(n)$.

### (c) Big-$\Theta$ Notation — Asymptotic Tight Bound (Exact Order)

$$\Theta(g(n)) = \left\{ f(n) \;\middle|\; \exists\, c_1, c_2 > 0,\, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } c_1 g(n) \le f(n) \le c_2 g(n) \; \forall\, n \ge n_0 \right\}$$

**Plain English Intuition:** $f(n)$ grows **at the same rate as** $g(n)$, sandwiched between two constant multiples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Fundamental Relationship):** $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if and only if $f(n) = O(g(n))$ **AND** $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$.

## 2.2 Lesser-Known Notations (For Completeness)

| Notation | Name | Intuitive Meaning | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| $o(g(n))$ | Little-$o$ | Strictly slower growth than $g(n)$ | Low |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | Little-$\omega$ | Strictly faster growth than $g(n)$ | Low |

## 2.3 Common Complexity Classes (Ranked Best to Worst)

The hierarchy of polynomial and exponential complexities that students must memorize:

$$O(1) \prec O(\log n) \prec O(\sqrt{n}) \prec O(n) \prec O(n \log n) \prec O(n^2) \prec O(n^3) \prec O(2^n) \prec O(n!) \prec O(n^n)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic (Top to Bottom = Increasing Time):** "Logarithmic, Linear, N log N, Quadratic" → **"Little Lawyers Need Quality"**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation / Algorithm | Time Complexity | Best Case | Worst Case | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Array Index Access | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Random access |
| Linear Search | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | Unsorted array |
| Binary Search | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Sorted array |
| Bubble Sort | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | Educational |
| Merge Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Stable sort |
| Quick Sort | $O(n \log n)$ avg | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | General purpose |
| Heap Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | In-place sort |
| Matrix Multiply (Naïve) | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^3)$ | Strassen improves to $O(n^{2.807})$ |
| Fibonacci (Recursive) | $O(2^n)$ | $O(2^n)$ | $O(2^n)$ | Use DP for $O(n)$ |
| Tower of Hanoi | $O(2^n)$ | — | — | Classical recursion |

## 2.5 Rules for Asymptotic Simplification (Exam Quick Wins)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 — Drop Lower-Order Terms:** $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \Rightarrow O(n^2)$
>
> **Rule 2 — Drop Constant Multipliers:** $7n \log n \Rightarrow O(n \log n)$
>
> **Rule 3 — Loops:** A loop running $n$ times → $O(n)$. Nested loops $k$ levels deep → $O(n^k)$.
>
> **Rule 4 — Sequential Statements:** Add complexities, then simplify (dominance rule).
>
> **Rule 5 — Logarithm Rule:** $\log_b(n^k) = k \log_b(n) = O(\log n)$.
>
> **Rule 6 — Polynomial vs Exponential:** For any $k$, $n^k = o(2^n)$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Query Optimization:** Query planners choose between $O(n)$ and $O(\log n)$ index lookups.
- **Network Routing:** Dijkstra's $O((V+E)\log V)$ vs Bellman-Ford $O(VE)$ matters for large graphs.
- **Cryptography:** RSA security relies on the *hardness* (no known polynomial algorithm) of factoring $O(2^{n/2})$ operations.
- **Embedded Systems:** $O(1)$ algorithms are mandatory in real-time systems (anti-lock braking, pacemakers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constant Time Operation

```c
int x = arr[5];      // Step 1: array index lookup
x = x + 1;            // Step 2: addition
printf("%d", x);      // Step 3: print
```

**Derivation:**

$$T(n) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$$

Each step is independent of $n$. Apply simplification Rule 2 (drop constants):

$$T(n) = O(1)$$

**Conclusion:** Array indexing is **constant time**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Linear Search $T(n)$

```c
int linearSearch(int arr[], int n, int key) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {       // loop runs n times
        if (arr[i] == key)              // 1 comparison per iteration
            return i;                   // return on match
    }
    return -1;
}
```

**Derivation of Worst Case (key not in array):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{worst}}(n) &= \underbrace{n}_{\text{loop iterations}} \cdot \underbrace{1}_{\text{comparison per iter}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{final return}} \\
&= n + 1
\end{aligned}$$

Apply Rule 1 (drop lower-order constant):

$$\boxed{T_{\text{worst}}(n) = O(n)}$$

**Best Case (key at first index):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{best}}(n) &= 1 \text{ comparison} \\
&= O(1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Average Case (key equally likely at any position):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}}(n) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} i \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n+1}{2} \\
&= O(n)
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Binary Search Recurrence

Binary search on a sorted array of size $n$ discards half each step:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c, \quad T(1) = d$$

**Step-by-step Unrolling:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + c + c \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3c \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + kc
\end{aligned}$$

The recursion terminates when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, i.e., $2^k = n$, giving:

$$k = \log_2 n$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(1) + (\log_2 n) \cdot c \\
&= d + c \log_2 n
\end{aligned}$$

Apply asymptotic rules (drop constant $d$, absorb constant $c$):

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(\log n)}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Nested Loops (Quadratic)

```c
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {       // outer loop: n times
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {   // inner loop: n times
        printf("%d ", i*j);          // O(1) per iteration
    }
}
```

**Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} 1 \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} n \\
&= n \cdot n \\
&= n^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n^2)}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Triple-Nested with Decreasing Inner Loop

```c
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = i; j < n; j++) {
        for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) {
            count++;
        }
    }
}
```

**Derivation (Inner triple summation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i}^{n-1} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 1 \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i}^{n-1} n \\
&= n \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (n - i) \\
&= n \left( n + (n-1) + (n-2) + \cdots + 1 \right) \\
&= n \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n^2(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n^3 + n^2}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Drop lower-order terms and constants:

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n^3)}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Recursive Fibonacci (Exponential)

```c
int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);
}
```

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c$

**Lower Bound Argument:** The recursion tree has $2^k$ nodes at depth $k$. The depth is $n$, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\ge 2^{n/2} \\
\Rightarrow T(n) &= \Omega(2^{n/2}) = \Omega(2^n)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(2^n) \text{ (exponential, impractical for } n > 40)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is why **Dynamic Programming** is used to reduce Fibonacci to $O(n)$ time — by memoizing repeated subproblems.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Algorithm | Recurrence (if any) | Final Complexity | Dominant Step |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | — | $O(n)$ | Single loop |
| Binary Search | $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ | $O(\log n)$ | Halving |
| Nested Loop (2-deep) | — | $O(n^2)$ | Double summation |
| Merge Sort | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Master Thm Case 2 |
| Naïve Rec. Fibonacci | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ | $O(2^n)$ | Branching tree |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Growth Rate Comparison (Mermaid Plot)

The following Mermaid **functional architecture flow** models the comparative growth hierarchy among standard complexity classes, including input-size thresholds where each class becomes intractable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CONSTANT O1 - Always Fastest"]
    B["LOGARITHMIC O LOG N - Halving Strategy"]
    C["LINEAR O N - Single Pass"]
    D["N LOG N - Divide and Conquer Sorting"]
    E["QUADRATIC O N SQUARED - Nested Loops"]
    F["EXPONENTIAL O 2N - Recursive Brute Force"]
    G["FACTORIAL O N FACT - Permutations"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    H["Input n equals 10 - All tractable"]
    I["Input n equals 50 - 2 to the N begins to fail"]
    J["Input n equals 1000 - N squared still OK"]
    K["Input n equals 100000 - Only O N LOG N survives"]

    A -.-> H
    F -.-> I
    E -.-> J
    D -.-> K
```

## 4.2 Asymptotic Notation Relationship (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SET["SET OF ALL FUNCTIONS f(n)"]
        direction TB
        X["f(n) = 3n squared plus 5n plus 100"]
    end

    O_BOX["BIG-O - Upper Bound - O of n squared"]
    THETA_BOX["BIG-THETA - Tight Bound - Theta of n squared"]
    OMEGA_BOX["BIG-OMEGA - Lower Bound - Omega of n squared"]

    X --> O_BOX
    X --> THETA_BOX
    X --> OMEGA_BOX

    O_BOX -.Sandwich.- THETA_BOX
    THETA_BOX -.Sandwich.- OMEGA_BOX
```

## 4.3 Recursion Tree for Fibonacci (Showing Exponential Blowup)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["fib of n"]
    L1["fib of n minus 1"]
    R1["fib of n minus 2"]
    L2A["fib of n minus 2"]
    L2B["fib of n minus 3"]
    R2A["fib of n minus 3"]
    R2B["fib of n minus 4"]

    ROOT --> L1
    ROOT --> R1
    L1 --> L2A
    L1 --> L2B
    R1 --> R2A
    R1 --> R2B

    NOTE["NOTE - Repeated subproblems L2A appears twice - exponential blowup - use DP to memoize"]
    L2A -.annotation.- NOTE
```

## 4.4 Time Complexity Analysis Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1 - Identify Input Size n"]
    S2["STEP 2 - Identify Primitive Operations per Statement"]
    S3["STEP 3 - Express T of n as Sum or Recurrence"]
    S4["STEP 4 - Apply Asymptotic Simplification Rules"]
    S5["STEP 5 - State Final Big-O Result"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    OUT["OUTPUT - Big-O Classification and Engineering Recommendation"]
    S5 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define time complexity. Differentiate between worst-case, best-case, and average-case time complexity with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Time complexity is a measure of the amount of computational time an algorithm takes, expressed as a function $T(n)$ of the input size $n$, typically using asymptotic Big-$O$ notation.

- **Best Case:** Minimum time taken over all inputs of size $n$. For *linear search*, best case is $O(1)$ (key found at the first position).
- **Worst Case:** Maximum time taken over all inputs of size $n$. For *linear search*, worst case is $O(n)$ (key absent or at last position).
- **Average Case:** Expected time, averaged uniformly over all inputs of size $n$. For *linear search*, average case is $O(n)$.

**(1 mark each for definition + best + worst/average distinction)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State and explain Big-$O$, Big-$\Omega$, and Big-$\Theta$ notations. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Big-$O$ ($O(g(n))$):** Asymptotic upper bound — $f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for large $n$. Represents **worst-case** growth.
- **Big-$\Omega$ ($\Omega(g(n))$):** Asymptotic lower bound — $f(n) \ge c \cdot g(n)$ for large $n$. Represents **best-case** growth.
- **Big-$\Theta$ ($\Theta(g(n))$):** Asymptotic tight bound — both upper and lower bounds hold. Represents the **exact order of growth**.

**Relationship:** $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if and only if $f(n) = O(g(n))$ **and** $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$. **(1 mark each for the three definitions + 0.5 for relation.)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Derive the time complexity of the following code segments and express them in Big-$O$ notation:**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**

```c
// Segment 1
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = i; j < n; j++) {
        for (int k = 0; k < j; k++) {
            sum = sum + arr[i] + arr[j] + arr[k];
        }
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i}^{n-1} \sum_{k=0}^{j-1} 1 \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i}^{n-1} j \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \left( \frac{(n-1)n}{2} - \frac{(i-1)i}{2} \right) \\
&\approx \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{n^2 - i^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left( n \cdot n^2 - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} i^2 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left( n^3 - \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} \right) \\
&\approx \frac{n^3}{2} - \frac{n^3}{6} = \frac{n^3}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying asymptotic rules:

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n^3)}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct identification of triple-nested summation — **2 marks**
- Correct inner summation evaluation — **2 marks**
- Correct middle summation using arithmetic series — **1.5 marks**
- Final Big-$O$ simplification — **1.5 marks**

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**

```c
// Segment 2
int p = 0;
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i = i * 2) {
    p = p + 1;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

This is a **logarithmic loop** because $i$ doubles each iteration. The sequence of $i$ values is: $1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots, 2^k$.

Let $k$ be the number of iterations. The loop terminates when $i > n$, i.e., $2^k > n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
2^k &= n \\
k &= \log_2 n
\end{aligned}$$

Total operations: $T(n) = k = \log_2 n$.

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(\log n)}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Identification of logarithmic pattern — **2 marks**
- Setting up $2^k \le n$ inequality — **2 marks**
- Solving for $k$ — **1.5 marks**
- Final Big-$O$ expression — **1.5 marks**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Solve the following recurrence relations and determine the asymptotic time complexity using iterative substitution / Master Theorem logic.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**

$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$

**This represents the recurrence for Merge Sort.**

**Solution via Recursion Tree:**

Level 0: Cost = $n$

Level 1: Two subproblems of size $n/2$, each with cost $n/2$. Total = $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$.

Level 2: Four subproblems of size $n/4$, each with cost $n/4$. Total = $4 \cdot (n/4) = n$.

**General Level $i$:** Total work = $n$.

Number of levels: $\log_2 n + 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= (\log_2 n + 1) \cdot n \\
&= n \log_2 n + n
\end{aligned}$$

Applying asymptotic rules:

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n \log n)}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct recursion tree construction — **2.5 marks**
- Work per level computation — **2 marks**
- Number of levels derivation — **1.5 marks**
- Final Big-$O$ — **1 mark**

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**

$$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$

**Solution via Recursion Tree:**

Level 0: Cost = $n$

Level 1: Four subproblems of size $n/2$. Total = $4 \cdot (n/2) = 2n$.

Level 2: Sixteen subproblems of size $n/4$. Total = $16 \cdot (n/4) = 4n$.

**General Level $i$:** Total work = $n \cdot 2^i$.

Number of levels: $\log_2 n$.

Total work:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n \cdot 2^i \\
&= n \cdot (2^{\log_2 n} - 1) \\
&= n \cdot (n - 1) \\
&= n^2 - n
\end{aligned}$$

Applying asymptotic rules:

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n^2)}$$

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Recursion tree construction — **2 marks**
- Geometric series identification — **2.5 marks**
- Summation evaluation — **1.5 marks**
- Final Big-$O$ — **1 mark**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams (Time Complexity):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to drop constants:** Writing $T(n) = 3n + 5$ and then *incorrectly* stating $O(3n + 5)$ instead of $O(n)$. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing little-$o$ with Big-$O$:** Students write $f(n) = o(n^2)$ when they mean $O(n^2)$. These are *different* — $o$ is a strict inequality.
>
> 3. **Incorrect summation in nested loops:** Writing $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} 1 = n$ instead of $n^2$. Examiner deducts **1.5 to 2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Skipping the step of stating the recurrence:** For recursive functions, KTU examiners expect you to **first write $T(n) = \ldots$** before solving. Omission loses **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Stating Big-$O$ instead of Big-$\Theta$ when asked for exact complexity:** If a question says "find the exact order", answer must be $\Theta$, not $O$. Examiner deducts **0.5 marks**.
>
> 6. **Not specifying worst-case explicitly:** Writing "linear search is $O(n)$" without saying "in the worst case" loses **0.5 marks**.
>
> 7. **Misapplying log rules:** Writing $O(\log n^2) = O((\log n)^2)$ — **WRONG**. $O(\log n^2) = O(2 \log n) = O(\log n)$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity — Module 1 Rapid-Fire Recap:**
>
> - Time complexity $T(n)$ measures the **number of primitive operations**, not wall-clock time.
> - **Big-$O$** → Worst-case upper bound. **Big-$\Omega$** → Best-case lower bound. **Big-$\Theta$** → Exact tight bound.
> - **Always specify the case** (worst / best / average) when stating complexity — KTU examiners check this.
> - **Drop constants** and **drop lower-order terms** in asymptotic simplification.
> - **Loop running $n$ times** → $O(n)$. **Loop with $i = i \cdot 2$ or $i = i/2$** → $O(\log n)$. **Nested $k$ loops** → $O(n^k)$.
> - **Recurrences are solved by** unrolling, recursion trees, or the Master Theorem.
> - **Hierarchy (best to worst):** $O(1) \prec O(\log n) \prec O(n) \prec O(n \log n) \prec O(n^2) \prec O(2^n) \prec O(n!)$.
> - **$\Theta(g(n))$ iff $f(n) = O(g(n))$ AND $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$.**
> - **In KTU exams, "Time complexity" defaults to worst-case Big-$O$ unless stated otherwise.**
> - **Polynomial vs Exponential:** Any polynomial $n^k$ is dominated by $2^n$ for large $n$.
> - **Logarithm property:** $\log_b(n^k) = k \log_b(n) = O(\log n)$ — exponents inside log become multiplicative constants outside.
> - **Amortized analysis** is a separate advanced concept — do *not* confuse with average-case.
> - For **recursive algorithms**, the first step is **always to write down $T(n) = \ldots$** before solving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Asymptotic Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — ASYMPTOTIC NOTATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Asymptotic Notations** are formal mathematical symbols used in computer science to characterize the *running time* (time complexity) or *space consumption* (space complexity) of an algorithm as the input size $n$ approaches a limiting value, typically infinity. They describe the *order of growth* of functions, abstracting away hardware-dependent constants and lower-order terms to expose the intrinsic efficiency of an algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Asymptotic notations are classified as the *primary descriptive vocabulary* of algorithm analysis under the **Algorithm Analysis** unit of Module 1. Mastery of $\mathcal{O}$, $\Omega$, $\Theta$, $o$, and $\omega$ is **mandatory** for Part A, Part B, and university viva voce.

The five standard asymptotic notations are:

| Symbol | Name | Bound Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathcal{O}(g(n))$ | Big-Oh | Upper Bound (Worst Case) |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | Big-Omega | Lower Bound (Best Case) |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | Big-Theta | Tight Bound (Average/Exact Order) |
| $o(g(n))$ | Little-Oh | Strict Upper Bound |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | Little-Omega | Strict Lower Bound |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Speedometer of Code"**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car and want to know how fast it *can* go. You don't care about the exact current speed (which depends on traffic, gear, fuel). You want to know the **maximum capability**. Big-Oh is exactly that — it tells you the *worst-case ceiling* on an algorithm's resource consumption. Big-Omega tells you the *guaranteed minimum performance* you will always get. Big-Theta tells you the car consistently cruises at a known, predictable rate. Asymptotic notations ignore the "noise" of constants and focus only on how the algorithm *scales* — the *governing trend*, not the daily fluctuations.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Algorithm Cost

For every algorithm, KTU examiners expect you to report complexity under three input scenarios:

- **Best Case ($\Omega$):** The *minimum* cost over all possible inputs of size $n$.
- **Average Case ($\Theta$ or expected value):** The *expected* cost over a probability distribution of inputs.
- **Worst Case ($\mathcal{O}$):** The *maximum* cost over all possible inputs of size $n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Industry and academia almost always quote the **worst-case** bound ($\mathcal{O}$) because it provides a *guarantee* — a hard ceiling the program will never exceed. This is a primary reason KTU board questions weight $\mathcal{O}$ analysis heavily.

### 1.4 Why Do We Need Asymptotic Analysis?

The motivation is rooted in the **Limits of Empirical Benchmarking**. Suppose two sorting algorithms sort an array of $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ integers on a 2024 laptop, and the first takes $0.05$ seconds while the second takes $0.02$ seconds. You **cannot conclude** Algorithm 2 is asymptotically faster — the constants differ. For $n = 100{,}000{,}000$, Algorithm 1 may crush Algorithm 2. Asymptotic notations strip away these misleading constants to expose the *true mathematical growth behavior*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The variable $n$ universally denotes the *input size* (number of array elements, bits in a number, vertices in a graph, characters in a string). The variable $c > 0$ is a **positive real constant**, and $n_0 \geq 0$ is the **threshold input size** beyond which the bound becomes valid.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Notations — Mathematical Definitions

### 2.1.1 Big-Oh — $\mathcal{O}(g(n))$ — Asymptotic Upper Bound

**Formal Definition:**

$$\mathcal{O}(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \exists \, c > 0,\, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ such that } 0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \, \forall \, n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

**Plain English:** $f(n)$ grows *no faster than* $g(n)$ for sufficiently large $n$. The constant $c$ absorbs hardware/implementation-specific constants, and $n_0$ specifies "sufficiently large."

**Step-by-step logical meaning:**
- $c \cdot g(n)$: a *scaled-up* version of $g(n)$ that hugs $f(n)$ from above.
- $\forall n \geq n_0$: the bound holds *eventually* — small inputs are irrelevant.
- $0 \leq f(n)$: a non-negativity precondition (complexities are never negative).

### 2.1.2 Big-Omega — $\Omega(g(n))$ — Asymptotic Lower Bound

**Formal Definition:**

$$\Omega(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \exists \, c > 0,\, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ such that } 0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \, \forall \, n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

**Plain English:** $f(n)$ grows *at least as fast as* $g(n)$ for sufficiently large $n$. The role of $c$ and $g(n)$ is now to *hug from below*.

### 2.1.3 Big-Theta — $\Theta(g(n))$ — Asymptotic Tight Bound

**Formal Definition:**

$$\Theta(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \exists \, c_1, c_2 > 0,\, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ such that } 0 \leq c_1 \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 \cdot g(n) \, \forall \, n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

**Plain English:** $f(n)$ is *sandwiched* between two scaled copies of $g(n)$. This is the strongest, most precise bound — it gives both an upper and a lower bound *with the same* $g(n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Equivalence Theorem (often tested):**
> $$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n)) \text{ AND } f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$$
> Whenever both the upper and lower bounds match (up to constant factors), the function is tightly bound.

### 2.1.4 Little-Oh — $o(g(n))$ — Strict Upper Bound

**Formal Definition:**

$$o(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \forall \, c > 0,\, \exists \, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ such that } 0 \leq f(n) < c \cdot g(n) \, \forall \, n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

**Plain English:** $f(n)$ grows *strictly slower* than $g(n)$. The crucial difference from Big-Oh is the *universal quantifier* $\forall c > 0$ — no matter how aggressively you scale $g(n)$, $f(n)$ will eventually fall below it.

**Equivalent Limit Form (used in KTU problems):**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0 \implies f(n) = o(g(n))$$

### 2.1.5 Little-Omega — $\omega(g(n))$ — Strict Lower Bound

**Formal Definition:**

$$\omega(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \forall \, c > 0,\, \exists \, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ such that } 0 \leq c \cdot g(n) < f(n) \, \forall \, n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

**Equivalent Limit Form:**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty \implies f(n) = \omega(g(n))$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Device:** "Little-o is *strict* little Oh, Little-$\omega$ is *strict* little Omega." If you can swap equality for strict inequality universally, you have the *little* notation.

## 2.2 The Master Formula Sheet (KTU Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield KTU Formula Sheet**. Memorize it for Part A and Part B derivations.

| Notation | Bound Type | Existence Quantifier | Definition (Mathematical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathcal{O}(g(n))$ | Upper | $\exists c, \exists n_0$ | $f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | Lower | $\exists c, \exists n_0$ | $f(n) \geq c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | Tight | $\exists c_1, c_2, \exists n_0$ | $c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 g(n)$ |
| $o(g(n))$ | Strict Upper | $\forall c, \exists n_0$ | $f(n) < c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | Strict Lower | $\forall c, \exists n_0$ | $f(n) > c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$ |

### 2.3 Limit-Based Quick-Reference Table

| Limit Behavior | Conclusion |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$ | $f(n) = o(g(n)) = \mathcal{O}(g(n))$ |
| $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = c$ where $0 < c < \infty$ | $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ |
| $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$ | $f(n) = \omega(g(n)) = \Omega(g(n))$ |
| Limit does not exist (oscillation) | Use definition directly |

### 2.4 Common Complexity Classes (Hierarchy)

Ordered by *growth rate* from slowest to fastest:

$$\mathcal{O}(1) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(\log \log n) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(\log n) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(\log^k n) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n}) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(n) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(n \log n) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(n^2) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(n^3) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(2^n) \;<\; \mathcal{O}(n!)$$

### 2.5 Useful Algebraic Identities for Derivations

| Identity | Statement |
| :--- | :--- |
| Polynomial Rule | $a_k n^k + a_{k-1} n^{k-1} + \dots + a_0 = \Theta(n^k)$ |
| Logarithm Rule | $\log_a n = \Theta(\log_b n)$ for any constants $a, b > 1$ |
| Log-Power Rule | $(\log n)^k = o(n^\epsilon)$ for any $k, \epsilon > 0$ |
| Sum of Powers | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i^k = \Theta(n^{k+1})$ |
| Geometric Sum | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} r^i = \Theta(1)$ if $r < 1$, else $\Theta(r^n)$ |
| Stirling's Approximation | $n! \approx \sqrt{2\pi n}\,(n/e)^n$ |

### 2.6 Properties (Theorems Frequently Asked)

1. **Reflexivity:** $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(f(n))$, $f(n) = \Omega(f(n))$, $f(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
2. **Symmetry:** $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n)) \iff g(n) = \Omega(f(n))$.
3. **Transitivity:** If $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n))$ and $g(n) = \mathcal{O}(h(n))$, then $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(h(n))$.
4. **Transpose Symmetry:** $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n)) \iff g(n) = \omega(f(n))$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Asymptotic analysis is foundational in:
- **Compiler Design:** Determining register allocation strategies.
- **Database Indexing:** Choosing B-Tree vs. Hash indexing — $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ vs. $\mathcal{O}(1)$ average.
- **Network Routing:** Dijkstra's $\mathcal{O}((V+E)\log V)$ vs. Bellman-Ford's $\mathcal{O}(VE)$.
- **Machine Learning:** Gradient descent convergence rates — $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$.
- **Cryptography:** RSA decryption cost $\mathcal{O}(k^3)$ where $k$ is the key length.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked derivations are modeled on the **exact valuation pattern** of KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Proving $3n + 5 = \mathcal{O}(n)$

**Problem:** Prove using the formal definition that $f(n) = 3n + 5 \in \mathcal{O}(n)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Goal.** By definition, we must find $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 0$ such that $0 \leq 3n + 5 \leq c \cdot n$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Step 2 — Bound the function from above.** Drop the constant $5$ on the left side (since $5 \leq 5n$ when $n \geq 1$):

$$3n + 5 \leq 3n + 5n = 8n \quad \text{(valid for } n \geq 1\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the constants.**

$$c = 8, \quad n_0 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Verify the definition.**

$$0 \leq 3n + 5 \leq 8n \quad \forall n \geq 1$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$\therefore \; 3n + 5 = \mathcal{O}(n) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Proving $7n^2 + 3n + 2 = \Omega(n^2)$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Goal:** Find $c > 0$, $n_0 \geq 0$ such that $0 \leq c \cdot n^2 \leq 7n^2 + 3n + 2$ for $n \geq n_0$.

**Step 2 — Drop positive terms from the right to obtain a lower bound:**

$$7n^2 + 3n + 2 \geq 7n^2 \quad (\text{since } 3n + 2 \geq 0)$$

**Step 3 — Identify constants:**

$$c = 7, \quad n_0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Verify:**

$$0 \leq 7n^2 \leq 7n^2 + 3n + 2 \quad \forall n \geq 0 \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Proving $\frac{1}{2}n^2 - 3n = \Theta(n^2)$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Two-sided inequality required:**

$$c_1 n^2 \leq \frac{1}{2}n^2 - 3n \leq c_2 n^2$$

**Step 2 — Upper bound (Right side).** For $n \geq 1$, the term $-3n \leq 0$, so:

$$\frac{1}{2}n^2 - 3n \leq \frac{1}{2}n^2 \leq \frac{1}{2} n^2$$

Thus $c_2 = \frac{1}{2}$ works for $n \geq 1$.

**Step 3 — Lower bound (Left side).** For $n \geq 6$, we have $3n \leq \frac{1}{2}n^2$, so:

$$\frac{1}{2}n^2 - 3n \geq \frac{1}{2}n^2 - \frac{1}{2}n^2 = 0$$

That's too weak. Try $n \geq 12$: then $3n \leq \frac{1}{4}n^2$, giving:

$$\frac{1}{2}n^2 - 3n \geq \frac{1}{2}n^2 - \frac{1}{4}n^2 = \frac{1}{4}n^2$$

So $c_1 = \frac{1}{4}$.

**Step 4 — Combine constants.**

$$c_1 = \frac{1}{4}, \quad c_2 = \frac{1}{2}, \quad n_0 = 12$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

$$\frac{1}{4}n^2 \leq \frac{1}{2}n^2 - 3n \leq \frac{1}{2}n^2 \quad \forall n \geq 12$$

$$\therefore \; \frac{1}{2}n^2 - 3n = \Theta(n^2) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Proving $n^2 = o(n^3)$ via Limit Method

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the limit form:**

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^2}{n^3} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Since $L = 0$, the conclusion is:**

$$n^2 = o(n^3)$$

**Step 3 — Verification via definition.** For any $c > 0$, choose $n_0 = \left\lceil \frac{1}{c} \right\rceil + 1$. Then for all $n \geq n_0$:

$$n > \frac{1}{c} \implies \frac{1}{n} < c \implies \frac{n^2}{n^3} = \frac{1}{n} < c \implies n^2 < c \cdot n^3 \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Proving $n \log n = \omega(n)$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the limit:**

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \log n}{n} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \log n = \infty$$

**Step 2 — Since $L = \infty$:**

$$n \log n = \omega(n) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Proving Each Notation for a Given Function

This matrix shows the **strategy** to apply for each type of question KTU typically asks.

| Function $f(n)$ | Question Type | Recommended Strategy | Typical Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $5n^2 + 10n$ | $\mathcal{O}(?)$ | Drop lower-order, absorb constant | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ |
| $5n^2 + 10n$ | $\Omega(?)$ | Keep leading term with smaller constant | $\Omega(n^2)$ |
| $5n^2 + 10n$ | $\Theta(?)$ | Both bounds use $n^2$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $100n + \log n$ | $\mathcal{O}(?)$ | $100n$ dominates $\log n$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| $n!$ | Compare to $2^n$ | Use Stirling: $n! > 2^n$ for $n \geq 4$ | $n! = \omega(2^n)$ |
| $\log_2 n$ vs $\log_{10} n$ | $\Theta(?)$ | Change of base: $\log_a n = \log n / \log a$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |

---

## 3.7 Detailed Algebraic Simplification: $T(n) = 3n^3 + 2n^2 \log n + 5n + 100$

**Goal:** Show $T(n) = \Theta(n^3)$ formally.

**Upper bound ($\mathcal{O}(n^3)$):** For $n \geq 1$, $\log n \leq n$, hence:

$$3n^3 + 2n^2 \log n + 5n + 100 \leq 3n^3 + 2n^3 + 5n^3 + 100n^3 = 110 n^3$$

So $c_2 = 110$, $n_0 = 1$.

**Lower bound ($\Omega(n^3)$):** For $n \geq 1$:

$$3n^3 + 2n^2 \log n + 5n + 100 \geq 3n^3$$

So $c_1 = 3$, $n_0 = 1$.

**Conclusion:** With $c_1 = 3$, $c_2 = 110$, $n_0 = 1$:

$$3n^3 \leq T(n) \leq 110n^3 \quad \forall n \geq 1$$

$$\therefore T(n) = \Theta(n^3) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Asymptotic Notations

The following Mermaid block renders the **logical containment hierarchy** of the five asymptotic notations. Read it as: every $\Theta$ is also an $\mathcal{O}$ and an $\Omega$; every $\mathcal{O}$ is also an $o$ of something larger; etc.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["All Functions f(n)"] --> nodeB["Functions Bounded Above by g(n)"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Functions Bounded Below by g(n)"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Functions Tightly Bounded by g(n)"]

    nodeB --> nodeE["O-g-n: Big-Oh Upper Bound"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["o-g-n: Little-Oh Strict Upper"]

    nodeC --> nodeG["Omega-g-n: Big-Omega Lower Bound"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["omega-g-n: Little-Omega Strict Lower"]

    nodeD --> nodeI["Theta-g-n: Big-Theta Tight Bound"]

    nodeE -.->|"implies"| nodeI
    nodeG -.->|"implies"| nodeI

    style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style nodeE fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style nodeF fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100

    style nodeH fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style nodeI fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The dashed arrows represent the *transitive implication* — if $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n))$ **and** $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ with the *same* $g(n)$, then $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$.

```mermaid

    nodeA --> nodeB{"What is the value of L?"}

    nodeB -->|"0 < L less than infinity"| nodeD["f(n) = Theta-g-n"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Big-Oh notation. Show that $f(n) = 8n + 5$ is $\mathcal{O}(n)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that:

$$0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \quad \forall n \geq n_0$$

**Proof (1.5 marks):** We need to find $c, n_0$ such that $8n + 5 \leq c \cdot n$.

For $n \geq 1$: $\quad 8n + 5 \leq 8n + 5n = 13n$.

Choose $c = 13$, $n_0 = 1$. Then $0 \leq 8n + 5 \leq 13n$ for all $n \geq 1$.

$$\therefore 8n + 5 = \mathcal{O}(n) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between Big-Oh, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta notations with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Notation | Bound | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathcal{O}(g(n))$ | Upper bound — $f(n)$ grows no faster than $g(n)$ | $5n^2 + 3n = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | Lower bound — $f(n)$ grows at least as fast as $g(n)$ | $5n^2 + 3n = \Omega(n^2)$ |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | Tight bound — same upper and lower (up to constants) | $5n^2 + 3n = \Theta(n^2)$ |

**Key Statement (1 mark):** $\Theta(g(n))$ holds only when both $\mathcal{O}$ and $\Omega$ hold with the *same* $g(n)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q.A.(a) Define asymptotic notations $\mathcal{O}$, $\Omega$, and $\Theta$ with formal mathematical definitions. Using these definitions, prove that $\frac{1}{2}n^2 - 5n = \Theta(n^2)$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Formal Definitions (3 marks):**

$$\mathcal{O}(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \exists \, c > 0,\, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ with } 0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \text{ for } n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

$$\Omega(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \exists \, c > 0,\, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ with } 0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \text{ for } n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

$$\Theta(g(n)) = \left\{\, f(n) : \exists \, c_1, c_2 > 0,\, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ with } 0 \leq c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 g(n) \text{ for } n \geq n_0 \,\right\}$$

**Step 2 — Prove Upper Bound $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ (2 marks):** For all $n \geq 1$:

$$\frac{1}{2}n^2 - 5n \leq \frac{1}{2}n^2 \leq 1 \cdot n^2$$

So $c_2 = 1$, $n_0 = 1$ works for the upper bound.

**Step 3 — Prove Lower Bound $\Omega(n^2)$ (2 marks):** For $n \geq 20$:

$$5n \leq \frac{1}{4}n^2$$

$$\frac{1}{2}n^2 - 5n \geq \frac{1}{2}n^2 - \frac{1}{4}n^2 = \frac{1}{4}n^2$$

So $c_1 = \frac{1}{4}$, $n_0 = 20$ works for the lower bound.

**Conclusion:** With $c_1 = \frac{1}{4}$, $c_2 = 1$, $n_0 = 20$:

$$\frac{1}{4}n^2 \leq \frac{1}{2}n^2 - 5n \leq n^2 \quad \forall n \geq 20$$

$$\therefore \frac{1}{2}n^2 - 5n = \Theta(n^2) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

**Q.A.(b) Using the limit method, determine the asymptotic relationship between the following pairs of functions and justify: (i) $f(n) = n^{1.5}$ and $g(n) = n \log n$, (ii) $f(n) = \log^2 n$ and $g(n) = \sqrt{n}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) $n^{1.5}$ vs $n \log n$ (3.5 marks):**

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^{1.5}}{n \log n} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^{0.5}}{\log n}$$

Apply L'Hôpital's rule (treating $n$ as continuous):

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{0.5 \, n^{-0.5}}{1/n} = \lim_{n \to \infty} 0.5 \sqrt{n} = \infty$$

Since $L = \infty$:

$$n^{1.5} = \omega(n \log n) \quad \text{and} \quad n \log n = o(n^{1.5})$$

**(ii) $\log^2 n$ vs $\sqrt{n}$ (3.5 marks):**

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\log^2 n}{n^{0.5}}$$

Apply L'Hôpital's rule twice:

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2 \log n \cdot (1/n)}{0.5 \, n^{-0.5}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2 \log n}{0.5 \, n^{0.5}}$$

Apply L'Hôpital again:

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2/n}{0.25 \, n^{-0.5}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2}{0.25 \, n^{0.5}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{8}{n^{0.5}} = 0$$

Since $L = 0$:

$$\log^2 n = o(\sqrt{n}) \quad \text{and} \quad \sqrt{n} = \omega(\log^2 n)$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.B.(a) Explain in detail the five asymptotic notations ($\mathcal{O}$, $\Omega$, $\Theta$, $o$, $\omega$) with their formal definitions, indicating the type of bound each provides. Give one example for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Notation | Type of Bound | Example |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathcal{O}(g(n))$ | Upper Bound (worst case) | $3n^2 + 5n = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ |
| 2 | $\Omega(g(n))$ | Lower Bound (best case) | $3n^2 + 5n = \Omega(n^2)$ |
| 3 | $\Theta(g(n))$ | Tight Bound (exact order) | $3n^2 + 5n = \Theta(n^2)$ |
| 4 | $o(g(n))$ | Strict Upper Bound | $n^2 = o(n^3)$ |
| 5 | $\omega(g(n))$ | Strict Lower Bound | $n^3 = \omega(n^2)$ |

**Formal Definitions (to be stated for each):**

$$\mathcal{O}(g(n)) = \{f(n) : \exists c > 0, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \; \forall n \geq n_0\}$$

$$\Omega(g(n)) = \{f(n) : \exists c > 0, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \; \forall n \geq n_0\}$$

$$\Theta(g(n)) = \{f(n) : \exists c_1, c_2 > 0, n_0 \geq 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 \leq c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 g(n) \; \forall n \geq n_0\}$$

$$o(g(n)) = \{f(n) : \forall c > 0, \exists n_0 \geq 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 \leq f(n) < c \cdot g(n) \; \forall n \geq n_0\}$$

$$\omega(g(n)) = \{f(n) : \forall c > 0, \exists n_0 \geq 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 \leq c \cdot g(n) < f(n) \; \forall n \geq n_0\}$$

**Key Distinction (1 mark):** Big-Oh and Big-Omega use the *existential* quantifier $\exists$ for $c$ (a single $c$ suffices), while little-o and little-omega use the *universal* quantifier $\forall$ (must hold for *every* $c$).

---

**Q.B.(b) State and prove the equivalence theorem: $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ if and only if $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n))$ and $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$. Illustrate with the function $T(n) = 4n^3 + 7n^2 - 3n + 10$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Theorem Statement (1 mark):**

$$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff [f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n)) \text{ and } f(n) = \Omega(g(n))]$$

**Proof — ($\Rightarrow$ direction) (2 marks):**

Assume $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$. Then by definition, $\exists c_1, c_2, n_0$ such that:

$$c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 g(n)$$

From the left inequality, $c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \Rightarrow f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ with $c = c_1$.
From the right inequality, $f(n) \leq c_2 g(n) \Rightarrow f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n))$ with $c = c_2$.

**Proof — ($\Leftarrow$ direction) (2 marks):**

Assume $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n))$ with constants $c_2, n_0^{(2)}$ and $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ with constants $c_1, n_0^{(1)}$.

Let $n_0 = \max(n_0^{(1)}, n_0^{(2)})$. Then for all $n \geq n_0$:

$$c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 g(n)$$

Hence $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ with constants $c_1, c_2, n_0$.

**Illustration with $T(n) = 4n^3 + 7n^2 - 3n + 10$ (2 marks):**

**Upper bound:** For $n \geq 1$, all terms are dominated by $4n^3 + 7n^3 + 3n^3 + 10n^3 = 24n^3$:

$$T(n) \leq 24n^3 \implies T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^3) \text{ with } c_2 = 24$$

**Lower bound:** For $n \geq 1$:

$$T(n) = 4n^3 + 7n^2 - 3n + 10 \geq 4n^3$$

So $T(n) = \Omega(n^3)$ with $c_1 = 4$.

**Conclusion:** With $c_1 = 4$, $c_2 = 24$, $n_0 = 1$:

$$4n^3 \leq 4n^3 + 7n^2 - 3n + 10 \leq 24n^3 \quad \forall n \geq 1$$

$$\therefore T(n) = \Theta(n^3) \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Asymptotic Notation Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constants $c$ and $n_0$:** Students often write "$f(n) \leq g(n)$" without specifying the constant multiplier $c$ and the threshold $n_0$. **Always state $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 0$ explicitly** — losing these costs 1 mark each.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\mathcal{O}$ with $o$:** Big-Oh ($\mathcal{O}$) admits a *constant* $c$ (existential). Little-Oh ($o$) requires the inequality for *every* $c$ (universal). Writing $n = o(n)$ instead of $n = \mathcal{O}(n)$ is a **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Writing $\Theta$ when only one bound holds:** If a function $f(n) = 5n + 3$, it is $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ and $\Omega(n)$ but **not** $\Theta(n^2)$. Mixing bounds with different $g(n)$ invalidates $\Theta$.
>
> 4. **Omitting the "for all $n \geq n_0$" qualifier:** The bound must hold *eventually* — without this, you have not applied the definition correctly.
>
> 5. **Applying L'Hôpital incorrectly:** L'Hôpital is valid only for $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$ or $\frac{0}{0}$ forms. For indeterminate cases like $\frac{n \log n}{n^2}$, expand first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Asymptotic Notations**

- **Five notations:** $\mathcal{O}$ (upper), $\Omega$ (lower), $\Theta$ (tight), $o$ (strict upper), $\omega$ (strict lower).
- **Universal quantifier $\forall$** appears in *little* notations ($o$, $\omega$); **existential quantifier $\exists$** appears in *big* notations ($\mathcal{O}$, $\Omega$, $\Theta$).
- **The variable $c > 0$** is a positive real constant that absorbs implementation-specific constants; **$n_0 \geq 0$** is the threshold beyond which the bound holds.
- **Limit shortcut:** $L = 0 \Rightarrow o$, $0 < L < \infty \Rightarrow \Theta$, $L = \infty \Rightarrow \omega$.
- **Equivalence theorem:** $\Theta(g) \iff \mathcal{O}(g) \text{ AND } \Omega(g)$ — both bounds must use the *same* $g(n)$.
- **Transpose symmetry:** $\mathcal{O}(g) \iff \Omega$ flipped — i.e., $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(g(n)) \iff g(n) = \Omega(f(n))$.
- **Polynomial rule:** $a_k n^k + \dots + a_0 = \Theta(n^k)$.
- **Logarithm rule:** $\log_a n = \Theta(\log_b n)$ — base of log is irrelevant.
- **Hierarchy (slowest to fastest):** $1 < \log \log n < \log n < \sqrt{n} < n < n \log n < n^2 < 2^n < n!$.
- **Always report three cases:** Best ($\Omega$), Average ($\Theta$/expected), Worst ($\mathcal{O}$).
- **Proof template:** State goal $\to$ bound $f(n)$ $\to$ identify $c, n_0$ $\to$ verify inequality $\to$ conclude with $\blacksquare$.
- **Engineering mantra:** Quote **worst-case $\mathcal{O}$** in industry and academic papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrays: Linear Search and Binary Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arrays: Linear Search and Binary Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Array Data Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** An **Array** is a linear, homogeneous, contiguous data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the **same data type**, accessed via indices starting from `0` (in C/Java) or `1` (in mathematical/algorithmic notation).

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Homogeneity** | All elements must be of the same type |
| **Contiguity** | Elements are stored in adjacent memory locations |
| **Random Access** | Any element can be accessed in **O(1)** time using its index |
| **Fixed Size** | Size is decided at compile-time (static arrays) |

## 1.2 Linear Search (Sequential Search)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Search** is the simplest searching technique that scans each element of the array **sequentially** from the first to the last until the target element is found or the entire array is traversed.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Imagine you dropped your black wallet somewhere in your room. You don't remember where you kept it. What do you do? You start checking one spot at a time — the bed, then the table, then the shelf — until you find it. That is **Linear Search** in action.

## 1.3 Binary Search

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Search** is a **divide-and-conquer** searching algorithm that works on a **sorted array** by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. It compares the target element with the middle element; if they are not equal, the half in which the target cannot lie is eliminated.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Imagine searching for the word "**tortoise**" in an English dictionary. You wouldn't start from page 1 and flip one page at a time. Instead, you'd open roughly the middle, see if your word is in the first half or second half, and repeat. That is **Binary Search**.

## 1.4 Preconditions Comparison

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** Binary Search **MANDATORILY** requires a sorted array. If the array is unsorted, Binary Search will give **incorrect results** with certainty. Linear Search has **no such precondition**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Linear Search — Operational Breakdown

**How it works (step-by-step logic):**

1. Start from the **first index** `i = 0`.
2. Compare `arr[i]` with the **target key** `k`.
3. If `arr[i] == k`, return `i` (element found).
4. Else, increment `i = i + 1`.
5. If `i == n` (array size), return `-1` (not found).

**Why it works:** It guarantees completeness by checking *every* position. The trade-off is **linear time** in the worst case.

## 2.2 Binary Search — Operational Breakdown

**How it works (step-by-step logic):**

1. Define the search space using two pointers: `low = 0`, `high = n - 1`.
2. While `low ≤ high`:
   - Compute `mid = low + (high - low) / 2` (avoids integer overflow).
   - If `arr[mid] == key`, return `mid` (found).
   - Else if `key < arr[mid]`, set `high = mid - 1` (search left half).
   - Else, set `low = mid + 1` (search right half).
3. If loop terminates, return `-1` (not found).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `mid` Calculation — KTU Examiner's Favorite:** Always use `mid = low + (high - low) / 2` instead of `(low + high) / 2`. The latter can overflow for large values of `low` and `high` in languages like C/C++.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 Complexity Analysis Summary

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space Complexity | Precondition |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|------------------|--------------|
| **Linear Search** | **O(1)** | **O(n)** | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | None |
| **Binary Search** | **O(1)** | **O(log₂ n)** | **O(log₂ n)** | **O(1)** iterative / **O(log n)** recursive | **Sorted Array** |

### 2.3.2 Number of Comparisons Table

| Array Size `n` | Linear Search (Max) | Binary Search (Max) |
|----------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 8 | 8 | 3 |
| 16 | 16 | 4 |
| 32 | 32 | 5 |
| 1024 | 1024 | 10 |
| 1,048,576 | 1,048,576 | 20 |

### 2.3.3 Master Theorem Application for Binary Search

The recurrence relation for binary search is:

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c$$

Solving this recurrence by repeatedly substituting:

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k \cdot c$$

The base case is reached when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, which gives $k = \log_2 n$. Therefore:

$$T(n) = T(1) + c \cdot \log_2 n = O(\log_2 n)$$

### 2.3.4 Boundary Condition Checklist (Common KTU Pitfalls)

| Condition | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| `low ≤ high` | Loop continuation (NOT `low < high`) |
| `low = 0` | Start from first index |
| `high = n - 1` | Start from last index |
| `mid = low + (high - low)/2` | Overflow-safe midpoint |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Algorithm Used | Reason |
|--------|----------------|--------|
| **Database Indexing** | Binary Search (B-Trees) | Sorted data, O(log n) queries |
| **Phone Directory Lookup** | Binary Search | Names are alphabetically sorted |
| **Unsorted Log Analysis** | Linear Search | Cannot sort streaming data |
| **Small Arrays (n < 16)** | Linear Search | Cache locality beats log n gain |
| **Embedded Systems** | Linear Search | Lower code footprint, no recursion |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Linear Search — Exhaustive Trace

**Problem:** Search for **key = 47** in array `A = [15, 23, 47, 8, 91, 32]`, size `n = 6`.

| Iteration | Index `i` | `A[i]` | Compare with 47 | Result |
|-----------|-----------|--------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | 0 | 15 | 15 ≠ 47 | Continue |
| 2 | 1 | 23 | 23 ≠ 47 | Continue |
| 3 | 2 | 47 | 47 = 47 | **Found at index 2** |

**Total comparisons (Best case for this run):** 3

If key were absent (e.g., key = 50), the algorithm would scan **all 6 elements** and return `-1`.

---

## 3.2 Linear Search Algorithm — Pseudocode (KTU Standard)

```
ALGORITHM LinearSearch(A[0..n-1], key)
BEGIN
    FOR i ← 0 TO n-1 DO
        IF A[i] = key THEN
            RETURN i          // Element found
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN -1                 // Element not found
END
```

### 3.2.1 Complexity Derivation for Linear Search

The number of comparisons in the **worst case** (when the element is at the last position or absent) is:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 = n$$

Therefore, the worst-case time complexity is **O(n)**. In the best case (element at index 0), it is **O(1)**.

---

## 3.3 Binary Search — Exhaustive Trace

**Problem:** Search for **key = 47** in **sorted array** `A = [8, 15, 23, 32, 47, 91]`, size `n = 6`.

**Initial Setup:**

$$
\text{low} = 0, \quad \text{high} = 5, \quad \text{size} = 6
$$

### 3.3.1 Iteration 1

**Step 1 — Compute midpoint:**

$$
\text{mid} = \text{low} + \left\lfloor \frac{\text{high} - \text{low}}{2} \right\rfloor = 0 + \left\lfloor \frac{5 - 0}{2} \right\rfloor = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Compare:**

$$A[2] = 23, \quad 47 > 23$$

**Step 3 — Discard left half:**

$$\text{low} = \text{mid} + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

**New range:** `low = 3`, `high = 5`

### 3.3.2 Iteration 2

**Step 1 — Compute midpoint:**

$$
\text{mid} = 3 + \left\lfloor \frac{5 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Compare:**

$$A[4] = 47, \quad 47 = 47$$

**Step 3 — Result:** **Element found at index 4**. Algorithm terminates.

**Total comparisons:** 2 (versus 3 in linear search for this dataset).

---

## 3.4 Binary Search Algorithm — Pseudocode (Iterative, KTU Standard)

```
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], key)
BEGIN
    low ← 0
    high ← n - 1
    WHILE low ≤ high DO
        mid ← low + (high - low) / 2
        IF A[mid] = key THEN
            RETURN mid
        ELSE IF key < A[mid] THEN
            high ← mid - 1
        ELSE
            low ← mid + 1
        END IF
    END WHILE
    RETURN -1
END
```

### 3.4.1 Recurrence Relation and Asymptotic Solution

Let $T(n)$ denote the time taken to search an array of size $n$. In each step, the array size is halved:

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases}
c_1, & \text{if } n = 1 \\
T\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + c_2, & \text{if } n > 1
\end{cases}
$$

**Solving by substitution (Unrolling the recurrence):**

$$
T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c_2
$$

$$
T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2c_2
$$

$$
T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k \cdot c_2
$$

The recursion bottoms out when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, i.e., $k = \log_2 n$. Substituting:

$$
T(n) = T(1) + c_2 \cdot \log_2 n = O(\log_2 n)
$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Trace Matrix

**Setup:** Sorted array of 16 elements. Search for the **worst-case element** (one that always falls to the last comparison).

| Search Type | Number of Comparisons | Mathematical Form |
|-------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Linear Search | 16 | $O(n)$ |
| Binary Search | 4 | $O(\log_2 16) = O(4)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** For an array of **1 million** elements, Linear Search may take up to **1,000,000** comparisons, while Binary Search takes at most **20**. This is the power of logarithmic complexity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Linear Search — Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize i = 0]
    B --> C{i less than n}
    C -- No --> D[Return -1 not found]
    C -- Yes --> E{A of i equals key}
    E -- Yes --> F[Return i element found]
    E -- No --> G[Increment i = i + 1]
    G --> C
    D --> H([Stop])
    F --> H
```

## 4.2 Binary Search — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start]) --> I[Initialize low = 0 and high = n minus 1]
    I --> J{low is less than or equal to high}
    J -- No --> K[Return negative 1 not found]
    J -- Yes --> M[Compute mid as low plus high minus low divided by 2]
    M --> C{A of mid equals key}
    C -- Yes --> R[Return mid element found]
    C -- No --> L{key is less than A of mid}
    L -- Yes --> U[Update high equals mid minus 1]
    L -- No --> V[Update low equals mid plus 1]
    U --> J
    V --> J
    K --> E([Stop])
    R --> E
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow — When to Use Which Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Need to search an array] --> Q{Is the array sorted?}
    Q -- No --> L1[Use Linear Search]
    Q -- Yes --> R{Is array size small below 16?}
    R -- Yes --> L2[Use Linear Search better cache locality]
    R -- No --> R2{Is space critical and recursion deep?}
    R2 -- Yes --> B1[Use Iterative Binary Search]
    R2 -- No --> B2[Use Iterative or Recursive Binary Search]
    L1 --> X[Time complexity O of n]
    L2 --> X
    B1 --> Y[Time complexity O of log n]
    B2 --> Y
```

## 4.4 Array Memory Layout Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Index 0] --- A1[Index 1] --- A2[Index 2] --- A3[Index 3] --- A4[Index 4] --- A5[Index 5]
    A0 --- V0[8]
    A1 --- V1[15]
    A2 --- V2[23]
    A3 --- V3[32]
    A4 --- V4[47]
    A5 --- V5[91]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above depicts a **contiguous block of memory** where each index holds a value. The base address plus `index × sizeof(type)` gives the address of any element in **O(1)** time.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Linear Search. Write its best-case, average-case, and worst-case time complexities.**

**Model Answer:**

Linear Search is a sequential searching algorithm that visits each element of the array one by one, starting from the first index, and compares it with the target key until a match is found or the array is exhausted.

- **Best Case:** **O(1)** — Element is found at the first position.
- **Average Case:** **O(n)** — Element is found at the middle on average.
- **Worst Case:** **O(n)** — Element is at the last position or absent.
- **Space Complexity:** **O(1)** — No auxiliary data structure needed.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**Why is Binary Search applicable only on sorted arrays? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Binary Search relies on the **ordering property** of the array to discard half the search space at every step. The algorithm assumes that if the key is greater than the middle element, it must lie in the right sub-array; if smaller, it must lie in the left sub-array.

This assumption holds **only if the array is sorted in ascending (or descending) order**. If the array is unsorted, this assumption fails and the algorithm returns incorrect results.

**Counter-example:** Searching for `key = 10` in unsorted array `[20, 5, 30, 10, 25]`:
- `mid = 2 → A[2] = 30`, since `10 < 30`, algorithm discards right half containing `10`.
- Result: **Incorrect** (returns -1 even though 10 exists at index 3).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Linear Search algorithm with a suitable example. Derive its time complexity in the worst case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the iterative Binary Search algorithm and trace it for finding the element **65** in the sorted array `[12, 23, 34, 45, 56, 65, 78, 89, 90]`. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) Model Answer

**Linear Search Algorithm:**

```
ALGORITHM LinearSearch(A[0..n-1], key)
1. FOR i ← 0 TO n-1
2.     IF A[i] = key
3.         RETURN i
4. RETURN -1
```

**Example:** Search for key = 89 in `[10, 25, 35, 89, 100]`.

| Step | Index | Element | Match? |
|------|-------|---------|--------|
| 1 | 0 | 10 | No |
| 2 | 1 | 25 | No |
| 3 | 2 | 35 | No |
| 4 | 3 | 89 | **Yes — Return 3** |

**Worst-Case Time Complexity Derivation:**

In the worst case (element at last position or absent), the algorithm performs exactly $n$ comparisons:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 = n \implies T(n) = O(n)
$$

**Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ (no auxiliary storage).

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer

**Iterative Binary Search Algorithm:**

```
ALGORITHM BinarySearch(A[0..n-1], key)
1. low ← 0
2. high ← n - 1
3. WHILE low ≤ high
4.     mid ← low + (high - low) / 2
5.     IF A[mid] = key RETURN mid
6.     ELSE IF key < A[mid] THEN high ← mid - 1
7.     ELSE low ← mid + 1
8. RETURN -1
```

**Trace for key = 65 in `[12, 23, 34, 45, 56, 65, 78, 89, 90]`:**

Given: $\text{low} = 0$, $\text{high} = 8$, $\text{size} = 9$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\text{mid} = 0 + \left\lfloor \frac{8 - 0}{2} \right\rfloor = 4
$$

$A[4] = 56$, $65 > 56 \implies \text{low} = 5$.

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\text{mid} = 5 + \left\lfloor \frac{8 - 5}{2} \right\rfloor = 5 + 1 = 6
$$

$A[6] = 78$, $65 < 78 \implies \text{high} = 5$.

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\text{mid} = 5 + \left\lfloor \frac{5 - 5}{2} \right\rfloor = 5
$$

$A[5] = 65$, $65 = 65$ → **Element found at index 5**.

**Total Comparisons:** 3 (much better than 6 in linear search).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Compare Linear Search and Binary Search in terms of preconditions, complexity, and use-cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Trace Binary Search for finding element **50** in sorted array `[10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80]`. Show all iterations with low, high, and mid values. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) Model Answer — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Precondition** | Works on unsorted/sorted arrays | Requires **sorted** array |
| **Approach** | Sequential (brute-force) | Divide and Conquer |
| **Best Case Time** | O(1) | O(1) |
| **Average Case Time** | O(n) | O(log n) |
| **Worst Case Time** | O(n) | O(log n) |
| **Space Complexity** | O(1) | O(1) iterative / O(log n) recursive |
| **Array Access Pattern** | Sequential (cache-friendly) | Random (less cache-friendly) |
| **Use When** | Unsorted data, small arrays, infrequent searches | Sorted data, large arrays, frequent searches |
| **Code Complexity** | Very simple | Moderate (loop invariant handling) |

**Conclusion:** Binary Search is asymptotically superior but has stricter requirements. Linear Search remains relevant for small $n$ and unsorted datasets.

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — Detailed Trace

**Initial State:**

$$
\text{low} = 0, \quad \text{high} = 7, \quad \text{key} = 50
$$

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\text{mid} = 0 + \left\lfloor \frac{7 - 0}{2} \right\rfloor = 3
$$

$A[3] = 40$, $50 > 40 \implies \text{low} = 3 + 1 = 4$.

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\text{mid} = 4 + \left\lfloor \frac{7 - 4}{2} \right\rfloor = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

$A[5] = 60$, $50 < 60 \implies \text{high} = 5 - 1 = 4$.

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\text{mid} = 4 + \left\lfloor \frac{4 - 4}{2} \right\rfloor = 4
$$

$A[4] = 50$, $50 = 50$ → **Element found at index 4**.

**Iteration Summary Table:**

| Iteration | low | high | mid | A[mid] | Comparison | Action |
|-----------|-----|------|-----|--------|------------|--------|
| 1 | 0 | 7 | 3 | 40 | 50 > 40 | low = 4 |
| 2 | 4 | 7 | 5 | 60 | 50 < 60 | high = 4 |
| 3 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 50 | 50 = 50 | **Return 4** |

**Total Comparisons:** 3.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose 2–4 marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong loop condition:** Writing `low < high` instead of `low ≤ high` in Binary Search. This causes the algorithm to miss the last element.
> 2. **Overflow bug:** Using `mid = (low + high) / 2` instead of `mid = low + (high - low) / 2`. KTU examiners often test this for full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the sorted precondition:** Writing "Binary Search works on any array" is a **direct zero** in some papers.
> 4. **No trace table:** For 7-mark questions, KTU requires a *step-by-step trace table* with low, high, mid, and comparisons. Writing only the algorithm without tracing loses 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Confusing best and worst cases:** Linear Search best case is **O(1)**, not O(n). Stating otherwise loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Array** is a contiguous, homogeneous, fixed-size data structure with **O(1)** random access.
- **Linear Search** works on **both sorted and unsorted** arrays with **O(n)** worst-case time.
- **Binary Search** works **only on sorted arrays** with **O(log n)** worst-case time.
- The **mid calculation** `mid = low + (high - low) / 2` is **overflow-safe** and is the KTU-preferred form.
- The loop condition in Binary Search is **`low ≤ high`** (NOT `low < high`).
- Binary Search uses the **Divide and Conquer** paradigm; the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ solves to $O(\log_2 n)$.
- For an array of size $n$, Binary Search performs **at most $\lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil$** comparisons in the worst case.
- **Linear Search** is preferred for **small $n$ (typically $n < 16$)** and **unsorted** data; **Binary Search** is preferred for **large sorted** data.
- In KTU exams, always provide a **trace table** showing `low`, `high`, `mid`, and the comparison result for each iteration of Binary Search.
- The **space complexity** of iterative Binary Search is **O(1)**, while recursive Binary Search is **O(log n)** due to the call stack.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stack** is a linear, ordered data structure that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, wherein the element inserted most recently is the first one to be removed. It abstracts a container with exactly one open end, called the **Top**, through which all insertions (Push) and deletions (Pop) are performed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A stack is formally defined as an *Abstract Data Type (ADT)*, not merely a storage class. The ADT specifies the **logical behavior** (LIFO access), while the **physical implementation** (Array or Linked List) is decoupled from the interface.
