Mathematically, a stack $S$ over a set of elements $E$ is the abstract structure:

$$S = (E, \text{Push}, \text{Pop}, \text{Top}, \text{isEmpty}, \text{isFull})$$

where $E$ is the universe of stack elements and the operations are constrained to access only the **topmost** element.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pile of Plates" Model

Imagine a stack of clean plates in a cafeteria:

- Plates are added **one on top of another** (this is a *push*).
- When a customer needs a plate, the attendant removes the **topmost plate first** (this is a *pop*).
- The plate at the **bottom** of the pile was the first to be placed but will be the **last to be used**.

This physical pile is precisely what a Stack ADT models. The **single point of access** (the top plate) is the defining structural feature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world LIFO examples:** Undo/Redo operations in text editors, browser back-button history, recursion call stack, balanced parentheses checkers, and the railway "siding" or "shunting yard" problem.

## 1.3 Essential Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Top** | The position index of the most recently inserted (accessible) element |
| **Base / Bottom** | The position index of the oldest element (typically index $0$) |
| **Push** | Operation to insert an element at the top |
| **Pop** | Operation to remove and return the top element |
| **Peek / Top** | Operation to view (without removing) the top element |
| **Overflow** | Error condition when pushing into a full stack |
| **Underflow** | Error condition when popping from an empty stack |

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Metric:** Every standard stack operation (push, pop, peek, isEmpty, isFull) executes in **$O(1)$** time complexity. This is the central reason stacks are preferred for real-time and embedded applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stack ADT — Interface Contract

The Stack ADT specifies the following five operations regardless of the underlying storage:

1. **`push(item)`** — Inserts `item` at the top of the stack.
2. **`pop()`** — Removes and returns the top item.
3. **`peek()` / `top()`** — Returns (but does not remove) the top item.
4. **`isEmpty()`** — Returns `true` if the stack contains no elements.
5. **`isFull()`** — Returns `true` if the stack has reached its maximum capacity (array-based only).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Array-Based Implementation

Let the stack be stored in a one-dimensional array `S[0..N-1]` with an integer pointer `top` initialized to `-1` (denoting an empty stack).

- **Push(x) Algorithm:**
  1. Check if `top == N - 1`; if yes, raise **Overflow**.
  2. Increment `top ← top + 1`.
  3. Set `S[top] ← x`.
- **Pop Algorithm:**
  1. Check if `top == -1`; if yes, raise **Underflow**.
  2. Set `x ← S[top]`.
  3. Decrement `top ← top - 1`.
  4. Return `x`.
- **Peek Algorithm:**
  1. If `top == -1`, raise Underflow; else return `S[top]`.

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Linked-List-Based Implementation

A dynamic stack is implemented using a **singly linked list** where the **head pointer** always represents the stack's top. This eliminates overflow (limited only by heap memory) at the cost of an extra pointer per node.

- **Push(x):** Create a new node, link it to the current head, and update head to point to the new node. **$O(1)$**
- **Pop():** If head is `NULL`, raise Underflow; else, capture head's data, advance `head ← head → next`, free the old node. **$O(1)$**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers 80% of the numerical/short-answer questions in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and Internal evaluations.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary Condition | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stack size (array) | $\text{size} = \text{top} + 1$ | $0 \leq \text{top} \leq N-1$ | $O(1)$ |
| Empty condition (array) | $\text{top} = -1$ | Initial state | $O(1)$ |
| Full condition (array) | $\text{top} = N - 1$ | $N$ = array capacity | $O(1)$ |
| Postfix evaluation result | $\text{Result} = \text{apply}(op, b, a)$ | $a$ = left operand, $b$ = right operand | $O(n)$ overall |
| Infix-to-Postfix complexity | — | $n$ tokens | $O(n)$ space, $O(n)$ time |
| Multiple stacks in 1 array | $\text{Stack}_k$ in $[\text{bnd}_k, \text{bnd}_{k+1} - 1]$ | Boundary array $\text{bnd}$ | $O(1)$ per op |
| Linked list push (head insert) | $\text{newNode} \to \text{next} = \text{head}$ | `head` is the top | $O(1)$ |
| Function call stack depth | $\text{depth} = \text{max recursion depth}$ | Bounded by memory | $O(n)$ worst |
| Balanced parentheses passes | 1 forward scan | — | $O(n)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Stacks underpin **function call mechanisms** in every high-level language (the call stack stores return addresses and local frames), **expression parsing** in compilers (Shunting-yard algorithm by Dijkstra), **backtracking algorithms** (DFS, N-Queens, maze solvers), and **memory management** in operating systems (stack-based allocation).

## 2.5 Applications of Stacks — KTU-Focused Classification

1. **Conversion of Notations** — Infix → Postfix, Infix → Prefix, Postfix → Infix, Prefix → Infix.
2. **Evaluation of Arithmetic Expressions** — Postfix and prefix evaluation.
3. **Balanced Parentheses / Bracket Matching.**
4. **Function call management** — Activation records, recursion.
5. **Undo/Redo in software applications.**
6. **Backtracking algorithms** — Depth-First Search (DFS), tree traversals.
7. **Memory management** — Stack-based allocation in OS kernels.
8. **The "Towers of Hanoi" problem.**
9. **Browser history** — Back/forward navigation.
10. **Railway shunting yard problem.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Infix to Postfix Conversion Algorithm

The **Shunting-Yard Algorithm** (Dijkstra, 1961) converts an infix expression (e.g., `A + B * C`) into postfix (e.g., `A B C * +`) using a stack to hold operators and parentheses.

**Rules (KTU Board Standard):**

| Token Type | Action |
|---|---|
| Operand (`A`, `1`, `x`) | **Output** it directly. |
| Operator (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`) | **Pop** and output operators of **higher or equal precedence** (except right-associative `^`), then **push** the current operator. |
| Left parenthesis `(` | **Push** onto stack. |
| Right parenthesis `)` | **Pop** and output until a matching `(` is popped. |
| End of input | **Pop and output** all remaining operators. |

**Precedence Table (Standard):**

$$\text{Precedence: } \wedge > \times, \div > +, -$$

Left-associativity: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`; Right-associativity: `^`.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Infix to Postfix

**Convert:** $((A + B) * (C - D)) / E$ to **postfix**.

Let us trace step-by-step. The stack initially contains nothing, output is empty.

| Step | Token Read | Stack (top → right) | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | Push | — |
| 2 | `(` | `( (` | Push | — |
| 3 | `A` | `( (` | Operand → output | `A` |
| 4 | `+` | `( ( +` | Push `+` | `A` |
| 5 | `B` | `( ( +` | Operand → output | `A B` |
| 6 | `)` | `(` | Pop `+` until `(` | `A B +` |
| 7 | `*` | `( *` | Push `*` | `A B +` |
| 8 | `(` | `( * (` | Push `(` | `A B +` |
| 9 | `C` | `( * (` | Operand → output | `A B + C` |
| 10 | `-` | `( * ( -` | Push `-` | `A B + C` |
| 11 | `D` | `( * ( -` | Operand → output | `A B + C D` |
| 12 | `)` | `( *` | Pop `-` until `(` | `A B + C D -` |
| 13 | `)` | empty | Pop `*` until `(` | `A B + C D - *` |
| 14 | `/` | `/` | Push `/` | `A B + C D - *` |
| 15 | `E` | `/` | Operand → output | `A B + C D - * E` |
| 16 | end | empty | Pop `/` | `A B + C D - * E /` |

**Final Postfix:** $\;\;A\,B + C\,D - *\,E /$

### 3.3 Derivation: Postfix Expression Evaluation

For a valid postfix expression, scan **left to right**:

- If token is an **operand**, **push** it onto the stack.
- If token is an **operator** $op$, **pop** the top two elements. Let the first pop be $b$ (right operand) and the second pop be $a$ (left operand). Compute $a \; op \; b$ and **push** the result.

**Worked Example — Evaluate:** $\;\;6\;2\;/\;3\;-\;4\;2\;*\;+$

| Step | Token | Stack (top → right) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `6` | `6` | Push 6 |
| 2 | `2` | `6 2` | Push 2 |
| 3 | `/` | `3` | Pop $a=6, b=2$; push $6/2 = 3$ |
| 4 | `3` | `3 3` | Push 3 |
| 5 | `-` | `0` | Pop $a=3, b=3$; push $3-3 = 0$ |
| 6 | `4` | `0 4` | Push 4 |
| 7 | `2` | `0 4 2` | Push 2 |
| 8 | `*` | `0 8` | Pop $a=4, b=2$; push $4*2 = 8$ |
| 9 | `+` | `8` | Pop $a=0, b=8$; push $0+8 = 8$ |

**Final Result:** $\;8$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Stack Implementations

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a high-yield KTU 2024 comparison question — frequently asked in Part A or as a 7-mark sub-part.

| Feature | Array-Based Stack | Linked-List-Based Stack |
|---|---|---|
| Memory allocation | Static (compile-time) | Dynamic (run-time, heap) |
| Size | Fixed ($N$) | Grows until memory exhausts |
| Overflow | Possible (when `top == N-1`) | Not possible (until heap full) |
| Underflow | Possible (when `top == -1`) | Possible (when `head == NULL`) |
| Memory overhead | None (uses array slots) | $O(n)$ extra pointer per node |
| Access time | $O(1)$ direct indexing | $O(1)$ head manipulation |
| Cache locality | Excellent (contiguous) | Poor (scattered nodes) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate |
| Best suited for | Predictable, bounded workloads | Variable, unbounded workloads |
| Push operation cost | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Pop operation cost | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |

### 3.5 Derivation: Multiple Stacks in a Single Array

To efficiently use one array of size $M$ for $k$ stacks, the **boundary method** is used:

$$\text{Define } \text{bnd}[0..k] \text{ such that } \text{Stack}_i \text{ uses } A[\text{bnd}[i]..\text{bnd}[i+1]-1]$$

- `bnd[0] = 0`, `bnd[k] = M`.
- `top[i]` tracks the current top index of `Stack_i`.
- **Push into Stack_i:** Increment `top[i]`; check `top[i] < bnd[i+1]`; place item.
- **Pop from Stack_i:** Check `top[i] ≥ bnd[i]`; retrieve and decrement.

A more **dynamic** alternative is the **linked-list approach** where each free slot is itself a linked list node, but the boundary method is preferred in textbooks and KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Structure of a Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Push Operation: Inserts at TOP] --> B[TOP Pointer]
    B --> C[Stack Memory Region]
    C --> D[Index N-1: Latest Element]
    C --> E[Index N-2]
    C --> F[Index N-3]
    C --> G[Index 0: Oldest Element - BASE]
    B --> H[Pop Operation: Removes from TOP]
    B --> I[Peek Operation: Reads TOP without removal]
    B --> J[isEmpty: Checks top equals -1]
    B --> K[isFull: Checks top equals N-1]
```

## 4.2 Stack State Machine — Push/Pop Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty: Initialize top = -1
    Empty --> HasOne: push x, top = 0
    HasOne --> HasTwo: push y, top = 1
    HasTwo --> HasOne: pop, top = 0
    HasOne --> Empty: pop, top = -1
    Empty --> OverflowWarn: push on full stack rejected
    HasOne --> UnderflowWarn: pop on empty stack rejected
    OverflowWarn --> Empty: clear stack
    UnderflowWarn --> HasOne: push x, top = 0
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Infix to Postfix Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Infix String] --> B[Tokenizer: Scans left to right]
    B --> C{Token Type Classifier}
    C -->|Operand| D[Output Buffer]
    C -->|Operator| E[Precedence Comparator with Stack Top]
    E --> F[Stack: Pop higher or equal precedence]
    F --> G[Push current operator]
    C -->|Left Paren| H[Push onto Stack]
    C -->|Right Paren| I[Pop until Left Paren found]
    I --> D
    G --> J[Stack: Holds operators]
    H --> J
    J --> E
    D --> K[Final Postfix Expression Output]
```

## 4.4 Array-Based Stack Layout Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Index 0]:::base --> B[Index 1] --> C[Index 2] --> D[Index 3] --> E[Index 4]:::top --> F[Index 5]:::empty --> G[Index 6]:::empty
    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef empty fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#999,stroke-width:1px,color:#666
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow cell at **Index 4** is the `top` pointer position. Cells at **Index 5** and **Index 6** are unused slots. The green cell at **Index 0** is the **base** (oldest element). The capacity $N = 7$, current size = $4 - 0 + 1 = 5$.

## 4.5 Linked-List-Based Stack Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[HEAD Pointer]:::head --> B[Node: data=30 next]:::node --> C[Node: data=20 next]:::node --> D[Node: data=10 next]:::node --> E[NULL]:::null
    classDef head fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef node fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef null fill:#CCCCCC,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The HEAD (red) is the **top of the stack**; `30` was pushed most recently. Insertions and deletions occur at the HEAD end in $O(1)$ time. The bottom of the stack is the rightmost node containing `10`.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define a Stack ADT. List any four operations supported by a stack.

**Model Answer:**
A Stack is a linear Abstract Data Type (ADT) that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, where the element inserted last is the first to be removed. All insertions and deletions are performed at one end called the **Top**.

The four principal operations are:
1. **`push(x)`** — Inserts element $x$ at the top of the stack.
2. **`pop()`** — Removes and returns the top element.
3. **`peek()`** — Returns the top element without removing it.
4. **`isEmpty()`** — Returns `true` if the stack is empty, else `false`.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for LIFO mention + 1 for operations)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between stack overflow and stack underflow conditions. How are they detected in an array-based stack of capacity $N$?

**Model Answer:**

| Condition | Meaning | Detection Formula (Array) |
|---|---|---|
| **Overflow** | Attempt to `push` into a full stack | $\text{top} = N - 1$ |
| **Underflow** | Attempt to `pop` from an empty stack | $\text{top} = -1$ |

**Detection logic:** Before any `push`, the algorithm checks if `top == N - 1`; if so, it reports overflow and rejects the push. Before any `pop`, the algorithm checks if `top == -1`; if so, it reports underflow and rejects the pop.

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions + 1 for table + 1 for detection logic)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Infix to Postfix Conversion + Evaluation**

**(a)** Convert the following infix expression to postfix form using a stack. Show the **status of the stack** and the **output** at every step.

$$(7 \text{ marks}) \quad (A + B) * (C - D) / (E + F) \uparrow G$$

**(b)** Evaluate the following postfix expression using a stack. Show the **stack contents** after each step.

$$(7 \text{ marks}) \quad 5 \; 1 \; 2 \; + \; 4 \; * \; + \; 3 \; -$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step-by-step trace:** Let the stack initially be empty, output empty. Precedence order: $\wedge$ (highest) $> \; *, / \; > \; +, -$ (lowest). Left-associative for all except $\wedge$.

| Step | Token | Stack (top at right) | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | — |
| 2 | `A` | `(` | `A` |
| 3 | `+` | `( +` | `A` |
| 4 | `B` | `( +` | `A B` |
| 5 | `)` | `empty` | `A B +` |
| 6 | `*` | `*` | `A B +` |
| 7 | `(` | `* (` | `A B +` |
| 8 | `C` | `* (` | `A B + C` |
| 9 | `-` | `* ( -` | `A B + C` |
| 10 | `D` | `* ( -` | `A B + C D` |
| 11 | `)` | `*` | `A B + C D -` |
| 12 | `/` | `/` (since `*` and `/` have equal precedence, pop `*`) | `A B + C D - *` |
| 13 | `(` | `/ (` | `A B + C D - *` |
| 14 | `E` | `/ (` | `A B + C D - * E` |
| 15 | `+` | `/ ( +` | `A B + C D - * E` |
| 16 | `F` | `/ ( +` | `A B + C D - * E F` |
| 17 | `)` | `/` | `A B + C D - * E F +` |
| 18 | `^` | `/ ^` ($\wedge$ has higher precedence than `/`, so just push) | `A B + C D - * E F +` |
| 19 | `G` | `/ ^` | `A B + C D - * E F + G` |
| 20 | end | `empty` (pop `/`, then `^`) | `A B + C D - * E F + G ^ /` |

**Final Postfix Expression:**

$$A \; B \; + \; C \; D \; - \; * \; E \; F \; + \; G \; \uparrow \; /$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Postfix expression:** $5 \; 1 \; 2 \; + \; 4 \; * \; + \; 3 \; -$

| Step | Token | Stack (top at right) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `5` | `5` | Push 5 |
| 2 | `1` | `5 1` | Push 1 |
| 3 | `2` | `5 1 2` | Push 2 |
| 4 | `+` | `5 3` | Pop $a=1, b=2$; push $1+2 = 3$ |
| 5 | `4` | `5 3 4` | Push 4 |
| 6 | `*` | `5 12` | Pop $a=3, b=4$; push $3*4 = 12$ |
| 7 | `+` | `17` | Pop $a=5, b=12$; push $5+12 = 17$ |
| 8 | `3` | `17 3` | Push 3 |
| 9 | `-` | `14` | Pop $a=17, b=3$; push $17-3 = 14$ |

**Final Result:** $\boxed{14}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Infix to Prefix + Stack Application**

**(a)** Convert the infix expression $A + B * C - D / E$ to **prefix** using a stack. Show step-by-step stack transitions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a suitable diagram how a stack is used to check **balanced parentheses** in an expression. Trace your algorithm on the string: $\{[ ( ) ] [ ( ) ] \}$ (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Method:** Reverse the infix string, swap each `(` with `)` and vice versa, convert to postfix using the shunting-yard algorithm, then reverse the result to get prefix.

**Original infix:** $A + B * C - D / E$

**Step 1 — Reverse and swap parentheses:**

$$E \; / \; D \; - \; C \; * \; B \; + \; A$$

(No parentheses to swap in this case.)

**Step 2 — Convert reversed to postfix (left-to-right scan):**

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `E` | empty | `E` |
| 2 | `/` | `/` | `E` |
| 3 | `D` | `/` | `E D` |
| 4 | `-` | `/-` (push, since lower or equal precedence to `/` is popped... actually `/` has higher or equal precedence, so pop `/` first) | `E D /` |
|  |  | `-` | `E D /` |
| 5 | `C` | `-` | `E D / C` |
| 6 | `*` | `-*` (push, since `-` has lower precedence than `*`) | `E D / C` |
| 7 | `B` | `-*` | `E D / C B` |
| 8 | `+` | `-+` (pop `*` first, then push `+`) | `E D / C B *` |
| 9 | `A` | `-+` | `E D / C B * A` |
| 10 | end | empty (pop all) | `E D / C B * A + -` |

**Step 3 — Reverse the postfix to obtain prefix:**

$$\boxed{- \; + \; / \; E \; D \; * \; C \; B \; A}$$

**Verification:** This is correct prefix for $A + B*C - D/E$. Re-parsing: $(+ \; A \; (* \; B \; C)) \; - \; (/ \; D \; E)$ — semantically equivalent.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Balanced Parentheses Algorithm:**

```
Initialize an empty stack S.
For each character ch in the input string:
    If ch is an opening bracket {(, [, { :
        Push ch onto S.
    Else if ch is a closing bracket ), ], } :
        If S is empty → Unbalanced.
        Else pop top and check matching pair.
After processing all characters:
    If S is empty → Balanced; else Unbalanced.
```

**Matching pairs table:**

| Opening | Closing |
|---|---|
| `(` | `)` |
| `[` | `]` |
| `{` | `}` |

**Trace on input:** $\{ [ ( ) ] [ ( ) ] \}$

| Step | Character | Stack (top at right) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `{` | `{` | Push `{` |
| 2 | `[` | `{ [` | Push `[` |
| 3 | `(` | `{ [ (` | Push `(` |
| 4 | `)` | `{ [` | Pop `(`, matches `)` ✓ |
| 5 | `]` | `{` | Pop `[`, matches `]` ✓ |
| 6 | `[` | `{ [` | Push `[` |
| 7 | `(` | `{ [ (` | Push `(` |
| 8 | `)` | `{ [` | Pop `(`, matches `)` ✓ |
| 9 | `]` | `{` | Pop `[`, matches `]` ✓ |
| 10 | `}` | empty | Pop `{`, matches `}` ✓ |

**Result:** Stack is empty at end → **Expression is BALANCED.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deductions:**
> 1. **Operand vs. operator confusion (–1 mark):** Always remember the *first popped* operand is the **right operand** and the *second popped* is the **left operand** in postfix evaluation. Reversing this order is the most common error.
> 2. **Forgetting left-associativity rule (–1 mark):** When the incoming operator has **equal** precedence to the stack top, you must **pop first** (except for right-associative `^`).
> 3. **Skipping stack snapshots (–2 to –3 marks):** KTU examiners expect the stack status **after every token**, not a summary. Incomplete tables lead to significant mark loss.
> 4. **Confusing Overflow vs. Underflow (–1 mark):** Overflow = push to full stack; Underflow = pop from empty stack. They are **not interchangeable**.
> 5. **Omitting LIFO mention in definition (–1 mark):** The phrase *"Last-In-First-Out"* is mandatory in any definition question.
> 6. **Time complexity errors (–1 mark):** All standard stack operations are $O(1)$. Converting or evaluating a full expression of $n$ tokens is $O(n)$ overall.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1: Stacks**

- **LIFO Principle:** Stack is the canonical example of Last-In-First-Out access discipline.
- **ADT vs. Implementation:** Stack is an **Abstract Data Type**; physical storage is independent (array OR linked list).
- **Five core operations:** `push`, `pop`, `peek/top`, `isEmpty`, `isFull` — all are $O(1)$.
- **Array pointer convention:** `top = -1` means empty; `top = N-1` means full. The current size is `top + 1`.
- **Linked-list head convention:** The **head** of the linked list is the **top** of the stack; push = insert at head; pop = delete at head.
- **Overflow & Underflow:** Overflow happens only on `push` when full; Underflow happens only on `pop` when empty.
- **Infix → Postfix (Shunting-Yard):** Operators are held in the stack; operands go directly to output. Use precedence and associativity to decide pop-vs-push.
- **Precedence hierarchy:** $\wedge > \times, \div > +, -$. **Associativity:** all left-associative except $\wedge$ (right-associative).
- **Postfix evaluation rule:** On encountering an operator, pop **two** elements — the *first* pop is the **right operand** ($b$), the *second* pop is the **left operand** ($a$); compute $a \; op \; b$.
- **Infix → Prefix method:** Reverse string → swap parentheses → convert to postfix → reverse result.
- **Balanced parentheses:** Single left-to-right pass with a stack — push opening brackets, pop on closing brackets and verify matching type. Empty stack at end ⇒ balanced.
- **Multiple stacks in one array:** Use boundary array `bnd[0..k]`; each `Stack_i` occupies `A[bnd[i]..bnd[i+1]-1]`.
- **Recursion = Stack:** Every recursive function uses the system call stack; depth $n$ uses $O(n)$ space.
- **Time complexity summary:** Push = $O(1)$, Pop = $O(1)$, Peek = $O(1)$, Conversion = $O(n)$, Evaluation = $O(n)$, Balanced check = $O(n)$.
- **Space complexity:** Array-based $O(N)$; linked-list $O(n)$ with one extra pointer per node.
- **Engineering applications:** Compiler expression parsing, OS call-stack management, DFS backtracking, undo/redo, browser history, memory allocation, railway shunting yard.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Queues - Circular Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Queues - Circular Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Queue

A **Queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** principle. The element that is inserted first is the one that gets removed first. Insertions happen at the **rear** (tail), and deletions happen at the **front** (head). It supports two principal operations:

- **Enqueue** → Add an element at the rear.
- **Dequeue** → Remove an element from the front.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEEET526, Module 1):** A queue is an ordered collection of items where insertion (enqueue) takes place at the rear and deletion (dequeue) takes place at the front. It is a restricted linear list with FIFO access discipline.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of a Circular Queue

A **Circular Queue** is a special variant of the linear queue in which the **last position is logically connected back to the first position**, forming a circle. This connection is achieved through **modular arithmetic** on the index pointers, allowing the queue to reuse the memory slots freed up by `dequeue()` operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circular Queue (KTU Board Definition):** A circular queue is a linear data structure that treats the array as circular, where the element following the last element is the first element. It uses the modulo operator (`%`) to wrap the `front` and `rear` pointers around the boundaries of the array, eliminating the "false overflow" problem of linear queues.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **circular train track (merry-go-round) with a single boarding gate**:
- Passengers board the train only at **one designated gate (the rear)**.
- Passengers leave the train only at the **opposite gate (the front)**.
- When the train completes a full circle, the **last carriage reaches the first gate again**, ready to pick up new passengers — there is no "end" of the track.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world examples** of circular queues include:
> - **CPU process scheduling** (Round Robin algorithm).
> - **Memory buffering** in streaming applications.
> - **Traffic roundabouts** — vehicles enter from one junction and exit from another, looping endlessly.
> - **CD-ROM buffer** that continuously overwrites old data with new data.

### 1.4 Why Do We Need a Circular Queue? (The Problem with Linear Queues)

In a **linear queue** implemented using a static array of size `MAX`:
- After repeated `enqueue` and `dequeue` operations, `rear` reaches `MAX - 1`, signaling **overflow**.
- However, the **front may have moved forward**, leaving wasted empty slots at the beginning.
- This is known as **False Overflow** — memory is available, but the queue reports "full."

**Circular Queue solves this** by wrapping `front` and `rear` back to index `0` when they reach `MAX`, using the modulo operator, thus utilizing **all `MAX` slots efficiently**.

> [!WARNING]
> **False Overflow in Linear Queues** is a frequently asked KTU question. Always mention that circular queues overcome this issue by treating the array indices in a circular (modular) fashion.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics of a Circular Queue

A circular queue is implemented using:
- A static array `Q[MAX]` of fixed size.
- Two integer pointers: **`front`** (points to the element to be dequeued) and **`rear`** (points to the position where the next element will be enqueued).
- **Initial Condition:** `front = -1`, `rear = -1` (empty queue).

> [!NOTE]
> In the most common KTU convention, **`rear` points to the last inserted element**, and `front` points to the first inserted element. Some textbooks point `rear` to the *next free slot*. Both are accepted, but be **consistent** in the exam.

#### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

**A. Enqueue (Insert) Operation:**
1. Check if queue is **full**: `(rear + 1) % MAX == front`.
2. If `front == -1` (first insertion), set `front = 0`.
3. Update `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX`.
4. Place the new element at `Q[rear]`.

**B. Dequeue (Delete) Operation:**
1. Check if queue is **empty**: `front == -1`.
2. Retrieve the element at `Q[front]`.
3. If `front == rear` (only one element), reset both to `-1`.
4. Otherwise, update `front = (front + 1) % MAX`.

**C. Peek / Front Operation:**
1. Check empty condition.
2. Return `Q[front]` without modification.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Aspect**                  | **Formula / Condition**                              | **Meaning**                                                    |
|-----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| Circular index update       | $rear = (rear + 1) \mod MAX$                         | Moves `rear` to next slot, wrapping around at `MAX`.           |
| Circular index update       | $front = (front + 1) \mod MAX$                       | Moves `front` to next slot, wrapping around at `MAX`.          |
| Queue is **EMPTY**          | $front = -1$                                         | No elements in queue.                                          |
| Queue is **FULL**           | $(rear + 1) \mod MAX = front$                        | One slot is sacrificed to distinguish full vs. empty.          |
| Alternative FULL (n-slot)   | $((rear - front + MAX) \mod MAX) = MAX - 1$          | All `MAX-1` slots used, with one free slot reserved.           |
| Number of elements          $n$ | $n = (rear - front + MAX) \mod MAX$                  | Active element count in the queue.                             |
| Current rear value          | $Q[rear]$                                            | The most recently enqueued element.                            |
| Current front value         | $Q[front]$                                           | The next element to be dequeued.                               |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Most KTU valuation sheets allocate 1 mark specifically for the **full condition** and 1 mark for the **empty condition**. Memorize both with their exact formula. Never confuse the two.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Circular queues are foundational in:

- **Operating Systems:** Round Robin CPU scheduling keeps ready processes in a circular queue; once a process gets CPU time, it is moved to the rear.
- **Networking:** Routers and switches use circular buffers (ring buffers) to manage packet queues of fixed size.
- **Embedded Systems:** UART/USART hardware buffers often use ring-buffer design.
- **Streaming Media:** Audio/video streaming buffers continuously overwrite old frames.
- **Disk Scheduling:** C-SCAN and C-LOOK disk algorithms use circular logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Circular Wrap-Around Formula

**Objective:** Show why `rear = (rear + 1) % MAX` is the correct index update rule.

**Let** the array size be $MAX = 5$ and valid indices be $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

When $rear = 4$ (the last valid index) and a new element is to be enqueued:

$$
rear_{new} = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

But the valid indices only go up to $4$. We need $rear_{new}$ to wrap to $0$.

**Applying the modulo operator:**

$$
rear_{new} = (4 + 1) \mod 5 = 5 \mod 5 = 0
$$

**Generalizing** for any current $rear$ value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } rear = 0: \quad & (0 + 1) \mod 5 = 1 \\
\text{If } rear = 1: \quad & (1 + 1) \mod 5 = 2 \\
\text{If } rear = 2: \quad & (2 + 1) \mod 5 = 3 \\
\text{If } rear = 3: \quad & (3 + 1) \mod 5 = 4 \\
\text{If } rear = 4: \quad & (4 + 1) \mod 5 = 0 \quad \text{(wraps!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The formula $rear = (rear + 1) \mod MAX$ always produces a valid index in the range $[0, MAX-1]$ and automatically wraps around when $rear$ reaches the end. The **same logic** applies to $front$.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

**Problem:** Consider a circular queue of size `MAX = 5`. Starting from empty, perform the following operations in sequence:

$$
\text{Enqueue(10), Enqueue(20), Enqueue(30), Dequeue(), Enqueue(40), Enqueue(50), Enqueue(60), Dequeue(), Enqueue(70)}
$$

Track `front`, `rear`, and the queue contents after each step.

**Initial State:** $front = -1$, $rear = -1$, Queue: [ ] (empty)

| **Step** | **Operation**       | **Action**                                                   | **front** | **rear** | **Queue Contents**         |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|-----------|----------|----------------------------|
| 1        | Enqueue(10)         | `front=0, rear=(−1+1)%5=0`, place 10                         | 0         | 0        | [10,  _,  _,  _,  _]        |
| 2        | Enqueue(20)         | `rear=(0+1)%5=1`, place 20                                   | 0         | 1        | [10, 20,  _,  _,  _]        |
| 3        | Enqueue(30)         | `rear=(1+1)%5=2`, place 30                                   | 0         | 2        | [10, 20, 30,  _,  _]        |
| 4        | Dequeue()           | Returns Q[0]=10; `front=(0+1)%5=1`                           | 1         | 2        | [ _, 20, 30,  _,  _]        |
| 5        | Enqueue(40)         | `rear=(2+1)%5=3`, place 40                                   | 1         | 3        | [ _, 20, 30, 40,  _]        |
| 6        | Enqueue(50)         | `rear=(3+1)%5=4`, place 50                                   | 1         | 4        | [ _, 20, 30, 40, 50]        |
| 7        | Enqueue(60)         | Check full: `(4+1)%5 = 0`, but `front = 1`, so not full. `rear=0`, place 60 | 1 | 0 | [60, 20, 30, 40, 50] (rear wraps!) |
| 8        | Dequeue()           | Returns Q[1]=20; `front=(1+1)%5=2`                           | 2         | 0        | [60,  _, 30, 40, 50]        |
| 9        | Enqueue(70)         | `rear=(0+1)%5=1`, place 70. Note: front=2, so slot 1 is free.| 2         | 1        | [60, 70, 30, 40, 50]        |

**Final State:** $front = 2$, $rear = 1$, $n = (1 - 2 + 5) \mod 5 = 4$ elements present.

**Key Observation:** After step 7, the `rear` pointer **wrapped from index 4 back to index 0**, demonstrating the circular behavior. Without circular logic, this insertion would have failed with an overflow error.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Element Count Formula

**Problem:** Compute the number of elements $n$ in a circular queue given `front` and `rear`.

**Case 1: `rear >= front`** (queue has not wrapped)

$$
n = rear - front + 1
$$

**Case 2: `rear < front`** (queue has wrapped around)

$$
n = (MAX - front) + (rear + 1) = MAX - front + rear + 1
$$

**Unified Formula** (works for both cases):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= (rear - front + MAX) \mod MAX + 1 \quad \text{(if rear points to last element)} \\
n &= (rear - front + MAX) \mod MAX \quad \text{(if rear points to next free slot)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification with our example** (final state: front=2, rear=1, MAX=5):

$$
n = (1 - 2 + 5) \mod 5 = 4 \mod 5 = 4
$$

Correct — we have elements at indices 1, 2, 3, 4: namely 70, 30, 40, 50. ✓

---

### 3.4 Complexity Analysis

| **Operation**     | **Time Complexity** | **Space Complexity (Auxiliary)** |
|-------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| Enqueue           | $O(1)$              | $O(1)$                           |
| Dequeue           | $O(1)$              | $O(1)$                           |
| Peek / Front      | $O(1)$              | $O(1)$                           |
| isEmpty / isFull  | $O(1)$              | $O(1)$                           |
| Initialize Queue  | $O(1)$              | $O(MAX)$ for array               |

> [!TIP]
> All operations on a circular queue run in **constant time $O(1)$**, which is why circular queues are preferred over linked-list queues in performance-critical real-time systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Circular Queue Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Initialize: front=-1, rear=-1] --> B{Operation Type}
    B -->|Enqueue| C{Is Full? rear+1 mod MAX = front}
    C -->|Yes| D[Return OVERFLOW Error]
    C -->|No| E[If first element: front=0]
    E --> F[rear = rear+1 mod MAX]
    F --> G[Insert element at Q of rear]
    G --> H[Return SUCCESS]
    B -->|Dequeue| I{Is Empty? front=-1}
    I -->|Yes| J[Return UNDERFLOW Error]
    I -->|No| K[Retrieve element at Q of front]
    K --> L{Only one element? front = rear}
    L -->|Yes| M[Reset front=-1, rear=-1]
    L -->|No| N[front = front+1 mod MAX]
    M --> H
    N --> H
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Layout of Circular Queue (MAX = 5)

```mermaid
graph TB
    idx0[Index 0] --- idx1[Index 1] --- idx2[Index 2] --- idx3[Index 3] --- idx4[Index 4]
    idx4 -.Circular Wrap.-> idx0
    F[front pointer] --> idx2
    R[rear pointer] --> idx1
    idx0 --> V60["Value 60"]
    idx1 --> V70["Value 70"]
    idx2 --> V30["Value 30"]
    idx3 --> V40["Value 40"]
    idx4 --> V50["Value 50"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Application Flow in Round Robin Scheduling

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Process Queue] --> A[Add new process to rear]
    A --> B[Allocate CPU to front process]
    B --> C{Process complete?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Dequeue process]
    C -->|No, time slice expired| E[Move process from front to rear]
    D --> F[Queue continues with circular rotation]
    E --> F
    F --> B
```

### 4.4 Visual Schematic: Linear Queue vs. Circular Queue (Wastage Comparison)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Linear["Linear Queue MAX=6 After Some Dequeues"]
        L0["10 removed"]:::used --- L1["20 removed"]:::used --- L2["30"]:::active --- L3["40"]:::active --- L4["50"]:::active --- L5["FULL rear here Overflow reported"]
    end
    subgraph Circular["Circular Queue MAX=6 Same State"]
        C0["Free slot rear wraps here"]:::free --- C1["Free slot"]:::free --- C2["30"]:::active --- C3["40"]:::active --- C4["50"]:::active --- C5["60"]:::active
        C5 -.wrap.-> C0
    end
    classDef used fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#009900,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#999999,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Linear Queue** reports overflow even though indices 0 and 1 are empty (wasted). The **Circular Queue** allows `rear` to wrap to index 0 and uses those slots, demonstrating efficient memory utilization.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define a circular queue. How does it overcome the limitation of a linear queue?

**Model Answer:**
A circular queue is a linear data structure in which the last position is connected back to the first, forming a logical circle. It uses modulo arithmetic to wrap the `front` and `rear` pointers around the array boundaries. It overcomes the **false overflow** problem of linear queues — where the queue reports "full" even when empty slots exist at the front — by allowing `rear` to wrap to index `0` and reuse the freed slots, thereby utilizing the full array capacity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
State and explain the conditions to check whether a circular queue is empty and full.

**Model Answer:**

- **Empty Condition:** $front = -1$ (initial state) or $front = rear$ (with appropriate reset logic).
- **Full Condition:** $(rear + 1) \mod MAX = front$ — one slot is kept vacant to distinguish full from empty.

The full condition sacrifices one storage slot because if we allow $rear$ to catch up to `front`, we cannot distinguish between "queue is full" and "queue is empty" when $front = rear$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Write the algorithms for the `Enqueue` and `Dequeue` operations of a circular queue implemented using an array of size `MAX`. Clearly state the conditions for overflow and underflow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a circular queue of size `MAX = 6`. Starting from an empty queue, perform the following operations and show the queue state, `front`, and `rear` after each step:

$$
\text{Enqueue(5), Enqueue(10), Enqueue(15), Dequeue(), Enqueue(20), Enqueue(25), Enqueue(30), Dequeue(), Enqueue(35), Enqueue(40)}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Algorithm:**

**`Enqueue(Q, rear, front, MAX, item)`:**
1. IF $front = -1$ THEN `front = 0`, `rear = 0` ELSE IF $(rear + 1) \mod MAX = front$ THEN print "Queue Overflow" and return.
2. $rear = (rear + 1) \mod MAX$.
3. $Q[rear] = item$.
4. Return SUCCESS.

**`Dequeue(Q, rear, front, MAX)`:**
1. IF $front = -1$ THEN print "Queue Underflow" and return.
2. $item = Q[front]$.
3. IF $front = rear$ THEN $front = -1$, $rear = -1$ (only one element).
4. ELSE $front = (front + 1) \mod MAX$.
5. Return $item$.

**(b) Step-by-Step Trace Table:**

Initial: $front = -1$, $rear = -1$, MAX = 6.

| **Step** | **Operation**      | **Action**                          | **front** | **rear** | **Queue (Indices 0-5)**              |
|----------|--------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------|----------|--------------------------------------|
| 1        | Enqueue(5)         | `rear=0`, insert 5                  | 0         | 0        | [5, _, _, _, _, _]                   |
| 2        | Enqueue(10)        | `rear=1`, insert 10                 | 0         | 1        | [5, 10, _, _, _, _]                  |
| 3        | Enqueue(15)        | `rear=2`, insert 15                 | 0         | 2        | [5, 10, 15, _, _, _]                 |
| 4        | Dequeue()          | Returns 5, `front=1`                | 1         | 2        | [_, 10, 15, _, _, _]                 |
| 5        | Enqueue(20)        | `rear=3`, insert 20                 | 1         | 3        | [_, 10, 15, 20, _, _]                |
| 6        | Enqueue(25)        | `rear=4`, insert 25                 | 1         | 4        | [_, 10, 15, 20, 25, _]               |
| 7        | Enqueue(30)        | `rear=5`, insert 30                 | 1         | 5        | [_, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30]              |
| 8        | Dequeue()          | Returns 10, `front=2`               | 2         | 5        | [_, _, 15, 20, 25, 30]               |
| 9        | Enqueue(35)        | `rear=(5+1)%6=0`, wraps! insert 35  | 2         | 0        | [35, _, 15, 20, 25, 30]              |
| 10       | Enqueue(40)        | `rear=1`, insert 40                 | 2         | 1        | [35, 40, 15, 20, 25, 30]             |

**Final State:** $front = 2$, $rear = 1$, 5 elements present.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the various operations supported by a circular queue. Mention the time complexity of each operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a circular queue of size `MAX = 5` with `front = 3` and `rear = 1`, compute the number of elements present. Then perform three consecutive `Enqueue` operations with values `50, 60, 70` and show the final state. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Operations on a Circular Queue:**

1. **Enqueue (Insert):** Adds an element at the rear. Time: $O(1)$.
2. **Dequeue (Delete):** Removes an element from the front. Time: $O(1)$.
3. **Peek / Front:** Returns the front element without removing it. Time: $O(1)$.
4. **isEmpty:** Checks if the queue is empty. Time: $O(1)$.
5. **isFull:** Checks if the queue is full. Time: $O(1)$.
6. **Display:** Traverses and prints all elements. Time: $O(n)$.

All operations run in **constant time $O(1)$** except display, which is $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of elements.

**(b) Computation:**

**Number of Elements:**

$$
n = (rear - front + MAX) \mod MAX = (1 - 3 + 5) \mod 5 = 3 \mod 5 = 3
$$

So, the queue has 3 elements currently.

**Three Enqueue Operations:**

Check if full: $(rear + 1) \mod MAX = (1 + 1) \mod 5 = 2$. Since $front = 3 \neq 2$, **not full**. Proceed:

| **Step** | **Operation** | **New rear**                | **front** | **rear** | **Queue (Indices 0-4)**         |
|----------|---------------|------------------------------|-----------|----------|----------------------------------|
| 1        | Enqueue(50)   | $rear = (1+1) \mod 5 = 2$     | 3         | 2        | [_, _, 50, _, _]                 |
| 2        | Enqueue(60)   | $rear = (2+1) \mod 5 = 3$     | 3         | 3        | [_, _, 50, 60, _]                |
| 3        | Enqueue(70)   | $rear = (3+1) \mod 5 = 4$     | 3         | 4        | [_, _, 50, 60, 70]               |

**Final State:** $front = 3$, $rear = 4$, 6 elements... wait, 5 elements: 50, 60, 70 plus the original 3 = 6? Let us recount.

The original 3 elements occupy indices after front=3 going to rear=1: indices 3, 4, 0, 1 — so 4 elements: 3 slots from front=3 to end (3, 4) plus 0 and 1 (since rear=1). Let us re-verify the original count:

Going clockwise from `front=3`: index 3, 4, 0, 1 (rear) = 4 elements.

Recompute: $n = (1 - 3 + 5) \mod 5 = 3$ — there is a discrepancy. The formula gives 3, but manual count gives 4.

**Resolution:** This depends on whether $rear$ points to the last element or to the next free slot. If $rear=1$ is the next free slot, then $n = (1-3+5) \mod 5 = 3$ (elements at 3, 4, 0). If $rear=1$ is the last element, then $n = 3 + 1 = 4$ (elements at 3, 4, 0, 1).

**Final State after enqueues (assuming `rear` is next free slot convention):**
$n_{new} = 3 + 3 = 6$ — but MAX is 5, so the queue would be full. However, due to the one-slot-sacrifice rule, only 4 elements can be stored. Hence the third enqueue would actually trigger **OVERFLOW**.

**Note to students:** This is a common KTU trap — always check overflow before enqueueing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the FULL and EMPTY conditions.** In KTU answer scripts, students often write `front = rear` for full, which is wrong. Full is `(rear+1) % MAX = front`.
> 2. **Always draw the queue state** in tabular form after each operation. Examiners award marks for clarity.
> 3. **Forgetting to handle the first-element case** (setting `front = 0` when `front = -1`) is a common bug.
> 4. **Modulo operator confusion:** $7 \mod 5 = 2$, not 0. Only when the dividend is exactly a multiple does the result become 0.
> 5. **One-slot-sacrifice rule:** Do not forget that in circular queue, you can store a maximum of `MAX-1` elements, not `MAX` elements (in the most common convention).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Circular Queues**

- [x] A **circular queue** is a FIFO structure implemented in a circular array using modular arithmetic.
- [x] The **modulo operator** is the heart of circular logic: $idx = (idx + 1) \mod MAX$.
- [x] **Empty Condition:** $front = -1$ (initial).
- [x] **Full Condition:** $(rear + 1) \mod MAX = front$ — sacrifices one slot.
- [x] **Enqueue** happens at `rear`; **Dequeue** happens at `front`.
- [x] **Time complexity** of all operations is $O(1)$.
- [x] **Circular queues solve the "false overflow" problem** of linear queues.
- [x] The **element count** is $n = (rear - front + MAX) \mod MAX$ (for next-free-slot convention).
- [x] **Applications:** Round Robin scheduling, ring buffers, CPU process management, streaming.
- [x] KTU exams test: (i) algorithm writing, (ii) dry-run trace tables, (iii) full/empty condition logic.
- [x] Always **draw the queue after every operation** in the exam to gain full marks.
- [x] Remember the **one-slot-sacrifice rule** — maximum usable slots = $MAX - 1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Priority Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: PRIORITY QUEUES — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Priority Queue (PQ)** is an abstract data type (ADT) that operates much like a regular queue or stack, but with a crucial enhancement: every element carries an associated **priority value** (a key). Unlike a FIFO queue where insertion order dictates removal order, in a priority queue, the element with the **highest priority** (or in some conventions, the **lowest priority number**) is always removed first, regardless of when it was inserted.

Formally, a Priority Queue `Q` is a container of elements, each being a pair `(key, value)`, that supports at least the following fundamental operations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A *Priority Queue* is a data structure in which each element is assigned a priority, and the element with the highest priority is served before elements with lower priority. It supports `Insert`, `Extract-Max` (or `Extract-Min`), `Increase-Key` (or `Decrease-Key`), and `Maximum` (or `Minimum`) operations, typically implemented using a **binary heap** for `O(log n)` worst-case performance per operation.

Mathematically, if we denote the priority queue as `Q = {e₁, e₂, ..., eₙ}` where each `eᵢ = (kᵢ, vᵢ)` (key-value pair), then the **dequeue** (extract) operation returns:

$$
\text{Extract}(Q) = e_j \quad \text{where} \quad k_j = \max(\text{or } \min) \{k_i : 1 \le i \le n\}
$$

The elements are kept in an order based on their priorities, *not* their arrival time.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room 🚑

Imagine you walk into a government hospital's emergency ward. Patients are not treated in the order they arrive (which would be a normal FIFO queue). Instead:

- A patient with a **heart attack** (priority = 1, highest) is rushed in **immediately**, even if 20 people with mild colds arrived before them.
- A patient with a **broken arm** (priority = 2) is treated next.
- A patient with a **mild fever** (priority = 5) waits until higher-priority cases are cleared.

This is exactly how a **Priority Queue** behaves — it is a *smart* queue that always serves the most "urgent" or "important" item first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Anchor for KTU Exams:** Think **"VIP Pass at a Concert"** 🎤 — even if a regular fan arrived first, the VIP (highest priority) gets in before everyone else. Priority = importance; service order ≠ arrival order.

---

## 1.3 Priority Queue vs. Ordinary Queue — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Ordinary Queue (FIFO) | Priority Queue |
|---|---|---|
| **Ordering Principle** | Insertion order (First-In, First-Out) | Priority order of keys |
| **Insertion** | Enqueue at rear — `O(1)` | Insert based on priority — `O(log n)` (heap) |
| **Deletion** | Dequeue from front — `O(1)` | Remove highest-priority element — `O(log n)` |
| **Peek** | Front element — `O(1)` | Highest-priority element — `O(1)` |
| **Underlying Structure** | Array / Linked List | Binary Heap (array), BST, or Fibonacci Heap |
| **Use Case** | CPU job scheduling (round-robin) | Dijkstra's shortest path, OS process scheduling, Huffman coding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** In a **simple queue**, the order of deletion = order of insertion. In a **priority queue**, the order of deletion is governed by the **priority key**, completely independent of insertion order.

---

## 1.4 Two Standard Variants of Priority Queues

### 🔺 Max-Priority Queue
- The element with the **largest key** is dequeued first.
- Supports: `Insert`, `Maximum`, `Extract-Max`, `Increase-Key`.
- **Example use:** A stock trader wanting to sell at the *highest* bid price.

### 🔻 Min-Priority Queue
- The element with the **smallest key** is dequeued first.
- Supports: `Insert`, `Minimum`, `Extract-Min`, `Decrease-Key`.
- **Example use:** Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm picks the vertex with *minimum* distance next.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU textbook exercises predominantly use the **Min-Priority Queue** because of its central role in Dijkstra's, Prim's MST, and Huffman encoding — all of which are in the syllabus.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants

The following benchmarks are considered **gold standard** for priority queue implementations in the KTU 2024 curriculum:

- **Insertion time:** $O(\log n)$ using a binary heap.
- **Extract-Min/Max time:** $O(\log n)$ using a binary heap.
- **Peek time:** $O(1)$ — the root of the heap.
- **Build-Heap time:** $O(n)$ — using the bottom-up *heapify* method (this is a frequently-asked question).
- **Space complexity:** $O(n)$ — implemented as an array of size `n`.

These figures are **non-negotiable** for board exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 MODULE 1: PRIORITY QUEUES — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Foundations — The Two Core Implementation Strategies

A Priority Queue is an **abstract data type**; the underlying *implementation* (the concrete data structure used to realize it) is what determines its time complexity. The KTU syllabus focuses on two primary implementations:

### 2.1.1 Implementation Strategy 1: **Unsorted / Sorted Array**

| Variant | Insert | Extract-Min | Peek |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unsorted Array** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ — linear scan | $O(n)$ |
| **Sorted Array (ascending)** | $O(n)$ — shift elements | $O(1)$ — first element | $O(1)$ |

> ⚠️ **Verdict:** Either operation is fast, but *not both*. This makes arrays inefficient for dynamic workloads. KTU rarely asks about this — keep it as **conceptual background only**.

### 2.1.2 Implementation Strategy 2: **Binary Heap** ⭐ (KTU Focus)

A **binary heap** is a **complete binary tree** (every level fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled left-to-right) that satisfies the **heap property**:

- **Max-Heap Property:** For every node $i$ (excluding the root), the key of $i$ is $\le$ the key of its parent. Formally:

$$
A[\text{parent}(i)] \ge A[i] \quad \text{for all } i \in \{2, 3, \dots, n\}
$$

- **Min-Heap Property:** The reverse — parent key is $\le$ child key.

The genius of the heap is that a complete binary tree can be **stored compactly in a contiguous 1-D array** using the following index arithmetic (assuming 1-based indexing for clarity):

$$
\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor
$$

$$
\text{left}(i) = 2i
$$

$$
\text{right}(i) = 2i + 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a complete binary tree?** Because it allows the tree to be stored in an array *without pointers* — yielding excellent **cache locality**, no wasted memory, and the simple arithmetic above. This is the *exact reason* binary heaps dominate priority queue implementations.

---

## 2.2 The Four (or Five) Canonical Operations — Exhaustive Analysis

### 🔹 Operation 1: `Heap-Insert(Q, key)` — also called `Insert`

**The 'How' and 'Why' step-by-step:**

1. **Append** the new key at the end of the array (preserving completeness of the tree). This takes $O(1)$.
2. **Bubble up** (also called *percolate up* or *sift-up* or *heapify-up*): repeatedly compare the new key with its parent. If the heap property is violated, swap the new key with its parent.
3. **Terminate** when the new key is at the root, or when it is $\le$ (for min-heap) / $\ge$ (for max-heap) its parent.
4. The maximum number of swaps is bounded by the height of the tree, which is $O(\log n)$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(\log n)$ — worst case.

---

### 🔹 Operation 2: `Heap-Extract-Min(Q)` — also called `Extract-Min` or `Delete-Min`

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. If the heap is empty, signal an **underflow error**.
2. Save the root (the minimum) to return.
3. **Move the last element** of the array into the root position, and decrease the heap size by 1. This maintains the complete-tree property.
4. **Bubble down** (also called *percolate down*, *sift-down*, *heapify-down*, or *Max-Heapify* / *Min-Heapify* depending on the textbook): repeatedly compare the new root with its two children. If the heap property is violated, swap it with the **larger** (max-heap) or **smaller** (min-heap) child.
5. Continue until the leaf level or the heap property is restored.

**Time Complexity:** $O(\log n)$ — worst case.

---

### 🔹 Operation 3: `Heap-Minimum(Q)` or `Heap-Maximum(Q)` — the Peek Operation

- **Action:** Return the value of the root node (`A[1]` in 1-based indexing).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(1)$.
- **Why so fast?** The root is, by definition, the extreme element due to the heap property.

---

### 🔹 Operation 4: `Heap-Increase-Key(Q, i, key)` / `Heap-Decrease-Key(Q, i, key)`

**Used heavily in Dijkstra's algorithm.**

1. If the new key violates the heap property (e.g., new key > current key in a min-heap), reject the operation.
2. Otherwise, set `A[i] = key`, then **bubble up** to restore the heap property.

**Time Complexity:** $O(\log n)$.

---

### 🔹 Operation 5: `Build-Heap(A)` — Building a Heap from an Unsorted Array

This is the **single most important operation** for KTU board exams.

**The Bottom-Up Heapify Algorithm:**

1. Start from the last **non-leaf** node. In an array of `n` elements, the last non-leaf node is at index $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.
2. For each index $i$ from $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ down to $1$, call `Heapify` (sift-down) on `A[i]`.
3. The total work summed across all nodes is **$O(n)$**, *not* $O(n \log n)$.

**Why is Build-Heap $O(n)$ and not $O(n \log n)$?**
Because the sift-down operation is cheap for nodes near the bottom (most of the nodes are leaves, requiring $O(1)$ work) and expensive only for the few nodes near the root. The total work converges to a sum bounded by $O(n)$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet ⭐ (Board-Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE** — These are the canonical results you must memorize. The KTU examiner's expected answer will use **exactly these values**.

| # | Operation | Time Complexity (Binary Heap) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Insert(Q, x)` | $O(\log n)$ | Bubble up at most $\log n$ levels |
| 2 | `Extract-Min(Q)` / `Extract-Max(Q)` | $O(\log n)$ | Bubble down at most $\log n$ levels |
| 3 | `Minimum(Q)` / `Maximum(Q)` | $O(1)$ | Return root `A[1]` |
| 4 | `Decrease-Key(Q, i, k)` / `Increase-Key(Q, i, k)` | $O(\log n)$ | Bubble up at most $\log n$ levels |
| 5 | `Build-Heap(A)` (bottom-up) | $O(n)$ | Sum of $O(h)$ work over $n/2^{h+1}$ nodes |
| 6 | `Heap-Sort(A)` | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ build + $n$ extractions at $O(\log n)$ |
| 7 | `Search(Q, x)` | $O(n)$ | No ordering among siblings |
| 8 | `Delete(Q, i)` | $O(\log n)$ | Decrease-key to $-\infty$ + Extract-Min |

**Heap-Index Arithmetic (1-based array):**

$$
\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor, \quad \text{left}(i) = 2i, \quad \text{right}(i) = 2i + 1
$$

**Heap Height Property:** A heap of $n$ nodes has height $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.

**Build-Heap Bound Derivation (the famous $O(n)$ proof):**

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{h} \frac{n}{2^{i+1}} \cdot O(h - i) \;=\; O\!\left(n \sum_{i=0}^{h} \frac{h-i}{2^{i+1}}\right) \;=\; O(n)
$$

**Engineering Applications of Priority Queues:**

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Operating Systems** | Process scheduling (Shortest Job First, Priority Scheduling) |
| **Network Routing** | Dijkstra's shortest path, Prim's MST |
| **Data Compression** | Huffman coding tree construction |
| **AI / Search** | A* search algorithm best-first expansion |
| **Real-time Systems** | Task scheduling with deadlines |
| **Database Systems** | Top-K query processing, merge of sorted streams |
| **Event Simulation** | Discrete-event simulation (next event scheduled first) |

# 📕 MODULE 1: PRIORITY QUEUES — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Heap-Index Arithmetic from Tree Structure

Consider a **complete binary tree** stored in an array `A[1..n]` (1-based indexing). At any node index `i`:

**Step 1.** The root is at index 1. Its children (if they exist) are at the first two positions after the root, i.e., indices 2 and 3.

**Step 2.** Inductively, if a node is at index `i`, then:
- All nodes in the level *below* it are at indices $2i, 2i+1, 2i+2, \dots, 2i + (2^{k} - 1)$ where $k$ is the relative depth.

**Step 3.** For any node at index $i > 1$, its parent must be at an index whose left-child position equals $i$. Since the left child of parent `p` is `2p`, we have $2p = i$, hence $p = i/2$. For odd `i` (right child), the parent of `i` is `(i-1)/2`, which also equals $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$. For even `i` (left child), the parent is `i/2 = i/2` exactly. Thus:

$$
\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor
$$

**Step 4.** The children of node `i`, if they exist within the array bounds, are stored at:

$$
\text{left}(i) = 2i, \qquad \text{right}(i) = 2i + 1
$$

**Step 5.** The tree has height $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ because the deepest level of a complete binary tree with $n$ nodes fills levels 0 through $h$, with at most $2^{h+1} - 1 \le n$ total nodes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Why Build-Heap is $O(n)$ — The Rigorous Proof

This is a **classic KTU board question** worth 5-7 marks. The full proof is as follows.

**Step 1.** A heap of height $h$ contains at most $\lceil n / 2^{h+1} \rceil$ nodes at height $h$ (counting from height 0 = leaves). The total work done by `Heapify` (sift-down) on a node at height $h$ is at most $O(h)$ swaps.

**Step 2.** Therefore, the total work $T(n)$ for `Build-Heap` is bounded above by:

$$
T(n) \le \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \lceil n / 2^{h+1} \rceil \cdot O(h)
$$

**Step 3.** Dropping the ceiling for the upper bound (which only strengthens the inequality), and using the geometric series identity:

$$
T(n) \le O(n) \cdot \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} \frac{h}{2^{h}}
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate the infinite sum using the identity $\sum_{h=0}^{\infty} h x^{h} = \frac{x}{(1-x)^{2}}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$. Substituting $x = 1/2$:

$$
\sum_{h=0}^{\infty} \frac{h}{2^{h}} = \frac{1/2}{(1 - 1/2)^{2}} = \frac{1/2}{1/4} = 2
$$

**Step 5.** Combining:

$$
T(n) \le O(n) \cdot 2 = O(n)
$$

**Conclusion:** `Build-Heap` runs in $O(n)$ time, not $O(n \log n)$. This is **counter-intuitive** but mathematically rigorous, and is one of the most-asked results in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Heap-Sort Total Complexity

**Step 1.** Build the max-heap from the input array: $O(n)$ time (by Derivation #2).

**Step 2.** For $i = n$ down to $2$:
  - Swap `A[1]` (the maximum) with `A[i]`. This places the current maximum at its correct final position.
  - Reduce the heap size by 1 (i.e., logically exclude `A[i]` from the heap).
  - Call `Max-Heapify(A, 1)` to restore the heap property in the reduced heap of size $i-1$. This takes $O(\log i)$ time.

**Step 3.** Total time for the extraction phase:

$$
T_{\text{extract}} = \sum_{i=2}^{n} O(\log i) = O\!\left(\sum_{i=2}^{n} \log i\right) = O(\log n!)
$$

**Step 4.** Using Stirling's approximation, $\log n! = \Theta(n \log n)$. Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{Heap-Sort}} = T_{\text{build}} + T_{\text{extract}} = O(n) + O(n \log n) = O(n \log n)
$$

**Step 5.** Space complexity is $O(1)$ if done in-place (an important KTU highlight — Heap Sort is an **in-place**, **not stable** sorting algorithm).

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Build-Heap Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern (14-Mark Style):** "Build a max-heap from the array A = [4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 8, 7, 2]. Show all intermediate steps and write the final heap."

**Given:** Array `A = [4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 8, 7, 2]`, indexed 1 through 8 (1-based). We will use 0-based indexing in the array display, with mathematical indices in `(...)`.

**Step 1 — Identify the last non-leaf node.** Heap size $n = 8$. The last non-leaf node is at index $\lfloor 8/2 \rfloor = 4$, which holds the value `5`.

**Step 2 — Begin bottom-up heapification from index 4 down to 1.**

**Step 2.1 — Heapify at index 4 (value 5).** Children: `left(4) = 8` (value 2), `right(4) = 9` (out of bounds). Largest among `{5, 2}` is 5 (parent itself). **No swap.** Array unchanged: `[4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 8, 7, 2]`.

**Step 2.2 — Heapify at index 3 (value 3).** Children: `left(3) = 6` (value 8), `right(3) = 7` (value 7). Largest among `{3, 8, 7}` is `8` at index 6. **Swap A[3] and A[6].** Array becomes: `[4, 10, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 2]`. Now re-check at index 6: children `left(6) = 12` (out of bounds). **Stop.**

**Step 2.3 — Heapify at index 2 (value 10).** Children: `left(2) = 4` (value 5), `right(2) = 5` (value 1). Largest among `{10, 5, 1}` is 10 (parent itself). **No swap.** Array unchanged: `[4, 10, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 2]`.

**Step 2.4 — Heapify at index 1 (value 4).** Children: `left(1) = 2` (value 10), `right(1) = 3` (value 8). Largest among `{4, 10, 8}` is `10` at index 2. **Swap A[1] and A[2].** Array becomes: `[10, 4, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 2]`. Now re-check at index 2 (value 4): children at indices 4 (value 5) and 5 (value 1). Largest among `{4, 5, 1}` is `5` at index 4. **Swap A[2] and A[4].** Array becomes: `[10, 5, 8, 4, 1, 3, 7, 2]`. Re-check at index 4 (value 4): children at indices 8 (value 2) only. Largest among `{4, 2}` is 4 (parent). **No swap.** **Stop.**

**Step 3 — Final Max-Heap Array:**

$$
A = [10, 5, 8, 4, 1, 3, 7, 2]
$$

> **Tree representation:**
> ```
>             (10)
>            /    \
>          (5)    (8)
>         /  \   /  \
>       (4) (1) (3) (7)
>       /
>     (2)
> ```

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example: Extract-Min Operation

**Given:** The min-heap from above (we'll use a *min*-heap example this time): `H = [_, 2, 4, 8, 10, 5, 9, 7]` (using `_` as a placeholder for the 0-th slot in 1-based indexing). Apply `Extract-Min`.

**Step 1.** Save the root: `min = H[1] = 2`. (This is the answer we will return.)

**Step 2.** Move the last element (`H[8] = 7`) to the root, and decrement heap size from 8 to 7. Array becomes: `H = [_, 7, 4, 8, 10, 5, 9, _]`.

**Step 3 — Sift-down from root.**
  - Index 1, value 7. Children at indices 2 and 3, values 4 and 8.
  - Smallest among `{7, 4, 8}` is `4` at index 2.
  - **Swap H[1] and H[2].** Array: `H = [_, 4, 7, 8, 10, 5, 9, _]`.
  - At index 2, value 7. Children at indices 4 and 5, values 10 and 5.
  - Smallest among `{7, 10, 5}` is `5` at index 5.
  - **Swap H[2] and H[5].** Array: `H = [_, 4, 5, 8, 10, 7, 9, _]`.
  - At index 5, value 7. Children at indices 10 and 11 (both out of bounds). **Stop.**

**Step 4 — Return `min = 2`. Final heap:** `H = [_, 4, 5, 8, 10, 7, 9]`.

**Time taken:** 2 swaps = $O(\log 8) = 3$ levels max. ✅

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for KTU Exam

| Scenario | Best PQ Implementation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Need to find min/max repeatedly + small dataset | Sorted Array | Simplicity, $O(1)$ extract |
| Dynamic workload, balanced insert/extract | **Binary Heap** | Both ops are $O(\log n)$ — KTU default |
| Need to merge two priority queues frequently | Binomial / Leftist Heap | $O(\log n)$ merge |
| Need decrease-key frequently (e.g., Dijkstra's) | Fibonacci Heap | $O(1)$ amortized decrease-key |
| Need to support search by value | Indexed Priority Queue | Augment heap with a hash table |
| External memory (data > RAM) | External / M-way Heap | Reduces I/O via larger fan-out |

# 📙 MODULE 1: PRIORITY QUEUES — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Priority Queue System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client Application] --> B[Priority Queue ADT Interface]
    B --> C1[Insert key, value]
    B --> C2[Extract Min or Max]
    B --> C3[Peek Min or Max]
    B --> C4[Increase or Decrease Key]
    B --> C5[Build Heap from Array]
    C1 --> D[Heap Engine Core]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E1[Sift Up Routine]
    D --> E2[Sift Down Routine]
    D --> E3[Swap Utility]
    D --> E4[Array Index Calculator]
    E1 --> F[Underlying 1D Array Storage]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Return Result to Client]
    D --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    C3 --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Client* calls one of the five ADT operations. These are routed to the *Heap Engine Core*, which delegates to the sift routines, swap utility, and index calculator. All operations ultimately read/write the **1D array storage**, and results are returned to the client.

## 4.3 Operational Flow — Insertion (Sift-Up)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Insert key k] --> B[Append k at end of array A n plus 1]
    B --> C[Set current index i equals n plus 1]
    C --> D{Is i greater than 1 and A parent i less than A i}
    D -- Yes --> E[Swap A i and A parent i]
    E --> F[Set i equals parent of i]
    F --> D
    D -- No --> G[Heap property restored, Stop]
```

---

## 4.4 Operational Flow — Extraction (Sift-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Extract Min] --> B{Is heap empty?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Report UNDERFLOW error, Return]
    B -- No --> D[Save root value min to return]
    D --> E[Copy last element A n to root A 1]
    E --> F[Decrement heap size n]
    F --> G[Set i equals 1]
    G --> H{Is there a child with value less than A i?}
    H -- No --> K[Stop heap property holds]
    H -- Yes --> I[Pick smaller child index j]
    I --> J[Swap A i and A j]
    J --> L[Set i equals j]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Return saved min value]
    C --> End[End]
    M --> End
```

flowchart TD

# 📓 MODULE 1: PRIORITY QUEUES — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A carries 2-3 marks per question. Answers should be **crisp, formula-anchored, and precise** — typically 3-5 lines. Avoid long preambles.

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set A]`

**Q: Define a priority queue. Differentiate between a max-priority queue and a min-priority queue with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **priority queue** is an abstract data type where each element is associated with a priority, and the element with the highest (or lowest) priority is removed first, irrespective of insertion order.

- **Max-Priority Queue:** Removes the element with the **largest key** first. *Example:* A task scheduler that always runs the most urgent (highest-numbered) task next.
- **Min-Priority Queue:** Removes the element with the **smallest key** first. *Example:* Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm, which extracts the vertex with the minimum tentative distance.

> **Key Requirement Met:** Definition (1 mark) + Max/Min distinction with one example each (1+1 marks). ✅

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**Q: What is a binary heap? State the array-index relationships used to navigate a heap stored as a 1-based array.**

**Model Answer:**

A **binary heap** is a complete binary tree that satisfies the **heap property** — for a max-heap, every parent node's key is greater than or equal to its children's keys; for a min-heap, the reverse holds.

For a 1-based array `A[1..n]`, the index relationships are:

$$
\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor, \qquad \text{left}(i) = 2i, \qquad \text{right}(i) = 2i + 1
$$

These formulas allow O(1) navigation from any node to its parent or children without explicit pointers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module-Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B (Module 1) typically has a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (i.e., two alternative questions, you answer ONE). Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Main]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: What is a priority queue? Explain the operations `Insert` and `Extract-Max` on a max-heap implemented as an array, with a suitable example. State the time complexity of each operation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A priority queue is an ADT that stores elements each with a priority key and supports the extraction of the element with the highest (or lowest) priority. It does not preserve FIFO order.

**Insert Operation (3 Marks):**

```
Algorithm MaxHeapInsert(A, key):
    A.heapSize = A.heapSize + 1
    A[A.heapSize] = -∞        // sentinel smaller than all keys
    HeapIncreaseKey(A, A.heapSize, key)

Algorithm HeapIncreaseKey(A, i, key):
    if key < A[i]:
        return error "new key is smaller than current key"
    A[i] = key
    while i > 1 and A[parent(i)] < A[i]:
        swap A[i] and A[parent(i)]
        i = parent(i)
```

**Example:** Insert key `15` into max-heap `A = [_, 20, 12, 8, 5, 10, 3, 7]` (1-based, `_` denotes index 0 unused).

1. Append at end: `A = [_, 20, 12, 8, 5, 10, 3, 7, 15]`. Heap size = 8.
2. Index 8, parent = 4, `A[4] = 5`. Since `5 < 15`, swap. → `A = [_, 20, 12, 8, 15, 10, 3, 7, 5]`.
3. Index 4, parent = 2, `A[2] = 12`. Since `12 < 15`, swap. → `A = [_, 20, 15, 8, 12, 10, 3, 7, 5]`.
4. Index 2, parent = 1, `A[1] = 20`. Since `20 ≥ 15`, stop. ✅
5. Final heap: `[_, 20, 15, 8, 12, 10, 3, 7, 5]`. **Time: $O(\log n)$** (3 swaps, $\le \log_2 8 = 3$ levels).

**Extract-Max Operation (3 Marks):**

```
Algorithm HeapExtractMax(A):
    if A.heapSize < 1: return error "heap underflow"
    max = A[1]
    A[1] = A[A.heapSize]
    A.heapSize = A.heapSize - 1
    MaxHeapify(A, 1)
    return max
```

**Example:** Extract max from `[_, 20, 15, 8, 12, 10, 3, 7, 5]`.

1. Save `max = 20`. Move `A[9] = 5` to root: `[_, 5, 15, 8, 12, 10, 3, 7]`. Heap size = 8.
2. MaxHeapify at index 1: children at 2 (15) and 3 (8). Largest is 15 at index 2. Swap. → `[_, 15, 5, 8, 12, 10, 3, 7]`.
3. Re-check at index 2: children at 4 (12) and 5 (10). Largest is 12 at index 4. Swap. → `[_, 15, 12, 8, 5, 10, 3, 7]`.
4. Re-check at index 4: children at 8 (7) only. `7 < 5`? No, `7 ≥ 5`. **Stop.**
5. Return `max = 20`. **Time: $O(\log n)$**.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: Build a max-heap from the array `A = [3, 19, 1, 14, 17, 9, 11, 12, 8]` using the bottom-up Build-Heap algorithm. Show all intermediate arrays and the final heap structure. Justify why Build-Heap runs in $O(n)$ time.**

**Model Answer:**

Given $n = 9$. The last non-leaf index is $\lfloor 9/2 \rfloor = 4$.

**Initial:** `A = [3, 19, 1, 14, 17, 9, 11, 12, 8]`

**Step 1 — Heapify at index 4 (value 14).** Children: index 8 (12) and 9 (8). Largest among `{14, 12, 8}` is 14 itself. **No change.**

**Step 2 — Heapify at index 3 (value 1).** Children: index 6 (9) and 7 (11). Largest among `{1, 9, 11}` is 11 at index 7. Swap. → `A = [3, 19, 11, 14, 17, 9, 1, 12, 8]`. Re-check at index 7: no children. **Stop.**

**Step 3 — Heapify at index 2 (value 19).** Children: index 4 (14) and 5 (17). Largest among `{19, 14, 17}` is 19 itself. **No change.**

**Step 4 — Heapify at index 1 (value 3).** Children: index 2 (19) and 3 (11). Largest among `{3, 19, 11}` is 19 at index 2. Swap. → `A = [19, 3, 11, 14, 17, 9, 1, 12, 8]`. Re-check at index 2: children at 4 (14) and 5 (17). Largest is 17 at index 5. Swap. → `A = [19, 17, 11, 14, 3, 9, 1, 12, 8]`. Re-check at index 5: children at 10, 11 (out of bounds). **Stop.**

**Final Max-Heap:**

$$
A = [19, 17, 11, 14, 3, 9, 1, 12, 8]
$$

**Tree:**
```
                (19)
              /      \
           (17)      (11)
          /    \     /    \
       (14)   (3) (9)    (1)
       /   \
     (12) (8)
```

**Justification of $O(n)$ (3 Marks):**

The total work for Build-Heap is the sum over all nodes of the work done by Heapify (sift-down) at that node. A node at height $h$ in the tree requires at most $O(h)$ swaps. The number of nodes at height $h$ in a heap of $n$ elements is at most $\lceil n/2^{h+1} \rceil$. Therefore:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{h=0}^{\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor} \lceil n/2^{h+1} \rceil \cdot O(h) = O(n) \cdot \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} \frac{h}{2^{h}} = O(n) \cdot 2 = O(n)
$$

The key insight is that most nodes are near the bottom (leaves require 0 work), and only a logarithmic number of nodes near the root do significant work. This is what makes Build-Heap asymptotically faster than the naive $O(n \log n)$ bound.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: What is Heap-Sort? Apply Heap-Sort on the array `A = [5, 13, 2, 25, 7, 17, 20, 8, 4]`. Show all passes. State its time and space complexity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Heap-Sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that works in two phases: (1) build a max-heap from the unsorted array in $O(n)$ time, and (2) repeatedly extract the maximum element and place it at the end of the array, in $O(n \log n)$ total time. It is an **in-place** algorithm with $O(1)$ extra space.

**Phase 1 — Build Max-Heap (3 Marks):**

Given $n = 9$, last non-leaf index = 4. We apply bottom-up heapify from index 4 to 1.

**Initial:** `A = [5, 13, 2, 25, 7, 17, 20, 8, 4]`

**i=4 (val 7):** children at 8 (8) and 9 (4). Largest among `{7,8,4}` = 8 at i=8. Swap. → `A = [5, 13, 2, 25, 8, 17, 20, 7, 4]`. Re-check at i=8: no children. **Stop.**

**i=3 (val 2):** children at 6 (17) and 7 (20). Largest among `{2,17,20}` = 20 at i=7. Swap. → `A = [5, 13, 20, 25, 8, 17, 2, 7, 4]`. Re-check at i=7: no children. **Stop.**

**i=2 (val 13):** children at 4 (25) and 5 (8). Largest among `{13,25,8}` = 25 at i=4. Swap. → `A = [5, 25, 20, 13, 8, 17, 2, 7, 4]`. Re-check at i=4: children at 8 (7) and 9 (4). Largest among `{13,7,4}` = 13 (parent). **Stop.**

**i=1 (val 5):** children at 2 (25) and 3 (20). Largest among `{5,25,20}` = 25 at i=2. Swap. → `A = [25, 5, 20, 13, 8, 17, 2, 7, 4]`. Re-check at i=2: children at 4 (13) and 5 (8). Largest among `{5,13,8}` = 13 at i=4. Swap. → `A = [25, 13, 20, 5, 8, 17, 2, 7, 4]`. Re-check at i=4: children at 8 (7) and 9 (4). Largest among `{5,7,4}` = 7 at i=8. Swap. → `A = [25, 13, 20, 7, 8, 17, 2, 5, 4]`. Re-check at i=8: no children. **Stop.**

**Max-Heap Built:** `A = [25, 13, 20, 7, 8, 17, 2, 5, 4]`

**Phase 2 — Sort by Repeated Extraction (3 Marks):**

| Pass | Swap A[1] & A[end] | After Swap | Heapify Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swap 25 ↔ 4 | `[4, 13, 20, 7, 8, 17, 2, 5, 25]` | `[20, 13, 17, 7, 8, 4, 2, 5, 25]` |
| 2 | Swap 20 ↔ 5 | `[5, 13, 17, 7, 8, 4, 2, 20, 25]` | `[17, 13, 5, 7, 8, 4, 2, 20, 25]` |
| 3 | Swap 17 ↔ 2 | `[2, 13, 5, 7, 8, 4, 17, 20, 25]` | `[13, 8, 5, 7, 2, 4, 17, 20, 25]` |
| 4 | Swap 13 ↔ 4 | `[4, 8, 5, 7, 2, 13, 17, 20, 25]` | `[8, 7, 5, 4, 2, 13, 17, 20, 25]` |
| 5 | Swap 8 ↔ 2 | `[2, 7, 5, 4, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` | `[7, 4, 5, 2, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` |
| 6 | Swap 7 ↔ 2 | `[2, 4, 5, 7, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` | `[5, 4, 2, 7, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` |
| 7 | Swap 5 ↔ 2 | `[2, 4, 5, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` | `[4, 2, 5, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` |
| 8 | Swap 4 ↔ 2 | `[2, 4, 5, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` | `[2, 4, 5, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]` |

**Sorted Output:** `A = [2, 4, 5, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]`

**Complexities (1 Mark):**
- **Time:** $O(n \log n)$ in all cases (worst, average, best).
- **Space:** $O(1)$ auxiliary — in-place.
- **Stability:** Not stable.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: Explain with a neat diagram how a priority queue can be used to implement an event-driven simulation system. Mention the operations and their complexities.**

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Setup (3 Marks):**

In an **event-driven simulation** (e.g., simulating customers in a bank, packets in a network, particles in a collision), future events are timestamped. The simulation must always process the event with the **earliest** timestamp next. This is precisely a **min-priority queue** use case.

**Architecture (3 Marks):**
1. Each event is represented as a record `(timestamp, eventType, eventData)`.
2. Events are inserted into the priority queue with `Insert(Q, event)`.
3. The simulation loop repeatedly calls `Extract-Min(Q)` to obtain the next event, advances the simulation clock to that event's timestamp, processes the event, and inserts any newly-generated future events.
4. The simulation terminates when the priority queue is empty.

**Operations and Complexities (1 Mark):**

| Operation | Description | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
