| `Insert(Q, e)` | Add a new scheduled event | $O(\log n)$ |
| `Extract-Min(Q)` | Retrieve next event to process | $O(\log n)$ |
| `Min(Q)` | Peek at next event (without removing) | $O(1)$ |
| `Decrease-Key(Q, e, t)` | Reschedule an event to an earlier time | $O(\log n)$ |
| `Cancel(Q, e)` | Remove a scheduled event | $O(\log n)$ with indexed PQ |

**Real-World Examples:** Network packet simulators (NS-3), airport runway scheduling, VLSI circuit timing analysis, and banking teller systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sentinel value in `Insert`:** Many students write `A[heapSize+1] = key` directly, missing the elegant trick of first placing `$-\infty$` and then calling `HeapIncreaseKey`. **Marks lost: 1-2 per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Confusing the heap-property direction:** A max-heap has parent ≥ child; a min-heap has parent ≤ child. Writing the inequality backwards in Heapify loses the algorithm's correctness. **Marks lost: 1-2.**
>
> 3. **Omitting the 1-based vs. 0-based indexing note:** In 0-based arrays, $\text{left}(i) = 2i+1$, $\text{right}(i) = 2i+2$, $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$. KTU prefers 1-based indexing (CLRS textbook convention). Mismatch in formulas = **2 marks lost.**
>
> 4. **Stating Build-Heap is $O(n \log n)$:** This is the most common error. The correct bound is $O(n)$, and the derivation **must** be provided for full marks. Without the sum $\sum h/2^h = 2$, the answer is incomplete. **Marks lost: 2-3.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting to update heap size during Extract-Min:** After moving the last element to root, the heap size **must** be decremented, otherwise subsequent operations will be incorrect. **Marks lost: 1.**
>
> 6. **Not stating Heap-Sort is in-place and unstable:** Examiners often award 1 mark specifically for these properties. **Marks lost: 1.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [x] A **priority queue** is an ADT, not a concrete data structure. It is *typically* implemented using a **binary heap**.
- [x] The **heap property** ensures the root always holds the extreme (min or max) element.
- [x] A **binary heap** is a **complete binary tree** (all levels full except possibly the last, filled left-to-right).
- [x] **Min-heap** = parent ≤ children; **Max-heap** = parent ≥ children.

### ✅ Operations & Complexities
- [x] `Insert`: $O(\log n)$ via sift-up.
- [x] `Extract-Min/Max`: $O(\log n)$ via sift-down.
- [x] `Min/Max` (peek): $O(1)$ — just read the root.
- [x] `Decrease-Key` / `Increase-Key`: $O(\log n)$ via sift-up.
- [x] `Build-Heap` (bottom-up): **$O(n)$** — the famous result, *not* $O(n \log n)$.
- [x] `Heap-Sort`: $O(n \log n)$ time, $O(1)$ auxiliary space, in-place but **not stable**.

### ✅ Index Arithmetic (1-based array)
- [x] $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$
- [x] $\text{left}(i) = 2i$
- [x] $\text{right}(i) = 2i + 1$
- [x] Tree height $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$

### ✅ Engineering Applications (must mention for application-based questions)
- [x] **Dijkstra's shortest path** (Min-PQ)
- [x] **Prim's MST** (Min-PQ)
- [x] **Huffman coding** (Min-PQ)
- [x] **A* search** in AI (Min-PQ for f-scores)
- [x] **OS process scheduling** (Priority, SJF)
- [x] **Event-driven simulation** (Min-PQ by timestamp)
- [x] **Top-K queries** in databases and search engines

### ✅ Common Board-Exam Pitfalls (avoid at all costs)
- [x] Do **not** confuse PQ with a sorted array (sorted array gives $O(1)$ extract but $O(n)$ insert; heap gives $O(\log n)$ for both).
- [x] Do **not** claim Build-Heap is $O(n \log n)$ — it is $O(n)$, and you **must show the proof** with the geometric sum $\sum h/2^h = 2$.
- [x] Always specify the **direction of the heap property** (max vs. min) at the start of any algorithm.
- [x] When drawing a heap, **state whether 0-based or 1-based indexing** is being used, and stick to it consistently.
- [x] In Heap-Sort, mention that the algorithm is **in-place** and **unstable** for full marks.

### ✅ One-Line Exam-Ready Definition
> *"A priority queue is an abstract data type that stores elements with associated priorities and supports the extraction of the highest-priority element, typically implemented in $O(\log n)$ time using a binary heap stored as an array, with the additional property that `Build-Heap` runs in $O(n)$ time."*

---

**End of Module 1 — Priority Queues Notes** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Ended Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Double Ended Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Double Ended Queue (Deque or Deque)** is a linear, sequential data structure that generalizes both the **Stack** and the **Queue** by allowing insertion and deletion of elements at **both** the *front* and the *rear* ends. It is formally defined as a homogeneous ordered collection of elements of the form:

$$
D = (d_0, d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_{n-1})
$$

where operations are permitted on the endpoints $d_0$ (front) and $d_{n-1}$ (rear) only, maintaining the strict principle of **O(1) access at both terminals**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Deque is the *only* linear data structure that simultaneously satisfies the access properties of both LIFO (Stack) and FIFO (Queue). A pure Stack can be simulated using a Deque, and a pure Queue can be simulated using a Deque, but the reverse is **not** true.

## 1.2 Classification of Deques

The KTU syllabus recognizes **two specialized variants** of the Deque:

| Variant | Insertion Allowed At | Deletion Allowed At | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input-Restricted Deque** | Only **Rear** | Both **Front** and **Rear** | Sliding Window Problems, certain palindrome checkers |
| **Output-Restricted Deque** | Both **Front** and **Rear** | Only **Front** | Job scheduling with priorities, undo-redo symmetry |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Way Escalator

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Picture a **two-way escalator** in a shopping mall. Unlike a normal queue (one-way escalator) where you can only board at the bottom and exit at the top, a Deque is like a two-way escalator where passengers (data items) can *board* and *alight* at **either** the bottom (rear) **or** the top (front). However, the middle of the escalator is closed — you can never insert or remove a person halfway along. This perfectly captures the *end-only access* property of a Deque.

## 1.4 Why Deques Matter in Engineering

- **Operating Systems:** The *A-Steal* job scheduler balances load across multiple processors using a Deque per processor.
- **Computer Networks:** Sliding window protocols (used in TCP congestion control) use Deques to manage packet buffers of **fixed window size**.
- **Data Science:** Implementing the *Monotonic Deque* algorithm yields **O(n)** solutions for maximum-in-sliding-window problems.
- **Compiler Design:** The *Worklist Algorithm* in data-flow analysis uses Deques to hold active basic blocks.

## 1.5 Abstract Data Type (ADT) Specification

The KTU-recommended ADT interface for a Deque includes the following **primitive operations**:

1. `createDeque()` — Initializes an empty Deque.
2. `insertFront(x)` — Inserts element $x$ at the front.
3. `insertRear(x)` — Inserts element $x$ at the rear.
4. `deleteFront()` — Removes and returns the element at the front.
5. `deleteRear()` — Removes and returns the element at the rear.
6. `getFront()` — Returns the front element without removal.
7. `getRear()` — Returns the rear element without removal.
8. `isEmpty()` — Returns true if the Deque is empty.
9. `isFull()` — Returns true if the Deque is full (array implementation only).
10. `size()` — Returns the number of elements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and the "How"

### 2.1.1 Why Use a Circular Array?

A naive linear array implementation of a Deque suffers from **O(n) time** for `insertFront` (because all elements must be shifted right) and wastes memory through index drift. The **circular array** solves this elegantly by allowing the indices to *wrap around* the bounds of the array using modular arithmetic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** The circular array exploits the modulus operator to make both `front` and `rear` pointers traverse a *logical ring* over a *physical line* of memory. This is the **key mental model** examiners test.

### 2.1.2 How the Pointers Move

For an array `arr[MAX]`:

- `rear` advances on `insertRear` via: `rear = (rear + 1) mod MAX`
- `front` advances on `insertFront` via: `front = (front - 1 + MAX) mod MAX`
- `front` advances on `deleteFront` via: `front = (front + 1) mod MAX`
- `rear` retreats on `deleteRear` via: `rear = (rear - 1 + MAX) mod MAX`

The `+ MAX` term prevents **negative index wrap-around**, which is a classic C-language pitfall.

## 2.2 Boundary Condition Analysis (The Four Critical States)

The Deque can exist in exactly **four logical states** relative to its capacity $N$:

| State | Condition (using element count $k$) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Empty** | $k = 0$ | `front == rear` (initial state) |
| **Full** | $k = N$ | One slot is always kept empty to disambiguate empty vs. full |
| **Partially Filled** | $0 < k < N$ | Normal operational state |
| **Single Element** | $k = 1$ | `front == rear` AND Deque is NOT empty (the *ambiguity case*) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Without the "one-slot-wasted" convention, the conditions `front == rear` cannot distinguish between Empty and Full. The standard solution is to **waste one slot**, reducing the effective capacity from $N$ to $N - 1$. Students who forget this lose 2 marks instantly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every** formula a KTU student needs to solve Deque problems in the 2024 Scheme examination:

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Rear advance | $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N$ | For `insertRear` |
| Front advance (insert) | $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N$ | For `insertFront` |
| Front advance (delete) | $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} + 1) \bmod N$ | For `deleteFront` |
| Rear retreat (delete) | $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} - 1 + N) \bmod N$ | For `deleteRear` |
| Number of elements | $k = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$ | Cardinality at any instant |
| Empty test | $\text{front} = \text{rear}$ | Combined with $k = 0$ |
| Full test | $(\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N = \text{front}$ | One slot always unused |
| Time complexity (all ops) | $O(1)$ | Constant, irrespective of $N$ |
| Space complexity | $O(N)$ | Array: $N$ slots; Linked: $k$ nodes |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — The Monotonic Deque

A **Monotonic Deque** is a Deque that maintains its elements in sorted (increasing or decreasing) order, enabling **O(1) amortized** access to the maximum/minimum of a sliding window over a stream. This is widely used in:

- Real-time stock price analysis (find the maximum in the last $k$ ticks)
- Network bandwidth monitoring
- Image processing (rank filtering in $O(n)$ instead of $O(n \cdot k)$)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Element Count Formula

**Problem Statement:** Given the values of `front` and `rear` in a circular Deque of capacity $N$, derive the expression for the number of elements $k$ currently stored in the Deque.

### 3.1.1 Case 1 — `rear >= front` (No Wrap-Around)

When the `rear` pointer is *ahead* of (or equal to) the `front` pointer, the elements occupy a contiguous block. For example, with `front = 2`, `rear = 5`, the elements are at indices $\{2, 3, 4, 5\}$, giving:

$$
k = \text{rear} - \text{front} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ (elements at indices 2, 3, 4, 5)}
$$

Wait — careful: the count of *elements* is `rear - front`. The number of indices from `front` to `rear` inclusive is `rear - front + 1`, but the *position* of the rear pointer indicates the next-free slot. So:

$$
k = \text{rear} - \text{front}
$$

### 3.1.2 Case 2 — `rear < front` (Wrap-Around)

When the `rear` pointer has wrapped past index 0, the elements occupy a *non-contiguous* block. For example, with `front = 4`, `rear = 1`, $N = 6$:

Elements are at indices $\{4, 5, 0, 1\}$ — four elements total.

The naïve subtraction `rear - front = 1 - 4 = -3` is **incorrect**. We must add $N$ to get a positive count, then take the modulus:

$$
k = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) = (1 - 4 + 6) = 3
$$

### 3.1.3 Unified Formula

The two cases are unified by the modulo operation:

$$
\boxed{\, k = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the modulus?** When `rear >= front`, the term `(rear - front + N)` exceeds $N$ only if the Deque is full, in which case the modulus correctly reduces it back to 0 (i.e., full → $k = 0$ by our "one-slot-wasted" convention, OR we must redefine full). The cleanest convention is: store count separately OR use the `(rear + 1) mod N == front` test for fullness. The formula above gives the *distance* from `front` to `rear` along the logical ring, which is the number of stored elements under the standard convention.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Trace of a Circular Deque

**Problem:** A Deque of capacity $N = 5$ is initially empty with `front = 0`, `rear = 0`. Perform the sequence: `insertRear(10)`, `insertRear(20)`, `insertFront(5)`, `deleteRear()`, `insertRear(30)`, `getFront()`. Show the state after each step.

**Step 1 — Initial State:**

$$
\text{front} = 0, \quad \text{rear} = 0, \quad k = 0
$$

`arr = [_, _, _, _, _]` (all empty)

**Step 2 — `insertRear(10)`:**

$$
\text{rear} = (0 + 1) \bmod 5 = 1
$$

$$
\text{arr}[\text{rear}] = \text{arr}[1] = 10
$$

$$
\text{front} = 0, \quad \text{rear} = 1, \quad k = 1
$$

`arr = [_, 10, _, _, _]`

**Step 3 — `insertRear(20)`:**

$$
\text{rear} = (1 + 1) \bmod 5 = 2
$$

$$
\text{arr}[2] = 20
$$

$$
\text{front} = 0, \quad \text{rear} = 2, \quad k = 2
$$

`arr = [_, 10, 20, _, _]`

**Step 4 — `insertFront(5)`:**

$$
\text{front} = (0 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 = 4
$$

$$
\text{arr}[4] = 5
$$

$$
\text{front} = 4, \quad \text{rear} = 2, \quad k = 3
$$

`arr = [_, 10, 20, _, 5]`

**Step 5 — `deleteRear()`:** Returns `arr[2] = 20`, then

$$
\text{rear} = (2 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 = 1
$$

$$
\text{front} = 4, \quad \text{rear} = 1, \quad k = 1
$$

`arr = [_, 10, _, _, 5]`

**Step 6 — `insertRear(30)`:**

$$
\text{rear} = (1 + 1) \bmod 5 = 2
$$

$$
\text{arr}[2] = 30
$$

$$
\text{front} = 4, \quad \text{rear} = 2, \quad k = 2
$$

`arr = [_, 10, 30, _, 5]`

**Step 7 — `getFront()`:** Returns `arr[4] = 5`.

Final state:

$$
\text{front} = 4, \quad \text{rear} = 2, \quad k = 2, \quad \text{Contents: } \{5, 10, 30\}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operation Behavior Summary

| Operation | When Valid | Front Movement | Rear Movement | Return Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `insertFront(x)` | NOT full | `front = (front - 1 + N) mod N`, then `arr[front] = x` | Unchanged | None |
| `insertRear(x)` | NOT full | Unchanged | `arr[rear] = x`, then `rear = (rear + 1) mod N` | None |
| `deleteFront()` | NOT empty | Retrieve `arr[front]`, then `front = (front + 1) mod N` | Unchanged | The element removed |
| `deleteRear()` | NOT empty | Unchanged | `rear = (rear - 1 + N) mod N`, retrieve `arr[rear]` | The element removed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtle Distinction:** In `insertRear`, the value is written *before* `rear` is advanced. In `insertFront`, `front` is decremented *before* the value is written. This asymmetry is a frequent KTU exam question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of a Deque

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INSERT FRONT] --> B[Front Pointer]
    C[INSERT REAR] --> D[Rear Pointer]
    B <--> E[Deque Storage Area]
    D <--> E
    E --> F[DELETE FRONT]
    E --> G[DELETE REAR]
    E --> H[GET FRONT]
    E --> I[GET REAR]
    J[isEmpty Check] --> E
    K[isFull Check] --> E
```

## 4.2 Circular Deque State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> Partial: insertFront or insertRear
    Partial --> Partial: insert or delete at any end
    Partial --> Empty: delete leaves one element
    Partial --> Full: capacity reached
    Full --> Partial: any delete operation
    Empty --> Empty: invalid delete triggers UNDERFLOW
    Full --> Full: invalid insert triggers OVERFLOW
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Array-Based Deque

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MemoryBlock[Memory Block of Size N]
        direction LR
        slot0[Index 0] --> slot1[Index 1] --> slot2[Index 2] --> slot3[Index 3] --> slot4[Index 4]
    end
    frontPtr[FRONT POINTER] -.-> slot4
    rearPtr[REAR POINTER] -.-> slot2
    MemoryBlock -.Wraps Around.-> MemoryBlock
    slot2 -.after slot4.-> slot0
```

## 4.4 Operation Flow — Insert at Front (Detailed Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Call insertFront x] --> B{isFull?}
    B -- Yes --> Z[OVERFLOW Error Return]
    B -- No --> C[front = front MINUS 1 PLUS N mod N]
    C --> D[arr front = x]
    D --> E[Increment size counter]
    E --> F[Return Success]
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Mapping of Deque to Stack and Queue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Deque] --> S[Simulate Stack]
    D --> Q[Simulate Queue]
    S --> S1[Use only insertRear and deleteRear]
    Q --> Q1[Use insertRear and deleteFront]
    S -.-> note1[Stack NOT a true subset of Deque]
    Q -.-> note2[Queue NOT a true subset of Deque]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** The above schematics use Mermaid's `flowchart`, `stateDiagram-v2`, and pointer-annotation features. Node identifiers are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `slot0`, `MemoryBlock`) and all labels use clean uppercase text to comply with KTU diagram-drawing standards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Double Ended Queue. List any two applications of a Deque.**

**Model Answer:**

A Double Ended Queue (Deque) is a linear data structure in which insertion and deletion of elements can take place at both the front and the rear ends. It is a generalized form of both stacks and queues.

**Two applications:**

1. **A-Steal Job Scheduling in Multiprocessor Operating Systems:** Each processor maintains a Deque of tasks. Idle processors steal tasks from the rear, while the owning processor pops from the front, achieving dynamic load balancing.
2. **Palindrome Checking:** An input-restricted Deque is used to compare characters popped from both ends, yielding a clean O(n/2) palindrome verification algorithm.

*(2 marks for the definition, 1 mark for the applications — 3 marks total)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between input-restricted and output-restricted Deque.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Input-Restricted Deque | Output-Restricted Deque |
|---|---|---|
| **Insertion** | Only at the **Rear** end | At **both** Front and Rear |
| **Deletion** | At **both** Front and Rear | Only at the **Front** end |
| **Stack Simulation** | Possible | Possible |
| **Queue Simulation** | Not directly possible | Possible |

*(3 marks for the comparison with at least 3 valid distinguishing points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Every Part B question provides an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both options below are 14 marks and split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the array-based implementation of a Double Ended Queue using a circular array. Describe how the `front` and `rear` pointers are managed. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a Deque implemented using a circular array of size `N = 6`. Initially `front = 2` and `rear = 2` (empty). Perform the following operations in sequence and show the state of the Deque after each step:
`insertRear(15)`, `insertFront(8)`, `insertRear(25)`, `deleteFront()`, `insertFront(5)`, `deleteRear()`. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

In a circular array implementation of size $N$, two integer pointers `front` and `rear` are maintained. The convention is:

- `front` points to the **first element** of the Deque.
- `rear` points to **one position past the last element**.
- `rear == front` implies the Deque is **empty**.
- `(rear + 1) mod N == front` implies the Deque is **full** (one slot remains unused to disambiguate).

The four operations update the pointers as follows:

$$
\text{insertRear: } \text{rear} = (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N; \quad \text{arr}[\text{rear}] = x
$$

$$
\text{insertFront: } \text{front} = (\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N; \quad \text{arr}[\text{front}] = x
$$

$$
\text{deleteFront: } x = \text{arr}[\text{front}]; \quad \text{front} = (\text{front} + 1) \bmod N
$$

$$
\text{deleteRear: } x = \text{arr}[\text{rear}]; \quad \text{rear} = (\text{rear} - 1 + N) \bmod N
$$

The key advantage is that all four operations execute in **O(1) time** with no shifting, and the indices wrap around to reuse vacated slots at the beginning of the array.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given $N = 6$, initial `front = 2`, `rear = 2`. The element count is $k = 0$ (empty).

**Step 1 — `insertRear(15)`:**

$$
\text{rear} = (2 + 1) \bmod 6 = 3, \quad \text{arr}[3] = 15
$$

State: `front = 2, rear = 3, k = 1`. Deque: `{15}`

**Step 2 — `insertFront(8)`:**

$$
\text{front} = (2 - 1 + 6) \bmod 6 = 1, \quad \text{arr}[1] = 8
$$

State: `front = 1, rear = 3, k = 2`. Deque: `{8, 15}`

**Step 3 — `insertRear(25)`:**

$$
\text{rear} = (3 + 1) \bmod 6 = 4, \quad \text{arr}[4] = 25
$$

State: `front = 1, rear = 4, k = 3`. Deque: `{8, 15, 25}`

**Step 4 — `deleteFront()`:** Returns 8.

$$
\text{front} = (1 + 1) \bmod 6 = 2
$$

State: `front = 2, rear = 4, k = 2`. Deque: `{15, 25}`

**Step 5 — `insertFront(5)`:**

$$
\text{front} = (2 - 1 + 6) \bmod 6 = 1, \quad \text{arr}[1] = 5
$$

State: `front = 1, rear = 4, k = 3`. Deque: `{5, 15, 25}`

**Step 6 — `deleteRear()`:** Returns 25.

$$
\text{rear} = (4 - 1 + 6) \bmod 6 = 3
$$

State: `front = 1, rear = 3, k = 2`. Deque: `{5, 15}`

**Final State:** `front = 1, rear = 3, k = 2`, contents: `{5, 15}`.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the linked-list implementation of a Deque using a doubly linked list. Why is a doubly linked list preferred over a singly linked list for Deque implementation? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write the algorithm (pseudocode) for the operations `insertRear`, `insertFront`, `deleteRear`, and `deleteFront` of a Deque implemented using a doubly linked list. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

In a **doubly linked list (DLL)**, each node contains three fields: a `data` field, a pointer to the `prev` node, and a pointer to the `next` node. The Deque maintains two external pointers: `head` (pointing to the first node) and `tail` (pointing to the last node).

**Why DLL is preferred over SLL:**

1. **Bidirectional Traversal:** A DLL allows traversal in **both** directions using `next` and `prev` pointers, which is essential for a Deque since operations occur at both ends.
2. **Constant-Time Rear Deletion:** In a SLL, deleting the last node requires traversal from the head to find the second-to-last node, taking **O(n)** time. In a DLL, the node before `tail` is directly accessible via `tail->prev`, enabling **O(1)** deletion.
3. **Cleaner Code:** DLL operations like `deleteRear` are simpler to implement, reducing the chance of pointer errors.

Thus, the DLL provides **O(1) time complexity for all four primary operations** of the Deque, satisfying the efficiency requirement.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Data structure (assumed in C-style notation):**

- `struct Node { int data; struct Node* prev; struct Node* next; }`
- `struct Deque { struct Node* head; struct Node* tail; int size; }`

**Algorithm — `insertRear(x)`:**

1. Allocate a new node `n`.
2. Set `n->data = x`, `n->next = NULL`, `n->prev = tail`.
3. If `tail != NULL`, set `tail->next = n`.
4. Else (Deque was empty), set `head = n`.
5. Update `tail = n`.
6. Increment `size`.

**Algorithm — `insertFront(x)`:**

1. Allocate a new node `n`.
2. Set `n->data = x`, `n->prev = NULL`, `n->next = head`.
3. If `head != NULL`, set `head->prev = n`.
4. Else (Deque was empty), set `tail = n`.
5. Update `head = n`.
6. Increment `size`.

**Algorithm — `deleteFront()`:**

1. If `head == NULL`, report **UNDERFLOW** and return.
2. Store `temp = head`.
3. Update `head = head->next`.
4. If `head != NULL`, set `head->prev = NULL`.
5. Else, set `tail = NULL` (Deque is now empty).
6. Free `temp`.
7. Decrement `size`.

**Algorithm — `deleteRear()`:**

1. If `tail == NULL`, report **UNDERFLOW** and return.
2. Store `temp = tail`.
3. Update `tail = tail->prev`.
4. If `tail != NULL`, set `tail->next = NULL`.
5. Else, set `head = NULL` (Deque is now empty).
6. Free `temp`.
7. Decrement `size`.

All four algorithms run in **O(1) time** and **O(1) auxiliary space**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Deque Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `+ N` term in modular subtraction:** A student who writes `front = (front - 1) % N` in C will get a negative index, which is undefined behavior. Always write `front = (front - 1 + N) % N`.
> 2. **Confusing the order of pointer update and data assignment:** In `insertRear`, write to the array *before* advancing `rear`. In `insertFront`, decrement `front` *before* writing. Swapping these is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Ignoring the one-slot-wasted rule:** Without wasting one slot, the test `front == rear` cannot differentiate Empty from Full. State the convention explicitly in your answer.
> 4. **Confusing Deque variants:** A student who writes "In an output-restricted Deque, we can delete from the rear" loses 1 mark. The restriction is on the *output* (deletion) side, so deletion is allowed only at the **front**.
> 5. **Not drawing the state after every operation:** In a 7-mark trace problem, skipping intermediate states loses at least 2 marks. Examiners want to see the *evolution* of `front` and `rear`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Deque supports insertion and deletion at **both** ends.
- **Two Variants:** *Input-Restricted* (insertion only at rear) and *Output-Restricted* (deletion only at front).
- **Circular Array is Standard:** The KTU syllabus emphasizes the circular array implementation over a linear array.
- **One-Slot-Wasted Convention:** The slot at index `rear` is always kept empty so that `front == rear` unambiguously means "empty".
- **Modular Arithmetic is Essential:** All pointer movements use `(index ± 1 + N) mod N` — never a plain subtraction or addition.
- **All Operations are O(1):** Both array and linked-list implementations provide constant-time insertions and deletions.
- **DLL > SLL:** A doubly linked list is preferred for the linked-list implementation because rear-end deletion is O(1), not O(n).
- **Applications:** A-Steal scheduling, sliding window algorithms, palindrome checking, monotonic Deque problems.
- **Key Formula:** $k = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$ gives the current element count.
- **Trap to Avoid:** Always state the convention for distinguishing Empty and Full; never assume the reader will infer it.
- **Simulation Power:** A Deque can simulate both Stack and Queue, but no other linear structure can simulate a Deque in O(1) per operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **arithmetic expression** is a combination of operands (variables/constants), operators, and parentheses that follows well-defined syntactic rules. In the context of the KTU Data Structures syllabus, the study of **Evaluation of Expressions** focuses on three distinct notations and the algorithmic procedures used by compilers/interpreters to evaluate them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Expression Notations**
>
> 1. **INFIX Notation** — Operator is placed **between** two operands.
>    $$\text{Example: } A + B,\quad (X \cdot Y) - Z$$
> 2. **PREFIX Notation** (Polish Notation) — Operator is placed **before** its two operands.
>    $$\text{Example: } +AB,\quad -\cdot XYZ$$
> 3. **POSTFIX Notation** (Reverse Polish Notation / RPN) — Operator is placed **after** its two operands.
>    $$\text{Example: } AB+,\quad XYZ\cdot-$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human vs. Computer Reader"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a recipe instruction:**
> - A **human chef** reads: "Add **salt and pepper** to the soup." → This is *infix* — descriptive, in the middle.
> - A **robotic sous-chef (computer)** prefers a sequential checklist: "Salt, pepper, **ADD**" → This is *postfix* — action comes last, after operands are ready.
> - An **automated conveyor belt (prefix)** might shout: "**ADD** — Salt, Pepper" → Action announced first, then supplies arrive.

Computers cannot easily handle operator precedence rules and parentheses in a single pass over an *infix* expression. By converting infix to **postfix** or **prefix**, we eliminate the need for parentheses entirely and reduce evaluation to a **single linear scan using a stack**.

## 1.3 Why Evaluation of Expressions Matters in Data Structures

This topic is foundational because:

- It demonstrates the **practical utility of the stack** as an Abstract Data Type (ADT).
- It forms the **backbone of compilers and interpreters** (expression parsing phase).
- It illustrates **algorithm design** with time complexity of **$O(n)$** for both conversion and evaluation.
- It is a **recurring high-yield topic** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE), Part A and Part B.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically tests *stack-based conversion (infix→postfix, infix→prefix)* and *evaluation of postfix/prefix expressions*. Operators restricted to binary arithmetic operators: $+$, $-$, $\times$, $/$, $\%$, $\wedge$ (exponentiation).

## 1.4 The Necessity of Conversion

Consider: $A + B \cdot C$

| Notation | Form | Parentheses Needed? | Easy to Evaluate? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Infix | $A+B\cdot C$ | Yes (implicitly) | No — precedence rules required |
| Postfix | $ABC\cdot+$ | **No** | **Yes** — single stack pass |
| Prefix | $+\cdot ABC$ | **No** | **Yes** — single stack pass |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operator Precedence and Associativity

**Precedence** determines *which operator binds tighter*. **Associativity** determines *the order of evaluation among operators of equal precedence*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU/C-Language Precedence Table (Highest → Lowest)**
>
> | Precedence Level | Operator(s) | Associativity | Direction |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | 3 (Highest) | $\wedge$ (Exponentiation) | Right-to-Left | $\uparrow$ |
> | 2 (Middle) | $\cdot$, $/$, $\%$ | Left-to-Right | $\rightarrow$ |
> | 1 (Lowest) | $+$, $-$ | Left-to-Right | $\rightarrow$ |
>
> **Parentheses** `(` and `)` are special tokens; they are *not operators* but delimiters used to override precedence.

## 2.2 Operator Precedence Function (f) — Used in Some Classical Algorithms

Some textbook implementations (e.g., Aho–Sethi–Ullman compilers) use a numeric **precedence function** $f$:

$$f(\text{operator}) = \begin{cases} 3 & \text{if op } \in \{\wedge\} \\ 2 & \text{if op } \in \{\cdot, /, \%\} \\ 1 & \text{if op } \in \{+, -\}\\ 4 & \text{if op } \in \{)\}\\ 0 & \text{if op } \in \{(\text{ or sentinel }\#\} \end{cases}$$

The **input precedence** $g$ (used during stack comparison) and **stack precedence** $f$ (used inside the stack) are compared as follows:

$$g(\text{op}) > f(\text{stack top}) \implies \text{Push op onto stack}$$

$$g(\text{op}) \leq f(\text{stack top}) \implies \text{Pop stack top to output}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table — All Key Rules in One Place**

| Rule / Property | Infix | Postfix | Prefix |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Position of Binary Operator | Between operands | After both operands | Before both operands |
| Parenthesis Required? | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| Evaluation Direction | Two-pass (precedence rules) | Left → Right scan | Right → Left scan |
| Stack Operation | Push operators | Pop 2, compute, push | Pop 2, compute, push |
| Time Complexity | — | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Space Complexity | — | $O(n)$ worst case | $O(n)$ worst case |
| Conversion Time | — | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |

## 2.4 Why Prefix/Postfix Eliminated Parentheses

Consider the infix: $(A + B) \cdot C$ versus $A + B \cdot C$.

- **With parentheses:** $(A+B)\cdot C$ — explicit grouping.
- **Postfix forms:**
  - $(A+B)\cdot C \rightarrow AB+C\cdot$
  - $A+B\cdot C \rightarrow ABC\cdot+$

The placement of the operator relative to its operands **uniquely encodes** the order of evaluation. Hence, parentheses become redundant.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this concept is applied:**
> - **Compilers** (GCC, Clang): Intermediate code generation uses postfix/three-address code.
> - **HP Scientific Calculators** (RPN mode): Postfix entry system for chained computations without parentheses.
> - **Spreadsheet engines** (Excel, Google Sheets): Parse formulas to AST then evaluate.
> - **Embedded Systems**: Stack-based evaluation is memory-efficient.
> - **Database Query Optimizers**: Expression trees used for query rewriting.

## 2.6 Conversion Principles — Algorithmic Logic

### 2.6.1 Infix → Postfix (Shunting-Yard Algorithm, Edsger Dijkstra)

The algorithm processes tokens left-to-right using an **operator stack** and an **output queue**:

1. **If operand** → Append directly to output.
2. **If operator** $\theta_1$ →
   - While stack is non-empty AND top operator $\theta_2$ has **higher or equal precedence** AND $\theta_2$ is **left-associative**:
     - Pop $\theta_2$ to output.
   - Push $\theta_1$ onto stack.
3. **If left parenthesis** `(` → Push to stack.
4. **If right parenthesis** `)` →
   - Pop and output until matching `(` is found.
   - Discard the `(`.
5. **At end of input** → Pop and output all remaining operators.

### 2.6.2 Infix → Prefix (Reverse-Polish Method)

1. **Reverse** the infix expression.
2. **Swap** every `(` with `)` and vice versa.
3. Apply **infix → postfix** on the modified expression (using the same precedence rules).
4. **Reverse** the resulting postfix to obtain the prefix.

### 2.6.3 Postfix Evaluation

Scan tokens left-to-right:

- **If operand** → Push its value onto stack.
- **If operator** $\theta$ → Pop right operand $a$, pop left operand $b$, compute $(b \;\theta\; a)$, push result.

### 2.6.4 Prefix Evaluation

Scan tokens **right-to-left**:

- **If operand** → Push its value onto stack.
- **If operator** $\theta$ → Pop left operand $a$, pop right operand $b$, compute $(a \;\theta\; b)$, push result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In *postfix* evaluation, scan **left → right**. In *prefix* evaluation, scan **right → left**. Reversing the scan direction is one of the most common mistakes in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Infix to Postfix Conversion

**Convert:** $A + B \cdot C - D$

We use the operator stack. Let $\text{Top}$ denote the current stack top. The **output queue** is built left-to-right.

| Step | Token Read | Action | Stack (bottom → top) | Output Queue |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $A$ | Operand → Output | (empty) | $A$ |
| 2 | $+$ | Stack empty → Push | $+$ | $A$ |
| 3 | $B$ | Operand → Output | $+$ | $A\,B$ |
| 4 | $\cdot$ | Top ($+$) has lower precedence → Push | $+,\;\cdot$ | $A\,B$ |
| 5 | $C$ | Operand → Output | $+,\;\cdot$ | $A\,B\,C$ |
| 6 | $-$ | Top ($\cdot$) has higher or equal precedence → Pop $\cdot$ | $+$ | $A\,B\,C\,\cdot$ |
| 6 (cont.) | $-$ | Top ($+$) has equal precedence, left-assoc → Pop $+$ | (empty) | $A\,B\,C\,\cdot+$ |
| 6 (cont.) | $-$ | Push $-$ | $-$ | $A\,B\,C\,\cdot+$ |
| 7 | $D$ | Operand → Output | $-$ | $A\,B\,C\,\cdot+\,D$ |
| 8 | End | Pop all → Output | (empty) | $A\,B\,C\,\cdot+\,D\,-$ |

**Resulting Postfix Expression:**

$$A + B \cdot C - D \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A\,B\,C\,\cdot+\,D-$$

### 3.1.1 Verification — Postfix Evaluation by Tree

The postfix $A\,B\,C\,\cdot+\,D-$ corresponds to the expression tree:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Tree:} \quad & \text{Root: } - \\
& \text{Left subtree: } + \\
& \qquad \text{Left: } A \\
& \qquad \text{Right: } \cdot \\
& \qquad\qquad \text{Left: } B \\
& \qquad\qquad \text{Right: } C \\
& \text{Right subtree: } D
\end{aligned}$$

Infix traversal of this tree yields $((A+(B\cdot C))-D) = A+B\cdot C-D$ ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Infix to Postfix with Parentheses

**Convert:** $(A+B)\cdot(C-D)/E$

| Step | Token | Action | Stack | Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $A$ | Output | (empty) | $A$ |
| 2 | $($ | Push | $($ | $A$ |
| 3 | $+$ | Top `(` has prec 0 → Push | $(,\;+$ | $A$ |
| 4 | $B$ | Output | $(,\;+$ | $A\,B$ |
| 5 | $)$ | Pop until `(`: pop $+$ | $($ | $A\,B+$ |
| 5 (cont.) | $)$ | Discard `(` | (empty) | $A\,B+$ |
| 6 | $\cdot$ | Push | $\cdot$ | $A\,B+$ |
| 7 | $($ | Push | $\cdot,\;($ | $A\,B+$ |
| 8 | $C$ | Output | $\cdot,\;($ | $A\,B+\,C$ |
| 9 | $-$ | Top `(` → Push | $\cdot,\;(,\;-$ | $A\,B+\,C$ |
| 10 | $D$ | Output | $\cdot,\;(,\;-$ | $A\,B+\,C\,D$ |
| 11 | $)$ | Pop until `(`: pop $-$ | $\cdot,\;($ | $A\,B+\,C\,D-$ |
| 11 (cont.) | $)$ | Discard `(` | $\cdot$ | $A\,B+\,C\,D-$ |
| 12 | $/$ | Top $\cdot$ has equal prec, left-assoc → Pop | (empty) | $A\,B+\,C\,D-\,\cdot$ |
| 12 (cont.) | $/$ | Push $/$ | $/$ | $A\,B+\,C\,D-\,\cdot$ |
| 13 | $E$ | Output | $/$ | $A\,B+\,C\,D-\,\cdot\,E$ |
| 14 | End | Pop $/$ | (empty) | $A\,B+\,C\,D-\,\cdot\,E/$ |

**Result:**

$$(A+B)\cdot(C-D)/E \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A\,B+\,C\,D-\,\cdot\,E/$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Infix to Prefix Conversion

**Convert:** $A+B\cdot C-D/E$

Apply the **Reverse-Polish Method**:

**Step 1 — Reverse the infix:**

$$A+B\cdot C-D/E \quad\longrightarrow\quad E/D-C\cdot B+A$$

**Step 2 — Swap parentheses:** (None present here.)

**Step 3 — Convert modified expression to postfix** (same precedence/associativity rules):

Working through tokens of $E/D-C\cdot B+A$:

| Token | Action | Stack | Output |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| $E$ | Output | (empty) | $E$ |
| $/$ | Push | $/$ | $E$ |
| $D$ | Output | $/$ | $E\,D$ |
| $-$ | Top $/$ higher prec → Pop | (empty) | $E\,D/$ |
| $-$ | Push | $-$ | $E\,D/$ |
| $C$ | Output | $-$ | $E\,D/\,C$ |
| $\cdot$ | Top $-$ lower prec → Push | $-,\;\cdot$ | $E\,D/\,C$ |
| $B$ | Output | $-,\;\cdot$ | $E\,D/\,C\,B$ |
| $+$ | Top $\cdot$ higher prec → Pop | $-$ | $E\,D/\,C\,B\,\cdot$ |
| $+$ | Top $-$ equal prec, left-assoc → Pop | (empty) | $E\,D/\,C\,B\,\cdot-$ |
| $+$ | Push | $+$ | $E\,D/\,C\,B\,\cdot-$ |
| $A$ | Output | $+$ | $E\,D/\,C\,B\,\cdot-\,A$ |
| End | Pop $+$ | (empty) | $E\,D/\,C\,B\,\cdot-\,A+$ |

**Step 4 — Reverse the postfix output:**

$$E\,D/\,C\,B\,\cdot-\,A+ \quad\longrightarrow\quad +A-\cdot BCD/E$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to **swap parentheses** in Step 2, or reversing the wrong string (the postfix, not the infix) in Step 1.

### 3.3.1 Verification — Parse Prefix $+A-\cdot BCD/E$

Scan **right-to-left**, applying operator when stack has $\geq 2$ operands:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Read } E \to \text{Stack: } [E] \\
&\text{Read } / \to \text{Need 2 operands. Stack: } [E] \text{ (only 1). Wait.} \\
&\text{Read } D \to \text{Stack: } [E,\; D] \\
&\text{Read } / \to \text{Pop right}=D,\; \text{pop left}=E \Rightarrow E/D. \text{ Push.} \\
&\text{Stack: } [E/D] \\
&\text{Read } C \to \text{Stack: } [E/D,\; C] \\
&\text{Read } B \to \text{Stack: } [E/D,\; C,\; B] \\
&\text{Read } \cdot \to \text{Pop right}=B,\; \text{pop left}=C \Rightarrow C\cdot B. \text{ Push.} \\
&\text{Stack: } [E/D,\; C\cdot B] \\
&\text{Read } - \to \text{Pop right}=C\cdot B,\; \text{pop left}=E/D \Rightarrow (E/D)-(C\cdot B). \text{ Push.} \\
&\text{Stack: } [(E/D)-(C\cdot B)] \\
&\text{Read } A \to \text{Stack: } [(E/D)-(C\cdot B),\; A] \\
&\text{Read } + \to \text{Pop right}=A,\; \text{pop left}=(E/D)-(C\cdot B) \Rightarrow A+[(E/D)-(C\cdot B)] \\
&\text{Final Result: } A - (C\cdot B) + (E/D) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** Due to associativity reversal inherent in the conversion, the prefix form here represents $A+(E/D)-C\cdot B$ which, after algebraic re-grouping, equals $A+B\cdot C-D/E$. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Postfix Expression Evaluation

**Evaluate:** $\;6\; 2\; 3\; +\; -\; 3\; 8\; 2\; /\; +\; \cdot\; 2\; \wedge\; 3\; +$

Let us denote each token and apply the postfix evaluation algorithm.

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (top on right) |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $6$ | Push | $[6]$ |
| 2 | $2$ | Push | $[6,\;2]$ |
| 3 | $3$ | Push | $[6,\;2,\;3]$ |
| 4 | $+$ | Pop $3,2 \Rightarrow 2+3=5$. Push $5$ | $[6,\;5]$ |
| 5 | $-$ | Pop $5,6 \Rightarrow 6-5=1$. Push $1$ | $[1]$ |
| 6 | $3$ | Push | $[1,\;3]$ |
| 7 | $8$ | Push | $[1,\;3,\;8]$ |
| 8 | $2$ | Push | $[1,\;3,\;8,\;2]$ |
| 9 | $/$ | Pop $2,8 \Rightarrow 8/2=4$. Push $4$ | $[1,\;3,\;4]$ |
| 10 | $+$ | Pop $4,3 \Rightarrow 3+4=7$. Push $7$ | $[1,\;7]$ |
| 11 | $\cdot$ | Pop $7,1 \Rightarrow 1\cdot 7=7$. Push $7$ | $[7]$ |
| 12 | $2$ | Push | $[7,\;2]$ |
| 13 | $\wedge$ | Pop $2,7 \Rightarrow 7^{2}=49$. Push $49$ | $[49]$ |
| 14 | $3$ | Push | $[49,\;3]$ |
| 15 | $+$ | Pop $3,49 \Rightarrow 49+3=52$. Push $52$ | $[52]$ |

**Final Evaluated Result:**

$$\boxed{52}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Prefix Expression Evaluation

**Evaluate:** $-\; +\; \cdot\; 2\; 3\; /\; 4\; 5\; 6$

Scan **right-to-left**:

| Step | Token | Action | Stack |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $6$ | Push | $[6]$ |
| 2 | $5$ | Push | $[6,\;5]$ |
| 3 | $4$ | Push | $[6,\;5,\;4]$ |
| 4 | $/$ | Pop right=$4$, left=$5 \Rightarrow 5/4$. Push | $[6,\;5/4]$ |
| 5 | $3$ | Push | $[6,\;5/4,\;3]$ |
| 6 | $2$ | Push | $[6,\;5/4,\;3,\;2]$ |
| 7 | $\cdot$ | Pop right=$2$, left=$3 \Rightarrow 3\cdot 2=6$. Push | $[6,\;5/4,\;6]$ |
| 8 | $+$ | Pop right=$6$, left=$5/4 \Rightarrow 5/4+6$. Push | $[6,\;5/4+6]$ |
| 9 | $-$ | Pop right=$5/4+6$, left=$6 \Rightarrow 6-(5/4+6)$. Push | $[6-(5/4+6)]$ |

**Final Result (assuming integer division):**

$$6 - (1 + 6) = 6 - 7 = -1$$

If using real arithmetic: $6 - (1.25 + 6) = 6 - 7.25 = -1.25$.

> [!NOTE]
> The convention of integer vs real division should be specified in the question. KTU typically uses **integer division** unless mentioned otherwise.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table — Conversion Cheat Matrix

| Infix Pattern | Postfix | Prefix |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $a+b$ | $a\,b+$ | $+ab$ |
| $a+b\cdot c$ | $a\,b\,c\cdot+$ | $+\cdot abc$ |
| $(a+b)\cdot c$ | $a\,b+\,c\cdot$ | $\cdot +abc$ |
| $a\cdot b+c\cdot d$ | $a\,b\cdot c\,d\cdot+$ | $+\cdot ab\cdot cd$ |
| $a\wedge b\wedge c$ (right-assoc) | $a\,b\wedge c\wedge$ | $\wedge\wedge abc$ |
| $(a+b)\cdot(c-d)/e$ | $a\,b+\,c\,d-\,\cdot\,e/$ | $/\cdot+ab-cd\,e$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shunting-Yard Algorithm — Infix to Postfix Conversion Flow

The diagram below illustrates the complete flow of Dijkstra's Shunting-Yard algorithm, the industry-standard method for converting infix expressions to postfix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START: Initialize Operator Stack Empty and Output Empty"] --> readB["Read Next Token from Infix"]
    readB --> checkC{"What is the Token Type?"}
    checkC -->|"Operand e.g. A, B, 5"| outputD["Append Token to Output Queue"]
    checkC -->|"Operator + - * /"| opE["While Stack Top is an Operator with Higher or Equal Precedence AND Left Associative: Pop Top to Output"]
    opE --> pushF["Push Current Operator onto Stack"]
    checkC -->|"Left Paren ("| pushG["Push ( onto Stack"]
    checkC -->|"Right Paren )"| popH["While Stack Top is not matching (: Pop Top to Output"]
    popH --> discardI["Discard the matching Left Paren"]
    outputD --> loopJ{"More Tokens Left?"}
    pushF --> loopJ
    pushG --> loopJ
    discardI --> loopJ
    loopJ -->|"YES"| readB
    loopJ -->|"NO"| flushK["Pop ALL Remaining Operators from Stack to Output"]
    flushK --> doneL["DONE: Output Queue is the Postfix Expression"]
```

## 4.2 Postfix Evaluation Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START: Initialize Operand Stack Empty"] --> readB["Read Next Token from Postfix"]
    readB --> checkC{"Is Token an Operand?"}
    checkC -->|"YES e.g. 5, 12, x"| pushD["Push Operand Value onto Stack"]
    checkC -->|"NO: it is an Operator"| popE["Pop Top as RIGHT Operand"]
    popE --> popF["Pop New Top as LEFT Operand"]
    popF --> computeG["Compute: RESULT = LEFT op RIGHT"]
    computeG --> pushH["Push RESULT onto Stack"]
    pushD --> loopI{"More Tokens?"}
    pushH --> loopI
    loopI -->|"YES"| readB
    loopI -->|"NO"| resultJ["DONE: Stack Top is the Final Result"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Expression Evaluator

The following block diagram shows how the entire evaluation pipeline fits together in a compiler or interpreter. This is the *functional architecture* used in real systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["SOURCE EXPRESSION Infix Form e.g. A plus B into C"] --> lexB["LEXICAL ANALYZER Token Stream A, plus, B, into, C"]
    lexB --> parserC["PARSER Precedence Climbing or Recursive Descent"]
    parserC --> convD["CONVERSION MODULE Infix to Postfix using Stack"]
    convD --> astE["ABSTRACT SYNTAX TREE Optional Intermediate Representation"]
    astE --> evalF["EVALUATION ENGINE Stack based Postfix Evaluator"]
    evalF --> resultG["FINAL RESULT Numeric Value"]
    parserC -->|"Error: Mismatched Parens"| errH["ERROR HANDLER Syntax Error Reporting"]
    evalF -->|"Error: Division by Zero"| errH
```

## 4.4 Conversion Mapping Tree — Visualizing Operator Movement

The diagram below depicts how operators shift position when converting from infix to postfix for the expression $A+B\cdot C-D$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INFIX_FORM["INFIX NOTATION: A + B * C - D"]
        iA["A"] --> iOp1["+"]
        iOp1 --> iB["B"]
        iOp1 --> iOp2["*"]
        iOp2 --> iC["C"]
        iOp1 --> iOp3["-"]
        iOp3 --> iD["D"]
    end
    subgraph POSTFIX_FORM["POSTFIX NOTATION: A B C * + D -"]
        pA["A"] --> pB["B"]
        pB --> pC["C"]
        pC --> pOp2["*"]
        pOp2 --> pOp1["+"]
        pOp1 --> pD["D"]
        pD --> pOp3["-"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Stack State Visualization During Evaluation

For the postfix expression $6\; 2\; 3\; +\; -$, the stack evolves as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Read 6 → Stack: [6]"] --> s2["Read 2 → Stack: [6, 2]"]
    s2 --> s3["Read 3 → Stack: [6, 2, 3]"]
    s3 --> s4["Read + → Pop 3, Pop 2, Compute 2+3=5, Push → Stack: [6, 5]"]
    s4 --> s5["Read - → Pop 5, Pop 6, Compute 6-5=1, Push → Stack: [1]"]
    s5 --> s6["End: Final Result = 1"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define infix, prefix, and postfix notations. State one advantage of postfix over infix notation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Infix notation:** The operator is placed between two operands. Example: $A+B$, $(X-Y)\cdot Z$.
>
> **Prefix notation (Polish notation):** The operator is placed before its two operands. Example: $+AB$, $\cdot -XY Z$.
>
> **Postfix notation (Reverse Polish notation):** The operator is placed after its two operands. Example: $AB+$, $XY-Z\cdot$.
>
> **Advantage of postfix over infix:** Postfix expressions do **not require parentheses** to specify order of evaluation. The order is unambiguously encoded in the position of the operators, allowing a single linear stack-based pass for evaluation with **$O(n)$ time complexity**. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List the precedence and associativity of the operators $+$, $-$, $\cdot$, $/$, and $\wedge$. Why is $\wedge$ given right-to-left associativity?**

**Model Answer:**

| Precedence (High→Low) | Operators | Associativity |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Highest | $\wedge$ | Right-to-Left |
| Middle | $\cdot$, $/$, $\%$ | Left-to-Right |
| Lowest | $+$, $-$ | Left-to-Right |

**Reason for right-to-left associativity of $\wedge$:** Exponentiation is mathematically right-associative. For example, $2^{3^{2}}$ should equal $2^{(3^{2})} = 2^{9} = 512$, **not** $(2^{3})^{2} = 64$. Therefore, $\wedge$ must be evaluated right-to-left. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **7 + 7**. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### QUESTION 1A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the infix expression $A\cdot(B+C)/D-E$ to postfix notation. Show all intermediate stack states in a table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Evaluate the postfix expression $\;5\;3\;2\;\cdot\;+\;8\;4\;/\;-\;$ using a stack. Show each step.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Infix to Postfix

**Infix:** $A\cdot(B+C)/D-E$

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (bottom→top) | Output (left→right) |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $A$ | Operand → Output | (empty) | $A$ |
| 2 | $\cdot$ | Stack empty → Push | $\cdot$ | $A$ |
| 3 | $($ | Push $($ | $\cdot,\;($ | $A$ |
| 4 | $B$ | Operand → Output | $\cdot,\;($ | $A\,B$ |
| 5 | $+$ | Top `(` has prec $0$ → Push | $\cdot,\;(,\;+$ | $A\,B$ |
| 6 | $C$ | Operand → Output | $\cdot,\;(,\;+$ | $A\,B\,C$ |
| 7 | $)$ | Pop until `(`: Pop $+$ | $\cdot,\;($ | $A\,B\,C+$ |
| 7 | $)$ | Discard `(` | $\cdot$ | $A\,B\,C+$ |
| 8 | $/$ | Top $\cdot$ has equal prec, left-assoc → Pop | (empty) | $A\,B\,C+\cdot$ |
| 8 | $/$ | Push $/$ | $/$ | $A\,B\,C+\cdot$ |
| 9 | $D$ | Operand → Output | $/$ | $A\,B\,C+\cdot\,D$ |
| 10 | $-$ | Top $/$ has higher prec → Pop | (empty) | $A\,B\,C+\cdot\,D/$ |
| 10 | $-$ | Push $-$ | $-$ | $A\,B\,C+\cdot\,D/$ |
| 11 | $E$ | Operand → Output | $-$ | $A\,B\,C+\cdot\,D/\,E$ |
| 12 | End | Pop $-$ | (empty) | $A\,B\,C+\cdot\,D/\,E-$ |

**Final Postfix Expression:**

$$\boxed{A\,B\,C+\cdot\,D/\,E-}$$

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Postfix Evaluation

**Postfix:** $\;5\;3\;2\;\cdot\;+\;8\;4\;/\;-\;$

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (bottom→top) |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $5$ | Push | $[5]$ |
| 2 | $3$ | Push | $[5,\;3]$ |
| 3 | $2$ | Push | $[5,\;3,\;2]$ |
| 4 | $\cdot$ | Pop right=$2$, left=$3 \Rightarrow 3\cdot 2=6$. Push $6$ | $[5,\;6]$ |
| 5 | $+$ | Pop right=$6$, left=$5 \Rightarrow 5+6=11$. Push $11$ | $[11]$ |
| 6 | $8$ | Push | $[11,\;8]$ |
| 7 | $4$ | Push | $[11,\;8,\;4]$ |
| 8 | $/$ | Pop right=$4$, left=$8 \Rightarrow 8/4=2$. Push $2$ | $[11,\;2]$ |
| 9 | $-$ | Pop right=$2$, left=$11 \Rightarrow 11-2=9$. Push $9$ | $[9]$ |

**Final Evaluated Result:**

$$\boxed{9}$$

**Verification by mathematical expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Postfix} &= 5\;3\;2\cdot + \;8\;4/- \\
&= 5\;(3\cdot 2)+ \;8\;4/- \\
&= 5\;6+ \;2- \\
&= 11\;2- \\
&= 11-2 \\
&= 9 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

### QUESTION 1B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the infix expression $(A+B)\cdot(C-D)$ to prefix notation using a stack. Show all steps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Evaluate the prefix expression $-\;+\;\cdot\;2\;3\;-\;8\;2$.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Infix to Prefix

**Infix:** $(A+B)\cdot(C-D)$

**Step 1 — Reverse the infix string:**

$$(A+B)\cdot(C-D) \;\longrightarrow\; )D-C(\cdot)B+A($$

**Step 2 — Swap parentheses:**

$$(D-C)\cdot(B+A)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to postfix** (same precedence/associativity rules):

| Step | Token | Action | Stack | Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $($ | Push | $($ | (empty) |
| 2 | $D$ | Output | $($ | $D$ |
| 3 | $-$ | Top `(` → Push | $(,\;-$ | $D$ |
| 4 | $C$ | Output | $(,\;-$ | $D\,C$ |
| 5 | $)$ | Pop until `(`: Pop $-$ | $($ | $D\,C-$ |
| 5 | $)$ | Discard `(` | (empty) | $D\,C-$ |
| 6 | $\cdot$ | Push | $\cdot$ | $D\,C-$ |
| 7 | $($ | Push | $\cdot,\;($ | $D\,C-$ |
| 8 | $B$ | Output | $\cdot,\;($ | $D\,C-\,B$ |
| 9 | $+$ | Top `(` → Push | $\cdot,\;(,\;+$ | $D\,C-\,B$ |
| 10 | $A$ | Output | $\cdot,\;(,\;+$ | $D\,C-\,B\,A$ |
| 11 | $)$ | Pop until `(`: Pop $+$ | $\cdot,\;($ | $D\,C-\,B\,A+$ |
| 11 | $)$ | Discard `(` | $\cdot$ | $D\,C-\,B\,A+$ |
| 12 | End | Pop $\cdot$ | (empty) | $D\,C-\,B\,A+\cdot$ |

**Step 4 — Reverse the postfix:**

$$D\,C-\,B\,A+\cdot \;\longrightarrow\; \cdot+AB-CD$$

**Final Prefix Expression:**

$$\boxed{\cdot +AB-CD}$$

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Prefix Evaluation

**Prefix:** $-\;+\;\cdot\;2\;3\;-\;8\;2$

**Scan RIGHT to LEFT:**

| Step | Token | Action | Stack |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $2$ | Push | $[2]$ |
| 2 | $8$ | Push | $[2,\;8]$ |
| 3 | $-$ | Pop right=$8$, left=$2 \Rightarrow 8-2=6$. Push $6$ | $[6]$ |
| 4 | $3$ | Push | $[6,\;3]$ |
| 5 | $2$ | Push | $[6,\;3,\;2]$ |
| 6 | $\cdot$ | Pop right=$2$, left=$3 \Rightarrow 3\cdot 2=6$. Push $6$ | $[6,\;6]$ |
| 7 | $+$ | Pop right=$6$, left=$6 \Rightarrow 6+6=12$. Push $12$ | $[12]$ |
| 8 | $-$ | Pop right=$12$, left=$6 \Rightarrow 6-12=-6$. Push $-6$ | $[-6]$ |

**Final Evaluated Result:**

$$\boxed{-6}$$

**Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Prefix } &-\;+\;\cdot\;2\;3\;-\;8\;2 \\
&= -(+(2\cdot 3)+(8-2)) \text{ [reading outermost operator first]} \\
&= -((2\cdot 3)+(8-2)) \\
&= -(6 + 6) \\
&= -12
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Re-evaluation alert:** A common KTU pitfall is confusing the **order of popping** in prefix evaluation. When the operator $\theta$ is read, the **first popped operand is the RIGHT one** (top of stack) and the **second popped is the LEFT one** (next in stack). Computing $a\;\theta\;b$ where $a$ was popped second (left) and $b$ was popped first (right) gives the correct result. The verification above shows that the infix equivalent is $-(2\cdot 3 + 8 - 2) = -(6+6) = -12$. Re-checking the stack trace: At step 8, top=$12$, next=$6$. So computation is $6 - 12 = -6$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Most common mark-deduction points:**
>
> 1. **Wrong precedence comparison during conversion** — Forgetting that $\cdot$ and $/$ have **equal precedence** and are **left-associative**, so when $\cdot$ is on stack and $/$ arrives, $\cdot$ must be popped first.
> 2. **Swapping left/right operands during evaluation** — In postfix, popping gives **right operand first**, then **left operand**. Reversing this gives wrong answer.
> 3. **Wrong scan direction for prefix** — Prefix must be scanned **right-to-left**, not left-to-right.
> 4. **Forgetting to swap parentheses** during infix→prefix conversion.
> 5. **Missing End-of-Input flush** — At end of conversion, all remaining operators in stack must be popped to output.
> 6. **Integer vs real division ambiguity** — Always state the assumed division convention.
> 7. **Exponentiation associativity** — $\wedge$ is **right-associative**, so equal-precedence handling must respect this when popping from the stack.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Evaluation of Expressions**

- [x] **Three notations:** Infix (operator between), Prefix (operator before), Postfix (operator after).
- [x] **Precedence (high → low):** $\wedge$ → $\cdot,/, \%$ → $+,-$
- [x] **Associativity:** $\wedge$ is **right-to-left**; all others are **left-to-right**.
- [x] **Infix → Postfix** uses the **Shunting-Yard algorithm** (Dijkstra) with one operator stack and one output queue. **Single left-to-right scan.**
- [x] **Infix → Prefix** uses the **Reverse-Polish Method**: Reverse → Swap `(` `)` → Convert to postfix → Reverse.
- [x] **Postfix Evaluation:** Scan **left-to-right**. Operand → push. Operator → pop right, pop left, compute $(L \theta R)$, push result.
- [x] **Prefix Evaluation:** Scan **right-to-left**. Operand → push. Operator → pop right, pop left, compute $(L \theta R)$, push result.
- [x] **Time complexity:** $O(n)$ for both conversion and evaluation.
- [x] **Space complexity:** $O(n)$ worst case (stack height).
- [x] **Parentheses are NOT needed** in prefix or postfix; they are intrinsic to operator position.
- [x] **Special rule:** For $\wedge$ (right-associative), when comparing equal-precedence operators, **do NOT pop** the stack top — push the new operator instead.
- [x] **Verification trick:** Build the expression tree by traversing the postfix/prefix left-to-right (postfix) or right-to-left (prefix); an inorder traversal of that tree yields the original infix.
- [x] **KTU exam tip:** Always show **the complete stack state table** for full marks — partial table = partial marks.

---

> **End of Notes — Evaluation of Expressions | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEEET526 Data Structures | Module 1**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-Referential Structures
Dynamic Memory Allocation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Memory Allocation

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Memory Allocation (DMA)** is a memory management mechanism in which the storage requirements of data structures (such as arrays, linked lists, trees, and graphs) are determined and satisfied **at runtime** rather than at compile time. The memory is allocated from the **heap segment** of the process's address space using standard library functions defined in `<stdlib.h>`.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, DMA forms the foundational pillar upon which advanced non-linear data structures are constructed, since the size of such structures cannot always be predicted a priori (e.g., the number of nodes inserted into a linked list at runtime is unknown at the time of compilation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEEET526 – Module 2):**
> Dynamic memory allocation functions — `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `realloc()`, and `free()` — are core examinable elements. Students are expected to write C code snippets using these functions, perform pointer arithmetic, and compute memory block sizes.

The four canonical DMA functions (declared in `<stdlib.h>`) are:

| Function | Purpose | Initialization |
|----------|---------|----------------|
| `malloc()` | Allocates a block of memory | **Uninitialized** (garbage values) |
| `calloc()` | Allocates and **zero-initializes** memory | **Zero-initialized** |
| `realloc()` | Resizes a previously allocated block | Preserves old contents |
| `free()` | Deallocates a memory block | N/A (returns memory to heap) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Room" Model

Imagine you are traveling and need accommodation.

- **Static Allocation** (using fixed-size arrays) is like **buying a 5-room house** before your trip. You pay a fixed cost regardless of how many family members actually travel. If only 2 people come, 3 rooms sit empty. If 7 people come, 3 of them sleep on the floor. The size is fixed at **compile time** (before the trip begins).

- **Dynamic Allocation** is like **booking hotel rooms on-demand**. As your family members arrive, you request exactly the number of rooms needed. If someone leaves, you can return the room. The cost scales with usage, and the size is decided at **runtime**.

This on-demand flexibility is precisely what `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `realloc()`, and `free()` provide to a C program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Static memory resides on the **Stack**, while dynamically allocated memory resides on the **Heap**. The Heap is a much larger, manually-managed region that persists until explicitly freed or until the program terminates.

## 1.3 Why Dynamic Memory Allocation is Necessary

1. **Unknown Data Size at Compile Time** — e.g., a linked list growing based on user input.
2. **Efficient Memory Utilization** — only allocate what is needed, when it is needed.
3. **Data Structures with Variable Size** — stacks, queues, trees, graphs, hash tables.
4. **Persistence Across Function Calls** — heap memory is not destroyed when a function returns (unlike local stack variables).
5. **Large Object Storage** — the stack is typically limited (~1–8 MB), while the heap can utilize most of the available RAM.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of DMA — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 `malloc()` — Memory Allocation

**Syntax:**
```c
ptr = (castType *) malloc(size_in_bytes);
```

- Allocates a contiguous block of `size_in_bytes` bytes on the heap.
- Returns a `void *` (generic pointer) which must be **typecast** to the desired pointer type.
- On failure, returns `NULL`.
- Contents of the allocated block are **uninitialized** (indeterminate values).

**Example:**
```c
int *arr = (int *) malloc(5 * sizeof(int));
```
This allocates memory for 5 integers (typically 20 bytes on a 32-bit system, 40 bytes on 64-bit).

### 2.1.2 `calloc()` — Contiguous Allocation

**Syntax:**
```c
ptr = (castType *) calloc(number_of_elements, size_of_each_element);
```

- Allocates memory for an array of `number_of_elements`, each of `size_of_each_element` bytes.
- The memory is **zero-initialized** — every byte is set to `0`.
- On failure, returns `NULL`.

**Example:**
```c
int *arr = (int *) calloc(5, sizeof(int));
```
Allocates space for 5 integers and initializes all to `0`.

### 2.1.3 `realloc()` — Re-Allocation

**Syntax:**
```c
ptr = (castType *) realloc(ptr, new_size_in_bytes);
```

- Resizes a previously allocated memory block.
- If `new_size` is larger, the contents of the old block are preserved up to the minimum of the old and new sizes; new bytes are uninitialized.
- If `new_size` is smaller, the excess memory is truncated.
- May relocate the block to a new address if the existing block cannot be expanded in place.
- On failure, the original block remains untouched and `NULL` is returned.

### 2.1.4 `free()` — Deallocation

**Syntax:**
```c
free(ptr);
```

- Releases the memory pointed to by `ptr` back to the heap.
- Does **not** set `ptr` to `NULL` automatically — the programmer must do so to avoid **dangling pointers**.
- Calling `free()` on a pointer not obtained from `malloc/calloc/realloc` causes **undefined behavior**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for all size and address calculations related to DMA in the KTU PEEET526 syllabus.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| Total bytes for `n` elements | $B = n \times s$ | $n$ = number of elements, $s$ = size of one element in bytes |
| `malloc` invocation | $\text{ptr} = (\text{type}*)\ \text{malloc}(n \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{type}))$ | Allocates $B$ bytes, uninitialized |
| `calloc` invocation | $\text{ptr} = (\text{type}*)\ \text{count}(\text{type})\ \text{calloc}(n, s)$ | Allocates $B$ bytes, all zero |
| Pointer offset (in elements) | $\text{ptr} + k$ | Moves $k \cdot s$ bytes forward |
| Pointer offset (in bytes) | $(\text{char}*)(\text{ptr}) + k$ | Moves $k$ bytes forward |
| Address of $i$-th element | $\text{addr}_i = \text{base} + (i \cdot s)$ | For an array starting at `base` |
| Realloc size change | $\text{new}_B = \text{old}_B + \Delta B$ | Where $\Delta B$ may be positive or negative |
| Heap memory limits | $\text{Heap} \leq \text{Available RAM}$ | Theoretically up to virtual memory limits |

> **Note on notation:** The vertical bar symbol in any inline mathematical expression (e.g., absolute value) should be rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown table parsing conflicts.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Process memory management, kernel slab allocators.
- **Embedded Systems:** Memory pools in RTOS (FreeRTOS uses `pvPortMalloc`).
- **Databases:** Dynamic buffer pools for query execution.
- **Compilers:** Symbol table allocation grows dynamically during parsing.
- **Network Applications:** Variable-length packet buffer allocation.
- **Game Engines:** Dynamic asset loading, particle systems, real-time object pools.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Size Calculation for an Array of `n` Elements

**Problem Statement:** Derive the total number of bytes $B$ required to dynamically allocate an array of $n$ elements, where each element has size $s$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Define the basic quantities.**

Let $n$ denote the number of elements to be stored and $s$ denote the storage size of a single element in bytes. For a primitive data type in C, $s$ is obtained via the `sizeof` operator.

**Step 2 — Establish the byte-per-element relationship.**

Each element occupies exactly $s$ contiguous bytes in memory. Therefore, two adjacent elements are separated by an offset of exactly $s$ bytes.

**Step 3 — Sum the byte requirements linearly.**

Since the elements are stored contiguously in a single block, the total byte requirement is the linear sum of $s$ over $n$ elements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \underbrace{s + s + s + \cdots + s}_{n\ \text{times}} \\
&= n \times s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Specialize for common KTU examples.**

For an integer array: $s = \text{sizeof(int)} = 4$ bytes (on most modern systems).

$$
B = n \times 4
$$

For a `double` array: $s = \text{sizeof(double)} = 8$ bytes.

$$
B = n \times 8
$$

For a `struct` of mixed types: $s = \text{sizeof(struct\ Node)} = \text{sum of member sizes} + \text{padding}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** The transition from $\underbrace{s + s + \cdots + s}_{n\ \text{times}}$ to $n \times s$ uses the distributive property of multiplication over repeated addition. This is the foundation of every `malloc`/`calloc` size argument in C.

## 3.2 Derivation: Address of the $i$-th Element in a Dynamically Allocated Array

**Problem Statement:** Given a base pointer `base` of type `T *`, derive the memory address of the $i$-th element (0-indexed).

**Step 1 — Define the base address.**

Let $\text{base}$ denote the starting address of the allocated block (the value returned by `malloc`/`calloc`).

**Step 2 — Apply pointer arithmetic.**

In C, when you write `base + i` for a pointer of type `T *`, the compiler automatically scales the offset by `sizeof(T)`. Thus:

$$
\text{addr}_i = \text{base} + i \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)
$$

**Step 3 — Verify with byte-level reasoning.**

If we instead treat the pointer as a byte pointer (`char *`), the offset becomes the literal byte count:

$$
\text{addr}_i^{\text{byte}} = (\text{char}*)\text{base} + i \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)
$$

Both formulations yield identical results due to pointer arithmetic semantics in C.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Let `base = 1000` (decimal address), `T = int` (so $\text{sizeof}(T) = 4$), and $i = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{addr}_3 &= 1000 + 3 \cdot 4 \\
&= 1000 + 12 \\
&= 1012
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the intuition: element 0 at 1000, element 1 at 1004, element 2 at 1008, element 3 at 1012.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of DMA Functions

| Property | `malloc(n)` | `calloc(c, s)` | `realloc(p, n)` | `free(p)` |
|----------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Number of arguments | 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 |
| Initialization | None (garbage) | Zero-initialized | Old contents preserved | N/A |
| Returns | `void *` or `NULL` | `void *` or `NULL` | `void *` or `NULL` | `void` |
| Argument type | Total bytes | Count, then size | Pointer, new bytes | Pointer to free |
| Time overhead | Lowest | Slightly higher (zeroing) | May copy old block | Negligible |
| Typical use | Quick allocation | Safe initialization | Resizing arrays | Cleanup |
| Failure handling | Returns `NULL` | Returns `NULL` | Returns `NULL` (old block intact) | Undefined on bad pointer |

## 3.4 The `sizeof` Operator — Critical Detail

The `sizeof` operator is evaluated at **compile time** (for primitive types) and yields a value of type `size_t` (an unsigned integer). Using `sizeof` inside `malloc` ensures portability across platforms where `int` may be 2, 4, or 8 bytes.

**Correct:**
```c
int *p = (int *) malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
```

**Incorrect (non-portable):**
```c
int *p = (int *) malloc(10 * 4);   /* Assumes int is 4 bytes */
```

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Dynamic Memory Allocation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[Declare Pointer Variable on Stack]
    B --> C{Call malloc / calloc / realloc}
    C --> D[Request Bytes from Heap]
    D --> E{Heap Has Enough Free Block?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Allocate Block on Heap]
    F --> G[Return Base Address as void Pointer]
    G --> H[Typecast to Desired Pointer Type]
    H --> I[Store Address in Pointer Variable]
    I --> J[Use Memory for Computation]
    J --> K[Call free Pointer]
    K --> L[Return Block to Heap Free List]
    L --> M[Set Pointer to NULL to Avoid Dangling Pointer]
    M --> N[Program End]
    E -- No --> O[Allocation Failure]
    O --> P[Return NULL]
    P --> Q[Handle Error Gracefully]
    Q --> N
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
- Node `A` marks program initiation.
- Node `B` establishes the stack-resident pointer variable.
- Node `C` is the decision point for which allocation function to invoke.
- Node `E` represents the heap manager's internal check.
- On success (right branch), execution continues through `F → G → H → I`.
- On failure (left branch), the heap returns `NULL` and the program must handle the error to avoid dereferencing a null pointer.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Layout of a C Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC[Process Address Space]
        direction TB
        SEG1[High Address - 0xFFFFFFFF]
        SEG2[STACK SEGMENT - Grows Downward - Local Variables Function Parameters Return Addresses]
        SEG3[FREE SPACE - Unmapped Region]
        SEG4[HEAP SEGMENT - Grows Upward - Dynamic Allocation malloc calloc realloc]
        SEG5[BSS SEGMENT - Uninitialized Global and Static Variables]
        SEG6[DATA SEGMENT - Initialized Global and Static Variables]
        SEG7[TEXT SEGMENT - Compiled Machine Code - Read Only]
        SEG8[Low Address - 0x00000000]
        SEG1 --> SEG2
        SEG2 --> SEG3
        SEG3 --> SEG4
        SEG4 --> SEG5
        SEG5 --> SEG6
        SEG6 --> SEG7
        SEG7 --> SEG8
    end
```

**Explanation of the layout:**
- The **TEXT segment** at the bottom holds the compiled code.
- The **DATA segment** contains initialized globals.
- The **BSS segment** holds uninitialized globals (zeroed at startup).
- The **HEAP** grows upward from a lower boundary and is where `malloc`/`calloc`/`realloc` carve out blocks.
- The **STACK** grows downward from a higher boundary and holds local variables.
- The **FREE SPACE** between stack and heap dynamically expands and contracts.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heap Block Structure (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BLOCK[Single Heap Block Allocated by malloc]
        direction LR
        HDR[Header - Block Size and Metadata - Hidden from User]
        PAY[Payload - Usable Memory Returned to Programmer]
        FTR[Footer Optional - Boundary Tag]
        HDR --> PAY
        PAY --> FTR
    end
    USER[Programmer Receives Pointer to Payload Start]
    PAY -.-> USER
```

**Explanation:**
- The actual heap block contains a **header** (hidden bookkeeping metadata managed by the allocator).
- The **payload** is the usable memory returned by `malloc`.
- Some allocators also include a **footer** (boundary tag) for coalescing free blocks.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between static memory allocation and dynamic memory allocation. List any two DMA functions used in C.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Static Allocation | Dynamic Allocation |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------|
| When memory is allocated | At **compile time** | At **runtime** |
| Where memory resides | On the **stack** | On the **heap** |
| Size determination | Must be known in advance | Can be determined at runtime |
| Lifetime | Until the scope ends | Until explicitly `free()`d |
| Flexibility | Fixed size | Variable, resizable via `realloc` |

**Two DMA functions:**
1. `malloc(size)` — allocates `size` bytes, uninitialized.
2. `free(ptr)` — releases the memory block pointed to by `ptr`.

*(Any two from `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `free` are acceptable.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the difference between `malloc()` and `calloc()` in C? When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | `malloc()` | `calloc()` |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Number of arguments | 1 (total bytes) | 2 (count, size per element) |
| Initialization | Contents are **uninitialized** (garbage) | Contents are **zero-initialized** |
| Performance | Slightly faster | Slightly slower (zero-fills memory) |
| Typical syntax | `p = malloc(n * sizeof(int));` | `p = calloc(n, sizeof(int));` |

**Preference:**
- Use `calloc()` when you need **safe, predictable initial values** (e.g., counters, flags, string buffers where `'\0'` is desired).
- Use `malloc()` when you will **immediately overwrite** every element (e.g., reading data from a file into the buffer) and the zero-initialization cost of `calloc` is wasted work.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the syntax, purpose, and return value of `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `realloc()`, and `free()` with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a C program to dynamically allocate memory for an array of `n` integers using `malloc()`, accept `n` values from the user, compute and display the sum, and finally release the memory using `free()`. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) `malloc()`** *(~1.75 marks)*

**Syntax:** `ptr = (type *) malloc(size_in_bytes);`

**Purpose:** Allocates a contiguous block of `size_in_bytes` bytes on the heap. The contents are **uninitialized** (indeterminate).

**Return Value:** A `void *` pointing to the start of the allocated block, or `NULL` on failure.

**Example:**
```c
float *p = (float *) malloc(10 * sizeof(float));
```
This allocates space for 10 floats (typically 40 bytes) and returns a typed pointer.

---

**(ii) `calloc()`** *(~1.75 marks)*

**Syntax:** `ptr = (type *) calloc(count, size_in_bytes_per_element);`

**Purpose:** Allocates memory for an array of `count` elements, each of size `size_in_bytes_per_element`, and **zero-initializes** every byte.

**Return Value:** A `void *` to the allocated block, or `NULL` on failure.

**Example:**
```c
int *marks = (int *) calloc(30, sizeof(int));
```
Allocates space for 30 integers, all initialized to 0.

---

**(iii) `realloc()`** *(~1.75 marks)*

**Syntax:** `ptr = (type *) realloc(ptr, new_size_in_bytes);`

**Purpose:** Resizes a previously allocated block. Preserves old contents up to the minimum of old and new sizes; new bytes are uninitialized.

**Return Value:** A `void *` to the (possibly relocated) block, or `NULL` on failure (original block is preserved).

**Example:**
```c
marks = (int *) realloc(marks, 50 * sizeof(int));
```
Resizes the `marks` array to hold 50 integers.

---

**(iv) `free()`** *(~1.75 marks)*

**Syntax:** `free(ptr);`

**Purpose:** Releases the memory block pointed to by `ptr` back to the heap. After `free()`, `ptr` becomes a **dangling pointer** and must not be dereferenced.

**Example:**
```c
free(marks);
marks = NULL;   // Best practice to avoid dangling pointer
```

> **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget the importance of setting the pointer to `NULL` after `free()`. Marks are awarded for mentioning this defensive practice.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(void) {
    int n, i, sum = 0;
    int *arr;
    int *temp;

    printf("Enter the number of elements: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    /* Validate n to avoid nonsensical allocation */
    if (n <= 0) {
        printf("Invalid size.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Dynamic allocation using malloc */
    arr = (int *) malloc(n * sizeof(int));
    if (arr == NULL) {
        printf("Memory allocation failed.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Read values and accumulate sum */
    printf("Enter %d integers:\n", n);
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        scanf("%d", &arr[i]);
        sum += arr[i];
    }

    /* Display the result */
    printf("Sum = %d\n", sum);

    /* Release the memory */
    free(arr);
    arr = NULL;

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of key lines:**

| Line | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `#include <stdlib.h>` | Brings in `malloc` and `free` declarations. |
| `arr = (int *) malloc(n * sizeof(int));` | Allocates $B = n \times 4$ bytes. |
| `if (arr == NULL)` | Mandatory NULL check for allocation failure. |
| `scanf("%d", &arr[i]);` | Reads each integer using pointer arithmetic. |
| `free(arr); arr = NULL;` | Prevents **memory leak** and **dangling pointer**. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between a **dangling pointer** and a **memory leak**. How can each be prevented? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a C program that uses `calloc()` to allocate memory for `n` floating-point numbers, accepts input from the user, finds the maximum element using pointer notation, and uses `realloc()` to shrink the array to half its original size. Finally, free the memory. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Dangling Pointer:** *(3.5 marks)*

A dangling pointer is a pointer that **continues to reference a memory location after that memory has been freed** or after the object it points to has gone out of scope. Dereferencing a dangling pointer leads to **undefined behavior** — the program may read stale data, corrupt other allocations, or crash.

**Causes:**
1. Calling `free(p)` but continuing to use `p`.
2. Returning the address of a local (stack) variable from a function.

**Prevention:**
- Immediately after `free(p);`, set `p = NULL;`.
- Avoid returning addresses of local variables; use heap allocation or pass-by-pointer parameters.

**Memory Leak:** *(3.5 marks)*
