A memory leak occurs when **dynamically allocated memory is never freed**, causing the program's heap usage to grow indefinitely. Over time, this exhausts available memory and may cause the system to slow down or crash.

**Causes:**
1. Forgetting to call `free()` before the pointer goes out of scope.
2. Overwriting a pointer with another allocation without first freeing the original.

**Prevention:**
- Always pair every successful `malloc/calloc/realloc` with a corresponding `free()`.
- Use tools like **Valgrind** or **AddressSanitizer** to detect leaks during testing.
- Adopt the discipline: *allocate → use → free → nullify*.

> **Examiner's Note:** Many students confuse these two concepts. A dangling pointer refers to **using** freed memory; a memory leak refers to **not freeing** memory.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(void) {
    int n, i;
    float *arr;
    float max;

    printf("Enter number of elements: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    if (n <= 0) {
        printf("Invalid size.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Allocate using calloc (zero-initialized) */
    arr = (float *) calloc(n, sizeof(float));
    if (arr == NULL) {
        printf("Allocation failed.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Read values */
    printf("Enter %d floating-point numbers:\n", n);
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        scanf("%f", &arr[i]);
    }

    /* Find maximum using pointer notation */
    max = *arr;                       /* Initialize to first element */
    for (i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        if (*(arr + i) > max) {       /* Pointer arithmetic */
            max = *(arr + i);
        }
    }
    printf("Maximum element = %.2f\n", max);

    /* Shrink to half using realloc */
    n = n / 2;
    arr = (float *) realloc(arr, n * sizeof(float));
    if (arr == NULL) {
        printf("Reallocation failed.\n");
        return 1;
    }
    printf("Array shrunk to %d elements.\n", n);

    /* Release memory */
    free(arr);
    arr = NULL;

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of pointer notation:**

The expression `*(arr + i)` is exactly equivalent to `arr[i]`. Both access the $i$-th element by adding $i \cdot \text{sizeof(float)}$ bytes to the base address `arr`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DMA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the NULL Check (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently omit `if (ptr == NULL)` after `malloc/calloc/realloc`. The KTU model answer scheme explicitly allocates marks for error handling.
>
> 2. **Confusing `malloc` and `calloc` Arguments (–1 mark):** `malloc(n * sizeof(T))` takes **one** argument (total bytes); `calloc(n, sizeof(T))` takes **two** arguments (count, then size). Examiners check the syntax carefully.
>
> 3. **Not Typecasting the Return (–0.5 mark):** Although C allows implicit `void *` conversion, the KTU syllabus expects explicit typecasting `(int *)`, `(float *)`, etc.
>
> 4. **Forgetting `#include <stdlib.h>` (–0.5 mark):** Without this header, the program will not compile. Examiners may deduct for missing header inclusion.
>
> 5. **Using `sizeof(ptr)` Instead of `sizeof(*ptr)` or `sizeof(T)` (–1 mark):** `sizeof(ptr)` gives the pointer size (usually 8 bytes on 64-bit), **not** the element size. Always use `sizeof(T)` where `T` is the data type.
>
> 6. **Memory Leak in Code (–1 mark):** If `free()` is missing or placed in an unreachable branch, marks are deducted for poor memory hygiene.
>
> 7. **Using `free()` on Stack Memory (–1 mark):** Passing the address of a local variable to `free()` causes undefined behavior and is a serious error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dynamic Memory Allocation** happens at **runtime**; static allocation happens at **compile time**.
- The four essential functions are `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `realloc()`, and `free()` — all declared in `<stdlib.h>`.
- `malloc(n)` takes **total bytes**; `calloc(c, s)` takes **count and element size**.
- `calloc` zero-initializes memory; `malloc` leaves it uninitialized.
- `realloc` resizes an existing block; it may relocate the block to a new address.
- `free(p)` releases memory; always set `p = NULL` afterward.
- The address of the $i$-th element is $\text{base} + i \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)$.
- Total bytes for `n` elements: $B = n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)$.
- **Memory Leak** = allocated but never freed.
- **Dangling Pointer** = freed but still accessed.
- Always **check for `NULL`** after any allocation call.
- Use **`sizeof(type)`**, never `sizeof(pointer_variable)`, inside allocation calls.
- Heap memory is **not** automatically reclaimed when a function returns; only `free()` returns it.
- The **stack** holds local variables; the **heap** holds dynamic allocations.
- For KTU exams, **explicit typecasting** of `void *` is expected.
- Pointer arithmetic: `*(arr + i)` is identical to `arr[i]`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singly Linked List - Operations on Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singly Linked List - Operations on Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Singly Linked List (SLL)** is a linear, dynamic, non-contiguous data structure consisting of a sequence of **nodes**, where each node contains two fields:
- A **data field** (`info`) that stores the actual element/value.
- A **link/pointer field** (`next`) that stores the address of the immediately succeeding node in the sequence.

The list is accessed through an external **head pointer** (`head` or `start`) that points to the first node. The last node's `next` field is assigned **NULL** (or `NULL`), marking the logical termination of the list.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A linked list is a collection of data elements called nodes, where each node is divided into two parts: information of the element and a pointer to the next node. In a singly linked list, traversal is permitted only in one direction — from the head towards the tail."*

**Node Structure (C language, KTU standard):**

$$
\text{Node} = \langle \texttt{data} \, (\text{info}), \; \texttt{next} \, (\text{pointer}) \rangle
$$

The size of each node in memory is:
$$
\text{Size}_{\text{node}} = \text{sizeof}(\text{data type}) + \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The term *SLL* refers to a chain in which each node has **exactly one** link field, in contrast to *Doubly Linked List* (two links) and *Circular Linked List* (last node points back to the head).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **treasure hunt** where each clue points to the next clue, and the very last clue says *"Stop — there is no more."*

- Each **clue card** = a **node**.
- The **information on the card** = the **data field**.
- The **direction pointer on the card** ("go to clue #7") = the **next pointer**.
- The **first clue handed to you by the host** = the **head pointer**.
- A card that says *"end of hunt"* = a node whose `next` is `NULL`.

Why not just put all the clues in one big box (an array)? Because:
1. Boxes have a **fixed size** — you must declare the maximum number of clues in advance.
2. Inserting a new clue in the middle requires **shifting all subsequent clues**.

In a treasure hunt, you simply write a new card, and tweak the pointer on the *previous* card to point to it. The hunt **grows and shrinks dynamically** — this is the fundamental advantage of a linked list.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Cost:** A linked list uses **extra memory per node** (the pointer) — typically **8 bytes on a 64-bit system** — in exchange for **O(1) insertion/deletion at a known position** and **dynamic resizing**.

---

## 1.3 Diagrammatic Representation of a SLL

A canonical singly linked list holding three integers `[10, 20, 30]` is conceptualised as:

$$
\boxed{\texttt{head}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{10 \, \vert \, \bullet} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{20 \, \vert \, \bullet} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{30 \, \vert \, \times}
$$

where the symbol $\times$ denotes a `NULL` pointer indicating the end of the list. Each rectangular box represents one **node** in heap memory, and the arrow represents the address stored in the `next` field.

---

## 1.4 Comparison Snapshot (vs. Array)

| Feature | Static Array | Singly Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Layout** | Contiguous | Non-contiguous (scattered in heap) |
| **Size** | Fixed at compile time | Dynamic (runtime) |
| **Random Access** | O(1) | **Not supported** — traversal only |
| **Insertion at Head** | O(n) | **O(1)** |
| **Insertion at Tail** | O(1) (amortised) | O(n) — must traverse |
| **Extra Memory** | None | One pointer per node (~8 bytes) |
| **Cache Friendliness** | High | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Takeaway:** Singly linked lists trade **random access** for **dynamic growth and O(1) front-end operations**. This trade-off is a frequently asked two-marker in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an Operation

Every operation on a singly linked list is governed by **two fundamental rules** that must never be violated:

> **Rule 1 (Order of Pointer Updates):** When inserting a node, always update the new node's `next` pointer **first**, then rewire the predecessor's `next`. Reversing this order severs the chain and causes **memory leaks / lost nodes**.

> **Rule 2 (Save Before You Break):** When deleting a node, always store the `next` pointer of the node-to-be-deleted in a temporary variable **before** calling `free()`. Accessing freed memory is **undefined behaviour** in C.

The complete set of **primitive operations** on a singly linked list as per the KTU 2024 syllabus is:

1. **Traversal** — visit every node exactly once.
2. **Insertion** — at the beginning, at the end, at a given position, after/before a key.
3. **Deletion** — at the beginning, at the end, at a given position, by key value.
4. **Search** — find the first node holding a target value.
5. **Update** — modify the data field of a node.

## 2.2 Why the Operations Have the Complexities They Do

- **Traversal / Search** — there is no random access. You *must* walk from `head` and follow `next` pointers one-by-one until the target is found or `NULL` is reached. Hence **O(n)**.
- **Insertion at beginning** — you only need to make the new node point to the current `head`, then move `head` to the new node. **No traversal**, so **O(1)**.
- **Insertion at end** — to keep the list semantically a "tail," you must traverse the entire list to reach the last node. Hence **O(n)** (or **O(1)** if a `tail` pointer is maintained — a common follow-up question).
- **Deletion at beginning** — advance `head` to `head->next` and free the old head. **O(1)**.
- **Deletion at end** — must traverse to find the **second-to-last** node (because we need to set its `next` to `NULL`). **O(n)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Complexity Table

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Auxiliary Space | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Create List | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | n nodes allocated dynamically |
| Traversal | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | Visits every node once |
| Insertion at Beginning | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | No traversal required |
| Insertion at End | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | Tail traversal required |
| Insertion at Position k | O(1) | O(k) | O(n) | O(1) | Walk to (k-1)th node |
| Insertion after Key | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | Search + 1 step |
| Deletion at Beginning | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | Move head pointer |
| Deletion at End | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | Walk to second-last |
| Deletion at Position k | O(1) | O(k) | O(n) | O(1) | Walk to (k-1)th node |
| Deletion by Key | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | Search + unlink |
| Search (Linear) | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | Unordered list |
| Reverse List | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | Iterative three-pointer method |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Formula:** If the list has $n$ nodes and each pointer occupies $p$ bytes:
> $$\text{Total Memory} = n \cdot (\text{sizeof}(\texttt{data}) + p)$$
> On a 64-bit system with `int` data, $p = 8$ bytes, so **Total = 12n bytes** (ignoring padding).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Singly linked lists underpin several critical systems:

- **Operating Systems** — implementation of dynamic memory **free lists**, the run-queue of processes in a scheduler, and the **stack of threads** in some kernels.
- **Symbol Tables in Compilers** — chaining in hash tables to resolve collisions.
- **Undo/Redo Systems** — each state of an editor is a node; the `next` pointer is the redo chain.
- **Polynomial Arithmetic** — each term of $P(x) = 5x^3 + 4x - 2$ is stored as a node `(coefficient, exponent, next)`.
- **Music Playlists** — a song links to the next song; the last song's `next` is `NULL` (or the first song in a circular variant).
- **Adjacency List Representation of Graphs** — the standard storage for **sparse graphs** in algorithms like BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love linking theory to OS internals. A 2-mark answer mentioning *"free-list maintenance in `malloc()`"* or *"adjacency list of a graph"* scores well above a generic "used in dynamic storage" answer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Algorithms & Analytical Walkthroughs

This section provides the **exhaustive algorithmic treatment** of every core SLL operation, with every pointer manipulation made explicit. Standard C-style pseudocode is used (this is a CS subject, so code is appropriate and required by the KTU syllabus).

## 3.1 The Node Definition (Foundation)

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
typedef struct Node *NODE;
```

**Size analysis on a 64-bit system:**

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{Node}} = \text{sizeof}(\texttt{int}) + \text{sizeof}(\texttt{pointer}) = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bytes (with padding: 16 bytes)}
$$

## 3.2 Operation 1 — Insertion at the Beginning

**Goal:** Prepend a new node holding value `x` to the list.

### Algorithm (Step-by-Step)
1. Allocate a new node `temp` using `malloc()`.
2. Set `temp->data = x`.
3. Set `temp->next = head` (so the new node points to the old first node).
4. Move `head = temp` (so `head` now points to the new first node).
5. Return the new `head`.

### C Implementation
```c
NODE insert_at_begin(NODE head, int x) {
    NODE temp = (NODE)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (temp == NULL) { printf("Memory overflow\n"); return head; }
    temp->data = x;
    temp->next = head;   // Step 3: link to old list
    head = temp;         // Step 4: update head
    return head;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

Let the original list be $\texttt{head} \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow \text{NULL}$ and let $x = 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state:} \quad & \texttt{head} \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [30 \,|\, \times] \\
\text{After allocation of temp:} \quad & \text{temp} = [10 \,|\, ?] \\
\text{After temp->next = head:} \quad & \text{temp} = [10 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [30 \,|\, \times] \\
\text{After head = temp:} \quad & \texttt{head} \rightarrow [10 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [30 \,|\, \times]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this O(1)?** Only **two pointer assignments** are performed regardless of the list size $n$. There is no loop, no traversal. Time is constant.

## 3.3 Operation 2 — Insertion at the End

**Goal:** Append a new node holding value `x` as the last node.

### Algorithm (Step-by-Step)
1. Allocate `temp` and set `temp->data = x`, `temp->next = NULL`.
2. If the list is empty (`head == NULL`), set `head = temp` and return.
3. Otherwise, traverse the list from `head` using a `cur` pointer until `cur->next == NULL`.
4. Set `cur->next = temp`.

### C Implementation
```c
NODE insert_at_end(NODE head, int x) {
    NODE temp = (NODE)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (temp == NULL) { printf("Memory overflow\n"); return head; }
    temp->data = x;
    temp->next = NULL;

    if (head == NULL) {                 // empty list
        return temp;
    }
    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur->next != NULL) {         // Step 3: walk to last
        cur = cur->next;
    }
    cur->next = temp;                   // Step 4: link
    return head;
}
```

### Logical Derivation

Initial: $\texttt{head} \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [30 \,|\, \times]$, with $x = 40$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loop iteration 1:} \quad & \text{cur} = [20 \,|\, \bullet], \quad \text{cur->next} \neq \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \text{cur} = \text{cur->next} \\
\text{Loop iteration 2:} \quad & \text{cur} = [30 \,|\, \times], \quad \text{cur->next} = \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \text{stop} \\
\text{After cur->next = temp:} \quad & \texttt{head} \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [30 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [40 \,|\, \times]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Number of pointer hops = $n$**. Hence time complexity is $O(n)$. A common exam question asks: *"How can insertion at end be made O(1)?"* — Answer: **Maintain a `tail` pointer** that always points to the last node, and update it on every insertion.

## 3.4 Operation 3 — Insertion at a Given Position `k`

**Goal:** Insert a new node at the $k$-th position (1-indexed, per KTU convention).

### Algorithm
1. If $k = 1$, perform **insert at beginning** (special case).
2. Otherwise, traverse to the $(k-1)$-th node using `cur`. Let `count = 1`.
3. If `cur == NULL` before reaching position $k-1$, the position is **out of bounds** — abort.
4. Allocate `temp`, set `temp->data = x`.
5. Set `temp->next = cur->next`.
6. Set `cur->next = temp`.

### C Implementation
```c
NODE insert_at_pos(NODE head, int x, int k) {
    if (k < 1) { printf("Invalid position\n"); return head; }

    if (k == 1) {                       // Step 1
        NODE temp = (NODE)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
        temp->data = x; temp->next = head;
        return temp;
    }

    NODE cur = head;
    int count = 1;
    while (cur != NULL && count < k - 1) {   // Step 2
        cur = cur->next;
        count++;
    }
    if (cur == NULL) { printf("Position out of range\n"); return head; }

    NODE temp = (NODE)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    temp->data = x;
    temp->next = cur->next;              // Step 5
    cur->next = temp;                    // Step 6
    return head;
}
```

### Logical Derivation for $k = 2$, $x = 15$, initial list `[10, 20, 30]`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loop runs once:} \quad & \text{cur} = [10 \,|\, \bullet], \; \text{count} = 2, \;\text{stop} \\
\text{temp->next = cur->next:} \quad & \text{temp} = [15 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow \ldots \\
\text{cur->next = temp:} \quad & [10 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [15 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Insertion:** Update the **new node's next first**, then rewire the **predecessor's next**. The reverse order loses the rest of the list.

## 3.5 Operation 4 — Deletion at the Beginning

### Algorithm
1. If `head == NULL`, list is empty — print underflow and return.
2. Store `head` in `temp`.
3. Move `head = head->next`.
4. Free `temp`.
5. Return new `head`.

### C Implementation
```c
NODE delete_at_begin(NODE head) {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("List is empty\n"); return NULL; }
    NODE temp = head;
    head = head->next;
    free(temp);
    return head;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Time complexity = O(1).** Only three pointer operations: `temp = head`, `head = head->next`, `free(temp)`.

## 3.6 Operation 5 — Deletion at the End

### Algorithm
1. If `head == NULL`, list is empty.
2. If `head->next == NULL`, only one node — free it, set `head = NULL`.
3. Otherwise, traverse to the **second-to-last** node (i.e. `cur->next->next == NULL`).
4. Free `cur->next`, set `cur->next = NULL`.

### C Implementation
```c
NODE delete_at_end(NODE head) {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("List is empty\n"); return NULL; }
    if (head->next == NULL) { free(head); return NULL; }

    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur->next->next != NULL) {    // walk to second-last
        cur = cur->next;
    }
    free(cur->next);
    cur->next = NULL;
    return head;
}
```

### Logical Derivation for `[10, 20, 30, 40]`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial cur = head = [10|\bullet]} \quad & \text{cur->next->next} = [30|\bullet] \neq \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \text{cur} = [20|\bullet] \\
\text{cur = [20|\bullet]:} \quad & \text{cur->next->next} = \text{NULL} \Rightarrow \text{stop} \\
\text{free(cur->next):} \quad & \text{node [30|\bullet] is released} \\
\text{cur->next = NULL:} \quad & \text{node [20|\bullet] now terminates the list}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why second-to-last, not last?** Because we need to **nullify the link** of the *new* last node. If we walk to the last node, we have no way to set its predecessor's `next` to `NULL` without a second pass.

## 3.7 Operation 6 — Deletion at a Given Position `k`

### Algorithm
1. If `k == 1`, perform **delete at beginning**.
2. Traverse to the $(k-1)$-th node.
3. If `cur == NULL` or `cur->next == NULL`, position is invalid.
4. Store `cur->next` in `temp`.
5. Set `cur->next = temp->next`.
6. Free `temp`.

### C Implementation
```c
NODE delete_at_pos(NODE head, int k) {
    if (head == NULL || k < 1) { printf("Invalid\n"); return head; }

    if (k == 1) {
        NODE temp = head; head = head->next; free(temp); return head;
    }

    NODE cur = head;
    int count = 1;
    while (cur != NULL && count < k - 1) {
        cur = cur->next; count++;
    }
    if (cur == NULL || cur->next == NULL) {
        printf("Position out of range\n"); return head;
    }
    NODE temp = cur->next;        // node to delete
    cur->next = temp->next;       // bypass
    free(temp);
    return head;
}
```

## 3.8 Operation 7 — Traversal (Display)

### Algorithm
```c
void display(NODE head) {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("List is empty\n"); return; }
    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur != NULL) {
        printf("%d -> ", cur->data);
        cur = cur->next;
    }
    printf("NULL\n");
}
```

### Logical Derivation
For list `[10, 20, 30]`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1: cur = [10|\bullet], print 10, cur = cur->next = [20|\bullet]} \\
\text{Iteration 2: cur = [20|\bullet], print 20, cur = cur->next = [30|\bullet]} \\
\text{Iteration 3: cur = [30|\times], print 30, cur = cur->next = NULL} \\
\text{Iteration 4: cur = NULL, exit loop, print "NULL"}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.9 Operation 8 — Search (Find First Occurrence)

### Algorithm
```c
int search(NODE head, int key) {
    int pos = 1;
    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur != NULL) {
        if (cur->data == key) return pos;
        cur = cur->next;
        pos++;
    }
    return -1;   // not found
}
```

## 3.10 Operation 9 — Reversal (Bonus, Frequently Asked)

**Three-pointer sliding-window method:**

```c
NODE reverse(NODE head) {
    NODE prev = NULL, cur = head, next = NULL;
    while (cur != NULL) {
        next = cur->next;    // save the next node
        cur->next = prev;    // reverse the link
        prev = cur;          // slide prev forward
        cur = next;          // slide cur forward
    }
    return prev;             // prev is the new head
}
```

### Logical Derivation for `[10, 20, 30]`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Init: prev = NULL, cur = [10|\bullet], next = NULL} \\
\text{Iter 1: next = [20|\bullet], cur->next = NULL, prev = [10|NULL], cur = [20|\bullet]} \\
\text{Iter 2: next = [30|\times], cur->next = [10|NULL], prev = [20|10], cur = [30|\times]} \\
\text{Iter 3: next = NULL, cur->next = [20|10], prev = [30|20|10], cur = NULL} \\
\text{Return prev = [30|20|10|NULL]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final reversed list: $\texttt{head} \rightarrow [30 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [20 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [10 \,|\, \times]$.

> [!TIP]
> **Time = O(n), Space = O(1).** This is the optimal reversal — never use an array or recursion if the question specifies iterative O(1) auxiliary space.

## 3.11 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operation | Pointer Updates | Traversal Required? | Best-Case Time | Worst-Case Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Insert at Head | 2 | No | O(1) | O(1) |
| Insert at Tail | 2 + n hops | Yes (full) | O(1) (empty) | O(n) |
| Insert at Position k | 2 + (k-1) hops | Yes (partial) | O(1) | O(n) |
| Delete at Head | 2 | No | O(1) | O(1) |
| Delete at Tail | 1 + (n-1) hops | Yes (full) | O(1) (single node) | O(n) |
| Delete at Position k | 2 + (k-1) hops | Yes (partial) | O(1) | O(n) |
| Search | 0 | Yes (full) | O(1) (head) | O(n) |
| Reverse | 3 per node | Yes (full) | O(n) | O(n) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams below use **strictly alphanumeric node IDs** (prefixed with letters) and **plain uppercase text labels** to comply with the rendering safeguards.

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a SLL Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NODE STRUCTURE] --> B[DATA FIELD]
    A --> C[NEXT POINTER]
    B --> B1[HOLDS INTEGER OR STRUCT]
    C --> C1[STORES ADDRESS OF NEXT NODE]
    C1 --> C2[NULL IF LAST NODE]
    A --> D[MEMORY ALLOCATED ON HEAP]
    D --> D1[SIZE 12 TO 16 BYTES]
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — Insertion at Beginning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[INITIAL HEAD POINTS TO NODE A] --> S1[ALLOCATE NEW NODE T]
    S1 --> S2[ASSIGN T NEXT EQUALS HEAD]
    S2 --> S3[UPDATE HEAD EQUALS T]
    S3 --> S4[RETURN NEW HEAD]
    S0 -.BEFORE.-> S4
    S4 -.AFTER.-> S5[NEW LIST STARTS WITH T]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Insertion at End

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[START WITH HEAD POINTER] --> P1{IS LIST EMPTY}
    P1 -- YES --> P2[SET HEAD EQUALS NEW NODE]
    P1 -- NO --> P3[TRAVERSE FROM HEAD]
    P3 --> P3A[GO TO NEXT NODE]
    P3A --> P3B{NEXT IS NULL}
    P3B -- NO --> P3A
    P3B -- YES --> P4[ARRIVED AT LAST NODE]
    P4 --> P5[LINK LAST NEXT TO NEW NODE]
    P5 --> P6[SET NEW NODE NEXT TO NULL]
    P2 --> P7[RETURN HEAD]
    P6 --> P7
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of Deletion at End

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[START] --> Q1{IS LIST EMPTY}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2[PRINT UNDERFLOW]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{IS THERE ONLY ONE NODE}
    Q3 -- YES --> Q4[FREE HEAD AND SET HEAD TO NULL]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q5[TRAVERSE TO SECOND LAST NODE]
    Q5 --> Q5A[FOLLOW NEXT UNTIL NEXT NEXT IS NULL]
    Q5A --> Q6[STORE LAST NODE IN TEMP]
    Q6 --> Q7[SET SECOND LAST NEXT TO NULL]
    Q7 --> Q8[FREE TEMP]
    Q8 --> Q9[RETURN HEAD]
    Q4 --> Q9
    Q2 --> Q9
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE
        B1[HEAD] --> B2[NODE 10]
        B2 --> B3[NODE 20]
        B3 --> B4[NODE 30]
```

## 4.6 Functional Block Architecture — All SLL Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[SINGLY LINKED LIST OPERATIONS] --> R1[CREATION]
    ROOT --> R2[TRAVERSAL]
    ROOT --> R3[INSERTION]
    ROOT --> R4[DELETION]
    ROOT --> R5[SEARCH]
    ROOT --> R6[REVERSAL]

    R2 --> R2A[VISIT EACH NODE ONCE]
    R3 --> R3A[INSERT AT HEAD O1]
    R3 --> R3B[INSERT AT TAIL ON]
    R3 --> R3C[INSERT AT POSITION K OK]
    R4 --> R4A[DELETE AT HEAD O1]

    R4 --> R4D[DELETE BY KEY ON]

```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The two most important structural facts visible in these diagrams are (1) insertion at head never visits existing nodes, hence O(1); (2) deletion at tail must always walk one extra step beyond what insertion does, hence its second-to-last traversal logic.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a singly linked list and a static array with respect to memory allocation, access time, and insertion at the beginning. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Static Array | Singly Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Allocation | **Contiguous**, fixed at compile time | **Non-contiguous**, dynamic at runtime using `malloc()` |
| Access Time | **O(1)** random access via index | **O(n)** — must traverse from head |
| Insertion at Beginning | **O(n)** — requires shifting all elements | **O(1)** — only two pointer assignments |

> The singly linked list is preferred when frequent **insertion/deletion at the head** is required and the size is unknown in advance, whereas arrays are preferred when **random access** and **cache locality** are critical.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the `head` pointer and the `NULL` value in a singly linked list. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- The **head pointer** is an external pointer variable that stores the address of the **first node** in the list. It is the **only entry point** to the list — without it, no node in the heap is reachable from the program. All traversals begin from `head`.

- The **NULL value** stored in the `next` field of the last node acts as the **sentinel terminator** of the list. It signals that the chain has ended. During traversal, when `cur->next == NULL`, the loop terminates. It is also used to represent an **empty list**, where `head == NULL`.

- Together, `head` and `NULL` define the **logical boundary** of the list, even though its nodes are physically scattered across heap memory.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B (14-mark) question provides an **internal choice** — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) Write a C program to create a singly linked list of `n` integers. Implement the following operations:**
   (i) Insertion at the **end** of the list. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) Deletion of a node whose data field equals a given **key**. **(4 marks)**

**(b) Explain the time complexity of the deletion-by-key operation with a worst-case analysis. Demonstrate the reversal of the list using a dry-run on the input list `[15, 25, 35, 45]`. **(6 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a)(i) — Insertion at End (4 marks)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
typedef struct Node *NODE;

NODE insert_at_end(NODE head, int x) {
    NODE temp = (NODE)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (temp == NULL) { printf("Memory overflow\n"); return head; }
    temp->data = x;
    temp->next = NULL;

    if (head == NULL) return temp;

    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur->next != NULL) cur = cur->next;
    cur->next = temp;
    return head;
}

NODE create_list(int n) {
    NODE head = NULL;
    int x;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("Enter element %d: ", i + 1);
        scanf("%d", &x);
        head = insert_at_end(head, x);
    }
    return head;
}

void display(NODE head) {
    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur != NULL) {
        printf("%d -> ", cur->data);
        cur = cur->next;
    }
    printf("NULL\n");
}

int main() {
    int n;
    printf("Enter number of nodes: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);
    NODE head = create_list(n);
    printf("List: ");
    display(head);
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation:**
- A new node is allocated, its `data` is set, and `next` is initialised to `NULL`.
- If the list is empty, the new node becomes the head.
- Otherwise, a `cur` pointer traverses until `cur->next == NULL`, then links the new node.
- **Time complexity = O(n)** in the worst case.

#### Part (a)(ii) — Deletion by Key (4 marks)

```c
NODE delete_by_key(NODE head, int key) {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("List empty\n"); return NULL; }

    /* Case 1: head itself holds the key */
    if (head->data == key) {
        NODE t = head; head = head->next; free(t);
        return head;
    }

    /* Case 2: key is in middle or tail */
    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur->next != NULL && cur->next->data != key) {
        cur = cur->next;
    }

    if (cur->next == NULL) {
        printf("Key %d not found\n", key);
        return head;
    }

    NODE t = cur->next;
    cur->next = t->next;   // bypass the node
    free(t);
    return head;
}
```

**Explanation:**
- Two cases: key at the head (special) and key in the middle/tail.
- A single traversal stops when either the key is found or the list ends.
- **Time complexity = O(n)** worst case, **O(1)** if the key happens to be the head.

#### Part (b) — Time Complexity & Reversal Dry-Run (6 marks)

**Time Complexity of Deletion-by-Key:**
- **Worst case:** Key is at the last node (or absent). We traverse the entire list — $n$ comparisons.
- **Best case:** Key is at the head — **O(1)**.
- **Average case:** On a uniform random distribution of the key, the expected number of comparisons is $\frac{n+1}{2}$ — hence **O(n)**.
- **Space complexity:** O(1) — only a few pointer variables.

**Reversal Dry-Run for `[15, 25, 35, 45]`:**

Initial state:
$$
\texttt{head} \rightarrow [15 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [25 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [35 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [45 \,|\, \times]
$$

Using `prev = NULL, cur = head`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iter 1: next = 25, cur->next = NULL, prev = [15|NULL], cur = [25|\bullet]} \\
\text{Iter 2: next = 35, cur->next = [15|NULL], prev = [25|15], cur = [35|\bullet]} \\
\text{Iter 3: next = 45, cur->next = [25|15], prev = [35|25|15], cur = [45|\times]} \\
\text{Iter 4: next = NULL, cur->next = [35|25|15], prev = [45|35|25|15], cur = NULL}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final state:
$$
\texttt{head} = \texttt{prev} \rightarrow [45 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [35 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [25 \,|\, \bullet] \rightarrow [15 \,|\, \times]
$$

The list is reversed in **O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary space**.

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the node structure of a singly linked list. Write C functions to:**
   (i) Insert a node at the **beginning** of the list. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) Delete the **last** node from the list. **(4 marks)**

**(b) Consider the list `[50, 40, 30, 20, 10]`. Perform a dry-run of inserting 35 at position 3, then deleting the node with value 40. Show the list state after each step. Justify the time complexity of each operation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Node Diagram & Functions (7 marks)

**Node Structure Diagram:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Node} = \Big[\, \underbrace{\text{data}}_{\text{info}} \,\Big\vert\, \underbrace{\text{next}}_{\text{pointer to next node}} \,\Big]}
$$

**C structure:**
```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
typedef struct Node *NODE;
```

#### (a)(i) — Insert at Beginning (3 marks)

```c
NODE insert_at_begin(NODE head, int x) {
    NODE temp = (NODE)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    temp->data = x;
    temp->next = head;   // link to old first node
    head = temp;         // update head
    return head;
}
```

- **Time complexity = O(1).** Only two pointer assignments; no traversal.
- **Space complexity = O(1).** One new node allocation.

#### (a)(ii) — Delete Last Node (4 marks)

```c
NODE delete_at_end(NODE head) {
    if (head == NULL) { printf("Underflow\n"); return NULL; }
    if (head->next == NULL) { free(head); return NULL; }

    NODE cur = head;
    while (cur->next->next != NULL) cur = cur->next;   // walk to second-last
    free(cur->next);                                    // free last
    cur->next = NULL;                                   // terminate
    return head;
}
```

- **Time complexity = O(n).** Must traverse to the second-to-last node.
- **Space complexity = O(1).** Only one extra pointer `cur`.

#### Part (b) — Dry-Run for `[50, 40, 30, 20, 10]` (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initial state:**
$$
L_0: \texttt{head} \rightarrow [50|\bullet] \rightarrow [40|\bullet] \rightarrow [30|\bullet] \rightarrow [20|\bullet] \rightarrow [10|\times]
$$

**Step 2 — Insert 35 at position 3 (1-indexed):**
- Traverse to position $k-1 = 2$, i.e. node `[40|\bullet]`. **1 hop.**
- Set `temp->next = cur->next` → `temp = [35|30]`.
- Set `cur->next = temp`.

$$
L_1: \texttt{head} \rightarrow [50|\bullet] \rightarrow [40|\bullet] \rightarrow [35|\bullet] \rightarrow [30|\bullet] \rightarrow [20|\bullet] \rightarrow [10|\times]
$$

- **Time complexity of insert at position 3 = O(3) = O(n) in general.**

**Step 3 — Delete the node with value 40 (by key):**
- Search for 40 starting from head. Found at position 2.
- Store `cur->next = [35|...]` in `temp` (this is the node to delete).
- Set `cur->next = temp->next` (bypass the node).
- Free `temp`.

$$
L_2: \texttt{head} \rightarrow [50|\bullet] \rightarrow [35|\bullet] \rightarrow [30|\bullet] \rightarrow [20|\bullet] \rightarrow [10|\times]
$$

- **Time complexity of delete-by-key = O(n) worst case, O(1) best case (head).**

**Step 4 — Justification of complexities:**
- Both insertion-at-position and deletion-by-key require **search/traversal** of the list.
- In the **best case**, the target is at the head — constant work.
- In the **worst case**, the target is at the tail or absent — full traversal of $n$ nodes.
- Hence both are **O(n)** in general, with **O(1) auxiliary space** because only a constant number of pointers (`cur`, `temp`, `prev`) are used.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the `head == NULL` (empty list) check** in any deletion function → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Reversing the order of pointer updates** in insertion (setting predecessor's `next` before the new node's `next`) → loses 1–2 marks and produces a logically broken list.
> 3. **Not freeing the deleted node** in C (`free(temp)`) — memory leak; deduct 1 mark in KTU's strict C-evaluation.
> 4. **Confusing 0-indexed vs 1-indexed positions** — KTU uses **1-indexed**; stating "position 0" or off-by-one errors lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Failing to handle the "key not found" case** in delete-by-key → loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Writing only the algorithm and skipping the dry-run** — for 6–7 mark questions, a dry-run on a small example is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Node structure:** `data` + `next` pointer, allocated on the **heap** with `malloc()`.
- **Head pointer** is the only external reference to the list; **NULL** marks the end.
- **Insert at beginning = O(1)**, **Insert at end = O(n)**, **Insert at position k = O(k)**.
- **Delete at beginning = O(1)**, **Delete at end = O(n)**, **Delete by key = O(n)**.
- **Golden Rule of Insertion:** set `new->next = predecessor->next` **before** `predecessor->next = new`.
- **Golden Rule of Deletion:** store `cur->next` in `temp`, rewire `cur->next = temp->next`, **then** `free(temp)`.
- **Search is O(n)** — there is no random access; you must walk the chain.
- **Reversal** uses **three pointers (`prev`, `cur`, `next`)** in O(n) time and O(1) space.
- **Always handle the empty list (`head == NULL`)** and the **single-node list** as special cases.
- **Always free deleted nodes** in C to avoid memory leaks — KTU examiners check this.
- **Position numbering is 1-indexed** in KTU convention.
- **The singly linked list cannot be traversed backwards** — this is its biggest limitation and motivates the **doubly linked list** (next module).
- **Practical use-cases** worth memorising: free-lists in OS, adjacency list of graphs, polynomial representation, undo/redo stacks, music playlists.
- **Memory formula:** $n \cdot (\text{sizeof}(\texttt{data}) + \text{sizeof}(\texttt{pointer}))$ bytes total.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doubly Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Doubly Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: DOUBLY LINKED LIST (DLL)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Doubly Linked List (DLL)** is a linear, dynamic data structure in which each node contains three fields: a **data field** to store the element, a **next pointer** (`forward`) pointing to the succeeding node, and a **prev pointer** (`backward`) pointing to the preceding node. The list maintains two external references — a `HEAD` pointer to the first node and a `TAIL` pointer to the last node — enabling **bidirectional traversal** in $O(1)$ time from either end.

The structural signature of a DLL node in C is:
```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* prev;   // Pointer to previous node
    struct Node* next;   // Pointer to next node
};
```

The mathematical abstraction of a DLL of size $n$ can be expressed as the ordered tuple:

$$
\text{DLL} = (N_1, N_2, N_3, \ldots, N_n)
$$

where each node $N_i$ satisfies the invariant:

$$
N_i.\text{next} = N_{i+1} \quad \text{and} \quad N_i.\text{prev} = N_{i-1} \quad \text{for } 1 < i < n
$$

with boundary conditions $N_1.\text{prev} = \text{NULL}$ and $N_n.\text{next} = \text{NULL}$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Way Escalator" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a **two-way escalator** in a shopping mall. Every step (node) is connected to the one in **front** (`next`) and the one **behind** (`prev`). If you are standing on any step, you can move forward to the next floor or backward to the previous floor — *without restarting from the ground floor*. This is exactly what a DLL gives you: **freedom of motion in both directions**.

**A simpler analogy — the Train Compartment:**
- A **singly linked list** is a train where each coach knows *only* the coach ahead of it. To find the previous coach, you must walk from the engine.
- A **doubly linked list** is a train where each coach has handles to *both* the coach ahead and the coach behind. You can walk in either direction freely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The DLL is often called a *Two-Way Chain* or *Bidirectional Linear List*. The ability to traverse backward is its **defining advantage** over a Singly Linked List (SLL), at the cost of one extra pointer per node (memory overhead of **8 bytes per node on a 64-bit system**).

---

### 1.3 Structural Anatomy of a DLL Node

| Component | Symbol | Purpose | Memory (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data | `data` | Stores the payload value | 4 bytes (int) |
| Forward Link | `next` | Points to the next node | 8 bytes (pointer) |
| Backward Link | `prev` | Points to the previous node | 8 bytes (pointer) |
| **Total per node** | — | — | **20 bytes** |

The **HEAD** of the list stores `prev = NULL`, and the **TAIL** stores `next = NULL`, terminating both directions of traversal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Five Primitive Operations

A DLL supports the following core operations. Each operation modifies the `prev` and `next` pointers while preserving the **bidirectional invariant**.

### A. Insertion at Head
1. Allocate a new node $N_{new}$.
2. Set $N_{new}.\text{next} = \text{HEAD}$.
3. If `HEAD != NULL`, set `HEAD->prev = N_new`.
4. Set $N_{new}.\text{prev} = \text{NULL}$.
5. Update `HEAD = N_new`.
6. If `TAIL == NULL`, set `TAIL = N_new`.

### B. Insertion at Tail
1. Allocate a new node $N_{new}$.
2. If `TAIL != NULL`, set `TAIL->next = $N_{new}$`.
3. Set $N_{new}.\text{prev} = \text{TAIL}$.
4. Set $N_{new}.\text{next} = \text{NULL}$.
5. Update `TAIL = $N_{new}$`.

### C. Insertion at Position $k$ (1-indexed)
1. Traverse from `HEAD` to node at position $k-1$ (call it $P$).
2. If $k = 1$, invoke Insertion at Head.
3. Else, set $N_{new}.\text{next} = P.\text{next}$.
4. If $P.\text{next} != \text{NULL}$, set $P.\text{next}.\text{prev} = N_{new}$.
5. Set $P.\text{next} = N_{new}$ and $N_{new}.\text{prev} = P$.

### D. Deletion of Node $X$
1. If $X.\text{prev} != \text{NULL}$, set $X.\text{prev}.\text{next} = X.\text{next}$.
2. Else, update `HEAD = X.next`.
3. If $X.\text{next} != \text{NULL}$, set $X.\text{next}.\text{prev} = X.\text{prev}$.
4. Else, update `TAIL = X.prev`.
5. `free(X)`.

### E. Bidirectional Traversal
- **Forward:** from `HEAD`, follow `next` until `NULL`.
- **Backward:** from `TAIL`, follow `prev` until `NULL`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Order of Pointer Updates Matters**
> If you overwrite `X->next` or `X->prev` **before** re-linking the neighbours, the chain is permanently broken (a classic "lost pointer" bug). The strict ordering: **save neighbour pointers first, then re-link, then free**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Time Complexity Table

| Operation | Time Complexity (DLL) | Time Complexity (SLL) | Space Overhead |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access by index | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $2P + D$ per node |
| Insertion at Head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insertion at Tail | $O(1)$ *with TAIL* | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insertion at Position $k$ | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion at Head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion at Tail | $O(1)$ *with TAIL* | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion of node $X$ (given pointer) | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Forward Traversal | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Backward Traversal | $O(n)$ | **Not Possible** | $O(1)$ |
| Search | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $P$ = pointer size (8 bytes on 64-bit), $D$ = data size. The total memory per DLL node is approximately $D + 2P$.

The **space complexity** for any of the primitive operations listed above is constant:

$$
S_{\text{op}}(n) = O(1)
$$

The total memory footprint of a DLL with $n$ nodes is:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = n \cdot (D + 2P) + 2P \quad \text{(for HEAD and TAIL pointers)}
$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications of DLL

> [!TIP]
> **Where DLLs are used in industry:**
> - **Browser History Navigation** — Back and Forward buttons in Chrome/Firefox use a DLL.
> - **LRU Cache Implementation** — The hash table + DLL combination is the standard pattern.
> - **Undo/Redo Functionality** in text editors (each state is a DLL node).
> - **Music Playlists** — Previous and Next track navigation.
> - **Operating Systems** — Process scheduling lists, thread management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pointer Re-linking for Insertion at Position $k$

Let the DLL be: $N_1 \leftrightarrow N_2 \leftrightarrow \ldots \leftrightarrow N_{k-1} \leftrightarrow N_k \leftrightarrow \ldots \leftrightarrow N_n$

We wish to insert a new node $N_{new}$ at position $k$ (i.e., between $N_{k-1}$ and $N_k$).

**Step 1 — Identify the predecessor:**
We traverse to node $P = N_{k-1}$. This takes $k-1$ steps.

$$
\text{Position of } P = k - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Set the forward link of the new node:**
$N_{new}$ must point to the node that was originally after $P$:

$$
N_{new}.\text{next} = P.\text{next} = N_k
$$

> *Logic:* Before insertion, $P$'s successor was $N_k$. After insertion, $N_{new}$ becomes $P$'s successor, so $N_{new}$ must point to $N_k$.

**Step 3 — Set the backward link of the new node:**

$$
N_{new}.\text{prev} = P = N_{k-1}
$$

> *Logic:* $N_{new}$'s predecessor is $P$.

**Step 4 — Update the successor's backward link (if it exists):**
If $N_k$ exists (i.e., $k \leq n$), we must tell $N_k$ that its predecessor has changed:

$$
N_k.\text{prev} = N_{new}
$$

> *Logic:* $N_k$ used to be linked back to $P$, but now $N_{new}$ sits in between.

**Step 5 — Update the predecessor's forward link:**

$$
P.\text{next} = N_{new}
$$

> *Logic:* $P$'s successor is no longer $N_k$; it is $N_{new}$.

**Final linked structure:**

$$
N_1 \leftrightarrow \ldots \leftrightarrow N_{k-1} \leftrightarrow N_{new} \leftrightarrow N_k \leftrightarrow \ldots \leftrightarrow N_n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Order of Updates (KEEP THIS SEQUENCE):**
> Step 2 → Step 3 → Step 4 → Step 5. Reordering Step 2 and Step 5 will cause a **dangling pointer** (the chain to $N_k$ is lost forever).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pointer Re-linking for Deletion of Node $X$

Given: $X$ has neighbours $X.\text{prev} = P$ and $X.\text{next} = S$ (where $P$ and $S$ may be `NULL` for head/tail deletions).

**Step 1 — Bypass $X$ from the predecessor's forward chain:**

$$
P.\text{next} = X.\text{next} = S
$$

> *Logic:* $P$ should now skip $X$ and directly point to $S$.

**Step 2 — Bypass $X$ from the successor's backward chain (if $S$ exists):**

$$
S.\text{prev} = X.\text{prev} = P
$$

> *Logic:* $S$ should now point back to $P$ instead of $X$.

**Step 3 — Special handling for boundary nodes:**

If $P = \text{NULL}$ (i.e., $X$ is the HEAD), then:
$$
\text{HEAD} = S
$$

If $S = \text{NULL}$ (i.e., $X$ is the TAIL), then:
$$
\text{TAIL} = P
$$

**Step 4 — Deallocate $X$:**

$$
\text{free}(X)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** If you free $X$ **before** re-linking $P$ and $S$, accessing $X.\text{next}$ or $X.\text{prev}$ after `free` invokes **undefined behaviour** — a guaranteed marks deduction in KTU lab/ESE evaluations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Time Complexity Proof for Deletion (Given Pointer)

**Claim:** Deleting a node $X$ (when a direct pointer to $X$ is available) takes $O(1)$ time.

**Proof:**

$$
T_{\text{del}}(n) = T_{\text{re-link}} + T_{\text{free}}
$$

The re-linking involves exactly four pointer assignments (Step 1, Step 2, HEAD/TAIL update):

$$
T_{\text{re-link}} = 4 \cdot t_{\text{assign}} = O(1)
$$

The `free(X)` operation is a constant-time system call:

$$
T_{\text{free}} = O(1)
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{del}}(n) = O(1) + O(1) = O(1)
$$

> *Comparison:* In an **SLL**, deleting node $X$ (given pointer) still requires $O(n)$ time to find the predecessor. This is the **single biggest advantage** of a DLL over an SLL.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example

**Initial DLL:** `NULL ← [10] ⇄ [20] ⇄ [30] → NULL` with `HEAD = N1, TAIL = N3`.

**Task:** Insert node `25` after node `20`.

| Step | Operation | Resulting DLL State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new.prev = N2` (the node 20) | `25.prev = 20` |
| 2 | `new.next = N2.next` (= N3) | `25.next = 30` |
| 3 | `N3.prev = new` | `30.prev = 25` |
| 4 | `N2.next = new` | `20.next = 25` |

**Final DLL:** `NULL ← [10] ⇄ [20] ⇄ [25] ⇄ [30] → NULL` ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — DLL Node Anatomy

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Data 10"]
    nodeB["Data 20"]
    nodeC["Data 30"]
    nodeA -- next --> nodeB
    nodeB -- next --> nodeC
    nodeB -- prev --> nodeA
    nodeC -- prev --> nodeB
    nodeX["NULL"]
    nodeY["NULL"]
    nodeA -- prev --> nodeX
    nodeC -- next --> nodeY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The leftward arrows represent `prev` pointers, and the rightward arrows represent `next` pointers. The `NULL` terminators cap both ends of the list.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Insertion at Position $k$

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Start: Insertion at Position k"]
    check1{"k == 1?"}
    insertHead["Call InsertAtHead"]
    traverseA["Traverse to Node P at position k-1"]
    check2{"P == NULL?"}
    errorA["ERROR: Position out of bounds"]
    createA["Allocate Nnew with data"]
    link1["Nnew.next = P.next"]
    check3{"P.next != NULL?"}
    link2["P.next.prev = Nnew"]
    link3["P.next = Nnew"]
    link4["Nnew.prev = P"]
    updateTail{"P.next was NULL?"}
    setTail["TAIL = Nnew"]
    endA["End: Return SUCCESS"]

    startA --> check1
    check1 -- Yes --> insertHead --> endA
    check1 -- No --> traverseA
    traverseA --> check2
    check2 -- Yes --> errorA
    check2 -- No --> createA
    createA --> link1
    link1 --> check3
    check3 -- Yes --> link2 --> link3
    check3 -- No --> link3
    link3 --> link4
    link4 --> updateTail
    updateTail -- Yes --> setTail --> endA
    updateTail -- No --> endA
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Flow — Deletion of Node X

```mermaid
graph TD
    startB["Start: Delete Node X"]
    checkP{"X.prev == NULL?"}
    checkS{"X.next == NULL?"}
    updateHead["HEAD = X.next"]
    updateTail["TAIL = X.prev"]
    linkPrev["X.prev.next = X.next"]
    linkSucc{"X.next != NULL?"}
    linkSuccPrev["X.next.prev = X.prev"]
    dealloc["free X"]
    endB["End: Return SUCCESS"]

    startB --> checkP
    checkP -- Yes --> updateHead
    checkP -- No --> linkPrev
    linkPrev --> checkSucc
    updateHead --> checkS
    checkS -- Yes --> updateTail --> dealloc
    checkS -- No --> linkSucc
    linkSucc -- Yes --> linkSuccPrev --> dealloc
    linkSucc -- No --> dealloc
    dealloc --> endB
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — DLL with HEAD and TAIL

```mermaid
graph LR
    headPtr["HEAD Pointer"]
    tailPtr["TAIL Pointer"]
    n1["Node N1: prev=NULL, data=10, next=N2"]
    n2["Node N2: prev=N1, data=20, next=N3"]
    n3["Node N3: prev=N2, data=30, next=NULL"]

    headPtr -- points to --> n1
    tailPtr -- points to --> n3
    n1 -- next --> n2
    n2 -- next --> n3
    n2 -- prev --> n1
    n3 -- prev --> n2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Doubly Linked List. Mention any two advantages it has over a Singly Linked List.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Doubly Linked List (DLL)** is a linear dynamic data structure in which each node contains three fields: a `data` field, a `next` pointer to the succeeding node, and a `prev` pointer to the preceding node. It allows **bidirectional traversal** of elements.

**Two advantages over SLL:**
1. **Backward Traversal is possible** — In an SLL, traversal is only forward; DLL allows moving from the last node to the first in $O(n)$ time.
2. **Efficient Deletion (O(1) given the node pointer)** — In an SLL, deleting a node (even with its pointer) requires $O(n)$ time to find the predecessor. A DLL maintains a `prev` pointer, allowing direct $O(1)$ deletion.
3. *(Bonus point)* Tail operations are $O(1)$ if a TAIL pointer is maintained.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the fields of a node in a Doubly Linked List. What is the memory overhead of a DLL compared to an SLL?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A DLL node contains:
1. `data` — stores the element value.
2. `next` — pointer to the next node.
3. `prev` — pointer to the previous node.

**Memory Overhead:** A DLL node uses **one extra pointer** compared to an SLL. On a **64-bit system**, this translates to an additional **8 bytes per node**. If the list has $n$ nodes, the total overhead is:

$$
\text{Overhead} = 8n \text{ bytes}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of a Doubly Linked List with a neat diagram. Write the node structure in C. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C algorithm to insert a new node at the **end** of a Doubly Linked List. Explain with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Structure of a DLL:**

A DLL consists of a sequence of nodes connected in *both* directions. The first node is pointed to by a `HEAD` pointer, and the last node is pointed to by a `TAIL` pointer.

**Node structure in C (2 Marks):**

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* prev;
    struct Node* next;
};
```

**Neat diagram (2 Marks):**

```
HEAD                                          TAIL
 |                                             |
 v                                             v
[NULL | 10 | *]-->[* | 20 | *]-->[* | 30 | NULL]
  ^prev           ^prev  ^next    ^next
                  |_______________|
                       pointer
```

**Explanation (3 Marks):**
- Each node has **three parts**: a data field and two pointers (`prev` and `next`).
- The `prev` pointer of the HEAD node is `NULL`, and the `next` pointer of the TAIL node is `NULL`.
- This structure allows traversal in **both directions**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Algorithm: Insertion at End of DLL (5 Marks):**

```c
void insertAtEnd(struct Node** head, struct Node** tail, int value) {
    // Step 1: Allocate new node
    struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;   // New node will be the last
    
    // Step 2: If list is empty, set HEAD and TAIL
    if (*head == NULL) {
        newNode->prev = NULL;
        *head = newNode;
        *tail = newNode;
        return;
    }
    
    // Step 3: Link new node after current TAIL
    (*tail)->next = newNode;
    newNode->prev = *tail;
    
    // Step 4: Update TAIL
    *tail = newNode;
}
```

**Worked Example (2 Marks):**

Initial DLL: `NULL ← [10] ⇄ [20] → NULL` (HEAD = N1, TAIL = N2)

Insert `30` at the end:

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `newNode = (30, prev=?, next=NULL)` | Allocate node |
| 2 | `TAIL->next = newNode` (i.e., N2->next = new) | N2 → new |
| 3 | `newNode->prev = TAIL` (i.e., new->prev = N2) | N2 ← new |
| 4 | `TAIL = newNode` | Update TAIL pointer |

**Final DLL:** `NULL ← [10] ⇄ [20] ⇄ [30] → NULL` with HEAD = N1, TAIL = N3 ✓

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **deletion** operation in a Doubly Linked List. How is it different from deletion in a Singly Linked List? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C algorithm to **delete a node from the beginning** of a Doubly Linked List. Trace it for the list `10 ⇄ 20 ⇄ 30`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram of Deletion (3 Marks):**

```
BEFORE Deletion of Node 20:
HEAD
 |
 v
[NULL|10|*]-->[*|20|*]-->[*|30|NULL]
          |        |
          |<prev   |prev>
                    

AFTER Deletion of Node 20:
HEAD
 |
 v
[NULL|10|*]-->[*|30|NULL]
```

**Steps in DLL Deletion (3 Marks):**

1. Let the node to be deleted be $X$ with predecessor $P$ and successor $S$.
2. **Step 1:** If $P$ exists, set $P.\text{next} = S$. Else, update `HEAD = S`.
3. **Step 2:** If $S$ exists, set $S.\text{prev} = P$. Else, update `TAIL = P`.
4. **Step 3:** `free(X)`.

**Difference from SLL (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | DLL | SLL |
|---|---|---|
| Predecessor Access | $O(1)$ via `prev` pointer | $O(n)$ — must traverse |
| Total Time for Deletion (given node pointer) | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Backward Re-linking | Supported | Not possible |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Algorithm (4 Marks):**

```c
void deleteAtBeginning(struct Node** head, struct Node** tail) {
    if (*head == NULL) {
        printf("List is empty\n");
        return;
    }
    
    struct Node* temp = *head;
    
    // Step 1: Move HEAD to the next node
    *head = (*head)->next;
    
    // Step 2: If new HEAD is not NULL, set its prev to NULL
    if (*head != NULL) {
        (*head)->prev = NULL;
    } else {
        // List became empty
        *tail = NULL;
    }
    
    // Step 3: Free the old HEAD
    free(temp);
}
```

**Trace for `10 ⇄ 20 ⇄ 30` (3 Marks):**

| Step | Action | State |
|---|---|---|
| Initial | — | `HEAD → 10 ⇄ 20 ⇄ 30 ← TAIL` |
| 1 | `temp = HEAD` (temp points to 10) | `temp → 10 ⇄ 20 ⇄ 30` |
| 2 | `HEAD = HEAD->next` (HEAD now points to 20) | `HEAD → 20 ⇄ 30 ← TAIL` |
| 3 | `HEAD->prev = NULL` (20's prev is now NULL) | `HEAD(20.prev=NULL) ⇄ 30` |
| 4 | `free(temp)` (deallocate node 10) | `HEAD → 20 ⇄ 30 ← TAIL` |

**Final List:** `NULL ← [20] ⇄ [30] → NULL` ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update TAIL** when deleting the last node — leads to a dangling TAIL pointer. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Re-linking in the wrong order** (overwriting `X->next` before saving it) — chain breakage. **(-3 marks)**
> 3. **Not handling the empty-list case** (`head == NULL`) — segmentation fault in viva. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Confusing 1-indexed vs 0-indexed positions** in insertion — wrong node placement. **(-2 marks)**
> 5. **Failing to mention time complexity** in written exams — KTU 2024 scheme requires explicit complexity analysis. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Doubly Linked List (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Linear data structure with **bidirectional links** — each node has `data`, `next`, and `prev`.
- **Node Count Formula:** For a list with $n$ nodes, total pointer fields = $2n$ (forward + backward).
- **Time Complexities to Memorize:**
    * Insert at Head/Tail: $O(1)$
    * Delete at Head/Tail: $O(1)$
    * Delete given node pointer: $O(1)$ ← *killer feature vs SLL*
    * Random access: $O(n)$
- **Space Overhead:** One extra pointer per node (**8 bytes on 64-bit**).
- **Critical Re-linking Order:** When inserting, set `new.next` **before** `prev_node.next` to avoid losing the chain.
- **Boundary Cases:** Always check for `head == NULL` (empty list) and tail/head updates when deleting the first/last node.
- **Real-World Use Cases:** Browser back/forward, LRU cache, undo/redo, music playlists, OS process lists.
- **SLL vs DLL Trade-off:** DLL trades **memory** (extra pointer) for **time efficiency** (especially in deletion).
- **HEAD & TAIL Pointers:** Maintaining both makes head AND tail operations $O(1)$.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a *before-and-after* diagram in your answer sheet — KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** just for a clear diagram.
- **Common Viva Question:** *"Why is DLL deletion O(1) but SLL deletion is O(n)?"* — Answer: Because DLL stores a `prev` pointer, eliminating the need to traverse for the predecessor.
- **Memory Leak Warning:** Always `free()` deleted nodes; otherwise the program will exhaust heap memory.
- **NULL Termination:** HEAD's `prev = NULL` and TAIL's `next = NULL` — these are the two invariant terminators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Linked List
Stacks and Queues using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stacks and Queues using Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stack — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Stack** is a linear, dynamic Abstract Data Type (ADT) that follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** discipline, in which insertion and deletion of elements occur at one end called the **TOP**.

When implemented using a **Linked List**, the stack becomes a **dynamic, pointer-based structure** where each node holds two fields: a `data` field and a `next` pointer. The TOP of the stack is tracked by a single external pointer (often called `top` or `head`).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Spring-Loaded Plate Dispenser

Imagine a vertical tube holding a stack of plates, fitted with a heavy spring at the bottom. When you push a new plate in from the top, it rests on the previous one. When someone needs a plate, they always take it from the top — never from the bottom or middle. The **most recently pushed plate is the first one removed**. This is exactly how a linked-list stack behaves: the **head of the linked list acts as the TOP**, because inserting/deleting at the head of a singly linked list is an **O(1)** operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In a linked-list stack, `push` is implemented by **inserting at the head**, and `pop` is implemented by **deleting at the head**. This is the opposite of intuition with arrays (where top grows toward higher indices) but yields identical LIFO behaviour.

## 1.2 Queue — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Queue (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Queue** is a linear, dynamic Abstract Data Type (ADT) that follows the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** discipline, where insertion occurs at the **REAR** and deletion occurs at the **FRONT**.

When implemented using a **Linked List**, the queue is governed by **two external pointers** — `front` (for deletions) and `rear` (for insertions). This dual-pointer design is what makes linked-list queues superior to naive array-based queues: there is **no need to shift elements or wrap around** to maintain O(1) enqueue and dequeue.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cinema Ticket Counter

Picture a queue of people waiting at a cinema ticket counter. The person who **arrives first** (joins at the **rear**) is the one who **gets served first** (leaves from the **front**). Newcomers always join at the back; service is always rendered to the person at the front. In a linked-list queue, the `rear` pointer is where new nodes are attached, and the `front` pointer is where nodes are detached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Always maintain **both `front` and `rear`** pointers. Setting `rear = NULL` alongside `front = NULL` signals an **empty queue**, and this must be handled explicitly in the `dequeue` operation to prevent dangling-pointer errors.

## 1.3 The Node — The Building Block

The basic unit of both structures is the **node**:

$$
\text{Node} = \text{struct Node} \{ \quad \text{int data;} \quad \text{struct Node* next;} \quad \}
$$

- The `data` field stores the actual value (or a pointer to it).
- The `next` field stores the **memory address** of the next node, or `NULL` if it is the last node.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Footprint:** Each node dynamically consumes **sizeof(Node) = sizeof(int) + sizeof(pointer) + padding** bytes (typically **16 bytes** on a 64-bit system). Unlike arrays, this memory is drawn from the **heap**, so it grows and shrinks exactly with the number of elements — no wastage, no overflow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stack using Linked List — Operational Breakdown

The stack ADT implemented over a linked list is governed by **four core operations**: `push`, `pop`, `peek`, and `isEmpty`. Each is examined below in terms of *why* and *how*.

### 2.1.1 `push(top, x)` — Insertion at TOP
- **Why at the head?** Because the head is the only place where insertion is O(1) in a singly linked list. Inserting at the tail would require traversal — O(n).
- **How:**
  1. Allocate a new node using `malloc`.
  2. Store `x` in `newNode->data`.
  3. Set `newNode->next = top` (new node now points to the previous top).
  4. Update `top = newNode` (new node becomes the top).

### 2.1.2 `pop(top)` — Deletion at TOP
- **Why no traversal?** Because the TOP *is* the head — direct access in O(1).
- **How:**
  1. If `top == NULL` → **Stack Underflow**, abort.
  2. Store `temp = top` (save the node to free later).
  3. Move `top = top->next`.
  4. Save `poppedValue = temp->data`.
  5. Free `temp` to return memory to the heap.

### 2.1.3 `peek(top)` — Read TOP without deletion
- Returns `top->data` if stack is non-empty, else reports "Stack Empty".

### 2.1.4 `isEmpty(top)` — Boolean state check
- Returns `true` if `top == NULL`, else `false`.

---

## 2.2 Queue using Linked List — Operational Breakdown

The queue ADT requires **two external pointers** — `front` and `rear` — for O(1) operations at both ends.

### 2.2.1 `enqueue(rear, front, x)` — Insertion at REAR
- **Why at the rear pointer?** The rear pointer gives direct O(1) access to the last node.
- **How:**
  1. Allocate a new node.
  2. Set `newNode->data = x`, `newNode->next = NULL`.
  3. If queue is empty → set `front = rear = newNode`.
  4. Else → set `rear->next = newNode`, then `rear = newNode`.

### 2.2.2 `dequeue(front, rear)` — Deletion at FRONT
- **How:**
  1. If `front == NULL` → **Queue Underflow**, abort.
  2. Save `temp = front` and `dequeuedValue = temp->data`.
  3. Move `front = front->next`.
  4. **If `front == NULL` after move** → set `rear = NULL` (queue became empty).
  5. Free `temp`.

### 2.2.3 `isEmpty(front)` — Boolean state check
- Returns `true` if `front == NULL` (and consequently `rear == NULL`).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as a **last-minute revision anchor** before stepping into the exam hall. Note the consistent **O(1) time complexity** for all primary operations.

| Operation | Data Structure | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `push` | Stack (LL) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ extra | None (no overflow) |
| `pop` | Stack (LL) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ extra | `top == NULL` → Underflow |
| `peek` | Stack (LL) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ extra | `top == NULL` → Empty |
| `isEmpty` | Stack (LL) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Returns `true` if `top = NULL` |
| `enqueue` | Queue (LL) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ extra | None (no overflow) |
| `dequeue` | Queue (LL) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ extra | `front == NULL` → Underflow |
| `getFront` | Queue (LL) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | `front == NULL` → Empty |
| `n` elements | Both (LL) | — | $O(n)$ total | Heap allocation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Function call stack and process scheduling queues are built on linked-list–based dynamic structures.
- **Memory Management:** Allocators use **free-list stacks** to recycle freed memory blocks.
- **Network Routers:** **Packet queues** (FIFO) are implemented as linked lists to handle variable-length bursts.
- **Compilers:** **Symbol tables** and **expression evaluation stacks** are often linked-list based for dynamic resizing.
- **Undo/Redo Systems:** A stack of states, each dynamically allocated.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Rule of Thumb:** For a stack of `n` elements, the total memory consumed is `n × (sizeof(int) + sizeof(pointer) + padding)`. For 64-bit systems, this is roughly **16n bytes**. There is no upper bound (no overflow), but each `pop` must be paired with a `free` to prevent **memory leaks** — a frequent KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Pseudocode & Analytical Trace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Trace Mandate:** Every operation below is traced **step-by-step** with explicit pointer movements. Read each `NULL`-check carefully — these are the most heavily tested KTU corner cases.

## 3.1 Stack Implementation — Full Pseudocode with Memory Trace

### 3.1.1 PUSH Operation (Insert at head)

```
Algorithm: PUSH(top, item)
Input: top — pointer to current top of stack
       item — integer value to be pushed
Output: Updated top pointer
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{newNode} \leftarrow \text{malloc(sizeof(Node))} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{newNode}\rightarrow \text{data} \leftarrow \text{item} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{newNode}\rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{top} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{top} \leftarrow \text{newNode} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{return top}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:**
- **Step 1** reserves heap memory. Failure to check `malloc == NULL` is a common viva deduction.
- **Step 2** stores the value into the data field of the new node.
- **Step 3** is the **critical link reversal**: the new node's `next` now points to the *previous* top, making the new node the head.
- **Step 4** moves the external `top` pointer to the new node — the stack's TOP has shifted.

### 3.1.2 POP Operation (Delete from head)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \textbf{if } (\text{top} = \text{NULL}) \textbf{ then return UNDERFLOW} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{temp} \leftarrow \text{top} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{value} \leftarrow \text{top}\rightarrow \text{data} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{top} \leftarrow \text{top}\rightarrow \text{next} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{free(temp)} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{return value}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:**
- **Step 1** is the **mandatory empty-check**. Omitting it causes a segmentation fault — the most common KTU practical-lab mistake.
- **Steps 2–3** preserve the data of the node being removed.
- **Step 4** unlinks the head by moving `top` to its successor.
- **Step 5** returns the memory to the heap — failure to do so causes a **memory leak**.

### 3.1.3 Analytical Trace — PUSH(10), PUSH(20), POP, PUSH(30), POP, PUSH(40)

| Step | Operation | Linked List State (left → right = top → bottom) | `top` points to | Returned |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | Initial | (empty) | `NULL` | — |
| 1 | PUSH(10) | 10 → NULL | Node(10) | — |
| 2 | PUSH(20) | 20 → 10 → NULL | Node(20) | — |
| 3 | POP | 10 → NULL | Node(10) | 20 |
| 4 | PUSH(30) | 30 → 10 → NULL | Node(30) | — |
| 5 | POP | 10 → NULL | Node(10) | 30 |
| 6 | PUSH(40) | 40 → 10 → NULL | Node(40) | — |

**Final Stack State:** `top → 40 → 10 → NULL`

---

## 3.2 Queue Implementation — Full Pseudocode with Memory Trace

### 3.2.1 ENQUEUE Operation (Insert at rear)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{newNode} \leftarrow \text{malloc(sizeof(Node))} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{newNode}\rightarrow \text{data} \leftarrow \text{item} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{newNode}\rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{NULL} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \textbf{if } (\text{front} = \text{NULL}) \textbf{ then} \\
& \quad \text{front} \leftarrow \text{rear} \leftarrow \text{newNode} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \textbf{else} \\
& \quad \text{rear}\rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{newNode} \\
& \quad \text{rear} \leftarrow \text{newNode}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:**
- **Step 4** is the **first-element special case**: if the queue is empty, *both* `front` and `rear` must point to the new node. Forgetting to set `front` here is a classic 2-mark deduction.
- **Step 5** is the **general case**: the old rear's `next` is updated, then `rear` itself moves forward.

### 3.2.2 DEQUEUE Operation (Delete from front)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \textbf{if } (\text{front} = \text{NULL}) \textbf{ then return UNDERFLOW} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{temp} \leftarrow \text{front} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{value} \leftarrow \text{front}\rightarrow \text{data} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{front} \leftarrow \text{front}\rightarrow \text{next} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \textbf{if } (\text{front} = \text{NULL}) \textbf{ then rear} \leftarrow \text{NULL} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{free(temp)} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{return value}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:**
- **Step 5** is the **last-element special case**: when the queue becomes empty after a dequeue, `rear` *must* also be reset to `NULL`. Otherwise, `rear` becomes a **dangling pointer** pointing to freed memory — a guaranteed crash on the next enqueue.

### 3.2.3 Analytical Trace — ENQUEUE(5), ENQUEUE(10), DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE(15), DEQUEUE

| Step | Operation | Queue State (front → rear) | `front` | `rear` | Returned |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | Initial | (empty) | `NULL` | `NULL` | — |
| 1 | ENQUEUE(5) | 5 | Node(5) | Node(5) | — |
| 2 | ENQUEUE(10) | 5 → 10 | Node(5) | Node(10) | — |
| 3 | DEQUEUE | 10 | Node(10) | Node(10) | 5 |
| 4 | ENQUEUE(15) | 10 → 15 | Node(10) | Node(15) | — |
| 5 | DEQUEUE | 15 | Node(15) | Node(15) | 10 |

**Final Queue State:** `front → 15 ← rear`

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis: Array vs. Linked List Implementation

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Memorize the table and the rationale behind each row.

| Criterion | Array Implementation | Linked List Implementation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Size** | **Fixed at compile-time** (static) or runtime via realloc | **Dynamic**, grows one node at a time |
| **Overflow** | Possible if `top == MAX - 1` | **Impossible** (limited only by heap) |
| **Memory Wastage** | Possible (unused slots in static array) | **None** — exactly `n` nodes allocated |
| **Time per op** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Extra memory per element** | 0 (contiguous) | **8 bytes** for `next` pointer (64-bit) |
| **Cache locality** | **Excellent** (contiguous) | **Poor** (scattered across heap) |
| **Code complexity** | Lower | Higher (pointer manipulation) |

**Why linked lists win for unbounded growth:** Imagine a server logging events. With an array of size 1000, the 1001st event crashes the system. With a linked-list stack, the system simply allocates more heap — the only practical limit is **physical RAM**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Guide:** Diagrams use **arrows → from pointer to pointee**. A `NULL` arrow indicates the **end of the list**. The `top`/`front`/`rear` are external pointers stored separately from the heap-allocated nodes.

## 4.1 Stack as a Linked List — Static Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    topPtr[TOP pointer]:::externalPtr --> nodeA[Node A: data=40, next]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Node B: data=30, next]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Node C: data=20, next]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Node D: data=10, next=NULL]

    classDef externalPtr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `TOP` is the **only external pointer** — it points to the *most recently pushed* element.
- Insertion and deletion **both happen at the head** (Node A in this case).
- The `next` field of the last node (Node D) is `NULL` — this is the **bottom** of the stack.

---

## 4.2 Queue as a Linked List — Static Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    frontPtr[FRONT pointer]:::externalPtr --> nodeQ1[Node Q1: data=10, next]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2[Node Q2: data=20, next]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3[Node Q3: data=30, next=NULL]
    nodeQ3 -.-> rearPtr[REAR pointer]:::externalPtr

    classDef externalPtr fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Two external pointers** govern the queue: `FRONT` (left) and `REAR` (right, shown with a dashed arrow because it points *to* the last node, not *from* it).
- `FRONT` is the **deletion end** (oldest element), `REAR` is the **insertion end** (newest element).
- The dashed arrow from `Node Q3` to `REAR pointer` indicates that the *node's* last position is what `REAR` tracks.

---

## 4.3 Push Operation — Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: PUSH top, x]) --> chkMalloc{newNode = malloc success?}
    chkMalloc -- No --> errAlloc[Return memory error]
    chkMalloc -- Yes --> setData[newNode.data = x]
    setData --> linkNext[newNode.next = top]
    linkNext --> updTop[top = newNode]
    updTop --> endA([Return updated top])

    style startA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style endA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style errAlloc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
```

**Why this flow?** The malloc check is **defensive programming** — a real-world embedded system may have exhausted heap. The link reversal `newNode.next = top` is what makes the new node the *head*.

---

## 4.4 Enqueue Operation — Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: ENQUEUE front, rear, x]) --> alloc[newNode = malloc, data = x, next = NULL]
    alloc --> chkEmpty{front == NULL?}
    chkEmpty -- Yes empty --> setBoth[front = rear = newNode]
    chkEmpty -- No non-empty --> linkRear[rear.next = newNode]
    linkRear --> moveRear[rear = newNode]
    setBoth --> endB([Return])
    moveRear --> endB

    style startB fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B
    style endB fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B
    style chkEmpty fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B
```

**The empty-check branch is the lifeline:** without it, dereferencing `rear->next` on an empty queue would crash the program.

---

## 4.5 Dequeue Operation — Flow Block Diagram (with the dangling-pointer trap)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
