```mermaid
    startC([Start: DEQUEUE front, rear]) --> chkFront{front == NULL?}
    chkFront -- Yes --> underflow[Return UNDERFLOW error]
    chkFront -- No --> grab[value = front.data, temp = front]
    grab --> advance[front = front.next]
    advance --> chkBecameEmpty{front == NULL after move?}
    chkBecameEmpty -- Yes --> fixRear[rear = NULL, CRITICAL]
    chkBecameEmpty -- No --> freeOp[free temp]
    fixRear --> freeOp
    freeOp --> endC([Return value])

    style startC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style endC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style fixRear fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style underflow fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
```

> [!WARNING]
> **The Dangling-Pointer Trap:** Skipping the `rear = NULL` reset (highlighted in red) is the **#1 reason KTU students lose marks** in C practical exams. After dequeueing the last element, `rear` still points to the *freed* memory — the next `enqueue` will write to garbage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define a Stack ADT. List the four primary operations performed on a stack and state the time complexity of each when implemented using a linked list.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack ADT (1 mark):** A Stack is a linear Abstract Data Type that follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** principle. The insertion and deletion of elements happen at one end, called the **TOP**.

**Four primary operations (1 mark each, partial credit possible):**

| Operation | Description | Time Complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| `push(item)` | Inserts an item at the TOP | $O(1)$ |
| `pop()` | Removes and returns the TOP item | $O(1)$ |
| `peek()` / `top()` | Returns the TOP item without removing it | $O(1)$ |
| `isEmpty()` | Returns true if stack has no elements | $O(1)$ |

All operations are $O(1)$ because the linked list's head pointer gives **direct, constant-time access** to the top of the stack.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**In a queue implemented using a linked list, why must we maintain both `front` and `rear` pointers? What happens if `rear` is not reset to `NULL` after the queue becomes empty?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Why both pointers are needed (2 marks):** A queue requires **insertion at one end (rear)** and **deletion at the other end (front)**. The `front` pointer provides **O(1) access** to the element to be dequeued, and the `rear` pointer provides **O(1) access** to the position where new elements are enqueued. Without `rear`, enqueue would require **O(n) traversal** to the last node, defeating the purpose of a linked list.

**Consequence of not resetting `rear` (1 mark):** If the queue becomes empty (i.e., the last element is dequeued and `front` becomes `NULL`) but `rear` is **not** reset to `NULL`, then `rear` becomes a **dangling pointer** pointing to freed heap memory. The next `enqueue` operation will attempt to dereference this dangling pointer via `rear->next = newNode`, leading to **undefined behaviour, segmentation fault, or memory corruption**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, has an **internal choice** (OR), and typically splits into two 7-mark sub-parts. Solve both sub-parts to secure full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Stack Focus

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Style]
**Explain the implementation of a stack using a singly linked list. Write the algorithms for `PUSH` and `POP` operations, and discuss how memory is managed.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Node Structure (1 mark):**
```
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* next;
};
```
A separate external pointer `top` is maintained, initially set to `NULL`.

**2. PUSH Algorithm (3 marks):**
```
PUSH(top, item):
    newNode = (struct Node*) malloc(sizeof(struct Node))
    IF newNode == NULL THEN
        PRINT "Memory allocation failed"
        RETURN
    END IF
    newNode->data = item
    newNode->next = top       // link to old top
    top = newNode             // update top
END PUSH
```

**3. POP Algorithm (3 marks):**
```
POP(top):
    IF top == NULL THEN
        PRINT "Stack Underflow"
        RETURN -1
    END IF
    temp = top
    value = top->data
    top = top->next
    free(temp)               // release heap memory
    RETURN value
END POP
```

**Memory Management Discussion:** Each `PUSH` allocates one node from the heap; each `POP` must `free` the node it removes. The total memory at any time equals `n × sizeof(Node)`. Since memory is dynamically allocated, the stack **never overflows** as long as heap space is available — a key advantage over static array implementation.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**A stack implemented using a linked list is initially empty. Perform the following sequence of operations and show the state of the linked list after each step. Also, indicate the value returned by each `POP` or `PEEK` operation.**

`PUSH(15), PUSH(28), PUSH(7), POP, PUSH(42), PEEK, POP, POP`

**Model Solution Trace (7 Marks):**

| Step | Operation | Stack State (top → bottom) | `top` | Returned Value |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | (initial) | NULL | `NULL` | — |
| 1 | PUSH(15) | 15 → NULL | Node(15) | — |
| 2 | PUSH(28) | 28 → 15 → NULL | Node(28) | — |
| 3 | PUSH(7)  | 7 → 28 → 15 → NULL | Node(7) | — |
| 4 | POP      | 28 → 15 → NULL | Node(28) | **7** |
| 5 | PUSH(42) | 42 → 28 → 15 → NULL | Node(42) | — |
| 6 | PEEK     | 42 → 28 → 15 → NULL | Node(42) | **42** |
| 7 | POP      | 28 → 15 → NULL | Node(28) | **42** |
| 8 | POP      | 15 → NULL | Node(15) | **28** |

**Final Stack State:** `top → 15 → NULL` (one element remaining, no further pop allowed without underflow).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Queue Focus

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]
**Explain the implementation of a linear queue using a singly linked list. Write the algorithms for `ENQUEUE` and `DEQUEUE` operations with full pointer management.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Node Structure and Pointers (1 mark):**
```
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* next;
};
```
Two external pointers: `front` (deletion end) and `rear` (insertion end), both initialized to `NULL`.

**2. ENQUEUE Algorithm (3 marks):**
```
ENQUEUE(front, rear, item):
    newNode = (struct Node*) malloc(sizeof(struct Node))
    newNode->data = item
    newNode->next = NULL
    IF front == NULL THEN            // empty queue
        front = rear = newNode
    ELSE
        rear->next = newNode
        rear = newNode
    END IF
END ENQUEUE
```

**3. DEQUEUE Algorithm (3 marks):**
```
DEQUEUE(front, rear):
    IF front == NULL THEN
        PRINT "Queue Underflow"
        RETURN -1
    END IF
    temp = front
    value = front->data
    front = front->next
    IF front == NULL THEN            // last element removed
        rear = NULL
    END IF
    free(temp)
    RETURN value
END DEQUEUE
```

The empty-queue check in `ENQUEUE` and the `rear = NULL` reset in `DEQUEUE` are the **two critical special cases** that distinguish a correct implementation from a buggy one.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**A queue implemented using a linked list is initially empty. Trace the following operations and show the queue state (front → rear) after each step:**

`ENQUEUE(11), ENQUEUE(22), DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE(33), ENQUEUE(44), DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE(55)`

**Model Solution Trace (7 Marks):**

| Step | Operation | Queue State (front → rear) | `front` | `rear` | Returned |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | (initial) | (empty) | `NULL` | `NULL` | — |
| 1 | ENQUEUE(11) | 11 | Node(11) | Node(11) | — |
| 2 | ENQUEUE(22) | 11 → 22 | Node(11) | Node(22) | — |
| 3 | DEQUEUE     | 22 | Node(22) | Node(22) | **11** |
| 4 | ENQUEUE(33) | 22 → 33 | Node(22) | Node(33) | — |
| 5 | ENQUEUE(44) | 22 → 33 → 44 | Node(22) | Node(44) | — |
| 6 | DEQUEUE     | 33 → 44 | Node(33) | Node(44) | **22** |
| 7 | ENQUEUE(55) | 33 → 44 → 55 | Node(33) | Node(55) | — |

**Final Queue State:** `front → 33 → 44 → 55 ← rear`

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Before You Write the Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the empty-stack/queue check before POP/DEQUEUE (-2 marks):** If your algorithm does not test `top == NULL` or `front == NULL` before dereferencing, the examiner will deduct 2 marks instantly. Always begin pop/dequeue with the underflow check.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to update `rear = NULL` after the queue becomes empty (-2 marks):** This is a dangling-pointer bug. The examiner will look for it. If your algorithm correctly resets `rear`, you earn full marks; if not, you lose 2 marks even if the rest is perfect.
>
> 3. **Confusing array-stack semantics with linked-list-stack semantics (-1 mark):** In an array stack, TOP increments; in a linked-list stack, TOP *moves to a new node*. Drawing a linked list with TOP pointing to the *bottom* node is a conceptual error.
>
> 4. **Omitting `free()` in POP/DEQUEUE (-1 mark):** Memory leak. Examiners specifically look for this in the C/Practical syllabus.
>
> 5. **Drawing the linked list with arrows reversed (-1 mark):** The `next` pointer of a node points **forward toward the bottom/rear**, not backward. Arrows should go from the *head* toward the *tail*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Stack = LIFO; Queue = FIFO.** Memorize these two acronyms — they are the foundation of 80% of theory questions.
- [x] **Stack uses ONE external pointer (`top`); Queue uses TWO (`front` and `rear`).** This is the single biggest structural difference.
- [x] **All four primary operations (push, pop, enqueue, dequeue) are $O(1)$** in linked-list implementations.
- [x] **Underflow conditions:** `top == NULL` (stack), `front == NULL` (queue).
- [x] **No overflow possible** in linked-list stacks/queues — heap exhaustion is the only limit.
- [x] **PUSH inserts at the head; POP deletes at the head.** This is the linked-list trick that makes everything $O(1)$.
- [x] **ENQUEUE inserts at `rear`; DEQUEUE deletes at `front`.** Never reverse this — it breaks FIFO.
- [x] **Always reset `rear = NULL` when the queue becomes empty after a dequeue.** Dangling pointer prevention.
- [x] **Always `free()` the removed node in POP/DEQUEUE.** Memory hygiene.
- [x] **Total memory for `n` elements** is `n × (sizeof(int) + sizeof(pointer) + padding)`, i.e., roughly `16n` bytes on a 64-bit system.
- [x] **Trade-off vs. array:** Linked list = dynamic size, no overflow, no wastage, but extra pointer memory and poor cache locality.
- [x] **Time to draw a linked-list stack/queue in the exam = 30 seconds.** Practice the ASCII art: `top → 40 → 30 → 20 → 10 → NULL`.

> **End of Module 2 — Stacks and Queues using Linked List**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polynomial Representation using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polynomial Representation using Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme Data Structures (PEEET526), **Polynomial Representation using a Linked List** is a dynamic, memory-efficient method of storing a polynomial $P(x)$ of degree $n$ in computer memory. Instead of using a fixed-size array of size $(n+1)$ to hold all coefficients (including zeros), each non-zero term of the polynomial is stored as an individual **node** in a **singly linked list** (ordered typically in descending order of exponent). Each node contains exactly **two data fields** — the `coefficient` and the `exponent` (power) — and **one pointer field** (`next`) that links it to the subsequent term.

A general polynomial is mathematically expressed as:

$$P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + a_{n-2} x^{n-2} + \dots + a_1 x^1 + a_0 x^0$$

where each $a_i$ represents the **coefficient** and $i$ represents the **exponent (power)**. In a linked list representation, only the **non-zero terms** $(a_i \neq 0)$ are retained, which dramatically reduces memory consumption for **sparse polynomials**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polynomial representation falls under *Linear Data Structures – Applications of Linked Lists*. Students must know (a) node structure, (b) creation, (c) addition, and (d) evaluation. Multiplication is treated as an extended application.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are writing a shopping list, but instead of writing all 10 items in fixed slots on a paper (an **array**), you write each item on a separate small **sticky note** and glue them together with a string. If you want to insert a new item in the middle, you don't need to erase or shift anything — you just make a new sticky note and adjust the string.

- The **sticky note** = a **Node**
- The **item name and quantity** = **Coefficient and Exponent**
- The **string connecting them** = the **Pointer (next)**

This is precisely what a linked-list-based polynomial does. It is the ideal choice for **sparse polynomials** like $P(x) = 5000x^{5000} + 3x^{12}$ where storing all 5001 coefficients as an array would waste enormous memory, but a linked list needs only **two nodes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not an array?** An array of size $(n+1)$ reserves memory for *every* term from $x^0$ to $x^n$. For $P(x) = 2x^{1000} + 5$, an array wastes 999 slots storing zeros. The linked list stores only **2 nodes** — a massive saving. This is the **sparse representation advantage**.

### 1.3 Standard Node Structure (C-style)

```c
struct PolyNode {
    int coeff;              // Coefficient of the term
    int exp;                // Exponent (power) of the term
    struct PolyNode *next;  // Pointer to the next term
};
```

- **Memory per node:** Depends on the system architecture; on a **32-bit system** with two `int` and one pointer, it is **12 bytes**; on **64-bit systems** it becomes **16 bytes** (plus padding). The size in KTU board questions is conventionally assumed as: `sizeof(int) = 2 bytes`, `sizeof(pointer) = 2 bytes`, so each node is **6 bytes**.
- **Header pointer:** A separate pointer `head` (or `poly`) points to the first node. If `head == NULL`, the polynomial is **zero**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to draw a polynomial linked list, always draw a separate **HEAD** box (a rectangular block, not a circle) and an arrow from `HEAD` to the first node. Nodes are drawn as two-part circles or two-box rectangles to clearly distinguish `coeff` and `exp`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic of a Polynomial Linked List

The polynomial linked list follows an **ordered, descending sequence** based on the exponent value. The general operating rules are:

1. **Ordering Rule:** Nodes are always arranged such that $\text{exp}_1 > \text{exp}_2 > \text{exp}_3 > \dots > \text{exp}_k$. This descending order makes traversal, addition, and comparison operations **linear-time $O(n + m)$** instead of quadratic.
2. **Uniqueness Rule:** No two nodes in a single polynomial share the same exponent. If a term is inserted with a duplicate exponent, it must be **merged** by adding coefficients.
3. **Null Polynomial:** When all coefficients are zero, the list is empty (`head = NULL`), denoted as $P(x) = 0$.
4. **Trailing Pointer:** A `tail` pointer is often maintained to allow **$O(1)$ insertion at the end** during creation.

### 2.2 Standard Operations and Their Purpose

| Operation | Purpose | Why It Matters |
|-----------|---------|----------------|
| **Create Polynomial** | Reads terms and builds the list dynamically | Foundation for all other operations |
| **Display Polynomial** | Traverses and prints in human-readable form | Used in debugging and final output |
| **Add Two Polynomials** | Merges two sorted lists to produce a new sum | Core application in symbolic computation |
| **Subtract Polynomials** | Adds the additive inverse of the second polynomial | Subset of addition logic |
| **Multiply Polynomials** | Performs pairwise multiplication of every term of P1 with P2 | Time-intensive: $O(n \cdot m)$ |
| **Evaluate Polynomial** | Computes $P(x_0)$ for a given value $x_0$ using Horner's rule | Used in numerical methods |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every formula, boundary condition, and time-complexity metric required for KTU 2024 ESE and internal exams on this topic. All symbols use `\vert` for absolute value to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Concept / Symbol | Mathematical Expression / Definition | Unit / Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| General Polynomial | $P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i x^i$ | Degree $n \in \mathbb{N}$ | Compact mathematical form |
| Number of Nodes | $k$ = count of non-zero terms | $k \leq n+1$ | Determines list length |
| Memory per Node (KTU default) | $2 \times \text{sizeof(int)} + \text{sizeof(pointer)}$ | **6 bytes** (16-bit) | Memory calculation |
| Polynomial Evaluation (Naive) | $P(x_0) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i \cdot x_0^{\,i}$ | Time $O(k \cdot n)$ | Direct substitution |
| Horner's Rule | $P(x_0) = (\dots((a_n x_0 + a_{n-1})x_0 + a_{n-2})x_0 + \dots) x_0 + a_0$ | Time $O(k)$ | Efficient evaluation |
| Addition Complexity | Traverse both lists once | $O(n + m)$ | Where $n, m$ are list lengths |
| Multiplication Complexity | Every term of P1 with every term of P2 | $O(n \cdot m)$ | Most expensive operation |
| Insert at End (with tail) | Move tail and link new node | $O(1)$ | During creation |
| Insert at End (no tail) | Traverse to last node | $O(k)$ | Without auxiliary pointer |
| Sparse Polynomial | $k \ll n+1$ | Many zeros skipped | Memory-efficient |
| Zero Polynomial | $P(x) = 0$ | `head = NULL` | Boundary case |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Polynomial linked lists are not merely academic constructs. They find direct application in:

- **Computer Graphics:** Representing Bezier and B-spline curves where control points translate to polynomial terms.
- **Signal Processing:** FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filter coefficients, often very sparse.
- **Scientific Computing:** Symbolic algebra systems like Mathematica and SymPy internally use list-based sparse representations.
- **Compiler Design:** Polynomial time complexity is fundamental in asymptotic analysis; the linked list representation is used in teaching asymptotic $O$-notation.
- **Cryptography:** Polynomial arithmetic over finite fields forms the basis of NTRU and lattice-based post-quantum cryptography.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Algorithm: Addition of Two Polynomials (Detailed Derivation)

Let $P_1(x)$ have terms $(a_i, e_i)$ and $P_2(x)$ have terms $(b_j, f_j)$. Both lists are sorted in **descending order of exponent**. The goal is to construct $P_3(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$.

**Logical Steps of the Algorithm (Two-Pointer Merge Technique):**

1. **Initialize** three pointers: `p1 = head1`, `p2 = head2`, and `head3 = NULL`. Also initialize `tail3 = NULL` to track the last node of the result.
2. **Loop** while `p1 != NULL` **AND** `p2 != NULL`. Compare the exponents of the current nodes.
3. **Case A — Exponents are equal** ($\text{exp}_1 = \text{exp}_2$):
   - Compute sum of coefficients: $c = a_i + b_j$.
   - If $c \neq 0$, create a new node with `(coeff = c, exp = exp1)` and append to result list.
   - Advance **both** `p1` and `p2` to their next nodes.
4. **Case B — Exponent of P1 is larger** ($\text{exp}_1 > \text{exp}_2$):
   - Copy `(coeff = a_i, exp = e_i)` to a new node and append to result.
   - Advance **only** `p1`.
5. **Case C — Exponent of P2 is larger** ($\text{exp}_2 > \text{exp}_1$):
   - Copy `(coeff = b_j, exp = f_j)` to a new node and append to result.
   - Advance **only** `p2`.
6. **After loop termination**, append all remaining nodes of the non-NULL list to the result.
7. **Return** `head3` as the resultant polynomial.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Polynomial Addition

**Given:**
$$P_1(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7, \quad P_2(x) = 4x^4 + 2x^3 + 9x^2 + 1$$

**Step 1 — Initial State:**

Both `p1` and `p2` point to the head of their respective lists. `head3 = NULL`, `tail3 = NULL`.

| Step | p1 → (coeff, exp) | p2 → (coeff, exp) | Comparison | Action | Result List |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | (5, 4) | (4, 4) | exp equal | c = 5+4 = 9; new node (9, 4) | 9x⁴ |
| 2 | (3, 2) | (2, 3) | exp of p2 > exp of p1 | copy (2, 3) | 9x⁴ + 2x³ |
| 3 | (3, 2) | (9, 2) | exp equal | c = 3+9 = 12; new node (12, 2) | 9x⁴ + 2x³ + 12x² |
| 4 | (7, 0) | (1, 0) | exp equal | c = 7+1 = 8; new node (8, 0) | 9x⁴ + 2x³ + 12x² + 8 |
| 5 | NULL | NULL | Loop ends | Done | Final result |

**Final Result:**

$$P_3(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x) = 9x^4 + 2x^3 + 12x^2 + 8$$

### 3.3 Algorithm: Polynomial Multiplication (Naive Pairwise Method)

For $P_1$ with $n$ terms and $P_2$ with $m$ terms, multiplication requires nested traversal:

**Step-by-step derivation:**

1. For each term $(a_i, e_i)$ in $P_1$:
2. For each term $(b_j, f_j)$ in $P_2$:
3. Compute the product term: new coefficient $c = a_i \cdot b_j$ and new exponent $e = e_i + f_j$.
4. Insert this term into the **result polynomial** $P_3$, **merging** with any existing node that has the same exponent (by adding coefficients).

**Mathematical Form:**

$$P_3(x) = P_1(x) \times P_2(x) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x^{e_i} \right) \times \left( \sum_{j=1}^{m} b_j x^{f_j} \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} a_i b_j \, x^{e_i + f_j}$$

**Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot m)$ for generation of all pairwise products, plus $O(k \cdot (n \cdot m))$ for insertion/merge if not optimized.

### 3.4 Algorithm: Polynomial Evaluation Using Horner's Rule

**Naive form:**

$$P(x_0) = a_n \cdot x_0^{\,n} + a_{n-1} \cdot x_0^{\,n-1} + \dots + a_1 \cdot x_0 + a_0$$

This requires $k$ exponentiations and $k$ multiplications per term. For $k$ terms, total cost is $O(k \cdot n)$ where $n$ is the maximum exponent.

**Horner's Nested Form (Transformation):**

$$P(x_0) = (\dots((a_n x_0 + a_{n-1}) x_0 + a_{n-2}) x_0 + \dots + a_1) x_0 + a_0$$

**Step-by-step derivation for $P(x) = 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 6x + 7$ evaluated at $x_0 = 2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(2) &= (\dots((4)(2) + 5)(2) + 6)(2) + 7 \\
&= ((8 + 5)(2) + 6)(2) + 7 \\
&= ((13)(2) + 6)(2) + 7 \\
&= ((26 + 6)(2) + 7) \\
&= ((32)(2) + 7) \\
&= (64 + 7) \\
&= 71
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification using naive method:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(2) &= 4(2)^3 + 5(2)^2 + 6(2) + 7 \\
&= 4(8) + 5(4) + 12 + 7 \\
&= 32 + 20 + 12 + 7 \\
&= 71
\end{aligned}$$

Both methods yield **71** ✓. Horner's rule uses only **$k$ multiplications and $k$ additions** — total $O(k)$ time, which is the optimal complexity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Horner's rule is frequently asked in 3-mark and 7-mark questions. Memorize the nested bracket form. When you write the evaluation in the exam, **show the substitution step-by-step** — partial marks are awarded for intermediate results.

### 3.5 Time Complexity Analysis Matrix

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space Complexity |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|------------------|
| Create Polynomial | $\Omega(k)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ for new nodes |
| Display | $\Omega(k)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(1)$ auxiliary |
| Add (n terms + m terms) | $\Omega(\min(n, m))$ | $\Theta(n + m)$ | $O(n + m)$ | $O(n + m)$ new list |
| Multiply (n × m pairs) | $\Omega(1)$ if one is zero | $\Theta(n \cdot m)$ | $O(n \cdot m)$ | $O(n \cdot m)$ worst |
| Evaluate (Naive) | $\Omega(k \cdot n)$ | $\Theta(k \cdot n)$ | $O(k \cdot n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Evaluate (Horner) | $\Omega(k)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(1)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: Node Structure of a Polynomial Linked List

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD["HEAD"] -->|next| N1["coeff: 5 | exp: 4"]
    N1 -->|next| N2["coeff: 3 | exp: 2"]
    N2 -->|next| N3["coeff: 7 | exp: 0"]
    N3 -->|next| NULL["NULL"]
```

**Interpretation:** The polynomial represented is $P(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7$. Notice that only **3 nodes** are used, even though the polynomial has degree 4 (which would need 5 array slots). This is the **sparse representation advantage**.

### 4.2 Diagram: Adding Two Polynomials — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: P1 head pointer"] --> C{"p1 NULL?"}
    B["Input: P2 head pointer"] --> D{"p2 NULL?"}
    C -- No --> E["Read exp1 from p1"]
    C -- Yes --> Z["Append remaining p2 nodes"]
    D -- No --> F["Read exp2 from p2"]
    D -- Yes --> Y["Append remaining p1 nodes"]
    E --> G{"Compare exp1 vs exp2"}
    F --> G
    G -- "exp1 == exp2" --> H["coeff = coeff1 + coeff2"]
    G -- "exp1 > exp2" --> I["Copy node1 to result"]
    G -- "exp1 < exp2" --> J["Copy node2 to result"]
    H --> K{"coeff != 0?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["Create new node, append to result"]
    K -- No --> M["Skip, advance both pointers"]
    I --> N["Advance p1 only"]
    J --> O["Advance p2 only"]
    L --> P["Advance p1 and p2"]
    M --> P
    N --> C
    O --> C
    P --> C
    Y --> Q["Return result head"]
    Z --> Q
```

### 4.3 Diagram: Polynomial Multiplication — Pairwise Product Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Read P1 head, P2 head"] --> L1["For each node i in P1"]
    L1 --> L2["For each node j in P2"]
    L2 --> CMP["Multiply coeff: c = a_i * b_j"]
    CMP --> EXP["Add exponent: e = exp_i + exp_j"]
    EXP --> CK{"Does result P3 contain exp = e?"}
    CK -- Yes --> ADD["Add c to existing coeff"]
    CK -- No --> INS["Insert new node with c and e"]
    ADD --> NEXTJ["Advance j"]
    INS --> NEXTJ
    NEXTJ --> MJ{"More j nodes?"}
    MJ -- Yes --> L2
    MJ -- No --> NEXTI["Advance i"]
    NEXTI --> MI{"More i nodes?"}
    MI -- Yes --> L1
    MI -- No --> DONE["Return P3 head"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Horner's Rule Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START["Start: result = a_n"] --> LOOP{"Next node exists?"}
    LOOP -- Yes --> STEP["result = result * x0 + next_coeff"]
    STEP --> LOOP
    LOOP -- No --> OUTPUT["Output: result = P x0"]
```

**Example trace for $P(x) = 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 6x + 7$ at $x_0 = 2$:**

| Iteration | Current Node (coeff) | Computation | result |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|--------|
| 0 | (4, 3) | Initialize | 4 |
| 1 | (5, 2) | $4 \times 2 + 5$ | 13 |
| 2 | (6, 1) | $13 \times 2 + 6$ | 32 |
| 3 | (7, 0) | $32 \times 2 + 7$ | 71 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the linked list with a **separate rectangular HEAD block** (not a circle) followed by circular/oval nodes. Use arrows for `next` pointers. The last node should clearly show `NULL` or a diagonal cross. Examiners specifically check for this convention.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a polynomial. Explain how a polynomial can be represented using a linked list with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **polynomial** $P(x)$ is a mathematical expression of the form $P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + a_1 x + a_0$, where $a_i$ are coefficients and $i$ are non-negative integer powers.

In a **linked list representation**, each non-zero term is stored as a node containing two fields — `coeff` and `exp` — plus a `next` pointer. Only non-zero terms are stored, making it ideal for sparse polynomials.

**Example:** $P(x) = 6x^4 + 4x^2 + 5$ is represented as: HEAD → (6, 4) → (4, 2) → (5, 0) → NULL. Only 3 nodes are used instead of 5 array slots.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Compare array representation and linked list representation of polynomials. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Array Representation | Linked List Representation |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Memory | Fixed $(n+1)$ slots; wastes space for zeros | Dynamic; stores only non-zero terms |
| Access Time | $O(1)$ random access by index | $O(k)$ sequential traversal |
| Insertion/Deletion | $O(n)$ shifting required | $O(1)$ pointer adjustment |
| Best For | Dense polynomials | **Sparse polynomials** |

**Advantage of array:** Fast direct access to any term. **Disadvantage of array:** Wastes memory for missing terms.
**Advantage of linked list:** Memory efficient for sparse polynomials. **Disadvantage of linked list:** No random access; extra pointer overhead per node.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]** (a) Define the node structure for polynomial representation. Write a function to create a polynomial linked list with $n$ terms read from the user. (7 marks)
(b) Write a function to add two polynomials $P_1$ and $P_2$ represented as linked lists and return the resultant polynomial. Explain with a dry-run example. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Node Structure Definition (2 marks):**
```c
struct PolyNode {
    int coeff;
    int exp;
    struct PolyNode *next;
};
```

**Create Function (5 marks):**
```c
struct PolyNode* createPoly(int n) {
    struct PolyNode *head = NULL, *tail = NULL, *newNode;
    int i, c, e;
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("Enter coeff and exp: ");
        scanf("%d %d", &c, &e);
        if (c == 0) continue;     // skip zero terms
        newNode = (struct PolyNode*)malloc(sizeof(struct PolyNode));
        newNode->coeff = c;
        newNode->exp = e;
        newNode->next = NULL;
        if (head == NULL) {
            head = tail = newNode;
        } else {
            tail->next = newNode;
            tail = newNode;
        }
    }
    return head;
}
```

**Explanation:** The function allocates $n$ nodes dynamically using `malloc`. It uses a `tail` pointer for **$O(1)$ insertion at end**. Zero coefficients are skipped to maintain sparsity.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

```c
struct PolyNode* addPoly(struct PolyNode *p1, struct PolyNode *p2) {
    struct PolyNode *p3 = NULL, *tail = NULL, *newNode;
    int c;
    while (p1 != NULL && p2 != NULL) {
        if (p1->exp == p2->exp) {
            c = p1->coeff + p2->coeff;
            if (c != 0) insertAtEnd(&p3, &tail, c, p1->exp);
            p1 = p1->next;
            p2 = p2->next;
        } else if (p1->exp > p2->exp) {
            insertAtEnd(&p3, &tail, p1->coeff, p1->exp);
            p1 = p1->next;
        } else {
            insertAtEnd(&p3, &tail, p2->coeff, p2->exp);
            p2 = p2->next;
        }
    }
    while (p1 != NULL) {
        insertAtEnd(&p3, &tail, p1->coeff, p1->exp);
        p1 = p1->next;
    }
    while (p2 != NULL) {
        insertAtEnd(&p3, &tail, p2->coeff, p2->exp);
        p2 = p2->next;
    }
    return p3;
}
```

**Dry Run Example (3 marks from part b):**
- $P_1$: 5x⁴ + 3x² + 7
- $P_2$: 4x⁴ + 2x³ + 9x² + 1
- Walk through the cases as shown in the worked example of Section 3.2.
- Result: $P_3 = 9x^4 + 2x^3 + 12x^2 + 8$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]** (a) Write a function to multiply two polynomials represented as linked lists and explain with a suitable example. (7 marks)
(b) Write a function to evaluate a polynomial at a given value of $x$ using Horner's rule. Evaluate $P(x) = 3x^3 + 2x^2 + 5x + 4$ at $x = 2$ and show all intermediate steps. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Multiplication Algorithm (4 marks):**
```c
struct PolyNode* multiplyPoly(struct PolyNode *p1, struct PolyNode *p2) {
    struct PolyNode *p3 = NULL, *tail = NULL, *newNode, *temp;
    int c, e;
    for (temp = p1; temp != NULL; temp = temp->next) {
        for (struct PolyNode *t = p2; t != NULL; t = t->next) {
            c = temp->coeff * t->coeff;
            e = temp->exp + t->exp;
            // Check if exp e already exists in p3
            struct PolyNode *search = p3, *prev = NULL;
            while (search != NULL && search->exp > e) {
                prev = search;
                search = search->next;
            }
            if (search != NULL && search->exp == e) {
                search->coeff += c;   // merge terms
            } else {
                newNode = (struct PolyNode*)malloc(sizeof(struct PolyNode));
                newNode->coeff = c;
                newNode->exp = e;
                newNode->next = search;
                if (prev == NULL) p3 = newNode;
                else prev->next = newNode;
            }
        }
    }
    return p3;
}
```

**Example (3 marks):** Multiply $P_1 = 2x^2 + 3$ and $P_2 = 4x + 1$.
- $(2x^2)(4x) = 8x^3$
- $(2x^2)(1) = 2x^2$
- $(3)(4x) = 12x$
- $(3)(1) = 3$
- **Result:** $P_3 = 8x^3 + 2x^2 + 12x + 3$.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Horner's Rule Function (3 marks):**
```c
int evaluatePoly(struct PolyNode *head, int x) {
    int result = 0;
    for (struct PolyNode *t = head; t != NULL; t = t->next) {
        result = result * x + t->coeff;
    }
    return result;
}
```

**Evaluation Trace for $P(x) = 3x^3 + 2x^2 + 5x + 4$ at $x = 2$ (4 marks):**

| Step | Operation | result |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Initialize with coeff of $x^3$ | 3 |
| 2 | $3 \times 2 + 2$ | 8 |
| 3 | $8 \times 2 + 5$ | 21 |
| 4 | $21 \times 2 + 4$ | 46 |

**Final Answer:** $P(2) = 46$.

**Verification (1 mark):**
$$P(2) = 3(8) + 2(4) + 5(2) + 4 = 24 + 8 + 10 + 4 = 46 \checkmark$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check `coeff == 0` after addition** — when adding two terms with the same exponent, the result might be zero. If you create a node with `coeff = 0`, examiners will deduct **at least 1 mark** for poor logic.
> 2. **Confusing array indexing with linked list traversal** — In arrays, you access `a[i]`. In linked lists, you must use `temp = temp->next`. Writing `temp->next[i]` is a **syntax error worth 0 marks** for that sub-part.
> 3. **Not freeing allocated memory** — For full marks on memory management questions, mention that dynamically allocated nodes should be freed using `free()` to prevent memory leaks.
> 4. **Skipping the dry-run** — In 14-mark questions, a dry-run example is **mandatory** and carries **2–3 marks**. Never write code without tracing it on sample data.
> 5. **Wrong ordering of terms** — KTU expects polynomials in **descending order of exponent**. Drawing a list in ascending order will lose **1 mark** for the diagram.
> 6. **Missing HEAD pointer in diagram** — Drawing only the nodes without a `HEAD` block is considered an incomplete representation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Node Structure:** Every polynomial node has exactly **two data fields** (`coeff`, `exp`) and **one pointer** (`next`).
- **Ordering Convention:** Always maintain **descending order of exponents** for both efficiency and examiner preference.
- **Sparse vs Dense:** Use linked lists for **sparse** polynomials (many zero terms) and arrays for **dense** polynomials.
- **Default Memory per Node:** In KTU board numericals, assume `sizeof(int) = 2` bytes and `sizeof(pointer) = 2` bytes → **6 bytes per node** on 16-bit systems.
- **Addition Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$ using the **two-pointer merge technique**, not $O(n \times m)$.
- **Multiplication Time Complexity:** $O(n \times m)$ — pairwise product of all terms.
- **Horner's Rule:** Reduces evaluation from $O(k \cdot n)$ to **$O(k)$** by replacing powers with iterated multiplication and addition.
- **Boundary Cases:** Always handle (i) empty list, (ii) zero coefficient after addition, (iii) one polynomial being zero, (iv) both being zero.
- **Display Format:** A polynomial $5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7$ should be displayed with `+` between terms, `x^exp` notation, and **no trailing `+`**.
- **Free Memory:** After operations, every `malloc`-allocated node should be `free`-d to avoid memory leaks — a 1-mark differentiator in KTU 2024.
- **HEAD Convention:** Draw HEAD as a **rectangle** (not a node) with an arrow pointing to the first actual node.
- **Common Real-World Use:** Signal processing filters, computer graphics curves, symbolic algebra systems, and cryptography.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Trees # 🌳 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Tree** is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of a finite set of **nodes** (vertices) connected by **edges** (links). It is a special case of a **graph** that is **acyclic** (contains no cycles) and **connected**, where exactly one path exists between any two nodes. Formally, a tree $T$ is defined as a tuple $T = (V, E)$ where: - $V$ is a finite, non-empty set of **nodes**. - $E$ is a set of **directed/unordered edges** connecting pairs of nodes. - There is exactly one designated **root node**. - Every other node (except root) has exactly **one parent** and **zero or more children**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The term *Trees* in this module specifically focuses on **Binary Trees, Binary Search Trees (BST), AVL Trees, and Tree Traversals**. B-Trees and B+ Trees are covered in advanced electives and OS modules. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠 Imagine a **family tree** or an **organizational chart of a company**: - The **CEO** sits at the top → this is the **Root Node**. - The CEO's direct reports (CTOs, CFOs, CMOs) are **Children** of the root. - Each of those managers has their own subordinates — making them **Internal Nodes**. - Employees with no one reporting to them are **Leaf Nodes** (external nodes). - The connection lines (boxes joined by lines) are the **Edges**. A real-world analogy: Consider a **directory structure** on your computer. The root is `C:\` (Windows) or `/` (Linux). Inside it, you have folders (`Users`, `Program Files`). Inside `Users`, you have individual user folders, and so on. You cannot go in circles — there's only one way to reach any file from the root. That is a **tree**. --- ## 1.3 Essential Terminology (KTU Board Examiner Vocabulary) | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Root** | The topmost node with no parent. | | **Parent** | A node that has one or more children directly below it. | | **Child** | A node that descends from another node. | | **Sibling** | Nodes that share the same parent. | | **Leaf / External Node** | A node with **zero children**. | | **Internal Node** | A node with **at least one child**. | | **Edge** | The connecting link between a parent and a child. | | **Path** | A sequence of nodes and edges between two nodes. | | **Depth of Node** | Length of the path from the root to that node. | | **Height of Node** | Length of the longest path from that node to a leaf. | | **Height of Tree** | Height of the root node. | | **Degree of Node** | The number of children a node has. | | **Degree of Tree** | The maximum degree among all nodes. | | **Subtree** | A tree formed by a node and all its descendants. | | **Forest** | A collection of disjoint trees. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Fact:** In any tree with $n$ nodes, the number of edges is exactly $n - 1$. This is a fundamental property tested in KTU exams. --- ## 1.4 Types of Trees (Syllabus-Relevant) 1. **General Tree** — Each node can have any number of children. 2. **Binary Tree** — Each node has **at most 2 children** (left and right). 3. **Binary Search Tree (BST)** — A binary tree where left child < parent < right child. 4. **AVL Tree** — A **self-balancing** BST where height difference (balance factor) of any node is $-1, 0,$ or $+1$. 5. **Complete Binary Tree** — All levels are completely filled except possibly the last, which is filled left-to-right. 6. **Full Binary Tree** — Every node has either **0 or 2 children**. 7. **Perfect Binary Tree** — All internal nodes have 2 children AND all leaves are at the same level. 8. **Skewed Tree** — Every node has only one child (left-skewed or right-skewed). # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Trees Let $T$ be a tree with $n$ nodes and height $h$: - **Edge Count Property:** Number of edges in a tree = $n - 1$ - **Root Property:** A tree with $n \geq 1$ has exactly one root. - **Path Property:** There exists exactly **one unique simple path** between any two nodes. - **Connectedness Property:** A tree with $n$ nodes is always connected. - **Acyclic Property:** A tree contains **no cycles**. ### Why These Properties Matter (The "Why" Behind It) Each edge in a tree contributes exactly **one connection** between two nodes. Since one node (the root) has no incoming edge, the remaining $n-1$ nodes each must have exactly one incoming edge from their parent. Hence, total edges = $n-1$. This is foundational for proving many tree theorems. --- ## 2.2 Binary Tree — The Core KTU Focus A **Binary Tree (BT)** is a tree where each node has at most two children, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**. ### Maximum Number of Nodes For a binary tree of height $h$ (root at height 0): $$N_{max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$$ **Why?** Each level $\ell$ (where $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \dots, h$) can contain at most $2^\ell$ nodes. Summing the geometric series: $$\sum_{\ell=0}^{h} 2^\ell = 2^{h+1} - 1$$ ### Minimum Number of Nodes For a binary tree of height $h$: $$N_{min}(h) = h + 1$$ **Why?** The minimum occurs when each level has exactly one node (a skewed tree). ### Height vs. Nodes Relationship $$h = \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor \quad \text{(for a complete/perfect binary tree)}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU exam!** | Concept | Formula | Notation | |---------|---------|----------| | Edges in a tree | $E = n - 1$ | $n$ = number of nodes | | Max nodes at level $\ell$ | $2^\ell$ | $\ell$ = level number (root = 0) | | Max nodes in BT of height $h$ | $2^{h+1} - 1$ | $h$ = height (root = 0) | | Min nodes in BT of height $h$ | $h + 1$ | Skewed tree | | Height of complete BT | $\lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor$ | $n$ = total nodes | | Internal nodes in full BT | $i + 1 = \ell$ | $i$ = internal, $\ell$ = leaves | | Total nodes in full BT | $n = 2i + 1$ | $i$ = internal nodes | | Degree of binary tree | $\leq 2$ | Per node | | AVL Balance Factor $BF$ | $h_L - h_R$ | $BF \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ | | Min nodes of AVL tree (height $h$) | $N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1$ | Recurrence (Fibonacci-like) | | Time complexity of BST search (avg) | $O(\log n)$ | Random tree | | Time complexity of BST search (worst) | $O(n)$ | Skewed tree | ### ⚠️ Markdown Table Safety Note: In the table above, vertical bars `|` for absolute value have been replaced with parentheses to avoid breaking markdown syntax. --- ## 2.4 Binary Search Tree (BST) — Ordered Variant A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a binary tree with the following ordering property: > For every node $X$ in the tree: > - All nodes in the **left subtree** of $X$ have keys **less than** $X$'s key. > - All nodes in the **right subtree** of $X$ have keys **greater than** $X$'s key. ### Why BST? (Real-World Utility) - **Database indexing** — enables $O(\log n)$ lookup. - **File system directories** — fast search and insertion. - **Auto-complete engines** — sorted dictionary lookups. - **Symbol tables** in compilers. --- ## 2.5 AVL Tree — Self-Balancing BST An **AVL Tree** (Adelson-Velsky and Landis, 1962) is a BST where, for every node, the heights of the left and right subtrees differ by **at most 1**. The **Balance Factor (BF)** is defined as: $$BF(\text{node}) = h_L - h_R$$ where $h_L$ = height of left subtree and $h_R$ = height of right subtree. For an AVL tree: $$BF \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$$ ### The Four Imbalance Cases (Rotation Triggers) | Case | Condition | Rotation Type | |------|-----------|---------------| | **LL** | Inserted into left subtree of left child | Single Right Rotation | | **RR** | Inserted into right subtree of right child | Single Left Rotation | | **LR** | Inserted into right subtree of left child | Left-Right Double Rotation | | **RL** | Inserted into left subtree of right child | Right-Left Double Rotation | --- ## 2.6 Tree Traversals (KTU Repeated Favorite!) Four fundamental ways to visit every node of a binary tree exactly once: | Traversal | Order | Memory Trick | |-----------|-------|--------------| | **Preorder** | Root → Left → Right | "**R**oot first" (Pre-fix) | | **Inorder** | Left → Root → Right | "**R**oot in-between" | | **Postorder** | Left → Right → Root | "**R**oot last" (Post-fix) | | **Level Order (BFS)** | Level by level, left to right | Uses a **queue** | ### Why Traversal Matters in Engineering - **Preorder** → Used to serialize/copy a tree. - **Inorder** on BST → Produces **sorted output** (critical for DB queries). - **Postorder** → Used to delete a tree (children first). - **Level Order** → Used in BFS, shortest path in unweighted graphs. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications | Domain | Tree Application | |--------|------------------| | **Operating Systems** | File systems, process scheduling (Red-Black trees) | | **Databases** | B-Trees/B+ Trees for indexing | | **Compilers** | Abstract Syntax Trees (AST), symbol tables | | **Computer Networks** | Trie for IP routing, multicast trees | | **Machine Learning** | Decision Trees, Random Forests, XGBoost | | **AI / Game Theory** | Game trees, Minimax search, Alpha-Beta pruning | | **Graphics** | BSP trees for 3D rendering, Quadtrees for spatial data | | **Networking Protocols** | Spanning Trees (STP in switches) | # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Number of Nodes in a Binary Tree of Height h **Given:** A binary tree where height $h$ means the root is at level 0 and the deepest leaf is at level $h$. **Step 1:** Count maximum nodes per level. Level 0 (root only): max 1 node = $2^0$ Level 1: max 2 nodes = $2^1$ Level 2: max 4 nodes = $2^2$ Level $\ell$: max $2^\ell$ nodes **Step 2:** Sum across all levels from $\ell = 0$ to $\ell = h$. $$N_{max}(h) = \sum_{\ell=0}^{h} 2^\ell$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the geometric series using the formula $\sum_{\ell=0}^{h} r^\ell = \frac{r^{h+1} - 1}{r - 1}$ with $r = 2$. $$ \begin{aligned} N_{max}(h) &= \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} \\ &= \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{1} \\ &= 2^{h+1} - 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** $$N_{max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$$ **Verification:** For $h = 0$: $N_{max} = 2^1 - 1 = 1$ ✓ (single root) For $h = 2$: $N_{max} = 2^3 - 1 = 7$ ✓ (perfect tree with 7 nodes) --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Height of a Complete Binary Tree with n Nodes **Given:** A complete binary tree with $n$ nodes. **Step 1:** Start from the minimum case. The minimum number of nodes for a complete binary tree of height $h$ is achieved when the last level has exactly one node: $$N_{min}^{complete}(h) = 2^h$$ (All levels 0 through $h-1$ fully filled = $2^h - 1$ nodes, plus 1 node at level $h$.) **Step 2:** The maximum for the same height is: $$N_{max}^{complete}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$$ **Step 3:** For a complete binary tree with $n$ nodes, we have: $$2^h \leq n \leq 2^{h+1} - 1$$ **Step 4:** Take $\log_2$ throughout: $$h \leq \log_2(n) \leq h + 1 - \epsilon$$ **Step 5:** Since $h$ is an integer, $h = \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor$. **Conclusion:** $$h = \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor$$ **Verification:** $n = 15$: $h = \lfloor \log_2(15) \rfloor = \lfloor 3.906 \rfloor = 3$ ✓ (Perfect tree of height 3 has 15 nodes) --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Number of Nodes in an AVL Tree of Height h **Given:** An AVL tree is height-balanced. For any node, $|h_L - h_R| \leq 1$. **Step 1:** Define $N(h)$ as the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $h$. **Step 2:** Base cases: $N(0) = 1$ (A single root has height 0 with 1 node) $N(1) = 2$ (Height 1 requires root + 1 child, since minimum nodes = $h+1 = 2$) **Step 3:** Recursive case — to minimize nodes at height $h$, we want one subtree of height $h-1$ and the other of height $h-2$ (since difference cannot exceed 1, and using $h-2$ saves nodes): $$ \begin{aligned} N(h) &= 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Solve the recurrence. This is a Fibonacci-like recurrence. $N(0) = 1$ $N(1) = 2$ $N(2) = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4$ $N(3) = 1 + 4 + 2 = 7$ $N(4) = 1 + 7 + 4 = 12$ $N(5) = 1 + 12 + 7 = 20$ **Step 5:** Compare with Fibonacci numbers $F_n$: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 34... We observe $N(h) = F_{h+3} - 1$, where $F_0 = 0, F_1 = 1$. **Verification:** $N(3) = F_6 - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7$ ✓ **Conclusion:** $$N(h) = F_{h+3} - 1 \approx \frac{\phi^{h+3}}{\sqrt{5}}$$ where $\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ (Golden Ratio). **Engineering Insight:** This means the height of an AVL tree with $n$ nodes is bounded by $O(\log n)$, which guarantees $O(\log n)$ search/insert/delete — the whole reason AVL trees exist! --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Inorder Traversal Produces Sorted Output for BST **Claim:** Inorder traversal of a BST yields elements in **ascending sorted order**. **Proof (by structural induction on the BST):** **Base Case:** An empty tree produces empty output — vacuously sorted. **Inductive Step:** Assume the claim holds for the left and right subtrees. Consider the BST rooted at node $X$ with key $k$. By BST property: - Every key in left subtree $< k$ - Every key in right subtree $> k$ Inorder traversal does: (Left subtree) → Visit root $X$ → (Right subtree). By induction hypothesis, inorder of left subtree yields keys $< k$ in sorted order. By induction hypothesis, inorder of right subtree yields keys $> k$ in sorted order. Concatenating: (sorted keys $< k$) → $k$ → (sorted keys $> k$) = sorted in ascending order. $\therefore$ The inorder traversal of any BST is sorted. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Number of Edges in a Tree = n - 1 **Proof (by induction on $n$):** **Base Case:** $n = 1$ (single root node). Edges = 0. Formula: $1 - 1 = 0$ ✓ **Inductive Step:** Assume for any tree with $k$ nodes, edges = $k - 1$. Consider a tree with $k+1$ nodes. There exists at least one leaf node $L$ (a tree with $\geq 2$ nodes always has $\geq 2$ leaves — proved separately). Remove leaf $L$ and its single connecting edge. The remaining structure is a tree with $k$ nodes. By induction hypothesis, it has $k - 1$ edges. Adding back the edge to leaf: total edges = $(k - 1) + 1 = k$. Substituting $k = n - 1$: edges = $n - 1$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Complexity Comparison of Tree Operations | Operation | BST (Avg) | BST (Worst / Skewed) | AVL Tree | Heap | |-----------|-----------|----------------------|----------|------| | **Search** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | | **Insert** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | **Delete** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | **Find Min/Max** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ for max (max-heap) | | **Space** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | | **Balancing** | Not self-balancing | N/A | Automatic rotations | Complete binary tree by nature | --- ## 3.7 Worked Example: Inserting into an AVL Tree **Insert sequence:** 30, 20, 10 **Step 1:** Insert 30 → Tree: `30` (Height = 0, BF = 0) ✓ Balanced **Step 2:** Insert 20 → Tree: `30` with left child `20`. BF(30) = $1 - 0 = 1$ ✓ Balanced **Step 3:** Insert 10 → Tree: `30` → left → `20` → left → `10`. BF(30) = $2 - 0 = 2$ ❌ Unbalanced (LL case) **Step 4:** Apply **Right Rotation** around 30: The new root becomes `20`. Its left child is `10`, and its right child is `30`. Resulting tree: `20` with left `10` and right `30`. Heights are equal, BF = 0 ✓ Balanced. **Final Tree Visualization (Mermaid in Section 4):** | Node | Left | Right | Height | |------|------|-------|--------| | 20 | 10 | 30 | 1 | | 10 | - | - | 0 | | 30 | - | - | 0 | # 🎨 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Tree Terminology Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[ROOT NODE
Depth: 0
Height: 3] --> B[Child B
Depth: 1
Height: 1] A --> C[Child C
Depth: 1
Height: 2] A --> D[Child D
Depth: 1
Height: 0
LEAF] B --> E[Grandchild E
Depth: 2
LEAF] B --> F[Grandchild F
Depth: 2
LEAF] C --> G[Grandchild G
Depth: 2
Height: 1] G --> H[Great Grandchild H
Depth: 3
LEAF] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Legend:** 🟡 Root, 🔵 Internal Nodes, 🟢 Leaves, 🟠 Internal Nodes (deeper) --- ## 4.2 Binary Tree Types — Visual Comparison ```mermaid graph TD subgraph FULL [FULL BINARY TREE] F1[1] --> F2[2] F1 --> F3[3] F2 --> F4[4] F2 --> F5[5] F3 --> F6[6] F3 --> F7[7] end style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style F2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style F3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style F4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style F5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style F6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style F7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 ``` ```mermaid graph TD subgraph COMPLETE [COMPLETE BINARY TREE] C1[1] --> C2[2] C1 --> C3[3] C2 --> C4[4] C2 --> C5[5] C3 --> C6[6] C3 --> C7[7] C4 --> C8[8] C4 --> C9[9] end style C1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C3 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C4 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C5 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C6 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C7 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C8 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style C9 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 ``` ```mermaid graph TD subgraph SKEWED [RIGHT-SKEWED TREE] S1[1] --> S2[2] S2 --> S3[3] S3 --> S4[4] end style S1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style S2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style S3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style S4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Binary Search Tree (BST) Example ```mermaid graph TD N50[50 ROOT] --> N30[30] N50 --> N70[70] N30 --> N20[20] N30 --> N40[40] N70 --> N60[60] N70 --> N80[80] N20 --> N10[10 LEAF] N40 --> N45[45 LEAF] style N50 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style N30 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style N70 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style N20 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style N40 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style N60 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style N80 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style N10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style N45 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` **Inorder Traversal Result:** 10, 20, 30, 40, 45, 50, 60, 70, 80 → **SORTED** ✓ --- ## 4.4 AVL Tree — Before and After Rotation (LL Case) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BEFORE [BEFORE ROTATION - UNBALANCED BF 2] B1[30
BF 2] --> B2[20
BF 1] B2 --> B3[10
BF 0 LEAF] end style B1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style B2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style B3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` ```mermaid graph TD subgraph AFTER [AFTER RIGHT ROTATION - BALANCED BF 0] A1[20
BF 0 ROOT] --> A2[10
BF 0] A1 --> A3[30
BF 0] end style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style A2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style A3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.5 AVL Tree — All Four Rotation Cases ```mermaid graph TD subgraph LL_CASE [LL CASE - Right Rotation] L1[Z BF 2] --> L2[Y BF 1] L2 --> L3[X inserted here] end style L1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 style L2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style L3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` ```mermaid graph TD subgraph RR_CASE [RR CASE - Left Rotation] R1[Z BF -2] --> R2[Y BF -1] R2 --> R3[X inserted here] end style R1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 style R2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style R3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.6 Tree Traversal — Step-by-Step Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[Visit Root Node] --> B{Choose Traversal} B -->|Preorder| P1[Process Root] P1 --> P2[Recurse Left Subtree] P2 --> P3[Recurse Right Subtree] B -->|Inorder| I1[Recurse Left Subtree] I1 --> I2[Process Root] I2 --> I3[Recurse Right Subtree] B -->|Postorder| PO1[Recurse Left Subtree] PO1 --> PO2[Recurse Right Subtree] PO2 --> PO3[Process Root] B -->|Level Order| L1[Enqueue Root] L1 --> L2[Dequeue and Process] L2 --> L3[Enqueue Children] L3 --> L4{Queue Empty?} L4 -->|No| L2 L4 -->|Yes| L5[END] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style L5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.7 Functional Block Architecture: BST Insertion Module ```mermaid graph TD START[Start BST Insert] --> SEARCH[Search for correct position
Compare key with current node] SEARCH --> DECIDE{Position Found?} DECIDE -->|No, reached NULL| CREATE[Create new node
Allocate memory
Set key value] CREATE --> LINK[Link new node as left or right child
based on key comparison] LINK --> BALANCE[Check Balance Factor
at each ancestor] BALANCE --> UNBAL{Unbalanced?} UNBAL -->|Yes| ROTATE[Apply AVL Rotation
LL, RR, LR, or RL] UNBAL -->|No| UPDATE[Update heights
Continue recursion unwind] ROTATE --> UPDATE UPDATE --> FINISH[Return Updated Tree] DECIDE -->|Yes, key exists| ERROR[Handle Duplicate
Skip or Update] ERROR --> FINISH style START fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style FINISH fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style CREATE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style ROTATE fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style ERROR fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🎯 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the following terms with respect to trees:** **(a) Height of a tree** **(b) Degree of a node** **(c) Skewed binary tree** #### 📋 Model Answer (3 Marks): **(a) Height of a tree (1 mark):** The height of a tree is the length of the longest path from the **root node** to a **leaf node**. It is equal to the height of the root node. By convention, the height of an empty tree is **-1** and the height of a tree with only the root is **0**. **(b) Degree of a node (1 mark):** The degree of a node is the **number of children** that node has. For a binary tree, the degree is at most 2. **(c) Skewed binary tree (1 mark):** A skewed binary tree is a binary tree in which every internal node has exactly **one child**, either to the left (left-skewed) or to the right (right-skewed). Such a tree essentially degenerates into a linked list. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Differentiate between a binary tree and a binary search tree (BST).** #### 📋 Model Answer (3 Marks): | Feature | Binary Tree | Binary Search Tree | |---------|-------------|-------------------| | **Definition** | A tree where each node has at most 2 children | A binary tree with the BST ordering property | | **Ordering** | No ordering constraint | Left subtree keys $< $ root $<$ right subtree keys | | **Search time** | $O(n)$ — must traverse all nodes | $O(\log n)$ average, $O(n)$ worst | | **Inorder traversal** | No specific pattern | Always yields **sorted** sequence | | **Use case** | Expression trees, decision trees | Searching, indexing, dictionaries | **(1.5 marks for correct comparison, 1.5 marks for valid differences.)** --- ## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is provided. --- ### 🎯 Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Construct a Binary Search Tree (BST) by inserting the following keys in the given order: **40, 60, 25, 30, 70, 15, 35, 10, 55, 65** Show the tree after each insertion. Write the **preorder, inorder, and postorder** traversals of the final BST. **(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the BST obtained in part (a) into an **AVL tree** by inserting the additional key **50**. Show all rotations with diagrams. #### 📋 Model Solution: ### Part (a) — BST Construction [7 Marks] **Step 1: Insert 40** Tree: `40` (root) **Step 2: Insert 60** (60 > 40, goes right) Tree: `40` → right → `60` **Step 3: Insert 25** (25 < 40, goes left) Tree: `40` → left → `25`, right → `60` **Step 4: Insert 30** (30 < 40, go left; 30 > 25, go right) Tree: `40` → left → `25` → right → `30`; right → `60` **Step 5: Insert 70** (70 > 40, go right; 70 > 60, go right) Tree: `40` → right → `60` → right → `70` **Step 6: Insert 15** (15 < 40, left; 15 < 25, left) Tree: `40` → left → `25` → left → `15` **Step 7: Insert 35** (35 < 40, left; 35 > 25, right; 35 > 30, right) Tree: `40` → left → `25` → right → `30` → right → `35` **Step 8: Insert 10** (10 < 40, left; 10 < 25, left; 10 < 15, left) Tree: `40` → left → `25` → left → `15` → left → `10` **Step 9: Insert 55** (55 > 40, right; 55 < 60, left) Tree: `40` → right → `60` → left → `55` **Step 10: Insert 65** (65 > 40, right; 65 > 60, right; 65 < 70, left) Tree: `40` → right → `60` → right → `70` → left → `65` **Final BST:** ``` 40 / \ 25 60 / \ / \ 15 30 55 70 / \ / 10 35 65 ``` **Traversals (3.5 marks for tree + 3.5 marks for traversals):** - **Preorder (Root → Left → Right):** 40, 25, 15, 10, 30, 35, 60, 55, 70, 65 - **Inorder (Left → Root → Right):** 10, 15, 25, 30, 35, 40, 55, 60, 65, 70 - **Postorder (Left → Right → Root):** 10, 15, 35, 30, 25, 55, 65, 70, 60, 40 > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** The inorder traversal yields keys in **ascending order (10, 15, 25, 30, 35, 40, 55, 60, 65, 70)**, confirming the BST property. ✓ ### Part (b) — Conversion to AVL by inserting 50 [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Insert 50 into existing BST. 50 > 40 → right; 50 < 60 → left; 50 < 55 → left Tree now has a path: 40 → 60 → 55 → 50 **Step 2:** Check balance factors from insertion point upward. - BF(50) = 0 ✓ - BF(55) = $h_L - h_R = 1 - 0 = 1$ ✓ - BF(60) = $h_L - h_R = 2 - 1 = 1$ ✓ - BF(40) = $h_L - h_R = 2 - 2 = 0$ ✓ **Wait — let me recheck BF(60):** Left subtree of 60 has height 2 (55 → 50), right subtree of 60 has height 1 (70 → 65). So BF(60) = $2 - 1 = 1$ ✓ **Actually, the tree IS still balanced!** No rotation is needed. However, if we consider the path and future insertions, let us verify all nodes: ``` 40 (BF: 2-2 = 0) ✓ / \ 25 60 (BF: 2-1 = 1) ✓ / \ / \ 15 30 55 70 (BF: 1-1 = 0) ✓ / \ / 10 35 65 \ 50 ← newly inserted ``` Wait — let me reconsider. 50 was inserted as left child of 55, not in 70's subtree. So the tree after inserting 50: ``` 40 / \ 25 60 / \ / \ 15 30 55 70 / \ / / 10 35 50 65 ``` **Balance Factors:** - Node 10: BF = 0 ✓ - Node 35: BF = 0 ✓ - Node 30: BF = $1 - 1 = 0$ ✓ - Node 15: BF = $1 - 0 = 1$ ✓ - Node 50: BF = 0 ✓ - Node 65: BF = 0 ✓ - Node 70: BF = $1 - 0 = 1$ ✓ - Node 55: BF = $1 - 0 = 1$ ✓ - Node 60: BF = $2 - 2 = 0$ ✓ - Node 25: BF = $2 - 2 = 0$ ✓ - Node 40: BF = $2 - 2 = 0$ ✓ **Conclusion:** After inserting 50, the BST remains a valid AVL tree. **No rotation is needed.** All balance factors lie in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. (1 mark for AVL definition, 4 marks for step-by-step insertion, 2 marks for final AVL verification) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:** > Students often confuse insertion position. Remember: in BST, if new key < current node, go LEFT; if greater, go RIGHT. Marking an insertion to the wrong side is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. --- ### 🎯 Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **preorder, inorder, postorder, and level-order** traversals of a binary tree. Construct a binary tree from the given traversals: **Preorder:** A B D E C F G **Inorder:** D B E A F C G **(b) [7 Marks]** What is an **AVL tree**? Explain the **four rotation cases** (LL, RR, LR, RL) with suitable diagrams. What is the **balance factor**? Why is it necessary to maintain $BF \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$? #### 📋 Model Solution: ### Part (a) — Tree Construction from Traversals [7 Marks] **Step 1: Identify the root.** Root is the **first element of preorder** = **A**. **Step 2: Locate A in inorder.** Inorder: `D B E A F C G` Position of A: index 4. Inorder splits into: - Left subtree inorder: `D B E` - Right subtree inorder: `F C G` **Step 3: Determine sizes of left and right subtrees.** - Left subtree has 3 elements - Right subtree has 3 elements Preorder breakdown (after removing root A): - Left subtree preorder: next 3 elements = `B D E` - Right subtree preorder: remaining = `C F G` **Step 4: Recursively construct left subtree.** Root of left = first of preorder = **B**. B's position in inorder `D B E` = index 1. - Left inorder: `D` - Right inorder: `E` - Left preorder: `D` - Right preorder: `E` Left subtree: ``` B / \ D E ``` **Step 5: Recursively construct right subtree.** Root of right = first of preorder = **C**. C's position in inorder `F C G` = index 1. - Left inorder: `F` - Right inorder: `G` - Left preorder: `F` - Right preorder: `G` Right subtree: ``` C / \ F G ``` **Final Constructed Tree:** ``` A / \ B C / \ / \ D E F G ``` **Traversal Verification:** | Traversal | Sequence | Match? | |-----------|----------|--------| | Preorder | A B D E C F G | ✓ | | Inorder | D B E A F C G | ✓ | (3 marks for algorithm explanation, 4 marks for tree construction) ### Part (b) — AVL Tree & Rotations [7 Marks] **AVL Tree Definition (1 mark):** An AVL tree is a **height-balanced Binary Search Tree** in which, for every node, the **difference in heights of left and right subtrees** (called the Balance Factor) is at most 1. **Balance Factor (1 mark):** $$BF(\text{node}) = h_L - h_R$$ where $h_L$ = height of left subtree, $h_R$ = height of right subtree. **Why $BF \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$? (1 mark)** This constraint guarantees that the height of an AVL tree with $n$ nodes is **strictly logarithmic**: $h = O(\log n)$. This ensures all operations (search, insert, delete) run in $O(\log n)$ time in the **worst case**, unlike an ordinary BST which can degenerate to $O(n)$ when skewed. **Four Rotation Cases (4 marks):** **Case 1: LL (Left-Left) — Right Rotation** ``` Z (BF = +2) Y / / \ Y → X Z / / X (X's right subtree becomes Y's left) ``` Triggered when a node is inserted into the left subtree of the left child. Solution: Single **right rotation** around Z. **Case 2: RR (Right-Right) — Left Rotation** ``` Z (BF = -2) Y \ / \ Y → Z X \ / X (X's left subtree becomes Y's right) ``` Triggered when inserted into right subtree of right child. Solution: Single **left rotation** around Z. **Case 3: LR (Left-Right) — Left-Right Double Rotation** ``` Z Z X / / / \ Y → X → Y Z \ / X Y ``` Triggered when inserted into right subtree of left child. Solution: First **left rotation** around Y, then **right rotation** around Z. **Case 4: RL (Right-Left) — Right-Left Double Rotation** ``` Z Z X \ \ / \ Y → X → Z Y / \ X Y ``` Triggered when inserted into left subtree of right child. Solution: First **right rotation** around Y, then **left rotation** around Z. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #2:** > When constructing a tree from preorder + inorder, students often misidentify the root or confuse inorder partitions. **Tip:** The FIRST element of preorder is ALWAYS the root. Then find it in inorder to split the tree. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #3:** > When showing rotations in AVL trees, students frequently forget to **update the heights** of the affected nodes after rotation. This loses 1–2 marks. Always write: "After rotation, update heights of X, Y, Z." --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### 🌲 Fundamental Tree Concepts - ⭐ A tree with $n$ nodes has **exactly $n - 1$ edges**. - ⭐ There is **exactly one unique path** between any two nodes in a tree. - ⭐ A tree with $n \geq 2$ nodes has **at least 2 leaves**. - ⭐ Removing any edge from a tree disconnects it. - ⭐ Adding any edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle. ### 🌲 Binary Tree Formulas - ⭐ Max nodes at level $\ell$ = $2^\ell$ - ⭐ Max nodes for height $h$ = $2^{h+1} - 1$ - ⭐ Min nodes for height $h$ = $h + 1$ - ⭐ Height of complete BT = $\lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor$ ### 🌲 Binary Search Tree (BST) - ⭐ Left subtree keys **<** root key **<** Right subtree keys. - ⭐ **Inorder traversal of a BST always yields sorted output.** - ⭐ Search/Insert/Delete complexity: $O(\log n)$ average, $O(n)$ worst case (skewed). ### 🌲 AVL Tree - ⭐ Balance Factor = $h_L - h_R \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ for every node. - ⭐ Minimum nodes of AVL of height $h$: $N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1$ (Fibonacci-like). - ⭐ Four rotation cases: **LL** (Right rotation), **RR** (Left rotation), **LR** (Left-Right), **RL** (Right-Left). - ⭐ All operations guaranteed $O(\log n)$ worst-case. ### 🌲 Tree Traversals — Memory Trick - ⭐ **Preorder**: Root **P**rocessed first (Pre = before). - ⭐ **Inorder**: Root processed **In** the middle. - ⭐ **Postorder**: Root processed **Post** (last). - ⭐ **Level Order (BFS)**: Uses a **queue**; visits level-by-level. ### 🌲 Tree Construction from Traversals - ⭐ **Preorder + Inorder** → Unique tree constructible. - ⭐ **Postorder + Inorder** → Unique tree constructible. - ⭐ **Preorder + Postorder** → **CANNOT** uniquely determine the tree (without more info). ### 🌲 Real-World Engineering Applications - ⭐ **Databases** → B-Tree / B+ Tree indexing. - ⭐ **Compilers** → Abstract Syntax Trees (AST). - ⭐ **Operating Systems** → File systems, process scheduling. - ⭐ **Machine Learning** → Decision Trees, Random Forests, XGBoost. - ⭐ **Networks** → Spanning Tree Protocol (STP), multicast routing. ### 🌲 Common KTU Exam Pitfalls (Avoid These!) - ⚠️ Confusing **depth** (from root down) with **height** (from node up to leaf). - ⚠️ Forgetting that **height of an empty tree = -1** (not 0). - ⚠️ Skipping the **height update** step after AVL rotations. - ⚠️ Misapplying BST property (placing equal keys — must be consistent, usually one side). - ⚠️ Off-by-one errors in level numbering (root at level 0 vs. level 1). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When answering tree questions, ALWAYS draw the diagram first. It earns partial credit even if your algorithmic explanation has minor errors. Tree-based questions are highly visual, and KTU examiners reward clear diagrams generously.

topics.title: Binary Trees - Tree Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET526 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Binary Trees - Tree Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Binary Tree** is a hierarchical, non-linear data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, conventionally referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**. It is formally defined as a finite set of nodes that is either empty or consists of a distinguished node called the **root**, together with two disjoint binary subtrees known as the **left subtree** and **right subtree**.

A **Tree Operation** refers to any algorithmic manipulation performed on a binary tree, including creation, insertion, deletion, traversal, searching, height computation, and node counting. These operations form the foundational toolkit for advanced structures such as **Binary Search Trees (BST)**, **AVL Trees**, **Heaps**, and **Expression Trees**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEEET526 - Module 3):** Students must master both recursive and iterative formulations of all tree operations. Examiners explicitly test the ability to **draw recursion stacks** and **trace pointer movements** during insertion/deletion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **corporate organizational chart**:
- The **CEO** sits at the top (root).
- The CEO has at most **two direct reports** (left child and right child) — say, a CTO and a CFO.
- Each of those reports may have their own direct reports, and so on.

This is exactly a binary tree! Now, "operations" on this chart would be:
- **Hiring a new employee** (Insertion) — finding the correct vacant position under an existing manager.
- **Firing an employee** (Deletion) — restructuring the team they managed so that their direct reports find a new supervising manager.
- **Performance review of all employees** (Traversal) — going through each employee in a systematic order: top-down (preorder), bottom-up (postorder), or by hierarchy level (level order).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics in Binary Trees:**
> - **Height (h):** Length of the longest path from root to a leaf. For a tree with $n$ nodes, $0 \leq h \leq n-1$.
> - **Depth of a node:** Length of the path from root to that node.
> - **Degree of a node:** Number of children (0, 1, or 2 for a binary tree).
> - **Total nodes** in a full binary tree of height $h$: $n = 2^{h+1} - 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Tree Operations

Tree operations are broadly classified into four functional families:

1. **Construction Operations**
   - Node creation (memory allocation).
   - Insertion (BFS-based position finding for a Complete Binary Tree, or BST-rule insertion for a Binary Search Tree).

2. **Query Operations**
   - Searching for a key value.
   - Finding the minimum/maximum element.
   - Computing tree height.
   - Counting nodes (total, internal, leaves).

3. **Traversal Operations**
   - **Depth-First Search (DFS):** Inorder (LNR), Preorder (NLR), Postorder (LRN).
   - **Breadth-First Search (BFS):** Level-order traversal.

4. **Modification Operations**
   - Deletion (leaf node, node with one child, node with two children).
   - Copying/Merging trees.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

**Why recursions dominate tree operations:**
Every subtree is itself a binary tree. Hence, the same algorithmic logic applied to the root can be **recursively applied** to its left and right subtrees. This is the **self-similar, fractal nature** of trees — and it is the single most important insight for solving KTU problems.

**How traversals differ:**
- **Inorder** of a BST gives **sorted ascending** output — used in KVL/KTH-value queries.
- **Preorder** is used to **serialize/reconstruct** a tree from its storage representation.
- **Postorder** is used when **children must be processed before the parent** (e.g., computing directory sizes, deleting a tree to prevent memory leaks).
- **Level-order** uses a **Queue** and visits nodes row-by-row (BFS pattern).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Property / Metric | Formula | Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum height of tree with $n$ nodes | $h_{min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Achieved in a **Complete Binary Tree** | nodes (height count) |
| Maximum height of tree with $n$ nodes | $h_{max} = n - 1$ | Achieved in a **skewed (degenerate) tree** | nodes |
| Total nodes in full binary tree of height $h$ | $N = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | All levels are completely filled | nodes |
| Number of leaf nodes in a full binary tree | $L = 2^{h}$ | For tree of height $h$ | nodes |
| Number of NULL pointers in any binary tree of $n$ nodes | $NULLs = n + 1$ | Holds for **any** binary tree | pointers |
| Internal nodes in a full binary tree | $I = 2^{h} - 1$ | $I = N - L$ | nodes |
| Maximum nodes at level $i$ (root at $i=0$) | $N_{level} = 2^{i}$ | For a non-empty level | nodes |
| Minimum nodes for height $h$ | $N_{min} = h + 1$ | Skewed tree | nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Tree operations underpin **compiler symbol tables**, **database indexing (B/B+ trees)**, **filesystem hierarchies**, **Huffman coding for data compression**, and **decision trees in machine learning**. Mastering these operations is non-negotiable for any software or systems engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of NULL Pointers in Any Binary Tree

**Claim:** A binary tree with $n$ nodes has exactly $n+1$ NULL child pointers.

**Proof:**

Each node in a binary tree has a maximum of **two pointer fields** (one for left child, one for right child).

Therefore, the **total number of pointer fields** available across all nodes is:
$$
\text{Total Pointers} = 2n
$$

Of these, each **edge** in the tree consumes exactly one pointer field (the parent pointing to the child).

Since a tree with $n$ nodes has exactly $n-1$ edges (the classical tree property), the number of **non-NULL pointers** is:
$$
\text{Non-NULL Pointers} = n - 1
$$

The number of **NULL pointers** is therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NULL Pointers} &= \text{Total Pointers} - \text{Non-NULL Pointers} \\
&= 2n - (n - 1) \\
&= 2n - n + 1 \\
&= n + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **every binary tree with $n$ nodes has exactly $n+1$ NULL child pointers**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum and Minimum Nodes for a Given Height $h$

**Claim 1 — Maximum Nodes:** A binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^{h+1} - 1$ nodes.

**Proof:**

Let $N(i)$ denote the maximum number of nodes at level $i$. The root is at level $0$.

At level $0$ (root): $N(0) = 1$.

At level $1$: Maximum $N(1) = 2 = 2^1$.

At level $2$: Maximum $N(2) = 4 = 2^2$.

By induction, at level $i$: $N(i) = 2^i$.

Total maximum nodes up to height $h$ is the sum of a geometric series:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{max} &= \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^{i} = 2^{0} + 2^{1} + \dots + 2^{h} \\
&= \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} \\
&= 2^{h+1} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Claim 2 — Minimum Nodes:** A binary tree of height $h$ has at least $h+1$ nodes.

**Proof:**

To achieve a tree of height $h$, we must place at least **one node on each level** from $0$ to $h$. The minimum number of such nodes is achieved by a **right-skewed (or left-skewed) tree** containing exactly one node per level:
$$
N_{min} = h + 1
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operation Complexities

The following matrix summarizes the time and space complexities for the major tree operations on a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**. This is essential for KTU's complexity-analysis questions.

| Operation | Average Case (Balanced) | Worst Case (Skewed) | Auxiliary Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| Search | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack |
| Insertion | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| Deletion | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| Min / Max | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| Height computation | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| Level-order (BFS) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ queue, $\Omega(1)$ worst for max width |
| Inorder / Preorder / Postorder | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| Counting nodes | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a Balanced Binary Tree:** $h = O(\log n)$, so all major operations are $O(\log n)$.
> **For a Skewed Tree:** $h = O(n)$, so operations degrade to $O(n)$.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Trace: BST Insertion of Keys {50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80}

Let us trace each step meticulously:

| Step | Key | Comparison Path | Action | Resulting Tree (root → leaves) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | Tree empty | Create root | `50` |
| 2 | 30 | $30 < 50$ → go left | Insert as left child of 50 | `50 → 30` |
| 3 | 70 | $70 > 50$ → go right | Insert as right child of 50 | `50 → 30, 70` |
| 4 | 20 | $20 < 50$ → left; $20 < 30$ → left | Insert as left child of 30 | `50 → 30(20), 70` |
